Atherosclerosis
Atherosclerosis is a disease of the arteries in which fatty material and plaque are deposited in the wall of an artery, resulting in narrowing of the arterial lumen and eventual impairment of blood flow.
When atherosclerosis builds up in coronary arteries we can get an MI
Blood clot and atherosclerosis

Cardiac Cycle
Systole and Diastole : 120/80
Systole = contraction phase (atria and ventricles have a systole)
Diastole = relaxation/refilling phase

Cardiac cycle
Atrial systole occurs in order to fill the ventricles up with the last bit of blood remaining in the left and rt atrium.
Ventricles usually fill automatically, up to about 70%
Atrial systole fills that last 30%

Cardiac Cycle
Blood fills the ventricles and the mmHg in the ventricles rises . When the muscular walls of the ventricles contract we have ventricular systole . Increased pressure in the ventricles and contraction of the papillary mm on the chordae tendineae both insure A-V valve closure. At the same time atrial pressure is now low (lower than venous pressure) and the atria can fill with returning blood as the ventricles are getting ready to eject.
Aortic and pulmonic valves are forced open as heart pressure rises to overcome the pulmonic and systemic resistance.
Once mmHg is greater in the heart than the vessels they are pumping into, blood leaves the ventricles. Then heart mmHg falls back down below peripheral resistance (pulmonary aa and aorta) the aortic and pulmonic valves are forced shut again… the heart then relaxes and the ventricles can fill up with blood again (since atria were already filling their pressure will exceed ventricular chamber pressure opening AV valves again).

Cardiac Cycle : Valves closing
During the relaxed phase (diastole) the aortic and pulmonic valves have closed so the heart can fill again (peripheral resistance forces them shut)
During Ventricular contraction (ventricular systole) the back mmHg from the blood getting squeezed in the ventricles forces the tricuspid and mitral valves shut (av valves)

Contractions
Relaxation/filling phase

Heart Sounds
1 st heart sound / S1 (lubb) – AV valves slamming shut as the ventricles contract to pump blood
…occurs during ventricular systole
2 nd heart sound / S2 (dubb) – aortic and pulmonic valves slam shut as peripheral resistance becomes > heart mmHg
….occurs during ventricular diastole

Abnormal heart sounds
Murmurs
Diastolic murmurs = arms and parts or ARMS PRTS
…During diastole the ventricles are refilling so AV valves should be open (if stenosis occurs = turbulent blood flow) while the pulmonic and aortic should be closed (unless they are regurgitating)
Systolic murmurs = aortic or pulmonic stenosis / mitral or tricuspid regurgitation
Murmurs are typically due to problems with the valves closing (regurgitant murmurs) or by difficulty of blood passing through a narrowed opening (stenotic murmurs).
AV valves

Where do we auscultate for heart sounds?
A PET Monkey
At the aortic, pulmonic valves, erb’s point (best place to auscultate S2), and the tricuspid and mitral valves.

Conducting a heartbeat signal
Cardiac muscle fibers fxn similarly to skeletal muscle fibers but remember that the fibers connect in branching networks . Stimulation to any part of the network sends impulses throughout the heart.
Throughout the heart are clumps and strands of specialized cardiac muscle tissue whose fibers are not their to necessarily contract, but rather send signals. They comprise the cardiac conduction system .
Functional Syncytium – fibers of muscle that conduct/contract at the same time; a mass of merging cells that act as a single unit.
Two fxnal syncytium in the heart = atrial syncytium and ventricular syncytium .
The cardiac conduction system coordinates the events of the cardiac cycle
The signal starts at the pacemaker of the heart which is the SA node or sinoatrial node. This signal is usually spontaneously generated at about 80x per minute in the adult but typically slowed down by the vagus nerve to 70bpm.

Conduction pathway
SA node- small, elongated mass of specialized cardiac muscle tissue it is in the right atrium near the opening of the sup 		vena cava. Its fibers are continous with the atrial syncytium. Reaches threshold spontaneously
in response to mv’mt of Ca++, Na+, and K+ ion concentrations
Atrial syncytium- mass of merging cells that act together in the atrium; starts with internodal atrial muscle which carry signals
to more distant areas of the atrium then the gap jxns (connections between m fibers) spread the signal around 		so the atria can contract together at virually the same time.
Junctional fibers- fibers of the conduction system which are continuous with atrial muscle fibers
AV node (atrioventricular)- located in the inf portion of the interatrial septum;  slowly receives signals from 		junctional fibers and transmits the signal inferiorly along to the AV bundle. (this is necessary because
the signal cannot pass through the fibrous skeleton of the heart p. 560)
AV bundle (of His)- larger fibers that transmit the signal more rapidly starting at the upper part on the 				interventricular septum
Bundle branches- continue down the rt and left portion of the septum and give rise to the purkinje fibers.
Purkinje fibers- large fibers that carry impulses to distant portions of the ventricles
Ventricular syncytium- ventricles contract N-SYNC

APHY 102

Digestion
Breakdown of foods into forms that the cells can absorb (through the cell membrane).
2 types:
Mechanical- physically breaks down large pieces into smaller pieces without altering their chemical nature (e.g. chewing, churning)
Chemical – breaks larger chemicals into smaller chemicals; largely dependent on catabolic enzymes.

Digestive system
The digestive tract is a muscular tube from the mouth to the anus with different regions specialized for different digestive/absorptive functions. Overall the organs of the digestive system are responsible for ingestion, propulsion, digestion, absorption, and defecation.
Mouth
Pharynx
Esophagus
Stomach
Small intestines
Large intestines (colon)
Rectum (anus)
Salivary glands
Liver
Gall Bladder
Pancreas
Alimentary canal
Accessory organs

Smooth Muscle
Most of the organs of the alimentary tract have to move food along to assimilate and excrete. Thus we need muscle tissue to do this. Making up a large portion of many of the organs in the alimentary canal is smooth muscle.
Smooth muscle- lacks striations, shorter fibers than skeletal, involuntary in nature.
In our alimentary canal the canal itself is changed in shape and size by circular and longitudinal fibers of smooth muscle.

One big tube
The alimentary canal is considered “open to the world”. It starts as an opening and ends as an opening.
4 layers of the alimentary canal:
Mucosal layer- makes mucus and digestive enzymes; function = protection and absorption
Submucosal layer- has many vessels (blood and lymph), nourishes tissue around itself, and carries away absorbed nutrients
Muscular layer- provides muscular contractions (peristalsis and segmentation); 2 layers of muscles running in two different directions. Circular muscles can constrict the tubes diameter, longitudinal muscles can shorten the length of the tube.
Serosa - outermost layer, allows digestive organs to move against each other with very little friction. What type of serous membrane would this be?

a lacteal is a lymphatic vessel that absorbs digested fat
Villi are fingerlike projections used to increase surface area on the mucosal surface

Mixing and moving food in the tube
Segmentation- non-rhythmical contraction of the alimentary canal, allows foods to mix with digestive enzymes and break down.
Peristalsis- rhythmic contraction of tubes of smooth muscle in the canal that propels food forward.
http://nutrition.jbpub.com/resources/animations.cfm?id=1&debug=0 [http://nutrition.jbpub.com/resources/animations.cfm?id=1&debug=0]
Reverse peristalsis = ?

Innervation of GI system
GI is controlled by the autonomic nervous system (apart from some conscious sphincter control)
Parasympathetic stimulates digestion, secretions, absorption, and excretion. Signals are sent to the nerve plexus of the GI tract mostly from the vagus nerve and additionally from pelvic splanchnic nn .
Sympathetic puts the brakes on digestion by closing down sphincters.
It is important to note that the GI system works on an extremely complex set of nerve plexi . The submucosal plexus and myenteric plexus.
Submucosal (aka Meissner’s ) plexus- controls secretions
Myenteric (aka Auerbach’s ) plexus- controls muscular layer and GI motility

Quick run-through
Food
Mouth- masticates and supplies salivary amylase, turns food into a bolus
Esophagus-takes to stomach
Stomach- breaks down protein, holds food, churns food
Small intestines- receives enzymes from pancreas to break down fat and carbs , receives bile from gallbladder to help with the fat.
Absorbs most of the nutrients
Large intestines- some absorption, dehydrates the waste, draws in most of the dietary water
Rectum- defecates (poops)

Mouth
Fxn of mouth: receives food, chews food, mixes with saliva ,and begins digestion
Chewing of food is known as mastication . Muscles for this process include those described in TIME. What nerve controls these?
First digestive enzyme comes into play- salivary amylase , begins breakdown of sugar.
Food is turned into a mucous covered bolus thanks to our salivary glands (parotid, submandibular , and sublingual). The bolus was chewed in order to increase surface area and the saliva allows easy passage down the esophagus.
Tongue- helps with mastication and has the taste buds.

Cheeks and lips
Not just for kissing
The cheeks form the lateral walls of the mouth and consist of skin, fat, muscles, and an inner moist lining.
Lips – our oral sphincters  , made up of skeletal mm, sensory receptors, and skin. The lips are also highly vascularized . This gives them their normal red color. So why in some cases do they turn blue or cyanotic (gr. dark blue).

Tongue
Located on the floor of the oral cavity, anchored to that floor by a frenulum ; the root of the tongue is anchored by the hyoid bone. The lingual tonsils are located on the root ( fxn = immunity)
Composed of skeletal muscle which is innervated by which nerve?
Fxn : mix food particles, push food to the back when swallowing, taste our food using its taste buds. Again, which nerves pick up taste?

Palate
Roof of the oral cavity; consists of hard anterior portion and a soft posterior portion.
Hard palate is formed by the palatine processes of the maxillary bones and the palatine bones.
Soft palate is composed of mucous membranes and muscle.
Uvula- downward extension of the soft palate that prevents food or fluid from entering the nasal cavity.

Muscles of mastication are controlled by CN V (mandibular division of trigeminal)
Taste sensation is carried by CN’s VII and IX and tongue movement is controlled by CN XII

Teeth
32 permanent or adult teeth- composed of two incisors, a cuspid (canine), two bicuspids, and three molars on either side, top and bottom. Fxn - mechanical breakdown of food.
3 rd set of molars are our wisdom teeth.
Crown= portion of above the gum line while the root = below the gum line; the neck is where these two areas meet. The root canal is located in the root of a tooth and contains blood vessels and nerves.
Enamel- hardest substance in the body is composed of calcium salts.
Periodontal ligament anchors the tooth in the jaw.

32 permanent or secondary teeth in the adult
Enamel – outermost covering of tooth, hardest substance in the body
Dentin- boneike, living cellular material within the enamel, surrounds the inner pulp
Pulp- central cavity filled with blood vessels, nerves, and connective tissue.
Cementum- bonelike material that surrounds the root of the tooth.

Salivary glands and saliva
3  major salivary glands:
Parotid- largest; located ant and inf to each ear (affected by the mumps); secretes watery serous fluid rich in amylase
Submandibular - located on floor of mouth on the inside surface of the lower jaw; secretes primarily watery serous fluid
Sublingual – located on the floor of the mouth inferior to the tongue; secretes primarily mucus.
The salivary glands are innervated by sympathetic and parasympathetic innervation . Parasympathetic causes a large amount of watery fluid to be produced. Sympathetic causes a small amount of viscous salive .

Moving down
As the food is swallowed the soft palate raises (prevents food from entering nasal cavity), the hyoid bone and larynx rise while the epiglottis (a flaplike structure attached to the top of the layrnx ) shuts off the trachea (windpipe), and muscles contract to send food down.
Food travels down the esophagus using peristalsis. The esophagus itself must pass through the diaphragm via the esophageal hiatus on its way to the stomach.
Food enters the stomach via the cardiac sphincter (lower esophageal sphincter).

Stomach
J-shaped organ located just inferior to the diaphragm in the upper left portion of the abdominal cavity.
Has thick folds lining the interior called rugae .
Fxn : mix food with gastric juice, begin protein digestion, very small amt of absorption, moves the food into the small intestines (where most absorption takes place of nutrients)
4 major portions: cardiac, fundic , body, and pyloric
High in HCl - begins to denature protein, protects body from harmful substances (some bacteria on our foods)
Has many cell types lining the walls all contributing to the gastric juice:
mucous cells- protects stomach from its own acid
chief cells- produce digestive enzymes ( pepsinogen )
parietal ( oxynctic ) cells- produce HCl and intrinsic factor (for B12 absorption)
When pepsinogen comes in contact with HCl it turns into pepsin which helps with protein digestion (turns large proteins into polypeptides)
Absorption in the stomach is very small- we absorb some alcohol and some drugs in the stomach, but most absorption occurs in the small intestines.

Regulation of gastric secretion and absorption in the stomach
Parasympathetic innervation stimulates the release of gastric juice along with gastrin (which we will discuss later)
Inhibition of gastric secretions come from somatostatin (an inhibitory hormone produced in several places in the GI tract and hypothalamus) and CCK.
Absorption in the stomach- small amounts of water along with some salts, drugs, and alcohol.

Stomach to small intestines
When food exits the stomach, it passes through the pyloric sphincter and enters the duodenum as chyme (partially broken down food mixed with HCl ). This is where most of the magic happens.
Small intestines is divided into the duodenum , jejunum , and ileum . The jejunum and ileum are suspended from the posterior abdominal wall by a double-layered fold of peritoneum called mesentery which supports the blood vessels, nerves, and lymphatic vessels that supply the intestinal wall. Draping over the small intestines is the greater omentum which prevents the spread of infection to other parts of the peritoneal cavity.
Most nutrient absorption occurs in the small intestines which is lined by millions of intestinal villi giving the internal surface a velvety appearance. These villi house many of our digestive enzymes in their membranes along with blood vessels and lacteals for absorption.

Small intestines to large intestines
After most nutrients have been absorbed from our food the leftovers (fiber and water) move through the ileocecal valve into the colon (large intestines).
Colon:
- Absorbs excess water and electrolytes
- Absorbs some vitamins and nutrients
- Is packed with bacteria or intestinal flora to finish digesting and absorbing (about the last 5-10% of nutrients). While they break down certain foods they can also synthesize vitamins for us such as K, B12, thiamine , and riboflavin.
- Forms and stores feces
Moves our contents out of the body through mass movements (commonly known as bowel movements) stimulated by a meal or the gastrocolic reflex.
*about 50% of the dry weight of our feces is bacteria*

Colon
Gets the name large intestines because its diameter is greater than that of the small intestines.
Consists of a cecum (first portion), ascending, transverse, descending, sigmoid, and rectal (which terminates as the anal canal).
Begins on lower right side of abdominal cavity where it ascends, crosses over to the left, then descends toward the pelvis. The most distal portion opens to the outside of the body as Uranus…. i mean the anus.
The typical appearance of the intestines ( kinda like a string of sausages) is created by a series of pouches called haustra which is formed by the constriction of longitudinal bands of muscles called teniae coli. While on the outer surface we see small collections of fat called appendaces epiploicae in the peritoneum.

Hormones, enzymes, and bile
In order to digest and absorb nutrients that we consume we need multiple hormones, enzymes, and secretions produced in various places in the body.
Main players here are the stomach, liver (gallbladder), and the pancreas.
If an organ is contributing to digestion and absorption but not actively involved with the movement of the food itself it is called an accessory organ of the digestive (GI) system.

Gastrin
Gastrin is a horomone produced in the stomach in response to food. Its main purpose is to stimulate gastric juice ( pepsinogen , HCl , mucus, etc..) secretion.
3 phases of gastrin secretion:
-cephalic phase – thoughts of food or when food is heading towards the stomach. Smell, taste, chewing can all stimulate the parasympathetic activity that releases gastrin .        			    (30-50%)
-gastric phase- presence of food in the stomach and extension of the stomach             (40-50%)
-intestinal phase- food is exiting the stomach and moving into the duodenum                        (5%)

CCK
Cholecystokinin - released by the intestinal wall. CCK is released in the proximal portion of the duodenum in response to the presence of fats and proteins entering into the intestines.
Purpose of CCK is to slow gastric motility.
Effect of CCK is hormonal- it enters the blood just outside the duodenum, tells the stomach to slow down and tells the pancreas to release digestive enzymes into the small intestines. At the same time it also tells the gallbladder to contract (we will see why in a jiffy)

Secretin
Secretin - again, made in the duodenal membrane. This hormone is produced in the presence of acidic chyme entering from the pyloric sphincter.
Secretin acts hormonally to tell the pancreas to send in bicarbonate ions to decrease the acidity entering the small intestines.

Bile
Bile is made in the liver and stored in the gallbladder.
Composed of bilirubin and biliverdin (bile pigments), bile salts, cholesterol, and electrolytes.
Bile is used to emulsify fats or break them down into little droplets that are easier to handle (both digestively and absorptive).
Bile is able to enter the duodenum via the bile duct when the gallbladder contracts in response to CCK.
Without bile, it would be very difficult to absorb vitamins A, D, E, and K (our fat soluble vitamins)
*bile pigments in the skin=jaundice*

Pancreas and its enzymes
The pancreas is the big player in producing the enzymes we use to break down our foods. The small intestines can further break down food with its enzymes in the intestinal wall, but pancreatic enzymes do the majority of the big work.
Pancreas provides the enzymes to break down carbs , fats, proteins, and nucleic acids. These enzymes are made in acinar cells of the pancreas.
Pancreatic amylase- breaks down sugars ( carbs )
Pancreatic lipase- breaks down the fats
Trypsin , chymotrypsin , and carboxypeptidase - break down the proteins
Nucleases - break down nucleic acids
* Trypsin starts out as trypsinogen until it reaches the small intestines where enterokinase turns it into its active form. Trypsin then activates chymotrypsin and carboxypeptidase.*
Relative location of esophagus, stomach, and pancreas in relation to the liver.

How do bile and pancreatic secretions reach the duodenum?
Coming from the gall bladder is the bile duct and coming from the pancreas is the pancreatic duct. When these two ducts reach the duodenum they join to form the ampulla of vater . This ampulla (or enlargement of a duct) is surrounded by the sphincter of Oddi

Intestinal Enzymes
Once bile and pancreatic enzymes have broken down the bulk of food we can use enzymes produced in our intestinal wall to finish the job.
Brunner’s glands- glands in the intestinal wall making mucous. Again, helps move stuff along
Microvilli - covered with peptidases (breaks peptides into aa’s ), sucrase , maltase, lactase (breaks down disaccharides sucrose, maltose, and lactose down into glucose, fructose, and galactose ) and intestinal lipase (splits fats into fatty acids and glycerol)

Absorption
Now that we have broken down the foods, we have to absorb the useful stuff.
The villi and microvilli increase small intestinal surface area enormously and the villi contain the vessels where the nutrients are absorbed.
Sugars and amino acids are absorbed by facilitated diffusion or active transport into the villi , then into the blood (circulation)
Fats dissolve through the villi membranes, reform triglycerides, and then attach to protein to move into the lymphatic system as chylomicrons before heading to the blood.

Removal of wastes
Proximal portion of large intestines reabsorbs the water and electrolytes, the rest passes through, feeds our useful bacteria, and leaves the body with a BM
Bowel movement via defecation reflex:
By holding the breath and contracting abdominal wall the feces is pushed down the rectum via the increased internal abdominal pressure. The rectum fills, distends, and triggers peristalsis in the smooth muscles of the descending colon. The internal anal sphincter relaxes followed by the external anal sphincter.

Anatomy of the GI tract
Please be familiar with the anatomy of the GI tract as well. This will cover a large portion of our lab exam. Also be able to trace a portion of food through the entire tract and know at which point it will come in contact with enzymes and where those enzymes come from.

3 types of Muscle
Skeletal- usually attached to bones, striated, multinucleated, long fibers, VOLUNTARY
Cardiac- found in the walls of the heart, single nucleus, self exciting, whole structure will contract as a syncytium, fibers are joined by intercalated discs, these mm are involuntary
Smooth- found in the walls of most viscera, blood vessels, and the skin, single nucleus, shorter fibers, has no troponin but instead has calmodulin that attaches to calcium for contraction. Slower to contract but can contract longer with same amount of ATP, these muscles are involuntary.
Visceral smooth- gut, has gap junctions, has rhythmicity (as in peristalsis), responds to stretch.

Skeletal muscle
A skeletal muscle is an organ (by definition of an organ) composed primarily of four things:
-skeletal muscle tissue- contracts
-nervous tissue- innervates
-blood- supplies oxygen, removes 			waste
-other connective tissues

Fxns of Skeletal Muscle
Mvmt via attachments to skeleton, other muscles, and skin (e.g. facial expression (levator labii superioris aliqui nasi = elvis), palmaris brevis)
Body heat- contraction of mm gives off heat and helps maintain homeostasis (e.g. shivering)
Blood flow- contraction of leg mm largely contributes to venous return

Related structures
Fascia- tough, film-like, dense connective tissue that surrounds, holds in place, and separates mm.
The particular type associated directly with the outside portion of the muscle is called deep fascia.
Tendon- projections from the connective tissue at the ends of a muscle that attaches muscle to bone; tendon fibers intertwine with periosteal fibers of the bone.
(ligaments attaches bone to bone)
Aponeurosis- flat sheet of connective tissue that acts like a tendon, attaching muscle to muscle or muscle to bone.

Separation of muscle fibers
More connective tissues surrounding muscle:
Epimysium- forms the outermost layer of the muscle (but lies within the fascia)
Perimysium- projects inward from the epimysium and surrounds fascicles (cords of muscle fibers within the muscle)
Endomysium – within the fascicles there is yet a further division of muscle called a muscle fiber…this division is surrounded by the endomysium
So…again : muscle – epimysium
fascicle - perimysium
m. fiber - endomysium
Epi- Greek for upon or over
Peri- Greek for around or near
Endo- Greek for within or inward
All of these layers of connective tissue enclose and separate all parts of a skeletal muscle. This organization allows for parts to move somewhat independently. Also, many nerves and blood vessels pass through these layers.

hierarchy of the muscle organ
muscle- all inclusive
fascicle- bundle of muscle fibers
fiber- muscle cell itself
each fiber forms from many undifferentiated cells that fuse during fetal development
resulting muscle cell is thus a thin, elongated cylinder with rounded ends that attach to the connective tissues associated with the muscle
myofibril- long thread-like structures that run parallel and extend through the sarcoplasm as fibers. They consist of two types of protein filaments:
actin- thin filament
myosin- thick filament
The way the actin and myosin are organized yields a striated appearance. The striations appear as light and dark in color and repeat along the fiber. This organizational structure is referred to as a sarcomere . Sarcomeres attached end to end yield the myofibrils.
Separation of muscle fibers
Skeletal muscle anatomy/phys

Skeletal muscle fiber terms
Again, it is important to note that when we talk about a skeletal muscle fiber, it is the same thing as saying a skeletal muscle cell.
Surrounding these fibers is a specialized muscle-cell membrane called a sarcolemma . The sarcolemma is composed of a true cell membrane (aka plasma membrane) along with an outer coat that fuses at the end of each muscle with tendon fibers. The tendon fibers group together to attach the muscle to its origin/insertion.

Skeletal muscle anatomy/phys
Again, in skeletal muscle we have a striated type appearance. These striations are composed of actin, myosin, their overlapping region, and their attachment points.
I band – light colored portion of band. Made up of the thin actin filaments.
A band – dark colored portion of band.
Made up on actin-myosin overlapping and of the thick myosin bands by themselves (H zone)
Z line – where the actin is attached and held in place
M line – where the myosin is attached and held in place
The Z line will also attach to myosin but not directly. Holding myosin to the Z-line will be  a large protein called titin (connectin)

Skeletal muscle terms
Sarcoplasm- the cytoplasm of a muscle cell; contains K+, Mg++, phosphate, and many mitochondria (which produce ATP). Myofibrils are suspended in the sarcoplasm within each muscle fiber.
Sarcoplasmic Reticulum- encircles and follows along parallel to the myofibrils; houses much of the Ca++ needed for contraction.
T-tubules (transverse) – membranous channels that extend into the sarcoplasm containing extracellular fluid (e.g. Na+ used when depolarizing cell)
Each t-tubule lies between two enlarged portions of the sarcoplasmic reticulum called cisternae.
Together, the t-tubules and the cisternae of the SR form a structure called a triad.

Skeletal muscle contraction
The ability of the skeletal muscle to contract is dependent upon actin and myosin joining together.
Myosin filaments are composed of a head and a tail. The head can attach to actin filaments forming a cross-bridge. When the cross-bridge forms, myosin is able to draw in actin and pull the sarcomere in (shortenening the distance from Z line to Z line)… yielding a contraction.
All together, this is known as the sliding filament mechanism
Skeletal muscle contraction

Skeletal muscle contraction
Steps needed to make a skeletal muscle contract:
An action potential travels in a motor nerve to its endings (myoneural jxn) on muscle fibers.
At each ending, the nerve secretes ACh
Channels open along the muscle to allow Na+ to flow in…this creates an action potential in the muscle fiber
An action potential flows along the muscle fiber similar to its flow along a nerve
The action potential depolarizes the muscle membrane. This causes the sarcoplasmic reticulum to release large quantities of Ca++.
The calcium ions initiate attractive forces between actin and myosin.
A fraction of a second later the calcium is pumped back into the sarcoplasmic reticulum and the muscle relaxes…waiting for next action potential.

Skeletal muscle contraction
Myoneural jxn?
Depolarization?
What keeps actin and myosin from binding all the time?

Stimulus for contraction
Synapse- a small place between a nerve and another cell (which can be another nerve or a cell it wishes to effect)
In muscle physiology we will more appropriately call the space where the axon and target muscle meet a neuromuscular (myoneural) junction.
The nerve providing a signal secretes chemical messengers called neurotransmitters at its presynaptic endplate. These neurotransmitters travel across a synaptic cleft and stimulate receptors on the postsynaptic endplate of another cell.
In this case, the neuron responsible for stimulating a particular muscle is called a motor neuron , the postsynaptic endplates are called motor endplates, and the union between the nerve and the muscle fibers it controls is called a motor unit .
In skeletal muscle cells, this neurotransmitter is acetylcholine or Ach which is synthesized in the cytoplasm of the motor neuron and stored in synaptic vesicles near the distal end of the axon.
When the ACh is secreted at the myoneural or neuromuscular junction is adheres to receptors, opens Na+ channels, and this begins the muscle impulse which travels in multiple directions depolarizing the muscle.
Going back to Neuro

Action potentials (a little more neuro)
Action Potential- exchange of ions inside and outside of a cell that cause depolarization . This depolarization can send a signal along the cell very rapidly and over large distances.
Typically involves Na+ and K+ exchange.
Sodium and potassium are known as electrolytes (charged particles from compounds that dissociated in water) … so just think of it as “turning on the electricity” when they rapidly move inside and outside the cell membranes.

Action potentials
Depolarization phase- Na+ channels open.
Peak- Na+ channels close
Repolarization phase- K+ channels open.
Na+
Na+
Na+
K+
K+
K+

Action Potential along a muscle:
Typically when we speak of action potentials we think of nerve-nerve communication, but the action potential in a motor unit works very similar.
In the case of nerve-muscle stimulus, a nerve sends a signal, muscle reacts to ACh at the motor endplate and opens local Na+ channels. The opening of these channels depolarizes the muscle fiber sending a “muscle impulse” along and around the entire strand of muscle fiber. This impulse allows Na+ to enter from the t-tubules, depolarizing the muscle tissue.
But, there is more to it than this…

Actin and myosin regulation
In order for the muslce to actually come to contraction, though, the actin and myosin must come in contact with each other.
So, what else is needed for contraction?
ATP and the movement of troponin and tropomyosin

Troponin and tropomyosin:
Tropomyosin is a strand of rod-shaped molecules that lie around the actin molecules (thin filament)
Troponin is a cluster of 3 proteins that holds tropomyosin in its place.
Troponin has specialized Ca++ receptors on its surface that react to calcium by changing shape or conformation. Calcium is easily released from the sarcoplasmic reticulum during muscle impulse due to increased permeability of the cisternae.

Troponin and Tropomyosin
TROPOMYOSIN - In a relaxed muscle, the MYOSIN HEADS of the thick myofilament lie against TROPOMYOSIN (blocking the myosin binding sites of actin) molecules of the thin myofilament. As long as the myosin heads remain in contact with tropomyosin nothing happens (id est,  a muscle remains relaxed).
TROPONIN - Troponin molecules have binding sites for calcium ions. When a calcium ion fills this site it causes a change in the shape (conformation) and position of troponin (which holds the tropomyosin in place). And, when troponin shifts, it pulls the tropomyosin to which it is attached. When TROPOMYOSIN is moved, the MYOSIN HEAD that was touching the tropomyosin now comes in contact with an underlying ACTIN molecule.
Troponin and Tropomyosin

Actin binding with myosin
ATP cocks the myosin heads back to attach to the actin binding sites that are now available
ATP
Cocked myosin head
ATP

Continued shortening of the sarcomere
Once the myosin head cocks back to adhere to the actin molecules it slightly bends toward the center line, drawing actin in slightly. Then it will release actin, straighten out, grab a new, closer actin molecule and repeat the same process. This process is called cross bridge cycling and requires ATP attachment and release to continue.

Voila…We have lift-off… Contraction of muscle occurs
We ARE figure skating……..BOOM!!!
- Chaz Michael Michaels

Summary of muscle contraction:
3 things needed for a muscle contraction:
1. neural stimulation- in the form of the neurotransmitter ________
2. Ca++ - which attaches to _____to cause a translocation of _______...freeing up the binding sites
3. ATP – which ____the ___ ____

Twitch, summation, tetany
Single muscle fiber fires, pulls on its attachment, then relaxes = twitch (following a twitch we will have a short latent period before the same fiber can fire again).
One or more muscle fibers fire, begin to relax, more fire, begin to relax, and so on = summation (this can create a sustained contraction due to the muscle fiber not being able to ‘fully’ relax before the next stimulus)
Tetany – fibers fire and none relax (gets stuck)

Gauging objects and recruitment:
We can pretty accurately guess the actual weight of objects and prepare our muscles for what they need to do. When looking at the whole muscle (organ) the force developed reflects 2 things:
1- the frequency at which individual muscle fibers are stimulated
2- how many fibers take part in the overall contraction of the muscle.
I pick up a pencil, It seems to be quite light and delicate thus I only stimulate a few (relatively) motor units to engage the pencil
Conversely, I am under a barbell loaded with 400lbs…I gauge the object as very heavy , then I actually take it off the rack and it is much heavier. So what happens then?
In this process I have now deeply stimulated my muscle tissue, this causes more and more motor units to become involved. Thus, I have recruited more help and the process is called recruitment.
It is good to note after this point that muscle involvement is directly proportional to the stimulus applied.

During the recruitment
As intensity of the stimulation increases, recruitment of motor units (many can be found in a single muscle organ)continues until finally all ‘possible’ motor units are activated in that muscle.

Relaxation
ACh is quickly broken down after the nerve impulse is stopped. ACh esterase decomposes ACh and its products move back to its original cell.
*Raid and sarin gas actually inhibits this process*
Ca++ pump moves calcium back into the SR and cross bridge is broken (troponin and tropomyosin translocate to cover binding sites again)
Since Ca++ is actively being ‘pumped’ back in we require ATP for both contraction and relaxation.

Replenishing ATP
During muscle contraction (and relaxation) we use ATP to run the pumps and cock the myosin heads. So how is that ATP replenished?
Creatine phosphate- stores up excess energy being produced by the numerous mitochondria in the cells and transfers this energy to ‘used-up’ ADP molecules.

Replenishing ATP
ATP             ADP + P
Creatine + P combines with ADP to replenish the phosphate and restore ATP
http://nutrition.jbpub.com/resources/animations.cfm?id=32&debug=0 [http://nutrition.jbpub.com/resources/animations.cfm?id=32&debug=0]
energy

Energy for muscles
Where does the ATP come from?
It comes from breaking down sugar. Remember we store sugar in our liver and muscles in the form of glycogen.
Glycogen
Glucose (6 carbons)           2x pyruvate (3C)
Energy + ATP

Energy for muscles
At rest and in non-strenuous activity these pyruvates can move into the mitochodria (which requires O2) to be broken down by the Kreb’s cycle (TCA) aerobic
During strenous activity, O2 runs short and pyruvate can no longer go into mitochondria. Instead, pyruvate converts to lactic acid to produce energy. Anaerobic
Lactic acid will eventually accumulate to the point of muscle failure (decrease pH will make muscle tissue increasingly more difficult to stimulate). When the body rests O2 is taken back into the body. The body then uses that O2 to make more ATP to convert lactic acid back to glucose in the liver. However long or however much oxygen is required by the liver to do this is called the oxygen debt.

Types of contractions
Isotonic - contraction of a muscle that shortens the muscle.
2 types: concentric and eccentric
Isometric – contraction of a muscle that does not shorten the muscle (postural muscles)

Types of contractions
Isotonic concentric – muscle contracts, shortens, and moves an object against gravity
Isotonic eccentric – muscle contracts, shortens, and guides an object with gravity
(negative portion of a weight lifting exercise)
Lat pull downs are a good example of these movements.

Degree of muscle contraction
When we are gauging an object to lift we can estimate how much the object will weigh through past experiences.
When we are lifting heavy objects or prepare ourselves to lift heavy objects we go through a process called recruitment in which increased numbers of muscle fibers become involved. Recruitment is proportional to the intensity of the stimulus.

Muscle tonus
Even when muscle appear to be at rest, its fibers undergo a certain degree of sustained contraction. This is called the muscle tone (or tonus). The tone is dependent on constant nervous innervation coming from the cord or brain.
Tone allows for posture, holding the body upright.
Certain diseases or injury to the nerve can alter tone and overall motor function.

Types of muscle fibers
Slow twitch muscle fibers- (Type I) always oxidative, are red in color (due to myoglobin), contain a large amount of mitochondria (to produce lots of ATP), and are difficult to fatigue. E.g. postural mm of the back
Fast twitch (Type IIa and Type IIb) may be oxidative or 	glycolytic
Type IIa- fatigue resistant fast twitch fibers, aka intermediate fibers due to its ability to act like both type I and type II
Type IIb- fast twitch glycolytic fibers, dependent on the breakdown of stored glycogen, white in color due to less myoglobin and poorer blood supply. Faster to work but much quicker to fatigue.
Many muscles with specialized function may have more of one type of fiber than another. All muscles include a combination of fiber types.

Smooth Muscle contraction
Again, smooth muscle is found througout the body but most is associated with the hollow internal organs of the gut. Two attributes that visceral smooth muscle has that makes it unique are rhythmicity and the ability to transmit impulses from cell to cell.
Contraction is similar to skeletal muscle contraction, yet in this case troponin is not present and it is replaced by a calcium binder called calmodulin. Also, most Ca++ used for contraction comes from the ECF.
Both ACh and NE affect smooth muscle contraction
Various hormones (e.g. oxytocin) and stretching can also affect contraction.
Smooth muscle is slower to contract and relax but can sustain muscle contraction much longer with the same amount of ATP.

Cardiac Muscle
Appears only in the heart, formed from striated cells joined end to end forming fibers interconnected in branching, three-dimensional networks.
Again, much of the calcium will come from the ECF, thus extracellular calium levels can affect the overall function of the heart.
One one fiber fires, they will work as a interconnected unit called a syncytium. Reactions occur in an all-or-none pattern.
Once contracted, the heart muscle has a longer refractory period than skeletal muscle, thus it cannot maintain a sustained contraction. All must relax before it can contract again.

Origins, Insertions, and Innervation
Origin – unmovable end
Insertion – movable end (moves twd origin)
Innervation – a particular nerve that controls a muscle is said to innervate that muscle. E.g. Long thoracic nerve innervates the serratus anterior.

Agonist, antagonist, and synergists
Prime mover or agonist – does most of the work for a particular mvmt. E.g. Deltoid is the agonist of arm abduction.
Synergist- helps agonists keep smooth control of joint, lever, or action. E.g. supraspinatus and rotator cuff for abduction of the arm
Antagonist – moves joint in opposite direction than the agonist. E.g. bicep is the agonist for flexion, tricep does extension, thus tricep is the antagonist of the bicep.
*crossed extensor reflex*
Agonist / antagonist

[Speaker Notes] Chapter 5, Opener, The structures of biomolecules define their functions. The elaborate structures of proteins are determined by the structures and chemical properties of their constituent amino acids. The relationship between structure and function in biochemistry is dramatically illustrated by diseases such as cystic fibrosis and sickle-cell anemia, which arise as a result of a single amino acid mutation that leads to loss of normal protein function. A change in amino acid structure at a specific location in the oxygen-carrying protein hemoglobin results in the undesired polymerization of hemoglobin in sickle-cell patients. Mutation of the normal, highly polar, glutamic acid in this position to the mutant, nonpolar, valine creates a hydrophobic surface that leads to association of the mutant hemoglobins. What are the features of amino acids that determine the structures of proteins? How do mutations affect protein structure and function?

[Speaker Notes] Chapter 7, Opener, Fluorescence microscopy image of a mouse immortalized cell line. The cells have been modified, and different human genes have been transfected into the cells so that they can be used as a tool for study of the human immune system. The cells have been stained with fluorescent antibodies for different cell structures. [Speaker Notes] Chapter 8, Opener, Many different therapeutic drugs have been developed to treat HIV infection. The three shown here—azidothymidine (AZT), nevirapine, and saquinivir—all have different modes of action against two enzymes critical to the HIV life cycle. AZT is a substrate analog that causes premature DNA chain termination. Nevirapine distorts the enzyme active site and thereby inhibits reverse transcription. Saquinivir blocks the active site of the HIV protease. How does knowledge of enzyme structure and mechanism help researchers to develop new drugs?

[Speaker Notes] Chapter 9, Opener, Complex polysaccharides in bacterial cell walls, the site of action of antibiotics. The cell surface of Gram-positive bacteria consists of a lipid bilayer membrane surrounded by complex polymers of sugars (abbreviated here as NAM and NAG) crosslinked by short peptide chains. Penicillin inhibits the crosslinking reaction. This causes weakening of the cell wall, which, coupled with continued growth of the bacteria, makes it impossible for cells to resist internal pressure, and eventually they rupture.

1.1 The Science of Biochemistry

The Origin of Biochemistry (1 of 4)
In 1828, the German chemist Friedrich Wöhler synthesized urea, a known urinary excretion product of mammals and hence a substance of biological origin, from ammonium cyanate, an inorganic mineral, in the laboratory

The Origin of Biochemistry (2 of 4)
Fermentation: an ancient application of biochemistry
Figure 1.1 An ancient application of biochemistry.

The Origin of Biochemistry (3 of 4)
In 1897, the German chemists Eduard and Hans Buchner found that extracts from broken yeast cells could carry out the entire process of fermentation of sugar into ethanol
Biological catalysts, known as enzymes, promote biochemical reactions in living systems
Synthesis of enzymes is controlled by genes
Genes are the units of hereditary information, encoded in the structure of deoxyribonucleic acid (D   N  A)

The Origin of Biochemistry (4 of 4)
In 1953, the American biologist James Watson and the English physicist Francis Crick described the double-helical structure of D N A
Figure 1.2 James Watson and Francis Crick with their hand-assembled wire model of the structure of D N A.
Defining Biochemistry

New Research Methods
Figure 1.3 The recent history of biochemistry as shown by the introduction of new research techniques.
1.2 The Elements and Molecules of Living Systems

Chemical Elements of Cells and Organisms (1 of 2)
Living systems are primarily composed of carbon (C), hydrogen (H), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N)
These are also the most abundant elements
However, other elements including sulfur (S) and
phosphorus (P) and certain ions, such as
are essential for the existence of
living systems on Earth as well

Chemical Elements of Cells and Organisms (2 of 2)
Figure 1.4 Periodic table pertinent to biochemistry.

Origin of Living Systems
It has been demonstrated (Stanley Miller’s experiments from 1953) that certain building blocks of complex biomolecules, such as amino acids, could have been produced under early (abiotic) Earth conditions (“Primordial Soup”)
How cells as the basic unit of living systems may have formed is unknown
However, many biochemists believe that ancient cells had already a ribonucleic acid (R N A)-based self-replication mechanism (“R N A World”)

Biological Macromolecules (1 of 6)
Four major classes of biological macromolecules are structurally and functionally essential to build and maintain living systems based on cells:
Nucleic acids (D N A and R N A)
Proteins
Polysaccharides (polymeric carbohydrates)
Lipids
These macromolecules are made up of smaller organic molecule subunits (e.g., amino acids are subunits or building blocks of proteins)

1.3 Distinguishing Characteristics of Living Systems

Seven Pillars of Life (1 of 2)
In 2002, the American biochemist Daniel Koshland summarized the essential attributes that distinguish living from nonliving things*:
A program (organized plan for constitution and regeneration; D N A)
Improvisation (changing the program as surroundings change; evolution)
Compartmentation (ability to be separate from environment; membranes)

Seven Pillars of Life (2 of 2)
Energy (ability to maintain order despite overall positive entropy)
Regeneration (compensation for environmental wear and tear; repair)
Adaptability (ability to respond to environmental changes)
Seclusion (operation of processes and pathways in isolation)
*From Koshland, D.E. (2002) The seven pillars of life. Science 295:2215-2216.
1.4 The Unit of Biological Organization: The Cell

Three Types of Cells (1 of 5)
Cells are the universal unit of life
There are three different types of cells:
bacterial
archaeal
eukaryotic
Although distinct in many aspects, bacterial and archaeal cells are summarized as prokaryotic cells
Cell type correlates with phylogeny (“Molecular Tree of Life”)

Three Types of Cells (2 of 5)

Based on ribosomal R N A sequence comparisons

1.5 Biochemistry and the Information Explosion

Bioinformatics
New scientific tools and techniques can generate and analyze increasing amount of biochemical and molecular biological information
Bioinformatics can be considered as information science applied to biology. Examples are
mathematical analysis of D N A sequence data
computer simulation of metabolic pathways
analysis of potential drug targets (enzymes or receptors) for structure-based drug design

Omics (1 of 5)
Genetics concerns itself with the location, expression, and function of individual genes or small groups of genes
Genomics expands the genetics approach and concerns itself with the entire genome, the totality of genetic information in an organism

Omics (2 of 5)
Some of the broader goals of genomics are to
determine the nucleotide sequence of the whole genome (of an organism)
assess the expression and function of each gene
understand the evolutionary relationships among genes in the same genome and with genomes of different organisms
Examples of other established “Omics” are Proteomics, Transcriptomics, Metabolomics, and Interactomics

Omics (3 of 5)
Genomics: the genetic landscape of a yeast cell
Figure 1.15 The genetic landscape of a yeast cell.

Omics (4 of 5)
Transcriptomics: the abundance of gene-specific m R N A s indicated by microarrays
Figure 1.13 A D N A microarray.

Omics (5 of 5)
Proteomics: separation of a large number of proteins from a cellular extract by two-dimensional gel electrophoresis
Figure 1.14 A display of the proteome by two-dimensional gel electrophoresis.

Chapter 1 Summary
The aim of biochemistry is to understand and explain living systems in molecular terms
Biochemistry bridges biological and chemical sciences on the level of molecules present in living systems
Living systems are composed of cells, which can be divided into three major types: bacterial, archaeal, and eukaryotic
Biochemistry is an experimental science and uses a variety of different tools and techniques, some of which generate large amount of information

10.1 The Molecular Structure and Behavior of Lipids

Major Functions and Properties of Lipids
Major functions of lipids include: energy storage, membrane structure, and signaling
Unlike carbohydrates, amino acids, nucleotides, or lipids have limited solubility in aqueous media
Most lipids are amphipathic and they contain both hydrophobic and hydrophilic regions

Fatty Acids (1 of 3)
are major constituents of lipids
In a fatty acid, a hydrophilic carboxylate group is attached to one end of a hydrocarbon chain (containing typically between 12 and 24 carbons)
In their hydrocarbon chains, unsaturated fatty acids contain one or more cis C–C double bonds, whereas saturated fatty acids contain none
The fluidity of fatty acids decreases as the chain length increases and the number of cis double bonds decreases

Fats (1 of 2)
In fat (or triacylglyceride), glycerol is esterified with three fatty acids
Fat can be used for metabolic energy storage because its carbon atoms are highly reduced
Fat can act as a source for energy or heat production, and for thermal insulation in organisms

10.2 The Lipid Constituents of Biological Membranes

Lipids, Micelles, Bilayers (1 of 2)
Lipids are major constituents of all biological membranes
Fatty acids tend to form spherical micelles, while lipids with one hydrophilic head group and two hydrophobic tails (hydrocarbon chains) promote the formation of a bilayer, the structure seen in biological membranes
The major classes of membrane-forming lipids are glycerophospholipids, glycoglycerolipids, sphingolipids, and glycerosphingolipids

is the precursor to all steroids

10.3 The Structure and Properties of Membranes and Membrane Proteins

Membrane Structure – The Fluid Mosaic Model (1 of 2)
Biological membranes consist of lipid bilayers, in which membrane proteins are embedded
Biological membranes can be considered as oriented two-dimensional liquids, in which lipids and proteins diffuse more or less freely within their plane (fluid mosaic model)
However, biological membranes also contain some defined structures or domains, such as protein complexes or lipid rafts

The insertion of proteins into the membrane bilayer (tan) is a cotranslational process and requires the ribosome (brown) and the translocon (purple), a multisubunit complex that acts as a protein conducting channel. 10.4 Transport across Membranes

Membrane Transport Processes (1 of 2)
There are three different modes of membrane transport processes:
Nonmediated transport
This mode of transport is slow
Diffusion across a membrane is more rapid for those solutes that are hydrophobic, and significantly slower for polar/charged solutes (membranes are effective barriers to the diffusion of polar/charged molecules)

Membrane Transport Processes (2 of 2)
Facilitated transport
Diffusion of certain solutes is accelerated by specific pores, carriers, or permeases
Active transport
Couples a thermodynamically favorable process (usually A T P hydrolysis) to achieve transport against a concentration gradient

Water Channels: Aquaporins (1 of 2)
Aquaporins are water channels that increase water transport in some tissues: erythrocytes, salivary glands, kidney
Plasma osmolarity varies considerably as the erythrocyte moves through lungs, capillaries, and kidneys, so rapid transport of water is critical to prevent rupture of the erythrocyte membrane
Aquaporins facilitate rapid transport of water and thereby maintain osmotic balance within a cell while preserving critical ion gradients

10.7 Cotransport Systems

The Sodium-Glucose Cotransport System (1 of 2)
In cotransport, the unfavorable movement of a substance through a membrane is coupled to the favorable transport of another substance
In the sodium-glucose cotransport system, the sodium gradient across the membrane provides the driving force for the unfavorable transport of glucose

Chapter 10 Summary (1 of 2)
Lipids are mostly hydrophobic
some are amphipathic, having hydrophobic and hydrophilic regions
A fatty acid consists of a hydrocarbon chain and a terminal carboxyl group
Fatty acids are present in fats (triacylglycerols) for energy storage and insulation, and in membranes as constituents of various types of lipids
Membranes are bilayer structures containing proteins and lipids in a fluid mosaic

Chapter 10 Summary (2 of 2)
Transport through membranes can be accomplished by three different modes:
nonmediated transport (diffusion)
facilitated transport (pores, permeases, or carriers)
active transport (A T P-driven or cotransport)

[Speaker Notes] Chapter 10, Opener, The depletion of neurotransmitters at neuron synapses is associated with some types of depression. Several anti-depressant drugs act by blocking the reuptake of neurotransmitters into presynaptic neurons (see red arrows in figure above). An example is shown by the membrane-bound dopamine transporter (cyan cartoon) bound to two Na∙ ions (purple spheres) and the tricyclic antidepressant nortriptyline (gray spheres). The reuptake of dopamine requires the cotransport of Na∙ ions via a mechanism that is similar to that used to transport amino acids across bacterial mem-branes (as shown by the bacterial LeuT transporter in green cartoon). How is transport of material across membranes regulated? What are the driving forces for transport? How does knowledge of the structures and mechanisms of trans-porters aid in the development of selective inhibitors as useful drugs?

11.1 A First Look at Metabolism

Overview of Metabolism (1 of 3)
Intermediary metabolism: synthesis (anabolic reactions) and degradation (catabolic reactions) of small molecules (metabolic intermediates)
Energy metabolism: intermediary metabolic pathways that generate or store energy
Central pathways: pathways accounting for large amounts of mass transfer and energy generation in a cell (heaviest traffic and highly conserved across organisms)

Overview of Metabolism (2 of 3)
Autotrophs (self feeding): can synthesize all organic
metabolites from
Heterotrophs (feeding on others): require reduced carbon compounds

Overview of Metabolism (3 of 3)
Figure 11.1 A brief overview of metabolism.
11.2 Freeways on the Metabolic Road Map

Central Pathways of Energy Metabolism (1 of 2)
Glycolysis
Citric acid cycle (Krebs cycle, T C A cycle)
Electron transport/oxidative phosphorylation
Fatty acid oxidation
Gluconeogenesis
Fatty acid synthesis
Photosynthesis
In aerobic organisms, all pathways funnel into citric acid cycle

Central Pathways of Energy Metabolism (2 of 2)

Separate Pathways for Biosynthesis and Degradation (1 of 2)
A pathway must be exergonic in the direction in which it proceeds
A pathway must be regulated to meet current physiological requirements
Reciprocal regulation ensures that a futile cycle will not occur, even though both pathways are localized in the same cell compartment (e.g., glycolysis and gluconeogenesis both take place in the cytosol)

Separate Pathways for Biosynthesis and Degradation (2 of 2)
Substrate cycle: enzymes of pathways that operate in opposite directions (glycolysis, gluconeogenesis) show control at enzymes catalyzing exergonic reactions that operate in opposite directions
11.3 Biochemical Reaction Types

General Types of Chemical Transformations (1 of 8)
Five types of chemical transformations are commonly found in living systems (cells):
Nucleophilic substitutions
Nucleophilic additions
Carbonyl condensations
Eliminations
Oxidations/reductions

General Types of Chemical Transformations (2 of 8)
Carbonyl carbons are common electrophiles in nucleophilic substitution reactions

General Types of Chemical Transformations (3 of 8)
Common nucleophiles include oxyanions, thiolates,carbanions, deprotonated amine, and imidazole

General Types of Chemical Transformations (4 of 8)
Nucleophilic substitution reactions
Pentavalent transition state

Nucleophilic Substitution Reactions (1 of 2)
Acyl substitution involves a tetrahedral intermediate

Nucleophilic Substitution Reactions (2 of 2)
Reactants in acyl substitutions

General Types of Chemical Transformations (5 of 8)
Nucleophilic addition reactions
Carbonyl carbons are well suited for addition reactions because the adjacent C or H is not a good leaving group

General Types of Chemical Transformations (6 of 8)
Carbonyl condensations
If the carbonyl to which the enolate ion adds is an aldehyde or ketone, then it is an aldol condensation; if it is an ester, then it is a Claisen condensation
Formation of the enolate ion gives the carbon atom a partial negative charge, enabling nucleophilic attack on a carbonyl carbon

Carbonyl Condensations
Aldol and Claisen condensations
These reactions are reversible (retro-aldol and retro-Claisen),
resulting in the cleavage of
keto compounds

General Types of Chemical Transformations (7 of 8)
Elimination reactions
hydroxycarbonyls are readily dehydrated
In this dehydration the H that is to be removed is slightly acidic because of its closeness to a carbonyl oxygen
Elimination Reactions

General Types of Chemical Transformations (8 of 8)
Oxidation–reduction reactions
is a widely used redox cofactor, which acts via a
reversible hydride ion (two-electron) transfer; most redox reactions involving metal ions are one-electron transfer reactions
These enzymes are called dehydrogenases; if the electron acceptor is oxygen, the enzyme is called an oxidase; if the reaction proceeds in the reverse direction, the enzyme is called a reductase

Oxidation as a Biological Energy Source (1 of 2)
Oxidation of glucose:
Biological systems break this overall reaction into small increments
Aerobic organisms:
citric acid cycle, oxidative
Glycolysis is a fermentation: an energy-yielding process that does not involve a change in oxidation state

Oxidation as a Biological Energy Source (2 of 2)
Some bacteria ferment glucose to acetate
some to ethanol
Some anaerobic bacteria use alternate electron acceptors:
Fat generates far more metabolic energy from oxidation than carbohydrate because it generates more reducing equivalents (a reducing equivalent is one mole of H atoms)

A T P as Free Energy Currency (1 of 3)
Currency is a medium of exchange, and free energy derived from A T P hydrolysis is exchanged for energy to drive endergonic processes, such as movement, maintenance of selective membrane permeability, active transport, sensory perception, and biosynthesis of complex molecules

Because A T P is the most abundant N T P, it is most often used as a phosphate donor

Metabolite Concentrations and Solvent Capacity (1 of 2)
The solvent-capacity of a cell refers to the finite capacity for the total amount of dissolved substances in a cell
To avoid exceeding the solvent-capacity, metabolites are

Kinetic Control of Substrate Cycles (1 of 3)
We previously determined the phosphofructokinase (P F K) reaction is favorable until [F-1,6-B P] approaches 308 times [F-6-P]
Taking into account the
the fructose 1,6-bisphophatase

Kinetic Control of Substrate Cycles (2 of 3)
What prevents these two reactions (one in glycolytic and one in gluconeogenetic direction) from proceeding simultaneously?

Kinetic Control of Substrate Cycles (3 of 3)
Regulation (kinetic control) can be imposed only on reactions displaced far from equilibrium
Each opposing pathway is favorable, waiting for the signal (such as an allosteric effector) to open. The allosteric effectors that allow each “floodgate” to open are different for each pathway, thus preventing both pathways from operating simultaneously

Major Sources of Energy for A T P Synthesis
Substrate-level-phosphorylation; energy comes from breakdown of a higher-energy substrate (e.g., phosphoenolpyruvate or creatine phosphate)
Oxidative phosphorylation; energy comes from exergonic redox reactions in mitochondria
Photophosphorylation; energy comes from light

Regulation of Pathways in Energy Metabolism
In general, enzymes that catalyze rate-limiting steps in energy generation (e.g., glycolysis) are inhibited by A T P and activated by A D P or A M P, while enzymes involved in biosynthesis are activated by A T P and inhibited by A D P or A M P
The true regulatory parameter is probably the adenylate energy charge, that is, the ratio of [A T P] versus
in a cell
11.5 Major Metabolic Control Mechanisms

Array of Regulatory Devices (1 of 2)
Control of enzyme levels (genetic regulation)
Control of enzyme activity: substrate-level, allosteric, covalent modification
Signal transduction (hormones, growth factors): intercellular control often exercised through action of a second messenger (the hormone is the first messenger)
Cyclic A M P – a second messenger

Array of Regulatory Devices (2 of 2)
Compartmentation: both physical (involving membranes); example: increase in membrane permeability to glucose in response to insulin secretion
Functional compartmentation, brought about by physical juxtaposition of sequentially acting enzymes; this allows high local concentration of intermediates, while maintaining low average concentration; this is important because the solvent capacity of the cell is limited

Cellular Location of Major Metabolic Pathways
Figure 11.10 Locations of major metabolic pathways within a eukaryotic cell.

Distributive Control of Metabolism (1 of 3)
Conventional wisdom: Flux through a metabolic pathway is controlled by the activity of one rate-limiting enzyme, usually the one enzyme in a pathway subject to allosteric regulation
Metabolic control analysis assigns each enzyme in a pathway a flux control coefficient (F C C), the relative increase in flux through a pathway relative to an increase in the activity of an enzyme in that pathway, whose value varies from 0 to 1

Distributive Control of Metabolism (2 of 3)
The flux control coefficient (F C C) indicates the regulatory significance of a particular enzyme within a metabolic pathway
if
the relevant enzyme is truly rate-limiting
if
the activity of the enzyme is irrelevant to
regulation

pathway has

Distributive Control of Metabolism (3 of 3)
In the distributive control of metabolism, activities of all enzymes in a pathway contribute toward regulating flux through the pathway
enzymes subject to allosteric control have the highest flux control coefficients
11.6 Experimental Analysis of Metabolism

Investigating and Defining Metabolic Pathways (1 of 5)
Goals:
identify enzymes, reactants, products, cofactors, stoichiometry of each reaction
understand how the pathway is regulated in the tissue under study
identify the physiological function and control of each enzyme

Investigating and Defining Metabolic Pathways (2 of 5)
Metabolism must be investigated at several levels of biological organization:
isolated or perfused organ—maintain integrity of the organ, while infusing drugs, nutrients or hormones in an isotonic solution and removing waste products
whole cell—tissue culture for eukaryotic cells, where clonal cell lines can be derived from disaggregated organs and tissues (cell and organism levels are the same for prokaryotes)

Investigating and Defining Metabolic Pathways (3 of 5)
Metabolism must be investigated at several levels of biological organization:
organelles—usually isolated by differential centrifugation from tissue homogenates (e.g., use of isolated mitochondria for analysis of electron transport and oxidative phosphorylation)
purified components—either isolated enzymes studied with respect to mechanism and control, or reconstituted systems such as the replisome

Investigating and Defining Metabolic Pathways (4 of 5)
Metabolism must be investigated at several levels of biological organization:
metabolomics – measuring the intracellular concentrations of each metabolite. Metabolites (small molecules) vary in physical and chemical properties allowing all of the thousands of low-M W metabolites to be extracted and assayed in one operation

Investigating and Defining Metabolic Pathways (5 of 5)
Metabolism is investigated using various tools and techniques:
noninvasive metabolic monitoring by nuclear magnetic resonance (measures shift in the frequency of absorbed electromagnetic radiation)
steps of a hypothetical metabolic pathway are also identified by analyzing mutants defective in individual steps of the pathway

Analyzing Mutants Defective in Individual Steps of a Metabolic Pathway

11.7 Tools of Biochemistry

Metabolic Profiling (1 of 2)
Metabolites can be identified by analytical methods such as 2 D N M R
Levels of metabolites under different conditions can be visualized using a “heat map”
Informatics approaches can then be used to reveal patterns in the data

Metabolic Profiling (2 of 2)
Figure 11A.1 Basic process of metabolic profiling.

Principal Component Analysis (P C A) (1 of 2)
P C A reduces multidimensional data sets to lower dimensions (principal components)
P C A can reveal the internal structure of the data that best explains variation
P C A analysis of certain body samples (blood, urine, etc.) can identify metabolic signatures of diseased patients (vs. a healthy control group)

Principal Component Analysis (P C A) (2 of 2)
This example shows the P C A analysis of 1 H N M R spectra of human blood sera from oral cancer patients (P C 1 is growth media and P C 2 is the presence or absence of a specific mutation)

The nuclei of certain isotopes possess a property called spin, which allows these nuclei to be studied by N  M  R  in the context of metabolic pathways

Chapter 11 Summary (1 of 2)
Metabolism is the totality of all chemical reactions occurring within a living system (such as a cell)
catabolism is the sum of breakdown phases, while anabolism is the sum of biosynthetic phases
most metabolic energy comes from the oxidation of substrates
Metabolic pathways are defined sequences of chemical reactions in a cell that produce energy or valuable materials, and are catalyzed by enzymes

Chapter 11 Summary (2 of 2)
The flux through metabolic pathways is controlled by regulating various parameters, such as enzyme concentration, enzyme activity, compartmentation, and presence of hormones
To fully understand a metabolic pathway, each reaction must be identified, how it is catalyzed and controlled
this requires experimentation at all levels of biological organization

12.5 Gluconeogenesis

Glucose Synthesis and Use (1 of 2)
The human brain requires 120 grams/day of glucose out of 160 needed by the entire body
Glycogen reserves can provide 190 g and glucose in body fluids is ~20 g; hence, the body contains about one day’s supply
When glucose is depleted (fasting or prolonged exercise), glucose must be synthesized from other sources, such as gluconeogenesis

Glucose Synthesis and Use (2 of 2)
Gluconeogenesis means (literally) synthesis of new glucose
Glucose is synthesized from non-carbohydrate precursors
Gluconeogenesis is like glycolysis in reverse, but three “irreversible” reactions must be reversed

Pasteur Effect
Louis Pasteur (1822–1895) observed that anaerobic yeast exposed to oxygen caused the rate of glucose utilization to decrease
Intracellular levels of glycolytic intermediates after oxygenation pointed to the conversion of F6P to F1,6B P as an important control point
Phosphofructokinase (P F K) was found to be inhibited by A T P and activated by A D P and A M P
Many experiments suggested that glycolysis depends on adenylate energy charge

12.10 A Biosynthetic Pathway That Oxidizes Glucose: The Pentose Phosphate Pathway

The Metabolic Requirement of the Pentose Phosphate Pathway (P P P)
The P P P is mainly needed to provide:
reducing equivalents (in the form of N A D P H) for reductive biosynthesis
ribose-5-phosphate (R5P) for nucleotide and nucleic acid synthesis

The Four Stages of P P P
Two oxidations (glucose 6-phosphate dehydrogenase and 6-phosphogluconate dehydrogenase) in stage 1 generate N A D P H and
Ribose 5-phosphate is produced in stage 2
Figure 12.32 Overall strategy of the pentose phosphate pathway.

The Oxidative Phase of P P P
and 1
pentose phosphate are generated from 1 glucose 6-phosphate and
Figure 12.33 Oxidative phase of the pentose phosphate pathway.

The Nonoxidative Phase of P P P (1 of 2)
The phosphopentose isomerase reaction
Ribulose 5-phosphate can also be converted to the pentose sugar xylulose 5-phosphate (X5P) by ribulose 5-phosphate epimerase.

The Nonoxidative Phase of P P P (2 of 2)
Transketolase and transaldolase reactions
R5P (a C5 sugar) and X5P (C5) are converted to glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate (G A P; C3) and sedoheptulose 7-phosphate (S7P; C7) by transketolase, which are further metabolized to fructose 6-phosphate (F6P; C6) and erythrose 4-phosphate (E4P; a C4 sugar) by transaldolase
Moreover, E4P and X5P can be altered to G A P and F6P in a second transketolase reaction

Chapter 12 Summary (1 of 3)
Glycolysis is a central pathway to extract energy from carbohydrates
it comprises of ten reactions that can be divided into two distinct phases (energy-investment and energy-generation phases)
it catabolizes glucose into pyruvate, yielding energy in form of A T P and N A D H
Glycolysis can proceed under anaerobic conditions as fermentation, or under aerobic conditions as cellular respiration
however, much more energy is generated under aerobic conditions

Chapter 12 Summary (2 of 3)
The major sites of regulation for glycolysis are the reactions catalyzed by phosphofructokinase, pyruvate kinase, and hexokinase
regulation is related to cellular energy needs and occurs via allosteric mechanisms
Gluconeogenesis is the synthesis of glucose from noncarbohydrate precursors
seven glycolytic enzymes are also used in gluconeogenesis, but three glycolytic enzymes are replaced by four specific ones to ensure thermodynamic favorability

Chapter 12 Summary (3 of 3)
Regulation of glycolysis and gluconeogenesis is highly coordinated
fructose 2,6-bisphosphate is the key regulator
Glycogen is the intracellular storage polysaccharide in animals
glycogen phosphorylase is the rate-limiting step in glycogen breakdown, while glycogen synthase is the rate-limiting step in glycogen synthesis
The pentose phosphate pathway is an alternative glucose oxidation pathway that generates N A D P H (for reductive biosynthesis) and pentose phosphates (for nucleotide synthesis)

13.1 Overview of Pyruvate Oxidation and the Citric Acid Cycle

Oxidative Processes in the Generation of Metabolic Energy
Figure 13.1 Oxidative processes in the generation of metabolic energy.
The citric acid cycle is the central pathway for oxidizing all metabolic fuels
Most of the energy yield from substrate oxidation in the citric acid cycle is stored in reduced electron carriers such as N A D H

Stages of Cellular Respiration (1 of 2)
Metabolic oxidation of organic substrates (cellular respiration) occurs in three stages:
In stage 1, carbon from metabolic fuels is incorporated into acetyl-C o A
In stage 2, the citric acid cycle oxidizes acetyl-C o A to
produce
reduced electron carriers, and a small
amount of A T P
In stage 3, the reduced electron carriers are reoxidized, providing energy for the synthesis of additional A T P

Stages of Cellular Respiration (2 of 2)
Figure 13.2 The three stages of respiration.
On the right, stage 1 is shown on top (green background color), stage 2 in the middle (yellow background color), and stage 3 at the bottom (blue background color). In eukaryotic organisms, these three stages are located in the mitochondria.

Structure of Mitochondria
Figure 13.3 Structure of the mitochondrion.
The reactions of stages 1 and 2 of respiration occur in the mitochondrial matrix. Reactions of stage 3 are catalyzed by membrane-bound enzymes in the inner mitochondrial membrane

13.3 The Citric Acid Cycle

The Fate of Carbon in the Citric Acid Cycle (1 of 2)
The citric acid cycle (C A C) comprises of eight reactions,
the product of the eighth reaction, oxaloacetate, and the product of the P D H complex, acetyl-C o A, are the reactants for the first reaction
The cycle generates two
three N A D H, and one
molecule per turn

The Fate of Carbon in the Citric Acid Cycle (2 of 2)
Figure 13.4 The fate of carbon in the citric acid cycle.
The carbon atoms transferred from acetyl-C o A are highlighted in blue. They are not highlighted after step 5 because succinate is symmetrical

Reaction 1 – Citrate Synthase
Hydrolysis of the thioester makes the forward reaction highly exergonic, ensuring the C A C can proceed even at low concentrations of oxaloacetate
Citrate Synthase Mechanism

Citrate Synthase Structure
Figure 13.12 Three dimensional structure of citrate synthase.

Reaction 2 – Aconitase
converts the prochiral citrate to the chiral ᴅ-isocitrate in a two-step isomerization reaction
Step  (a)  is a dehydration reaction that forms cis-aconitate
Step  (b)  is a hydration reaction that forms ᴅ-isocitrate

Reaction 3 – Isocitrate Dehydrogenase (1 of 2)
catalyzes an oxidative decarboxylation
oxidizes the
alcohol of isocitrate to form a keto
group β to the carboxyl group that will be removed as
The β-keto group of oxalosuccinate acts as an electron sink to stabilize the transition state anion
Energy released in the oxidative decarboxylation is stored as N A D H
Reaction 3 – Isocitrate Dehydrogenase (2 of 2)

Reaction 4 – α-Ketoglutarate Dehydrogenase Complex (1 of 2)
acts analogously to the pyruvate dehydrogenase complex
T P P is required for the decarboxylation of the α-keto acid
The energy of the oxidation reaction is conserved as NADH
The product, succinyl-C o A, is an energy-rich molecule

Reaction 4 – α-Ketoglutarate Dehydrogenase Complex (2 of 2)
Figure 13.13 Decarboxylation of α-ketoglutarate.

Reaction 5 – Succinyl-CoA Synthetase
performs a substrate level phosphorylation
Step 5: The succinyl-CoA synthetase reaction
The potential energy stored in the thioester (succinyl-C o A) is used to form a nucleoside triphosphate (A T P or G T P)

Succinyl-CoA Synthetase Mechanism
Figure 13.14 Covalent catalysis by the succinyl-C o A synthetase reaction.
The reaction uses an N-phosphohistidine Intermediate. A T P or G T P may be synthesized depending on the isozyme

Reaction 6 – Succinate Dehydrogenase
catalyzes the dehydrogenation of two saturated carbons to a double bond using enzyme-bound F A D
is membrane-bound and delivers electrons directly into the mitochondrial electron transport system via coenzyme Q
is inhibited by malonate, a structural analog of succinate

Reaction 7 – Fumarase
performs a hydration reaction
is highly stereospecific
the trans double bond of fumarate is specifically attacked, and only l-malate is formed
d-malate cannot be used as a substrate in the reverse reaction

Reaction 8 – Malate Dehydrogenase
Although malate dehydrogenase has a large standard positive free energy change (+29.7 kJ/mol), the reaction proceeds to the right in vivo because the highly exergonic citrate synthase keeps oxaloacetate (and the electron transport chain keeps N A D H) levels very low

13.5 Regulation of Pyruvate Dehydrogenase and the Citric Acid Cycle

Regulatory Mechanisms (1 of 3)
Regulation occurs mainly by controlling entry into the cycle (P D H complex and citrate synthase) and by controlling key irreversible reactions (isocitrate dehydrogenase and α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase)

Regulatory Mechanisms (2 of 3)
Cycle flux is primarily controlled by:
Allosteric activation of isocitrate dehydrogenase by A D P (energy state)
N A D+/N A D H ratio (redox state)
Inhibition of relevant enzymes by acetyl-C o A and succinyl-C o A (availability of energy-rich compounds)

13.6 Organization and Evolution of the Citric Acid Cycle

C A C Genes in All Three Domains of Life
Genes encoding C A C enzymes have been found in organisms of all three domains of life (Bacteria, Archaea, and Eukarya), including anaerobic chemotrophs
The latter harvest energy from glucose oxidation only, but still use an incomplete C A C (e.g., to generate biosynthetic precursors)
13.7 Citric Acid Cycle Malfunction as a Cause of Human Disease

Effects of Defects in C A C Enzymes
A complete lack of each of the at least eight C A C enzyme activity would be lethal for humans
However, specific mutations in C A C genes leading to defective enzymes have been linked to a number of neurodegenerative diseases (such as Leigh syndrome or other encephalopathies) and tumors (e.g., uterine and renal cell cancer, and malignant gliomas, a common type of brain tumor)
13.8 Anaplerotic Sequences : The Need to Replace Cycle Intermediates

C A C as a Source of Biological Intermediates (1 of 2)
The C A C serves as a source of intermediates for other pathways that use these intermediates
Anaplerotic (“filling up”) processes are required to restore these intermediates so the C A C can continue
Replenishment of oxaloacetate (which is used for gluconeogenesisis, transamination to aspartate, etc.) is the most important anaplerotic reaction

C A C as a Source of Biological Intermediates (2 of 2)
Figure 13.17 Major biosynthetic roles of some citric acid cycle intermediates.
Replenishment of Oxaloacetate
13.9 The Glyoxylate Cycle : An Anabolic Variant of the Citric Acid Cycle

Conversion from Fats to Carbohydrates (1 of 3)
Vertebrates cannot convert acetyl-C o A (and hence fatty acids) into glucose (and other carbohydrates) via gluconeogenesis
At least two highly exergonic reactions make conversions essentially irreversible:
phosphoenolpyruvate (P E P) to pyruvate (during glycolysis)
pyruvate to acetyl-C o A (acetyl-C o A production via P D H complex)

Conversion from Fats to Carbohydrates (2 of 3)
Oxalacetate can be converted into P E P via P E P carboxykinase, but it has to be generated from acetyl-C o A via C A C, which involves two decarboxylations of a C2 body and therefore has no net gain
The glyoxylate cycle allows the formation of glucose from acetyl-C o A by bypassing the two C A C decarboxylation steps (isocitrate and α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenases)

Conversion from Fats to Carbohydrates (3 of 3)
The glyoxylate cycle consists of three C A C enzymes, citrate synthase, aconitase, and malate dehydrogenase, and two additional enzymes, isocitrate lyase and malate synthase
In plant cells, the glyoxylate cycle is located in special organelles, glyoxysomes, usually in close proximity to lipid bodies and mitochondria
