13.10 Tools of Biochemistry

Detecting and Analyzing Protein–Protein Interactions
Multienzyme complexes are often held together by weak, noncovalent forces, making these complexes difficult to isolate and characterize
Multiple approaches are available to demonstrate and characterize protein–protein interactions
Bifunctional cross-linking reagents
Affinity chromatography and immunoprecipitation
Library-based methods (two-hybrid system)
Kinetic and Biosensor analyses

Bifunctional Cross-Linking Reagents
Contain two functional groups capable of forming covalent bonds with specific residues in closely associated proteins
proteins are only cross-linked if they interact
strong (covalent) interactions are formed from rather weak interactions (e.g., hydrogen bonds)

Biosensor Analysis – Surface Plasmon Resonance (S P R)
Figure 13A.1 Biacore analysis of a protein–protein association.
S P R is an optical property related to changes in the refractive index when a protein in solution interacts with a protein immobilized on a chip
S P R is able to measure the kinetics of association and dissociation, as well as the affinity of the interaction

The citric acid cycle (C A C) is a central pathway in the oxidation of carbohydrates, lipids, and proteins
C A C requires the entry of a two-carbon molecule, acetyl-C o A, to generate in one turn of eight reactions,
four reduced electron carriers (three
and
one
one A T P (or G T P), and two
Acetyl-C o A is synthesized from pyruvate by the pyruvate dehydrogenase (P D H) multienzyme complex, which performs three different activities using five essential cofactors

Chapter 13 Summary (2 of 2)
The P D H complex also produces per one pyruvate one
and one
While the P D H complex is primarily controlled by phosphorylation/dephosphorylation, the C A C is regulated at the level of fuel entry as well as at the level of irreversible reactions within the cycle
Anaplerotic reactions replace C A C intermediates that are consumed by biosynthetic pathways
In plant and bacteria, the glyoxylate cycle bypasses the C A C decarboxylation steps, allowing acetyl-C o A to undergo a net conversion to carbohydrates

14.1 The Mitochondrion: Scene of the Action

Overview of Oxidative Energy Generation (1 of 3)
Energy generation via metabolic oxidation of organic substrates occurs in three stages:
In stage 1, carbon from metabolic fuels is incorporated into acetyl-C o A
In stage 2, the citric acid cycle oxidizes acetyl-CoA to produce
reduced electron carriers, and a small amount of A T P
In stage 3, the reduced electron carriers are reoxidized, providing energy for the synthesis of additional A T P

Overview of Oxidative Energy Generation (2 of 3)
Relatively little A T P is generated per mole of glucose in stages 1 and 2 (including glycolysis and citric acid cycle)
Stages 1 and 2, however, produce 10 moles N A D H and 2
moles of F A D to
Reoxidation of N A D H and
in stage 3 (cellular
respiration, oxidative phosphorylation) provides most of the energy used for A T P synthesis
Stages 2 and 3 as well as part of stage 1 occur in mitochondria

Chapter 14 Summary (1 of 2)
Oxidative phosphorylation converts most of the energy captured from oxidative reactions to ATP
this process takes place in the mitochondria
ATP synthesis during oxidative phosphorylation is coupled
to the electron transfer from NADH and
This electron transfer is performed by the respiratory chain, comprising of four multisubunit protein complexes called complexes I–IV as well as ubiquinone/ubiquinol and cytochrome c

Chapter 14 Summary (2 of 2)
Complex Ⅰ, Ⅲ, and Ⅳpump protons from the mitochondrial matrix across the inner membrane, generating an electrochemical gradient termed proton motive force
This proton motive force drives the synthesis of ATP from ADP and Pi by ATP synthase (complex V)

Energy Transfer in Photosystems (1 of 2)
Light-harvesting pigments and their associated proteins are organized into membrane-embedded multi subunit complexes called photosystems
Plants use two distinct photosystems (P S  Ⅰ and Ⅱ  ); P S I and P S  Ⅱ contain antenna pigment molecules (such as chlorophylls) bound to light-harvesting complexes (L H C s) and a pair of special chlorophyll molecules which act as a reaction center

Energy Transfer in Photosystems (2 of 2)
When absorbing molecules are tightly packed, the energy of the excited molecule may be transferred to an adjacent molecule (resonance transfer) or the excited electron itself may be transferred to a nearby molecule with a slightly lower excited state (electron transfer)
Figure 15.8 Two modes of energy transfer following photoexcitation.

Energy Transfer in an L H C
Photons of light are absorbed by antennae pigment molecules and rapidly transferred to a reaction center
The reaction center excited state energy level is slightly lower, trapping the quanta of energy
Excitation of the reaction center starts a series of electron transfer reactions

Photosystems (P S s) were named in order of their discovery; P S Ⅰ absorbs light up to 700 nm and P S Ⅱ  absorbs light up to 680 nm
In each P S, the primary step is transfer of a light-excited electron to a reaction center

Schematic View of the Dark Reactions (Calvin Cycle) (1 of 2)
The Calvin cycle uses the A T P and N A D P H generated in the light reactions to fix atmospheric
into

The cycle can be divided into two stages:
Ⅰ

Schematic View of the Dark Reactions (Calvin Cycle) (2 of 2)
Figure 15.19 Schematic view of the Calvin cycle.

Calvin Cycle Stage I (1 of 4)
Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate (R u B P) accepts
Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate carboxylase (Rubisco) catalyzes this complex reaction, producing two
3-phosphoglycerate molecules

Calvin Cycle Stage I (2 of 4)
In the following reactions of this stage, the
3-phosphoglycerate molecules are phosphorylated by phosphoglycerate kinase to produce 1,
3-bisphosphoglyercate, which are then reduced to
glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate (G A P) by glyceraldehyde 3 phosphate dehydrogenase

Calvin Cycle Stage I (3 of 4)
The synthetic pathway follows a portion of the gluconeogenic pathway
G A P is isomerized to dihydroxyacetone phosphate ( D H A P) by triose phosphate isomerase
G A P and D H A P are combined by fructose bisphosphate aldolase to form fructose 1,6-bisphosphate (F B P)
F B P is dephosphorylated by fructose 1,6-bisphosphatase to yield fructose-6-phosphate (F 6 P)

Calvin Cycle Stage I (4 of 4)
F 6 P is first isomerized to glucose-6-phosphate (G 6 P) and then to glucose-1-phosphate (G 1 P)
G1P is the precursor to the storage polysaccharide amylose:

Calvin Cycle Stage II (1 of 2)
The six R u B P molecules (30 carbon atoms) needed to continue the cycle are obtained from two hexoses (two F 6 P molecules; 12 carbon atoms) and six trioses (two D H A P molecules and four G A P molecules; 18 carbon atoms)

Calvin Cycle Stage II (2 of 2)
The final step is the phosphorylation of ribulose 5-phosphate by ribulose-5-phosphate kinase to form R u B P
The equation for the overall dark reaction is then:

Chapter 15 Summary (1 of 3)
Photosynthesis captures electromagnetic radiation and converts it into chemical energy of reduced organic compounds
It accounts for all fixation of atmospheric
into
biological molecules and the production of nearly all
atmospheric
Photosynthetic processes can be divided into light reactions and dark reactions
light reactions use electromagnetic radiation (from sunlight) to extract electrons from
producing
reducing potential, and a proton gradient that drives
A T P synthesis

Chapter 15 Summary (2 of 3)
dark or carbon reactions reduce
into
carbohydrates
In higher plants and algae, both types of reactions take place in the chloroplasts
Energy from absorption of photons by antenna pigments is first transferred to reaction centers and then to photosystems (P S) I or II
P S Ⅱ oxidizes
while P S Ⅰ reduces
Both drive the transport of protons across the thylakoid membrane, providing a p H gradient to produce A T P via the chloroplast A T P synthase

Chapter 15 Summary (3 of 3)
The dark or carbon reactions are accomplished by the Calvin cycle, which can be divided into two stages
In the first stage,
is added to ribulose
1,5-bisphosphate (R u B P), which is then cleaved and reduced to triose phosphates that are then combined
to form hexose phosphates
In the second stage, most of triose phosphates and hexose phosphates are used to regenerate R u B P for the first stage, some is converted into starch (or other carbohydrates)

The catabolic pathways of lipid metabolism are shown in red, while the anabolic pathways are indicated in blue. The electron flows from lipid oxidation is highlighted in yellow

Fat Open parenthesis Triacylglycerol super asterisk close parenthesis
as the Major
Energy Reserve in Most Organisms
Fat is highly reduced (high energy yield) and anhydrous (no bound water)
*Triacylglycerol (T A G) is the preferred name for what used to be called triglycerides.

Primary Sources of T A G s
Diet
De novo biosynthesis in the liver
Storage depots in adipocytes (“fat cells”)
Figure 16.2 Overview of fat digestion, absorption, storage, and mobilization in the human.

Emulsifying T A G s with Bile Salts in the Intestine (1 of 2)
Because of their insolubility, fats are usually emulsified with bile salts or complexed with proteins as lipoproteins
Emulsification with bile acids occurs in the intestine
Then, T A G s are digested by pancreatic lipase into glycerol and free fatty acids
Free fatty acids are transported into the lymph system, resynthesized into T A G s, then complexed with protein to form chylomicrons, the least dense of the lipoproteins

Lipoprotein lipase binds chylomicron (and V L D L) at the capillaries and hydrolyzes T A G s to glycerol and free fatty acids for uptake into cells

Cholesterol Transport and Utilization in Animals
L C A T abbreviates the enzyme lecithin: cholesterol acyltransferase
Cholesterol in plasma lipoproteins exists as free cholesterol and cholesterol esters
Prolonged hypercholesterolemia hastens the formation of atherosclerotic plaque; total plasma cholesterol (cholesterol plus cholesterol esters) above 200 mg/100 mL is a major risk factor for coronary artery disease

Receptor-Mediated Endocytosis of L D L s – The Clathrin-Coated Pit Structure
Figure 16.8 Structure of a clathrin-coated pit.

The L D L Receptor and Cholesterol Homeostasis (1 of 2)
Starting in 1972, Brown and Goldstein performed studies
on familial hypercholesterolemia (FH):
Normal serum cholesterol	<200
Homozygous FH	650–1000
(death of heart disease by age 20)
Heterozygous FH	350–500
(death often in 30s or 40s or later)
Brown and Goldstein used cultured fibroblasts to demonstrate cholesterol is taken up by the L D L receptor (FH patients are deficient in this receptor)

The L D L Receptor and Cholesterol Homeostasis (2 of 2)
These experiments identified a new process by which cells interact with their environment – receptor-mediated endocytosis
Cholesterol regulates its own level inside cells by controlling its de novo biosynthesis, storage, and L D L receptor density
Figure 16.7 Receptor-mediated endocytosis of L D L.

L D L Receptors Involved in Cholesterol Metabolism
Figure 16.9 Involvement of L D L receptors in cholesterol uptake and metabolism.

Cholesterol, L D L, and Atherosclerosis (1 of 2)
Why do diets rich in saturated fatty acids elevate L D L?
Why do polyunsaturated fatty acids, particularly omega-3 fatty acids (like linolenic acid) lower L D L?
L D L are subject to oxidation—oxidized L D L can be taken up by scavenger receptors

Cholesterol, L D L, and Atherosclerosis (2 of 2)
This process (called atherogenesis) yields almost unlimited uptake of cholesterol into a cell and produces a cholesterol-gorged foam cell, which is a precursor to atherosclerotic plaque
There are no degradative pathways for cholesterol within the cell
H D L can return excess cholesterol to the liver for passage through the bile duct to the intestine, leading to excretion

Mobilization of Stored Fat (1 of 2)
Synthesis of T A G and its deposition in adipose cells is unlimited
Mobilization of stored fat (lipolysis) is hormonally controlled via c A M P
Figure 16.10 Mobilization of adipose cell triacylglycerols by lipolysis.
A T G L : adipose triglyceride lipase; H S L : hormone-sensitive lipase; M G L : monoacylglyceride lipase

Mobilization of Stored Fat (2 of 2)
Mediated by epinephrine during stress situations and by glucagon during fasting
Other hormones regulate the process under different conditions
Figure 16.10 Mobilization of adipose cell triacylglycerols by lipolysis.
A T G L : adipose triglyceride lipase; H S L : hormone-sensitive lipase; M G L : monoacylglyceride lipase
16.2 Fatty Acid Oxidation

Early Experiments on Fatty Acid Oxidation (1 of 2)
Early insight into fatty acid oxidation by Knoop in 1904 using phenyl derivatives of fatty acids
First use of metabolic tracers (long before radioisotopes)
Figure 16.11 Oxidation of phenyl derivatives of fatty acids in Knoop’s experiment.

Early Experiments on Fatty Acid Oxidation (2 of 2)
Knoop proposed the oxidation of fatty acids occurred in a stepwise fashion at carbon 3, releasing a two-carbon fragment
Release of a two-carbon fragment would occur in each step of oxidation
Figure 16.11 Oxidation of phenyl derivatives of fatty acids in Knoop’s experiment.

Overview of the Fatty Acid Oxidation Pathway
Fatty acids are converted (activated) in the cytosol to fatty acyl-C o A. Short-chain and medium-chain fatty acyls (up to 10C) can enter mitochondria via nonmediated diffusion, while long-chain (12C or more) fatty acyls are transported into mitochondria by the carnitine-acyltransferase system. In mitochondria, fatty acyls are catabolized to acetyl-C o A via beta-oxidation
Figure 16.12 Overview of the fatty acid oxidation pathway.

Fatty Acid Activation in the Cytosol
A series of acyl-C o A synthetases specific for short-chain (2-3 C), medium-chain (4-12 C), or long-chain (10-20 C) fatty acids catalyzes the formation of a fatty acid thioester conjugate with coenzyme A (activated fatty acid)
Figure 16.13 Mechanism of acyl-C o A synthetase reactions.

Fatty Acid Transport into Mitochondria – the Carnitine Acyltransferase System (1 of 2)
While short-chain and medium-chain fatty acyls (up to 10C) can diffuse into mitochondria without the need of a specific transport system, long-chain (12C or more) fatty acyls require the carnitine-acyltransferase system to enter mitochondria

Fatty Acid Transport into Mitochondria – the Carnitine Acyltransferase System (2 of 2)
Figure 16.14 The carnitine acyltransferase system, for transport of long-chain fatty acyl-C o A s into mitochondria.

Overview of β-Oxidation (1 of 2)
In the mitochondrial matrix, fatty acyls are oxidized in a series of four enzymatic steps (first dehydrogenation, hydration, second dehydrogenation, and thiolytic cleavage), in which the fatty acyl chain is shortened by two carbons at a time
The released two-carbon fragment is acetyl-C o A

Overview of β-Oxidation (2 of 2)
Figure 16.15 Outline of the β-oxidation of fatty acids.

Reaction 1 – Acyl-C o A Dehydrogenase
Figure 16.16 Fate of reducing equivalents derived from fatty acyl-C o A dehydrogenation.
ETF = electron-transferring flavoprotein

Reactions 2 and 3 – Enoyl-C o A Hydratase and L-3-Hydroxyacyl-C o A Dehydrogenase
Both reactions are stereospecific
Because carbon 3 is β with respect to the carboxyl carbon, this pathway has been called β-oxidation
Figure 16.15 Outline of the β-oxidation of fatty acids.

Reaction 4 – Thiolase
Catalyzes the thiolytic cleavage of 3-ketoacyl-C o A
Each cycle shortens the acyl chain by two carbons
The final thiolytic cleavage generates two molecules of acetyl-C o A

Oxidation of Unsaturated Fatty Acids
Two reactions prepare unsaturated acyl-C o A for β-oxidation:
Enoyl-C o A isomerase converts the cis to trans double bond, which is then acted on by enoyl-C o A hydratase (at right)
2,4-Dienoyl-C o A reductase is used in the oxidation of poly unsaturated fatty acids

2,4-Dienoyl-C o A Reductase
Figure 16.17 β-oxidation pathway for polyunsaturated fatty acids.

Oxidation of Odd-Numbered Fatty Acids (1 of 2)
Odd-numbered fatty acids can be degraded via β-oxidation to propionyl-C o A and acetyl-C o A
Metabolism of propionyl-C o A (to succinyl-C o A) requires three different enzymes (a carboxylase, an epimerase and a mutase) and a coenzyme (deoxyadenosylcobalamin)
derived from vitamin

Oxidation of Odd-Numbered Fatty Acids (2 of 2)
A deficiency of methylmalonyl-C o A mutase or synthesis of the
coenzyme from vitamin
causes
methylmalonic acid to accumulate resulting in severe acidosis and nervous system damage (methylmalonic acidemia)
Figure 16.18 Pathway for catabolism of propionyl-C o A.

Ketogenesis (1 of 3)
During fasting or starvation, oxaloacetate levels fall, limiting flux through citric acid cycle
Acetate (produced by the liver) is converted back to
acetyl-CoA:
Acetyl-CoA is converted to ketone bodies (i.e., acetoacetate, acetone, and β-hydroxybutyrate) by three to five different enzymes

Ketogenesis (2 of 3)
Ketogenesis occurs primarily in the liver, and it can be considered an “overflow pathway”
Ketone bodies can be transported from liver to other tissues for energy generation
In diabetes, where glucose utilization is impaired, ketogenesis is stimulated
Ketone bodies are responsible for the ketoacidosis in diabetes; accumulation of acetoacetate and β-hydroxybutyrate leads to ionization of their carboxyl groups

Ketogenesis (3 of 3)
Figure 16.19 Biosynthesis of ketone bodies in the liver.

Ketolysis (1 of 2)
The reconversion of ketone bodies to acetyl-CoA (ketolysis) uses in part the same enzymes as ketogenesis
However, H M G -CoA synthase and HMG-CoA lyase are replaced by β-ketoacyl-CoA transferase (K C T)
K C T transfers a CoA moiety from succinyl-CoA (and not acetyl-CoA) to acetoacetate, yielding acetoacetyl-CoA and succinate
K C T is present in all tissues except the liver, the main ketogenic organ

Fatty Acid Oxidation and Biosynthesis Compared (1 of 3)
Both occur via the same intermediates, but along different pathways
Figure 16.21 Chemical similarities between oxidation and synthesis of a fatty acid.

Fatty Acid Oxidation and Biosynthesis Compared (2 of 3)
Malonyl-CoA interacts with another acyl group carrier, that is, acyl carrier protein (A C P)
Figure 16.21 Chemical similarities between oxidation and synthesis of a fatty acid.

Fatty Acid Oxidation and Biosynthesis Compared (3 of 3)
A C P is a small (77 residues in E. coli) protein that binds the acyl group via a phosphopantetheine moiety with a reactive sulfhydryl (as seen in conzyme A)
Figure 16.22 Phosphopantetheine as the reactive unit in A C P and CoA.

Synthesis of Malonyl-CoA
Malonyl-CoA is synthesized from acetyl-CoA and bicarbonate by acetyl-CoA carboxylase (A C C)

Formation of Initial Substrates for Fatty Acid Synthesis
Malonyl/acetyl-CoA-A C P transacylase (M AT) loads fatty acid synthase with substrates
Figure 16.23 Malonyl/acetyl-CoA  A C P transacylase (M A T) loads fatty acid synthase with substrates.

Requirement for Reducing Equivalent and Energy
Reducing power (N A D P H) and energy (A T P) are required to drive the biosynthesis of lipid:
The net equation describes the process:

Type I and II Fatty Acid Synthesis
In all plants and most bacteria, the enzymes catalyzing this pathway are separate protein molecules (type II fatty acid synthesis)
In most nonplant eukaryotes and some bacteria, the enzymes needed for fatty acid synthesis are linked in a giant multienzyme protein (megasynthase or type I fatty acid synthase)
In animals, the complex is a homodimer of 273 kDa subunits, each of which has all seven active sites

Fatty Acid Elongation and Desaturation (1 of 3)
Elongation occurs in the endoplasmic reticulum and involves same reactions as those leading to palmitate, but using separate enzymes and acyl-CoAs instead of acyl-A C P s
For example:

Fatty Acid Elongation and Desaturation (2 of 3)
Desaturation takes place in a membrane-bound enzyme system in the endoplasmic reticulum; the first reaction is between C9 and C10
Animals cannot desaturate beyond C9–10; therefore, linoleic (18: 2Δ9,12) and linolenic (18: 3Δ9,12,15) acids must be provided in the diet (they are essential fatty acids)

Fatty Acid Elongation and Desaturation (3 of 3)
Figure 16.27 Fatty acid Δ9 desaturation system.
The fatty acid Δ9 desaturation system – Conversion of steroyl-coa to oleoyl-coa

Control of Fatty Acid Synthesis
Insulin promotes glucose uptake, promotes phosphorylation (activation) of pyruvate dehydrogenase, citrate lyase, acetyl-CoA carboxylase (A C C)
A M P-activated protein kinase and protein kinase A promote phosphorylation of acetyl-CoA carboxylase (inhibition)
Citrate (activation) and long-chain fatty acids (inhibition) are also allosteric modulators of A C C

16.7 Steroid Metabolism

Distinct Chemical Features of Steroids
Figure 16.33 Ring identification system (a), carbon numbering system (b) and structural conventions (c) used for steroids.
Steroids are the best known of the isoprenoids (built of one or more isoprene molecules)
Steroids are lipids based on a four ring structure

Biosynthesis of Cholesterol (1 of 4)
Figure 16.34 Biosynthesis of mevalonate and conversion to isopentenyl pyrophosphate and dimethylallyl pyrophosphate.
Mevalonate is synthesized from acetoacetyl-CoA and acetyl-CoA and in two catalytic steps via a 3-hydroxy-3-methylglutaryl-CoA (HMG-CoA) intermediate
1: HMG-CoA synthase; 2: HMG-CoA reductase

Biosynthesis of Cholesterol (2 of 4)
Figure 16.34 Biosynthesis of mevalonate and conversion to isopentenyl pyrophosphate and dimethylallyl pyrophosphate.
Isopentenyl pyrophosphate (or its isomer dimethylallyl pyrophosphate) are synthesized using four (or five) different enzymes

Biosynthesis of Cholesterol (3 of 4)
Figure 16.35 Conversion of isopentenyl pyrophosphate and dimethylallyl pyrophosphate to squalene.
Isopentenyl pyrophosphate reacts with its isomer, dimethylallyl pyrophosphate to form squalene in three catalytic steps

Biosynthesis of Cholesterol (4 of 4)
Figure 16.36 Conversion of squalene to cholesterol.
A cyclization (three steps) and a series of 19 additional reactions are required to produce cholesterol

Steroid Hormones (1 of 3)
Pregnenolone, the precursor of all steroid hormones, is synthesized from cholesterol in two catalytic steps

Lipid-Soluble Vitamins
Vitamins are organic compounds that are essential for normal growth and nutrition and are required in small quantities in the diet because they cannot be synthesized by the body
Vitamin D, A, E, and K are lipid-soluble isoprenoid compounds
Of these, however, only Vitamin D is a derivative of cholesterol, acts analogously to a steroid hormone and is involved in the regulation of calcium and phosphorus metabolism

Chapter 16 Summary (1 of 5)
Triacylglycerols (neutral fats) are high-energy fuel molecules that derive from three different sources:
the diet
de novo biosynthesis (liver)
storage depots in adipocytes
Dietary triacylglycerols are emulsified in the small intestine and broken down into free fatty acids and other products, which are both taken up by the intestinal mucosa and then reconverted into triacylglycerols

Chapter 16 Summary (2 of 5)
Triacylglycerols combine with apolipoproteins and other lipids to form chylomicrons, which travel through the lymphatic system and bloodstream to tissues, where the triacylglycerols in chylomicrons are broken down into fatty acids and glycerol, before fatty acids are transported into tissue cells
Triacylglycerols stored in adipose tissues are hydrolyzed in three steps to fatty acids and glycerol in a process tightly regulated by hormones, before fatty acids can be taken by tissue cells from the bloodstream

Chapter 16 Summary (3 of 5)
Fatty acids entering the cytosol of tissue cells are converted to fatty acyl-CoA and diffuse or are transported into the mitochondria
The β-oxidation pathway in the mitochondria breaks down most fatty acids in four enzymatic steps to acetyl-CoA units
Under conditions, in which further oxidation of acetyl-CoA through the citric acid cycle is limited, acetyl-CoA is used to synthesize ketone bodies

Chapter 16 Summary (4 of 5)
Fatty acid synthesis occurs via the stepwise addition of two-carbon fragments originated from malonyl-CoA using seven different catalytic activities, which are in eukaryotic cells performed by a multienzyme complex (megasynthase)
Triacylglycerols are formed by transferring the fatty acyl groups to the hydroxyl groups of glycerol 3-phosphate and diacylglycerol
Steroids, including cholesterol, are synthesized from
acetoacetyl-CoA and acetyl-CoA via

Chapter 16 Summary (5 of 5)
Cyclization of squalene results in the formation of cholesterol, the precursor for all steroid hormones
Orchestrated by the liver, cholesterol (and other lipids) is combined with apolipoproteins into various forms of packages (H D L, L D L, I D L, V L D L, etc.)
L D L s (low-density lipoproteins) are the major form of cholesterol transport to peripheral tissues
Specific receptors in cellular membranes bind L D L s and take them up via endocytosis, before L D L s are degraded inside the cell and the cholesterol cargo is released

[Speaker Notes] Chapter 16, Opener, The ruby-throated hummingbird winters in Central America and migrates to North America for the spring breeding season. These tiny birds must cross the Gulf of Mexico, covering more than 500 miles in a nonstop marathon. Prior to migration, the hummingbirds fatten up, accumulating about 2 g of fat in a total body weight of 4.5 g. The energy needed for a typical 20-hour crossing is supplied by the oxidation of 1.5 g of this stored fat.

17.1 Interdependence of the Major Organs in Vertebrate Fuel Metabolism

Major Fuel-Metabolizing Organs
Brain, muscle, liver, adipose tissue, and heart are the major fuel-metabolizing organs
Triacylglycerols (adipose tissue), protein (skeletal muscle), and glycogen (liver and muscle) are the major fuel reserves
Table 17.1 Profiles of the major vertebrate organs in fuel metabolism

Metabolic Interactions Among the Major Fuel-Metabolizing Organs
Figure 17.1 Metabolic interactions among the major fuel-metabolizing organs.

Fuel Characteristics of Major Organs (1 of 6)
Brain:
fastidious, requires 120 g/day of glucose
60% of a resting human’s daily consumption of glucose
20% of total oxygen consumption
no fuel reserves, but brain can adapt to fasting and use ketone bodies

Fuel Characteristics of Major Organs (2 of 6)
Muscle:
uses glucose, fatty acids, ketone bodies
resting—fatty acids are primary fuel, exertion—stored glycogen is primary fuel (muscle also possesses creatine phosphate stores to regenerate A T P)
lacks glucose 6-phosphatase, so glucose released by glycogenolysis cannot be exported for use by other tissues

Fuel Characteristics of Major Organs (3 of 6)
Muscle:
lactate is released during exertion because glycolysis produces pyruvate faster than the citric acid cycle can use it
Heart:
work output less variable than that of skeletal muscle - and continuous
highly aerobic, no energy reserves except low [creatine phosphate]
requires continuous supply of fuel and oxygen

Fuel Characteristics of Major Organs (4 of 6)
Adipose tissue:
major fuel depot through stored T A G s (enough calories to supply the whole body for two months or more)
some glucose metabolism must occur for synthesis of glycerol-3-phosphate for T A G synthesis; when [glucose] falls, free fatty acids are released for use by other tissues

Fuel Characteristics of Major Organs (5 of 6)
Liver:
synthesis of fuel components for other tissues: fatty acid synthesis and glucose synthesis, both from glycogen mobilization and gluconeogenesis
malonyl-C o A levels regulate fatty acid metabolism through inhibition of carnitine acyltransferase I, controlling fatty acid transport into mitochondria

Fuel Characteristics of Major Organs (6 of 6)
Liver:
buffers blood glucose partly through action of a high
hexokinase (“glucokinase”) and partly through control of the intracellular location of glucose transporters
Blood (connects organs):
vehicle of gas transport and metabolite transport
erythrocytes represent about half the total blood volume meet their energy needs primarily from glycolysis; no mitochondria in matured cells
17.2 Hormonal Regulation of Fuel Metabolism

Control of Fuel Metabolism in Mammals (1 of 3)
Maintenance of blood glucose levels is particularly critical to brain function
Major hormones:
Insulin
Glucagon
Epinephrine
Figure 17.2 Aspects of the control of blood glucose levels by pancreatic secretion of insulin and glucagon.

Control of Fuel Metabolism in Mammals (2 of 3)
Table 17.2 Major hormones controlling fuel metabolism in mammals

Control of Fuel Metabolism in Mammals (3 of 3)
Table 17.2 Major hormones controlling fuel metabolism in mammals

Actions of Glucagon That Lead to an Increase in Blood Glucose Levels
Figure 17.3 Actions of glucagon in liver that lead to a rise in blood glucose.

Increase of Blood Glucose Levels by Epinephrine
Epinephrine is released .by adrenal medulla in response to low blood glucose levels
It reacts with second messenger systems:
Muscle – activates adenylate cyclase and glycogenolysis
Adipose tissue – simulates breakdown of T A G
Pancreas – inhibits insulin secretion, stimulates glucagon secretion
Unlike glucagon, epinephrine effects are short-lived
It also functions as a neurotransmitter

Coordination of Energy Homeostasis (1 of 7)
A M P-activated protein kinase (A M P K)
a serine/threonine kinase that is activated when energy charge is low (high A M P levels)
stimulates pathways that lead to A T P production, while inhibiting A T P-utilizing pathways

Coordination of Energy Homeostasis (2 of 7)
Structure of the mammalian A M P K
Figure 17.4 Mammalian A M P-activated protein kinase (A M P K).

Coordination of Energy Homeostasis (3 of 7)
Mammalian target of rapamycin (m T O R)
like A M P K, a protein serine/threonine kinase found in all eukaryotes
active only under nutrient-rich conditions
promotes anabolic processes – cell proliferation, protein synthesis, lipid biosynthesis
was discovered during studies on the action of rapamycin, an immunosuppressant

Coordination of Energy Homeostasis (4 of 7)
AMPK and mTOR signaling pathways
Figure 17.5 A M P K and m T O R signaling pathways.

Coordination of Energy Homeostasis (5 of 7)
Sirtuins:
are a family of
-dependent protein deacetylases,
removing acetyl groups from protein lysine residues
are activated by high ratios of
and serve
as cellular redox state sensors
metabolic reprogramming proteins such as PGC-1α can be deacetylated by sirtuins, upregulating gluconeogenesis and mitochondrial biogenesis processes

Coordination of Energy Homeostasis (6 of 7)
Sirtuin reaction

Coordination of Energy Homeostasis (7 of 7)
SIRT1 (a sirtuin) and PGC-1α control the reprogramming of fuel utilization in response to fasting
Figure 17.6 P G C-1α and S I R T1 control the reprogramming of fuel utilization pathways in response to fasting.

Energy Generation Strategies in Proliferating (Abundant Nutrients) versus Nonproliferating (Scarce Nutrients) Cells (1 of 3)
AMPK, mTOR, and sirtuins evolved in unicellular organisms
Figure 17.7 Proliferating and nonproliferating cells use different metabolic strategies to generate energy.

Energy Generation Strategies in Proliferating (Abundant Nutrients) versus Nonproliferating (Scarce Nutrients) Cells (2 of 3)
Nondividing (nonproliferating) mammalian cells typically use oxidative metabolism to generate energy
Stimulation by growth factors causes differentiated cells to switch to glycolysis
Warburg (1925) noted rapidly dividing cancer cells metabolize glucose by aerobic glycolysis, but produce lactate rather than pyruvate (Warburg effect)

Energy Generation Strategies in Proliferating (Abundant Nutrients) versus Nonproliferating (Scarce Nutrients) Cells (3 of 3)
Figure 17.7 Proliferating and nonproliferating cells use different metabolic strategies to generate energy.

Fuel and Hormonal Control of Appetite (1 of 2)
In mammals, the brain coordinates whole-body energy homeostasis
A M P K activation promotes food intake
Insulin acts through PI3K (phosphoinositide 3-kinase) to inhibit food intake
Figure 17.8 Fuel and hormonal control of appetite in the arcuate nucleus of the hypothalamus.
F F A = free fatty acids; B C A A = branched chain amino acids

Fuel and Hormonal Control of Appetite (2 of 2)
Leptin has direct and indirect effects (via PI3K and  A M P K)
Adiponectin and ghrelin stimulate food intake via A M P K
Figure 17.8 Fuel and hormonal control of appetite in the arcuate nucleus of the hypothalamus.
F F A = free fatty acids; B C A A = branched chain amino acids
17.3 Responses to Metabolic Stress: Starvation, Diabetes

Major Events in the Storage and Use of Fuels in the Fed State, Unfed State, and Early Starvation
Figure 17.9 Major events in the storage, retrieval, and use of fuels in the fed and unfed states and in early starvation.

Diabetes Mellitus (1 of 2)
is a family of diseases resulting from insulin deficiency or defects in insulin response
The biochemical response to starvation is similar to the response to diabetes mellitus, in that alternative fuel sources must be found, because there is essentially a glucose deficiency

Diabetes Mellitus (2 of 2)
There are two common types of diabetes mellitus
Type 1 diabetes is caused by insulin deficiency (e.g., autoimmune destruction of pancreas β cells, mutation in insulin receptor, etc.)
Type 1 diabetes can be treated with (injections of) insulin

Metabolic Abnormalities in Diabetes
Figure 17.10 The metabolic abnormalities in diabetes.

Chapter 17 Summary (1 of 2)
Each organ or tissue of a multicellular organism has a distinctive profile of metabolic activities that allows it to serve its specialized functions
However, these tissues and organs must remain in constant communication to maintain homeostasis with respect to levels of nutrients (glucose), energy (A T P) and other factors
Glucose homeostasis is mainly controlled by the actions of three hormones – insulin, glucagon, and epinephrine

Chapter 17 Summary (2 of 2)
Two protein kinases, A M P K and m T O R, play central roles in orchestrating the metabolic activities of mammalian cells important for energy homeostasis by responding to A M P/A T P ratios, nutrient-poor/rich conditions, etc
Sirtuins act as metabolic sensors of the cellular redox state

18.2 Utilization of Ammonia: Biogenesis of Organic Nitrogen

Biogenesis of Organic Nitrogen (1 of 2)
Ammonia is toxic at high levels
All organisms assimilate

Figure 18.5 Reactions in assimilation of ammonia and major fates of the fixed nitrogen.

Glutamate Dehydrogenase
catalyzes a readily reversible reductive amination of α--ketoglutarate
in bacteria, it favors the formation of glutamate
in animals, where the intracellular concentration of
is low, the formation of α-ketoglutarate is favored

Glutamine Synthetase
generates a carboxamide using the amino acid side chain carboxyl group of glutamate
is called a synthetase (rather than a synthase) because it couples bond formation to A T P hydrolysis
Asparagine synthase catalyzes a similar reaction

Carbamoyl Phosphate Synthetase
generates carbamoyl phosphate, an intermediate for arginine and pyrimidine synthesis
either
or glutamine can serve as the nitrogen donor in
this reaction
18.3 The Nitrogen Economy and Protein Turnover

Nitrogen Demand and Supply (1 of 3)
The nitrogen economy summarizes the routes by which inorganic nitrogen is converted to ammonia and how this ammonia is utilized in the biosynthesis of organic nitrogen compounds such as amino acids
Rudolf Schoenheimer studied nitrogen metabolism in rats
in the 1930s; when fed
was excreted in urine, the rest was incorporated into proteins; of these a small fraction was recovered in Tyr, most was incorporated into other amino acids

Nitrogen Demand and Supply (2 of 3)
Importantly, the amount of unlabeled protein nitrogen excreted was equal to the amount of labeled nitrogen incorporated into rat proteins; thus a nitrogen equilibrium was maintained in the rat
Rats like most other organisms face the challenge that they do not have polymeric nitrogen compounds or other nitrogen deposits for storage
Animals must continuously replenish nitrogen supplies through diet

Nitrogen Demand and Supply (3 of 3)
In animals that do not consume a sufficient amount of nitrogen, proteins (mostly muscle proteins) are broken down and not replaced
Generally proteins are subject to continuous biosynthesis and degradation (protein turnover)
This allows replacement of damaged proteins and biological regulation
The average half-life of a protein in rat is 1 or 2 days
Ubiquitin and the 20S proteasome play an important role in intracellular protein degradation

The 20S Proteasome
is a nonlysosomal A T P-dependent protease
resent in the cytosol, proteasome action must be tightly controlled; it targets and degrades only proteins tagged with ubiquitin
Figure 18.6 Structure of the human proteasome.

Ubiquitin (1 of 2)
Human ubiquitin is a 76 residue protein expressed in all cells
Proteins targeted for degradation are covalently bonded to ubiquitin via an isopeptide bond; this reaction is catalyzed by a ubiquitin ligase

Ubiquitin (2 of 2)
Ubiquitin ligase also catalyzes the addition of ubiquitin to a previously conjugated ubiquitin
Polyubiquitin chains are  recognized by the proteasome resulting in degradation of the tagged protein
18.4 Coenzymes Involved in Nitrogen Metabolism

Pyridoxal Phosphate (P L P) (1 of 3)
serves as a coenzyme for the majority of enzymes that catalyze some chemical change at the α-, β-, or γ-carbons of the common amino acids, including:
decarboxylations
eliminations
racemizations
retro-aldol reactions

Pyridoxal Phosphate (P L P) (2 of 3)
All P L P requiring enzymes act via the formation of a Schiff base (aldimine) between the amino acid and coenzyme

Pyridoxal Phosphate (P L P) (3 of 3)
This formation of a Schiff base (or aldimine) allows an electron withdrawal onto the pyridine ring

Role of P L P in Transaminations
Figure 18.7 Involvement of pyridoxal phosphate in transamination.

Tetrahydrofolate (T H F) (1 of 2)
Coenzymes derived from the folic acid participate in the generation and utilization of single-carbon functional groups:
methyl
methylene
formyl

Tetrahydrofolate (T H F) (2 of 2)
Folic acid deficiency increases the risk of cardiovascular disease and birth defects in humans

Conversion of Folate to T H F (1 of 2)
T H F is the active coenzyme that is catalyzed from folate in two steps, both carried out by dihydrofolate reductase (D H F R)

Conversion of Folate to T H F (2 of 2)
D H F R is the target of multiple clinically useful antimetabolites (aminopterin and methotrexate can induce remission in acute leukemia)

Carrying One-Carbon Units by T H F
T H F carries activated one-carbon units for the formation of new C–S, C–C, and C–N bonds
5,10-methylene-T H F (at right) is carrying a methylene unit attached to the 5 and 10 positions that can be used to create new C–C bonds
5-methyl-T H F is used to transfer the methyl group to homocysteine, to form a C–S bond
10-formyl-T H F is used to create new C–N bonds

Amino acid degradation usually begins with conversion to the corresponding α-keto acid by transamination or oxidative deamination

Serum glutamate-oxaloacetate transaminase (S G O T) and serum glutamate-pyruvate transaminase (S G P T) are important in clinical diagnoses of heart and liver damage

Degradation to Pyruvate
Alanine, cysteine, glycine, serine, and threonine are degraded to pyruvate in mammals
Figure 18.13 Alanine, cysteine, glycine, serine, and threonine are degraded to pyruvate in mammals.

Degradation to Oxaloacetate (1 of 2)
Asparagine and aspartate are converted to oxaloacetate
Asparaginase is an example of enzyme-based chemotherapy
The E. coli enzyme is widely used in the treatment of childhood acute lymphoblastic leukemia

Degradation to Oxaloacetate (2 of 2)
Normal and malignant lymphocytes depend on the uptake of asparagine from the blood for growth, and asparaginase depletes circulating asparagine
Asparaginase treatment leads to complete remission in many cases

Degradation to Glutamate (1 of 2)
Glutamine, proline, arginine, and histidine are all degraded to glutamate

Degradation to Glutamate (2 of 2)
Histidine also undergoes a P L P-dependent decarboxylation to generate histamine, a potent vasodilator
Antihistamines are used to treat inflammation and allergic reactions

Methionine as a Precursor of S-adenosyl-l-Methionine (1 of 2)
Amino acids are used as precursors of important metabolites such as S-adenosyl-l-methionine, synthesized from methionine

Chapter 18 Summary (1 of 3)
Although inorganic nitrogen
is abundant, metabolism
most organisms is limited by nitrogen bioavailability
Some organisms can reduce
(nitrogen fixation) or nitrate
to generate ammonia, which can be utilized by all organisms
Protein are continually degraded (turned over) and replaced; most amino acids released by protein turnover are reutilized for protein synthesis

Chapter 18 Summary (2 of 3)
Multiple coenzymes (such as pyridoxal phosphate,
tetrahydrofolate, and vitamin
are required for various
reactions in nitrogen metabolism
The initial step of amino acid degradation is usually the removal of the α-amino group, either through transamination or oxidative deamination; in mammals, the resulting ammonia is converted to urea in the (Krebs–Henseleit) urea cycle
The capacity to synthesize amino acids varies greatly among organisms; mammals require about half of the 20 common amino acids in the diet

Chapter 18 Summary (3 of 3)
Amino acids are synthesized from intermediates of glycolysis, citric acid cycle, and pentose phosphate pathway
Amino acids are not only needed for protein synthesis, but are also precursors for the synthesis processes of other metabolites, such as purine nucleotides, pyrimidine nucleotides, glutathione, creatine phosphate, neurotransmitters, and hormones

19.1 Outlines of Pathways in Nucleotide Metabolism

Pathways for the De Novo Nucleotide Synthesis
Figure 19.1 Overview of nucleotide metabolism.
orange = ribonucleotide pathways; blue = deoxy-ribonucleotide pathways

Reutilization of Purine and Pyrimidine Bases and Nucleosides
Catabolic pathways are shown in blue; resynthesis of nucleotides by salvage pathways is shown in magenta
Figure 19.2 Reutilization of purine and pyrimidine bases and nucleosides.

Nucleic Acid Degradation (1 of 2)
Nucleic acids are degraded to oligonucleotides by endonucleases
These oligonucleotides are further cleaved by exonucleases called phosphodiesterases to nucleoside monophosphates
Nucleotidases remove the phosphate group (nucleoside kinase catalyses the A T P-dependent phosphorylation of nucleosides)
Nucleoside phosphorylase (below) catalyzes the reversible phosphorolysis of the glycosidic bond

Nucleic Acid Degradation (2 of 2)
Nucleoside phosphorylase (below) catalyzes the reversible phosphorolysis of the glycosidic bond

5 prime -Phosphoribosyl-α-ᴅ-1-
Pyrophosphate (PRPP) – A Central Metabolite in the De Novo and Salvage Pathways (1 of 2)
Utilizing nucleobases requires an activated sugar-phosphate donor, P R P P
P R P P is synthesized from ribose 5-phosphate and A T P by P R P P synthetase

5 prime - Phosphoribosyl-α-ᴅ-1-
Pyrophosphate (P R P P) – A Central Metabolite in the De Novo and Salvage Pathways (2 of 2)
P R P P can then be used by phosphoribosyl transferases in the synthesis of nucleoside monophosphates
19.2 De Novo Biosynthesis of Purine Ribonucleotides

Synthesis of the Purine Nucleotide I M P (1 of 2)
Inosine 5′-monophosphate (I M P) is the branch point in purine nucleotide synthesis
A M P (adenylate) and G M P (guanylate) are synthesized from I M P in short, separate pathways
Figure 19.3 Low-molecular-weight precursors to the purine ring.
Structure of I M P
Low-molecular weight precursors to the purine ring

Synthesis of the Purine Nucleotide I M P (2 of 2)
1: Glutamine-P R P P amidotrans-ferase; 2: Glycinamide ribo- nucleotide (G A R) synthetase; 3: G A R formyltransferase; 4: Formylglycinamide ribonucleotide (F G A R) amidotransferase; 5: 5′-Aminoimidazole ribonucleotide (A I R) synthetase; 6: A I R carboxylase; 7: N-Succinyl-5-aminoimidazole-4-carboxamide ribonucleotide (S A I C A R) synthetase; 8: S A I C A R lyase; 9: A I C A R formyltransferase; 10: I M P synthase
Figure 19.4 De novo biosynthesis of the purine ring.

Enzyme Organization in Purine Biosynthesis the Purinosome (1 of 2)
Enzyme organization facilitates the flow of intermediates through metabolic pathways
The ten reactions of the de novo purine biosynthesis are catalyzed by six enzymes that associate reversibly into the purinosome
Figure 19.5 Model of the purinosome.

Enzyme Organization in Purine Biosynthesis the Purinosome (2 of 2)
Two additional enzymes, C-1 T H F synthase and serine hydroxymethyltransferase (S H M T) also associate in this complex; these two enzymes provide the 10-formyl-T H F at its major sites of utilization (E3 and E9)
Figure 19.5 Model of the purinosome.

Pathways from I M P to G M P and A M P (1 of 2)
Activities of the two pathways are linked in two ways:
A T P is required for G M P synthesis and G T P is required for A M P synthesis
G M P allosterically inhibits the reaction from I M P to X M P and A M P inhibits the conversion of I M P to adenylosuccinate
These effects combine to promote A M P synthesis when G M P is abundant and vice versa
19.3 Purine Catabolism and Its Medical Significance

Catabolism of Purine Nucleosides to Uric Acid
Figure 19.7 Catabolism of purine nucleosides to uric acid.

Hyperuricemia and Gout (1 of 2)
In humans, purine catabolism ends in the excretion of uric acid, which is a good scavenger of free radicals, but is rather insoluble
In North American and Europe, about 3 in 1000 individuals suffer from hyperuricemia, a disease characterized by very high uric acid levels in the blood, often due to a deficiency of hypoxanthine-guanine phosphoribosyltransferase (H G P R T)

Hyperuricemia and Gout (2 of 2)
These very high uric acid levels lead to the formation of uric acid crystals and these crystals accumulate in joints (gout) causing inflammation and arthritis
Figure 19.8 Uric acid accumulation as a consequence of HGPRT deficiency.
H: Hypoxanthine G: Guanine

Treatment of Gout with Allopurinol
Allopurinol is converted to alloxanthine by xanthine dehydrogenase
Alloxanthine is a suicide inhibitor of xanthine dehydrogenase; it is converted to an active inhibitor by the target enzyme itself

Severe Combined Immunodeficiency Disease (S C I D) and Purine Catabolism (1 of 2)
S C I D is a rare hereditary disease marked by an inability to react to immune challenges
Some cases of S C I D are a result of a deficiency of adenosine deaminase (A D A) in purine catabolism
A D A acts on both adenosine and deoxyadenosine; when A D A is absent, deoxyadenosime accumulates and is converted to d A T P, an allosteric inhibitor of ribonucleotide reductase (an enzyme that begins the flow of nucleotides into D N A)

Severe Combined Immunodeficiency Disease (S C I D) and Purine Catabolism (2 of 2)
This causes other d N T P s to disappear and immune cells cannot proliferate
A D A deficiency was one of the first genetic diseases to be treated by gene therapy
19.4 Pyrimidine Ribonucleotide Metabolism

Pyrimidine Synthesis (1 of 3)
occurs by an unbranched pathway, at the nucleobase level and with U T P serving as an end product and a precursor to C T P
All six atoms in the pyrimidine ring are derived from only two precursors:
Aspartate
Carbamoyl phosphate

Pyrimidine Synthesis (2 of 3)
The synthesis of pyrimidines begins with the synthesis of carbamoyl phosphate
Carbamoyl phosphate synthetase joins ammonia (released from glutamine) with bicarbonate and phosphate (from A T P)

Pyrimidine Synthesis (3 of 3)
The structure of Escherichia coli carbamoyl phosphate synthetase shows an interior tunnel that connects the three active sites; this allows the transfer of unstable intermediates from site to site
Figure 19.10 Channeling in carbamoyl phosphate synthetase.

De Novo Synthesis of Pyrimidine Ribonucleotides
Carbamoyl phosphate synthetase; 2: Aspartate transcarbamoylase; 3: Dihydroorotase; 4: Dihydroorotate dehy-drogenase; 5: Orotate phosphoribosyltransferase; 6: O M P decarboxylase; 7: U M P kinase; 8: Nucleoside diphosphate kinase; 9: C T P synthetase
Figure 19.9 De novo biosynthesis of pyrimidine ribonucleotides.
Mechanism of the Glutamine-Dependent Amidotransferase Reaction

Multifunctional Enzymes in Eukaryotic Pyrimidine Metabolism (1 of 2)
The eight reactions leading to U T P and C T P are identical throughout the biological world; however, there are large differences in the enzymes used in various organisms
E. coli A TCase contains six each of two subunits, arranged as two catalytic trimers and three regulatory dimers

Multifunctional Enzymes in Eukaryotic Pyrimidine Metabolism (2 of 2)
In contrast, the eukaryotic A TCase is a multifunctional protein (called the C A D protein) catalyzing the first three reactions of the pathway
Eukaryotes also combine reactions five and six into the bifunctional U M P synthase
19.5 Deoxyribonucleotide Metabolism

Synthesis of Deoxyribonucleoside Triphosphate (1 of 2)
Most cells contain 5 to 10 times more R N A than D N A
Ribonucleotides serve multiple metabolic roles, while deoxyribonucleotides only make up D N A
Figure 19.11 Overview of deoxyribonucleoside triphosphate biosynthesis.

Synthesis of Deoxyribonucleoside Triphosphate (2 of 2)
Two specific processes make up d N T P biosynthesis:
Origin of deoxyribose
Origin of the thymine methyl group
Figure 19.11 Overview of deoxyribonucleoside triphosphate biosynthesis.

Reduction of Ribonucleotides to Deoxyribonucleotides (1 of 4)
All four ribonucleotides are reduced by ribonucleotide reductase (R N R), which uses a free radical mechanism
Figure 19.12 Structure of E. coli ribonucleotide reductase.

Reduction of Ribonucleotides to Deoxyribonucleotides (2 of 4)
Three classes of R N R are found in nature:
Class I is widespread and uses a catalytic Tyr radical
Class II uses B12 coenzyme to generate the radical
Figure 19.12 Structure of E. coli ribonucleotide reductase.

Reduction of Ribonucleotides to Deoxyribonucleotides (3 of 4)
Class III, which functions only under anaerobic conditions, uses S-adenosylmethionine to generate a glycine radical
Figure 19.12 Structure of E. coli ribonucleotide reductase.

Reduction of Ribonucleotides to Deoxyribonucleotides (4 of 4)
R N R is an α2β2 heterodimer in E. coli, yeast, and humans
The Tyr radical is in the small β-subunit, some 35 Å from the active site in the large α-subunit
Figure 19.12 Structure of E. coli ribonucleotide reductase.

N A D P H as the Ultimate Source of Electrons for R N R
Figure 19.14 Reductive electron transport sequences in the action of ribonucleotide reductase.
These electrons are shuttled through either thioredoxin (red arrows) or glutaredoxin (blue arrows)

Mechanism of Reduction of a Ribonucleoside Diphosphate by a Class I R N R
Figure 19.13 Mechanism of reduction of a ribonucleoside diphosphate by a class I ribonucleotide reductase.

Strict Regulation of R N R Activity and Specificity
Figure 19.15 Structural relationships among catalytic and allosteric regulatory sites in the RNR large subunit.
R N R has two classes of control sites, activity sites and specificity sites to ensure optimally balanced pools of D N A precursors are maintained

Structural Basis for R N R Inhibition by d A T P (1 of 3)
R1 α subunits are shown in light and dark blue
R2 β subunits are shown in red and orange
Regions binding d A T P that interact with β are shown in green
Figure 19.16 Structural basis for RNR inhibition by dATP.

Structural Basis for R N R Inhibition by d A T P (2 of 3)
In the presence of dA T P, the enzyme forms an α4β4 tetramer (shown); the A T P-cone domain of α2 buries a portion of β2 which blocks the proton-coupled electron transport chain and prevents catalysis
Figure 19.16 Structural basis for RNR inhibition by dATP.

Structural Basis for R N R Inhibition by d A T P (3 of 3)
In the presence of A T P (which competes for the same binding site with d A T P), the tetramer is not formed and the enzyme is activated
Figure 19.16 Structural basis for RNR inhibition by dATP.

Salvage and De Novo Synthetic Pathways to Thymidine Triphosphate
U M P serves as the immediate precursor to thymidine monophosphate (d T M P) through the action of thymidylate synthase (T S)
Figure 19.17 Salvage and de novo synthetic pathways to thymidine triphosphate.

The Thymidylate Synthesis Cycle
5,10-methylene- T H F is a carbon and electron donor in T S. This produces dihydrofolate which is reduced by dhydrofolate reductase to T H F. T H F receives another methylene unit from the action of serine hydroxymethyl-transferase.
Figure 19.18 The thymidylate synthesis cycle.

Salvage Enzymes for Deoxyribonucleotide Biosynthesis in Human Cells (1 of 3)
Purine salvage pathways often involve phosphoribosyltransferase reactions and P R P P; after phosphorylation the purine r N D P s can enter deoxy-ribonucleotide metabolism via R N R
Figure 19.19 Salvage enzymes for deoxyribonucleotide biosynthesis in human cells.

Salvage Enzymes for Deoxyribonucleotide Biosynthesis in Human Cells (2 of 3)
Pyrimidine salvage to deoxy-ribonucleotides generally involves A T P-dependent deoxyribonucleoside kinases
Human cells contain four different deoxyribonucleoside kinases
Figure 19.19 Salvage enzymes for deoxyribonucleotide biosynthesis in human cells.

Salvage Enzymes for Deoxyribonucleotide Biosynthesis in Human Cells (3 of 3)
Severe genetic diseases, involving either the nervous system or muscle function, arise in humans when mitochondrial deoxyribonucleotide salvage enzymes are deficient and mitochondria in affected tissues cannot maintain adequate amounts of mitochondrial D N A
Figure 19.19 Salvage enzymes for deoxyribonucleotide biosynthesis in human cells.

Cancer Treatment Using Inhibitors of Thymidylate Synthase (1 of 2)
Chemotherapy is the treatment of diseases with chemical agents
Inhibition of thymidylate synthase (T S) is an approach to cancer chemotherapy by causing specific inhibition of D N A synthesis
5-fluorouracil and 5-fluorodeoxyuridine were found to form 5-fluorodeoxyuridine monophosphate (F d U M P), a potent inhibitor of T S
Cancer Treatment Using Inhibitors ofThymidylate Synthase (2 of 2)

F d U M P—A Mechanism-Based Inhibitor of Thymidylate Synthase (1 of 2)
F d U M P exhibits irreversible inhibition of TS only in the presence of the other substrate, 5,10-methylene- T H F

F d U M P—A Mechanism-Based Inhibitor of Thymidylate Synthase (2 of 2)
Proteolysis of the TS-FdUMP-5,10-methylene- T H F complex led to the complex shown at the right
FdUMP is covalently linked to T H F and to the enzyme

Mechanism of the Reaction Catalyzed by Thymidylate Synthase
Figure 19.20 Mechanism of the reaction catalyzed by thymidylate synthase.

Structure of the Homodimeric Thymidylate Synthase from Escherichia coli
Figure 19.21 Structure of the homodimeric thymidylate synthase from E. coli.

Structure-Based (Rational) Drug Design (1 of 2)
In 1991 the crystal structure of the human thymidylate synthase enzyme was reported
Analysis of substrate binding interactions has led to design and synthesis of folate cofactor analogs (antifolates)

Structure-Based (Rational) Drug Design (2 of 2)
Antifolates compete effectively with 5,10-methylene-T H F for binding to T S (Ki values as low as 0.4 nM)
Three such inhibitors are shown here: Pemetrexed and raltitrexed are both used clinically to treat certain types of cancer
19.6 Virus-Directed Alterations of Nucleotide Metabolism

Nucleotide Modifications in Viruses (1 of 3)
Wyatt and Cohen had shown in 1952 that D N A of bacteriophage T4, T2, and T6 contains no cytosine but instead contains 5-hydroxymethylcytosine most of which were further modified by glycosylation

Nucleotide Modifications in Viruses (2 of 3)
Base modifications are unusual, but, when present, virus-coded enzymes with expanded substrate specificity can be used as targets for chemo-therapy of viral diseases
Herpes virus encodes for a thymidylate kinase (T K) with broad specificity compared to human T K; nucleoside analogs such as acyclovir are readily phosphorylated and interfere with viral replication

Nucleotide Modifications in Viruses (3 of 3)
Metabolic pathways leading to nucleotide modifications in E. coli infected by phages T2, T4, or T6; virus-coded enzymes are shown in red. H m = hydroxymethyl
Figure 19.22 Metabolic pathways leading to nucleotide modifications in E. coli infected by phages T2, T4, or T6.
19.7 Other Medically Useful Analogs

Treatment of Viral Infections with Pyrimidine Nucleoside Analogs
H I V infections can be treated with pyrimidine nucleoside analogs that can be converted to d N T P s, but that prevent replication after incorporation into D N A because of the absence of a 3′ hydroxyl terminus for chain extension

Chapter 19 Summary (1 of 2)
Purine and pyrimidine nucleotides arise within cells from nucleic acid breakdown, from reuse (or salvage) of performed nucleosides or nucleobases, or from de novo biosynthesis
Purine nucleotides are synthesized via 5′-phospho-ribosyl-α-ᴅ-1-pyrophosphate (P R P P) to inosine monophosphate (I M P), where separate pathways lead to adenine and guanine nucleotides

Chapter 19 Summary (2 of 2)
Pyrimidine synthesis begins with the formation of carbamoyl aspartate, which is converted in four steps to uridine monophosphate (U M P); U M P is the branching point for distinct routes to cytosine and uridine nucleotides
In most organisms, the ribonucleoside diphosphates are substrates for reduction of the ribose sugar in situ, yielding deoxyribonucleoside diphosphates
Thymine nucleotides are synthesized from deoxy- uridine nucleotide (d U M P) via a methylene group transfer

2.2 The Nature of Noncovalent Interactions

Types of Noncovalent Interactions (1 of 5)

Types of Noncovalent Interactions (3 of 5)
Charge–charge interactions
All noncovalent interactions are electrostatic in nature
The simplest electrostatic interactions are those between a pair of charged particles, called a salt bridge

Charge–Charge Interactions (1 of 3)
The attraction of the oppositely charged ions is governed by Coulomb’s Law:
with F = force, q = charge, and r = distance separating the charges

Charge–Charge Interactions (2 of 3)
Figure 2.4 Charge–charge interactions in an ionic crystal.
In an ionic crystal

Charge–Charge Interactions (3 of 3)
Coulomb’s Law applies in a vacuum. In a cell, charges are screened in solution by the medium that exists between the charges
The screening effect of a medium is represented by
the dielectric constant (water has a high dielectric constant, 80) giving rise to the equation:

Types of Noncovalent Interactions (4 of 5)
Dipoles and induced dipole interactions
Polar molecules have no net charge but an asymmetric internal distribution of charge
they have a dipole moment
with fractional charges
In (aqueous) cellular environments, polar molecules can be attracted by nearby ions (charge-dipole interactions) or by other polar molecules (dipole-dipole interactions)

Dipoles and Induced Dipole Interactions (1 of 4)
Some molecules do not have a permanent dipole moment, but are polarizable in the presence of an electrical field
Their dipole moment can be induced
Charge-induced dipole interactions are induced by charged molecules, while dipole-induced dipole interactions are induced by polar molecules with permanent dipole moment

Dipoles and Induced Dipole Interactions (2 of 4)
At close distance, the motion of electrons in one molecule can influence the motion of electrons in neighboring molecules
These molecules can mutually induce their dipole moment and attract each other
This kind of interaction is called dispersion force, induced dipole-induced dipole interaction, or van der Waals interaction

Dipoles and Induced Dipole Interactions (3 of 4)
Permanent and induced dipoles give rise to charge separation

Hydrogen bonding is a major factor in determining the structure and properties of biomolecules

a permanent dipole
high heat capacity
a density greater in liquid
relatively high dielectric constant

Hydrophilic Molecules in Aqueous Solution
Solvent can compete with intramolecular H-bond donors/acceptors
Figure 2.13 Exchange of internal hydrogen bonds for water hydrogen bonds.

Hydrophobic Molecules in Aqueous Solution
Solvent can form “clathrate” structures that surround nonpolar surface areas
Ordering water this way decreases entropy of the solvent
This factor—the “hydrophobic effect”—stabilizes protein structure by driving apolar side chains on amino acids together, to minimize formation of this ordered structure

2.4 Acid–Base Equilibria

Acid and Bases: Proton Donors and Acceptors (1 of 3)
The behavior of molecules in biochemical processes occurring in aqueous environment depends on their state of ionization, particularly protonation
Acids are proton
donors and bases are proton
acceptors (“Brønsted-Lowry definition”)
A strong acid dissociates (almost) completely into a proton and a conjugate base, while a weak acid dissociates only partially
the dissociated proton is transferred to water
yield a hydronium ion

Acid and Bases: Proton Donors and Acceptors (2 of 3)

2.6 Tools of Biochemistry

Electrophoresis (1 of 4)
It is defined as the motion of charged dispersed particles relative to a fluid under the influence of a spatially uniform electric field and is widely used for analytical separation of biological macroions
Common biochemical applications are:
agarose gel electrophoresis (mainly to separate D N A molecules)
polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (mainly to separate proteins under denaturing conditions)
isoelectric focusing (mainly to separate proteins)

Electrophoresis (2 of 4)

Electrophoresis (4 of 4)
Isoelectric focusing
While agarose and polyacrylamide gel electrophoreses (as used in biochemical laboratories) separate macroions based on their sizes (molecular weights), isoelectric focusing separates macroions based on their surface charge-associated isoelectric point (p I)
Macroions move in the electric field until the p H is reached where they have no net charge

Isoelectric Focusing
Figure 2A.4 Isoelectric focusing of proteins.

Chapter 2 Summary (1 of 3)
The structure and function of biomolecules is defined by various types of (weak) noncovalent interactions
Water is the medium of life. Hydrophilic compounds dissolve easily in water, while hydrophobic compounds dissolve only to a limited extent. Amphipathic compounds have both hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties and are used to form membrane bilayers surrounding cells and organelles
Charges on biomolecules are established by the ionization of weak acids and bases

Chapter 2 Summary (2 of 3)
Most physiological processes occur in the p H range between 6.5 and 8.0
The behavior of a weak acid and its conjugate base in
relationship to p H and
is described by the Henderson–
Hasselbalch equation
Solutions of weak conjugate acid/base that resist drastic
p H changes near their
when strong acid or base is
added are called buffer solutions
Many biomolecules are macroions (polyelectrolytes or polyampholytes) and their behavior in solution depends on p H and the presence of small ions

Chapter 2 Summary (3 of 3)
Biologically relevant macroions (such as nucleic acids and proteins) in solution can be separated by applying an electric field, in which these macroions start to migrate
As applied in biochemical laboratories, agarose and polyacrylamide gel electrophoreses separate macroions by sizes (molecular weights), while isoelectric focusing separates them according to their isoelectric points

20.1 Modular Nature of Signal Transduction Systems: An Overview of Hormone Action

Signal Transduction, Hormone Action, and Metabolic Coordination
Signal transduction is defined as the reception of an environmental stimulus by a cell, which leads to metabolic changes that adapt the cell to the stimulus
There are four major classes of agents in signal transduction; all act through receptors:
hormones (act at a distance)
growth factors (action is long-lasting)
neurotransmitters (secretion close to target cells)
pheromones (act upon cells in a different organism)

Hormones (1 of 3)
In vertebrates, hormones are small molecules secreted by endocrine glands, which travel through the bloodstream and bind to specific receptors on or in target cells
Figure 20.1 The major human endocrine glands and their central nervous system control centers.

Hormones (2 of 3)
Several hormones are involved in the coordination of carbohydrate metabolism:
epinephrine, secreted from the adrenal gland, promotes metabolic energy generation in muscle
Figure 20.1 The major human endocrine glands and their central nervous system control centers.

Hormones (3 of 3)
Several hormones are involved in the coordination of carbohydrate metabolism:
glucagon, secreted by the pancreas, acts upon liver to promote gluconeogenesis
insulin, secreted by the pancreas, promotes uptake of glucose into cells
Figure 20.1 The major human endocrine glands and their central nervous system control centers.

Hormone Classes and Their Receptors (1 of 2)
Chemically, hormones include
peptides or polypeptides (insulin, glucagon)
steroids (glucocorticoids and sex hormones)
amino acid derivatives (catecholamines such as epinephrine and thyroxine, derived from tyrosine)

Hormone Classes and Their Receptors (2 of 2)
Hormone receptors may be located in the plasma membrane or within the cell (nuclear receptors)
Three major classes of membrane-bound receptors
proteins that influence second messenger synthesis
ion channels
proteins with intrinsic enzyme activity

Hierarchical Nature of Hormone Control (1 of 2)
The pituitary is the first target for most hormones and is under hypothalmic control
Pituitary hormones then act on secondary targets
Figure 20.2 Hierarchical nature of hormone action in vertebrates.

Hierarchical Nature of Hormone Control (2 of 2)
Neural stimulation of the adrenal medulla releases epinephrine
Figure 20.2 Hierarchical nature of hormone action in vertebrates.
20.2 G Protein-Coupled Signaling

Receptors as Defined by Interactions with Drugs (1 of 4)
Before the structure of hormone receptors were known, tissues and cells had been categorized pharmacologically in terms of their response to epinephrine analogs

Receptors as Defined by Interactions with Drugs (2 of 4)
The epinephrine (adrenaline) analogs such as isoproterenol and propranolol were classified as acting either as:
agonists – agents that act similarly to epinephrine
antagonists – agents that block the action of epinephrine

Receptors as Defined by Interactions with Drugs (3 of 4)
Propranolol antagonizes
adrenergic receptors
(controls blood pressure)
Isoproterenol agonizes
adrenergic receptors
(used to treat asthma)

Receptors as Defined by Interactions with Drugs (4 of 4)
In general, agonists are substances that mimic the effects of a natural compound by binding and activating the same cellular receptor; antagonists are substances that counteract the effects of a natural compound by binding to the cellular receptor and blocking its action

Biological Actions of Some Adrenergic Receptors (1 of 4)
Adrenergic is based on adrenaline, an older term for epinephrine
Adrenergic receptors were originally thought to be adenylate cyclases

Transducer (G-protein)

Actions of nitric oxide:
Regulation of neurotransmission
Stimulating defense to microbial infection
Vasodilation

20.3 Receptor Tyrosine Kinases and Insulin Signaling

Characteristics of Receptor Tyrosine Kinases (R T K s)
R T K s possess a single membrane spanning domain, an extracellular ligand-binding domain and a cytoplasmic domain with tyrosine kinase activity
The insulin receptor is an R T K but is unique in that, it exists as a covalently-linked dimer even in the absence of ligand

General Structure of R T K s
Figure 20.9 Representative receptor tyrosine kinase families.

Activation of the Protein Kinase Domain of the Epidermal Growth Factor (E G F) Receptor
Once the agonist is bound, the monomeric R T K dimerizes and the kinase domain is autoactivated by phosphorylation. The activated R T K recruits intracellular signaling or adaptor proteins.
Figure 20.10 Activation of the protein tyrosine kinase of the E G F receptor.

Signal Pathways Involving the Insulin Receptor
Figure 20.11 Signaling pathways involving the insulin receptor, illustrative of R T K s. I N S, insulin.
20.4 Hormones and Gene Expression: Nuclear Receptors

Characteristics of Nuclear Receptors (1 of 2)
Hormonal effects occurring through G-proteins are short lived, while those occurring through nuclear receptors involve longer term changes related to growth or cell differentiation
Nuclear receptors bind steroid and related hormones, which can diffuse through the plasma membrane
Ligand-bound nuclear receptors act on D N A and affect gene transcription

Characteristics of Nuclear Receptors (2 of 2)
Nuclear receptors possess a zinc containing N-terminal D N A-binding domain and a C-terminal hormone-binding domain
Nuclear receptors bind to sites on the D N A called hormone response elements (H R E s)

Steroid Hormone Receptors as Drug Targets (1 of 2)
Tamoxifen is an estrogen receptor antagonist (it binds estrogen receptor but prevents the response)
The growth of some breast tumor cells is activated by estrogen; tamoxifen slows cell growth in these tissues

Steroid Hormone Receptors as Drug Targets (2 of 2)
R U 486 is a progesterone receptor antagonist and prevents the events necessary for a fertilized ovum to implant in the uterus
20.5 Signal Transduction, Growth Control, and Cancer

Signal Transduction and Cancer (1 of 2)
Most oncogenes (genes whose action is involved in carcinogenesis) are mutant forms of genes encoding growth factors or other agents in signal transduction
Table 20.4 Oncogene products as elements of signal transduction Pathways

Signal Transduction and Cancer (2 of 2)
Table 20.4 Oncogene products as elements of signal transduction Pathways
Source: Data from J.D. Watson, M. Gilman, J.Witkowski, and M. Zoller, Recombinat D N A , 2nd ed. (New York: scientific American Books,1992),p.339.

Conversion to Oncogenes
Proto-oncogenes can be converted to oncogenes by
infection by a virus or
mutation of the cellular proto-oncogene
Figure 20.14 Pathways by which proto-oncogenes can become oncogenes.

The Ras Oncogene Closely Resembles Normal Human Ras but Lacks G T Pase Activity
Ras protein is mutationally altered in many human tumors
Most mutations in ras are found in the switch regions which contact the G T P substrate
Figure 20.15 Structural basis for Ras activation by G T P.

p53 Is a Tumor Suppression Gene (1 of 2)
Tumor suppressor genes, in their normal form, serve to suppress tumors
p53 is a D N A-binding protein that signals a cell with major D N A damage to undergo apoptosis (programmed cell death)
Loss of p53 function leads to tumorigenesis
Roughly half of all human tumors possess mutations in the p53 gene

p53 Is a Tumor Suppression Gene (2 of 2)
p53-D N A complex. The most often mutated residues are shown in yellow.
Figure 20.16 Structure of the p53–D N A complex.

Chromosomal Translocation in Chronic Myelogenous Leukemia (1 of 2)
In this chromosomal translocation event, portions of genes 9 and 22 are linked, fusing the parts of two oncogenes (b c r and c-a b l)
Gene fusion yields a novel protein tyrosine kinase that stimulates growth-promoting pathways

Chromosomal Translocation in Chronic Myelogenous Leukemia (2 of 2)
Gleevec™ is a specific inhibitor of this oncoprotein and a very effective treatment for this form of leukemia

Neurons and Synapses
Figure 20.19 Multiple synapses on the body of a single neuron.
Each neuron in the brain communicates with roughly 1000 other cells. The synapses may be either stimulatory or inhibitory

Release of Neurotransmitters by Synaptic Transmission Events
Synapses use different neurotransmitters. For example, cholinergic synapses use acetylcholine, while dopaminergic synapses use dopamine
Figure 20.17 Transmission of a neural impulse across a synapse, such as a cholinergic synapse.

The Nicotinic Acetylcholine Receptor Is a Gated Ion Channel (1 of 3)
The nicotinc acetylchloline receptor undergoes an acetylcholine-dependent conformational change, opening the pore
Figure 20.18 The nicotinic acetylcholine receptor.

The Nicotinic Acetylcholine Receptor Is a Gated Ion Channel (2 of 3)
Acetylcholine is rapidly degraded in the synaptic cleft by acetylcholin-esterase, restoring resting potential

The Nicotinic Acetylcholine Receptor Is a Gated Ion Channel (3 of 3)
Nicotine is an agonist tubocurarine is an antagonist

Drug Abuse and Neurotransmitter Receptors
Many abused drugs act as agonists or antagonists of central nervous system neurotransmitter receptors

Prozac - A Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitor (1 of 2)
Secreted neurotransmitters have three fates:
Binding to postsynaptic receptors
Catabolism in the cleft
Reuptake into the cell for repacking into storage vesicles
Prozac selectively blocks serotonin reuptake, thereby increasing the amount that reaches the postsynaptic receptor

Prozac - A Selective Serotonin Reuptake Inhibitor (2 of 2)
Although originally marketed as an antidepressant, Prozac is used to treat a wide range of psychiatric disorders

Chapter 20 Summary (1 of 4)
Hormones are regulatory substances produced in and released from cells in one organ, transported in tissue fluids (such as blood) and sensed by cells in other organs
Sensing hormones depends on the presence of specific receptors, which hormones associate with to initiate signal transduction pathways that lead to defined changes in metabolism or gene expression

Chapter 20 Summary (2 of 4)
G protein-coupled receptors (G P C R s) are integral membrane proteins
A hormone binds at a specific site of a G P C R located on the extracellular side of the plasma membrane, which triggers the interaction of a G protein on the cytosolic side of the membrane
This interaction activates the G protein, which then initiates the synthesis of second messengers (such as cyclic A M P) to affect target metabolic processes

Chapter 20 Summary (3 of 4)
Receptor tyrosine kinases (R T K s) are integral membrane proteins containing protein kinase domains on their cytosolic side
Upon interaction of a R T K with a hormone (such as insulin) on the extracellular side, the protein kinas activity of the R T K is turned on to act on processes at the gene and metabolic levels
Steroid hormones and related signaling agents act at the gene levels, through binding to receptors that control transcription of target genes

Chapter 20 Summary (4 of 4)
Neurotransmission is a form of signal transduction in which the signaling agent, a neurotransmitter, is released immediately adjacent to the target receptor
Many psychiatric drugs and drugs of abuse act as agonists or antagonists of neurotransmitters
