21.1 Bacterial and Viral Genomes

Fundamental Terms of Genetic Information (1 of 2)
Gene – a chromosomal segment that encodes for a single polypeptide chain, an R N A molecule or plays a regulatory function
Genome – the total genetic information contained in a cell, an organism, or a virus

Fundamental Terms of Genetic Information (2 of 2)
Chromosome – a compact strand of D N A that is encoded with genes; composed of chromatin; formed during nuclear division
Chromatin – D N A complexed with histones and other proteins; typically dispersed throughout the nucleus during interphase

Properties of Bacterial and Viral Genomes (1 of 2)
Table 21.1 Properties of some bacterial and viral genomes

Properties of Bacterial and Viral Genomes (2 of 2)
⁎Circularly permuted means that all genomes haave the same linear sequence of genes, but the beginning and ending points vary among different genomes

Packaging of Eukaryotic D N A in the Nucleus (1 of 3)
All cells (bacterial, archaeal or eukaryotic) must be able to package D N A into a limited space

Packaging of Eukaryotic D N A in the Nucleus (2 of 3)
In bacteria, chromosomal compaction largely occurs by negative supercoiling

Packaging of Eukaryotic D N A in the Nucleus (3 of 3)
Eukaryotic D N A is confined to the nucleus and wrapped around protein assemblies called histones

Packaging of Bacterial D N A
Bacterial compaction of D N A occurs largely by negative supercoiling
This compact structure is called a nucleoid
The nucleoid exists in the cytosol, with a few attachments to the membrane
Figure 21.1 Structure of a bacterial nucleoid.
21.2 Eukaryotic Genomes

Comparison of Prokaryotic and Eukaryotic Genomes
Figure 21.2 Genome size.
Most eukaryotes require much larger genomes than prokaryotes. Note that bacteria have only one chromosome per cell, while eukaryotic genomes usually have two copies of each chromosome (with the exception of sex chromosomes; e.g. the X and Y chromosomes in male humans)

The Fast Reassociation Kinetics of Bovine D N A Corresponds to Repeated Sequences in the D N A (1 of 2)
E. coli have only one copy and reassociation occurs slowly
Figure 21.3 Reassociation kinetics of E. coli and bovine DNA.

The Fast Reassociation Kinetics of Bovine D N A Corresponds to Repeated Sequences in the D N A (2 of 2)
Bovine D N A reassociates in two distinct steps
slow step for non-repeated sequences
fast step for repeated sequences
Figure 21.3 Reassociation kinetics of E. coli and bovine DNA.

A T and C G Repeats Appear as Satellite Bands in a Density Gradient Experiment
A T repeats are less dense than average composition D N A
C G repeats are more dense than average composition D N A
After shearing and separating these repeat bands appear as “satellites” and are sometimes called satellite D N A
Figure 21.3 Reassociation kinetics of E. coli and bovine D N A.

Other Repetitive Sequences (1 of 2)
Duplication of functional genes
if a cell requires large amounts of a gene product such as the ribosome or t R N A, many copies of the gene may be found
A l u sequences
so named because of the A l u I restriction site found in the sequences
there are more than 1 million copies of these 300 bp sequences in the human genome

Other Repetitive Sequences (2 of 2)
Some intron sequences are also highly repetitive
the presence of introns (intervening sequences) increases the size of the eukaryotic genome
some eukaryotic genes do not have introns (most histone genes do not)

Gene Variants and Pseudogenes (1 of 2)
Variants of the same gene may be expressed in different tissues or at different stages of development
Some variants are produced via alternate splicing, but many will have a completely separate gene

Gene Variants and Pseudogenes (2 of 2)
Gene families often include pseudogenes
Until recently pseudogenes were thought to be nonexpressed genes with no biological function; however, it is more likely that the role of pseudogenes in the human genome is not understood
21.3 Physical Organization of Eukaryotic Genes: Chromosomes and Chromatin

The Nucleus (1 of 2)
The nuclear envelope contains pores (about 9 µm in diameter) to allow the free diffusion of small molecules between the nucleus and cytoplasm
Exportins and importins assist with the selective transport of RNA and protein
The nuclear pore complex is an assembly of 500 to 1000 protein molecules (nucleoporins)

The Nucleus (2 of 2)
Because protein synthesis occurs in the cytoplasm and m R N A synthesis occurs in the nucleus, transcription and translation cannot be directly coupled
Figure 21.6 A schematic model of the nuclear pore complex.

Chromosomes (2 of 2)
Telomeres are D N A repeat sequences (red in the bottom figure) protect the ends of the D N A from degradation and ensure each chromosome is copied completely

Chromatin Is Wound Around Histone and Nonhistone Proteins (1 of 3)
The total length of DNA in a human cell is about 2 m, but it all fits in a nucleus 10 µm in diameter
The gel shows the digestion of chromatin at successively longer intervals

Chromatin Is Wound Around Histone and Nonhistone Proteins (2 of 3)

The portions of D N A that cannot be digested are called nucleosomes

Higher Order Chromatin Structure (1 of 2)
Chromatin during interphase can exist in two major forms:
Euchromatin – transcriptionally active
Heterochromatin – much thicker transcriptionally inactive
Although chromatin appears disorganized within the cell, chromatin capture experiments (shown at right) demonstrate a higher degree of order

21.4 Nucleotide Sequence Analysis of Genomes

Host-Induced Restriction and Modification
The discovery and use of restriction enzymes to specifically cleave D N A is crucial to genome mapping and sequencing
Figure 21.13 Host-induced restriction and modification.

Restriction Digest of Bacteriophage λ D N A (1 of 2)
Restriction enzymes belong to three major systems – types I, II, and III
Each system is made up of a D N A methylase and an endonuclease that catalyzes a double stranded D N A break
Restriction enzymes are named according to the species and the strain. For example, EcoRI is the type I restriction enzyme from E. coli strain R

Restriction Digest of Bacteriophage λ DNA (2 of 2)
Restriction fragments such as those shown on the gel (top right) can be arranged in order to yield physical maps of D N A molecules (bottom right)
Figure 21.14 Fragmentation of bacteriophage λ D N A with restriction endonucleases EcoRI or BamHI.

Sequencing a Genome (1 of 3)
Once a restriction map of a genome is determined, the genome can be fragmented using restriction endonucleases
These fragments can then be cloned into vectors forming a library for sequence analysis
Each clone in the library possesses a sequence, and these sequences can be arranged using information from the restriction map

Sequencing a Genome (2 of 3)
Cloning a fragment of D N A  into plasmid vector and introducing the recombinant molecule into bacteria. In a gene library, hundreds or thousands  of D N A fragments of a defined organism are cloned into a plasmid vector
Figure 4A.2 Cloning a fragment of D N A into a plasmid vector and introducing the recombinant molecule into bacteria.

Sequencing a Genome (3 of 3)
“Shotgun sequencing” uses a process to generate random fragments; regions of overlapping sequences are used to arrange the sequences in the correct order
The Human Genome Project directed by Francis Collins and Craig Venter, utilized new processes to sequence the three billion bp in the human genome; this project begun in 1990 and yielded a “final” sequence in 2003

Mapping Large Genomes with Fluorescent In Situ Hybridization (FISH) (1 of 2)
The location of a gene on a chromosome can be identified by tagging an amplified D N A sequence with a fluorescent dye
This D N A probe is denatured and annealed in the presence of chromosome
The location of the dye is visualized using microscopy and identifies the regions of D N A complementary to the probe

Mapping Large Genomes with Fluorescent In Situ Hybridization (FISH) (2 of 2)
Four distinct probes representing different genes, visualized using FISH
Figure 21.17 Mapping genes by fluorescent in situ hybridization.

Locating Disease Genes – Mapping Polymorphisms (1 of 3)
MboI recognizes the restriction site G A T C
Individual A has two homologous chromosomes with identical MboI sites in this region
Figure 21.18 Analysis of a restriction fragment length polymorphism.

Locating Disease Genes – Mapping Polymorphisms (2 of 3)
Individual B has a restriction fragment length polymorphism (R F L P) in this region (G T T C is not cut by MboI)
Figure 21.18 Analysis of a restriction fragment length polymorphism.

Locating Disease Genes – Mapping Polymorphisms (3 of 3)
Southern blotting will identify a single 250 bp band for A, but two distinct bands for B (250 bp and 650 bp)
Figure 21.18 Analysis of a restriction fragment length polymorphism.

Southern Transfer and Hybridization
Figure 21.19 The principle of Southern transfer and hybridization.
Southern blotting allows detection of minute amounts of specific D N A sequences in a pool of unspecific D N A

D N A Fingerprinting (1 of 4)
Southern blotting has many applications including D N A fingerprinting in forensic analysis, determining paternity, analyzing ancient D N A, and checking food adulteration
Figure 21.21 Results of a    D N A forensic analysis in a rape investigation, involving repeat polymorphisms.
Subject 2 was at the crime scene!

D N A Fingerprinting (2 of 4)
This technique uses restriction fragment length polymorphisms (R F L P s) – no two individuals (except identical twins) have the same pattern of R F L P s
Figure 21.21 Results of a DN A forensic analysis in a rape investigation, involving repeat polymorphisms.
Subject 2 was at the crime scene!

D N A Fingerprinting (3 of 4)
Southern blotting analysis of a portion of an individual’s genome generates a D N A fingerprint that establishes the identity of the individual
Figure 21.21 Results of a DN A forensic analysis in a rape investigation, involving repeat polymorphisms.
Subject 2 was at the crime scene!

D N A Fingerprinting (4 of 4)
P C R can be used to amplify even very small amounts of D N A for this analysis
Most R F L P s are not single base changes, but changes in short tandem repeats
Figure 21.21 Results of a DNA forensic analysis in a rape investigation, involving repeat polymorphisms.
Subject 2 was at the crime scene!

D N A Sequence Variations in the Human Genome
Figure 21.20 D N A sequence variation in the human genome.

Size of the Human Genome (1 of 2)
The human genome comprises about three billion base pairs (bp)
20,000 — 25,000 genes (far fewer than the 100,000 predicted)
Results from the E N C O D E project suggest most of the noncoding regions in human D N A may play important functions
Any two humans differ in their D N A at 1 bp per 1000 bp

Size of the Human Genome (2 of 2)
The Human Genome Project costed $400 M
By 2013 the cost of sequencing an individual’s genome was $1000–$4000
Companies such as 23andMe analyze D N A from cheek swabs for disease markers such as cystic fibrosis
21.5 Tools of Biochemistry

Polymerase Chain Reaction (P C R) (1 of 2)
The invention of P C R by Kary Mullis in 1983 revolutionized molecular biology
P C R is able to exponentially amplify very small amounts of D N A in vitro
P C R requires a thermostable D N A polymerase (such as Taq polymerase), a pair oligonucleotide primers that flank the region to be amplified, d N T P s (deoxynucleotides), and a     D N A template

Polymerase Chain Reaction (P C R) (2 of 2)
Some applications of P C R are:
forensics – amplification of D N A from biological samples such as blood, semen, or hair
amplification of specific D N A sequences for ligation into an expression vector
quantitative P C R (q P C R) can be used to assess the abundance of target D N A in a sample
reverse transcription- P C R (R T-P C R) is used to monitor gene expression by analyzing m R N A levels in a sample

Three Cycles of P C R
Figure 21A.1 Three cycles of the polymerase chain reaction.

Chapter 21 Summary (1 of 2)
Bacterial, viral, and eukaryotic genomes vary in size and complexity
viral genomes, either R N A or D N A, encode as few as three proteins, or in some cases 175 or more
bacterial genomes usually consist of one circular double-stranded D N A molecule encoding between 1500 and 2000 genes
the human genome encodes about 21,000 genes; the genome is packaged in (linear) chromosomes; each chromosome is present in the nucleus in two copies (most eukaryotes are diploid) except for sex chromosomes (X and Y in males)

Chapter 21 Summary (2 of 2)
Chromosomal D N A is compacted by folding about histone octamers to form nucleosome core particles
these particles plus linker D N A make up chromatin
Sequence determination of complex genomes (such as those of humans) depend crucially on the development of new technologies, including the use of type II restriction endonucleases, fluorescent in situ hybridization (FISH), Southern transfer and hybridization, polymerase chain reaction (P C R), and cloning vectors

[Speaker Notes] Chapter 21, Opener, Packaging eukaryotic DNA within the nucleus. In order to fit within the nucleus, DNA is wrapped about histone core particles, as described in this chapter, forming a fiber some 11 nm in diameter. This fiber is further condensed by winding into a greatly thickened and shortened structure, which undergoes additional winding and compaction until it can fit within the nucleus.

22.1 Early Insights into D N A Replication

Information Transfer in the Cell
D N A  replication – D N A template-directed duplication of the genome prior to cell division
Transcription – D N A  template-directed biosynthesis of R N A
Reverse transcription – R N A  template-directed biosynthesis of D N A
Translation – m R N A template-directed biosynthesis of proteins

To Replicate D N A, the Strand Separation Occurs Simultaneously with D N A Synthesis (1 of 2)
D N A  strands are separated at regions called forks
Replication begins at one or more fixed sites called replication origins

To Replicate D N A, the Strand Separation Occurs Simultaneously with D N A Synthesis (2 of 2)
After replication each daughter cell receives one parental strand and one newly synthesized strand (semiconservative replication)

Bidirectional Replication in Bacteria
In bacteria, replication initiates from a fixed origin and proceeds bidirectionally
At the right, an autoradiogram of D  N  A showing two “Y” structures (red circles) – either or both could be a replication fork
Figure 22.1 Autoradiogram of a replicating E. coli chromosome after two generations of growth in
thymidine.
Figure 22.2 Demonstration of bidirectional replication by autoradiography.

Eukaryotic Replication of a Linear Chromosome (1 of 2)
Eukaryotic replication is bidirectional and proceeds from several fixed origins (indicated by the letter o at the top of the figure)
Figure 22.3 Bidirectional replication from several fixed origins (o) on a linear eukaryotic chromosome.

Eukaryotic Replication of a Linear Chromosome (2 of 2)
Forks advance until they meet another fork traveling in the opposite direction
Origins are programmed to initiate replication at fixed times in S phase
Figure 22.3 replication from several Bidirectional fixed origins (o) on a linear eukaryotic chromosome

Figure 22.28 Model for chromatin replication.
22.5 Initiation of D N A Replication

Initiation of Replication in Escherichia coli  (1 of 2)
Initiation of replication occurs
at the origin sequence,
which contains four 9 bp repeats
Figure 22.29 A model for the initiation of E. coli D N  A
replication at

Initiation of Replication in Escherichia coli  (2 of 2)
The three 13 bp repeats above the origin are A T rich and facilitate D N A bending
Figure 22.29 A model for the initiation of E. coli DNA
replication at

Preparing a Fission Yeast Replication Origin for Initiation
Replication in eukaryotes requires that replication origins become active at different times
Figure 22.30 Preparing a fission yeast replication origin for initiation.
22.6 Replication of Linear Genomes

The Problem of Completing the
-end in
Copying a Linear D N A Molecule
Excision of the R N A primer would leave a gap that cannot be filled
because there is no
primer
terminus to extend
Viruses have evolved three strategies to overcome this problem
Eukaryotes solve this problem via the use of telomeres
Figure 22.31 The problem of
completing the
end in
copying a linear D N A molecule.

Viruses Use Three Strategies to Complete the 5′-end (1 of 2)
Terminal redundancy and recombination (at right)
Covalently linking two strands at the end of the linear genome, so the final lagging strand can be replaced by D N A (as in a circular complex)
Figure 22.32 Strategies for replicating linear genomes.

Viruses Use Three Strategies to Complete the 5′-end (2 of 2)
Use of a d C M P-linked terminal protein (at right)
Figure 22.32 Strategies for replicating linear genomes.

Extension of Telomeric D N A by Telomerase  (1 of 2)
Telomeres are special tandemly repeated sequences located at the ends of each chromosome
Typically, one strand is G-rich and the other is C-rich
These repeated short segments of D N A prevent the loss of coding sequences after repeated rounds of replication

Extension of Telomeric D N A by Telomerase  (2 of 2)
Figure 22.33 Extension of telomeric D N A by human telomerase.
22.7 Fidelity of D N A Replication

D N A Proofreading Activity of D N A Polymerase (1 of 2)
D N A proofreading of D N A polymerase excises mismatched nucleotides to maintain fidelity in D N A replication
Accuracy is determined at the nucleotide insertion step and
the
exonucleolytic proofreading step
Mismatches increase
reducing the speed of
extension; this delay in extension allows unwinding into the exonuclease site

D N A Proofreading Activity of D N A Polymerase (2 of 2)
Figure 22.34 by a
-exonuclease site distant from the
polymerase site. Kinetic basis for preferential excision of mismatched nucleotides

D N A Polymerase Insertion Specificity (1 of 2)
Specificity during nucleotide insertion occurs at steps 2 and 3

D N A Polymerase Insertion Specificity (2 of 2)
Studies using d T T P analogs demonstrated that specificity during incorporation is due to both hydrogen-bonding and shape recognition

Huntington’s Disease Arises from D N A Polymerase Slippage Errors (1 of 2)
Huntington’s disease is an autosomal dominant neurological disease
The brain protein huntingtin contains a stretch of glutamine
residues, encoded by
-C A G-
Normal individuals have between 6 and 31 of these C A G repeats
Individuals with the disease have as many as 80

Huntington’s Disease Arises from D N A Polymerase Slippage Errors (2 of 2)
The number of repeats is thought to increase, if the D N A product slips out forming a loop during replication

D N T P Pool Imbalance Can Lead to Replication Errors
Figure 22.35 How d N T P pool imbalance can lead to replication errors
22.8 R N A Viruses: The Replication of RNA Genomes

R N A-Dependent R N A Replicases
Most R  N  A viruses have a single molecule of single-stranded
R  N  A that is the sense or
strand
Replicase uses the
strand to produce a
strand
which serves as a template for the synthesis of more
strands that are packaged into progeny virions
Rapid mutation rates are seen in R N A viruses as they do not possess proofreading mechanisms

Retroviruses Use Reverse Transcriptase to Produce D N A from an R N A Template
Figure 22.36 Simplified view of the retrovirus life cycle

Chapter 22 Summary (1 of 2)
D N A replication initiates from fixed replication origins and proceeds semiconservatively and bidirectionally
During D N A replication, D N A polymerases catalyze the template-directed incorporation of deoxyribo-nucleotides
moving in a
-to-
direction
Other proteins involved in various steps of D N A replication are D N A ligase, single-strand D N A-binding protein, primase, sliding clamp, clamp loaders, helicase, and topoisomerases

Chapter 22 Summary (2 of 2)
Initiation of D N A replication begins with duplex D N A unwinding to expose single-stranded templates
Special mechanisms such as telomerase activity are required to complete the replication process of both D N A strands
Several processes contribute to the high accuracy of D N A
replication including
-exonucleolytic proofreading,
polymerase insertion specificity, and maintenance of balanced D N A precursor pools

[Speaker Notes] Chapter 22, Opener, DNA replication is catalyzed by a dynamic multiprotein machine called the replisome. DNA polymerase, which catalyzes phosphodiester bond formation, is but one of the many proteins that must act in concert to faithfully copy each of the two DNA strands in a single replication fork.

23.1 DNA Repair

D N A Damage and Strategies for Repair (1 of 2)
D N A damage may be caused by:
radiation
environmental chemicals
endogenous reactive oxygen species
To function as an information storage molecule, there must be means to repair damaged D N A

D N A Damage and Strategies for Repair (2 of 2)
D N A repair encompasses many processes including
photoreactivation
removal of alkylguanines
nucleotide excision repair
base excision repair
mismatch repair
double-strand break repair
daughter strand gap repair
Some D N A modifications are also responsible for generating diversity (gene rearrangements)

Major Processes in Information Remodeling
Figure 23.1 A summary of the major processes in information remodeling.

Types of D N A Damage and Repair Processes
Figure 23.2 Types of D N A damage and repair processes.
One of the principal causes of cancer is genomic instability resulting from defective repair of D N A damage

Endogenous D N A-Damaging Reactions
Some of the most prominent DNA-damaging reactions are shown with their approximate frequency of occurrence, in number of damaging reactions per mammalian cell per day
Figure 23.3 Endogenous D N A -damaging reactions.

Structures of Thymine Dimer Photoproducts (1 of 2)
The cyclobutane dimer draws the thymine residues together, distorting the D N A and preventing replication; it is the most lethal photoproduct in phage or bacteria
Figure 23.4 Structures of cyclobutane and pyrimidine dimer photoproducts.

Structures of Thymine Dimer Photoproducts (2 of 2)
The 6–4 linked photoproduct induces inaccurate replication, and is the most mutagenic dimer
Figure 23.4 Structures of cyclobutane and pyrimidine dimer photoproducts.

Direct D N A Damage Repair by Photolyases
Photolyases use light energy to break the bonds linking pyrimidine rings
Mammals and frogs do not possess this repair mechanism
Figure 23.5 The thymine dimer photolyase.

Direct Repair of D N A Damage by Alkyltransferases (1 of 2)
Methylating agents, such as methylnitrosourea or ethyl-methanesulfonate (at right), yield modified bases
that
could induce mutagenesis or death if not repaired

Direct Repair of D N A Damage by Alkyltransferases (2 of 2)
Repair of mispairing caused by alkylation is performed by alkyltransferases, which transfer alkyl groups to active site cytsteines (these enzymes function only once)
Figure 23.6 Genetic consequences of D N A–guanine methylation.

Nucleotide Excision Repair (N E R) (1 of 2)
This enzyme system can repair thymine dimers created by bulky D N A adducts (below) or by UV-radiation
Figure 23.8 Metabolic activation of a carcinogenic polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon, followed by reaction of the activated dihydrodiol epoxide with a D N A d G M P residue.

Nucleotide Excision Repair (N E R) (2 of 2)
NER involves bending the D N A and endonuclease cleavage on either side of the dimer on the single D N A strand
Helicase, polymerase, and ligase complete the repair process
Figure 23.7 Excision repair of thymine dimers by the U v r A B C excinuclease of E. coli.

Base Excision Repair (B E R) Systems
The removal of uracil from D N A is one of the best-understood base excision repair (B E R) systems
Figure 23.9 Action of the D N A uracil repair system.
Uracil can arise from incorrect incorporation or deamination of cytosine;  uracil- D N A N-glycosylase (U N G) removes d U M P residues in D N A

Structure of the Uracil-Binding Pocket of Human U N G (1 of 2)
U N G acts by flipping the uracil base out of the D N A duplex and into a pocket where cleavage occurs
Figure 23.10 The uracil-binding pocket of human U N G.

Structure of the Uracil-Binding Pocket of Human U N G (2 of 2)
The pocket is small enough to exclude purine bases and also prevents thymine from binding through steric interactions between the C5 methyl group of thymine and Tyr-147
Figure 23.10 The uracil-binding pocket of human U N G.

Base Excision Repair of Oxidative Damage
8-oxoguanine is an abundant product under cellular oxidative stress conditions
A C-o x o G base pair becomes A-o x o G in a second round of replication (at right), and A-T after a third round
Both bacteria and humans have a B E R enzyme system to recognize and repair o x o G bases
In humans, the O G G 1 D N A glycosylase recognizes and cleaves the o x o G base

Structure of Human O G G 1 in Complex with Guanine (left) and 8-Oxoguanine (right)
Figure 23.11 Structure of human O G G 1 with guanine (top) and 8-oxoguanine (bottom) bound near the catalytic pocket.
8-oxoguanine is able to enter the binding pocket, while guanine is excluded

Methyl-Directed Mismatch Repair in Escherichia coli (1 of 3)
In E. coli, the mismatch repair enzymes identify the newly replicated strand by its lack of methylation at –G A T C– sites by M u t S
Figure 23.12 Methyl-directed mismatch repair in E. coli.

Methyl-Directed Mismatch Repair in Escherichia coli (2 of 3)
The M u t H L S complex scans the D N A for the nearest 5′- G A T C sequence, cleaving to the 5′ of G in the unmethylated strand and excising the D N A back past the mismatch
Figure 23.12 Methyl-directed mismatch repair in E. coli.

Methyl-Directed Mismatch Repair in Escherichia coli (3 of 3)
D N A polymerase III and ligase complete the repair
Figure 23.12 Methyl-directed mismatch repair in E. coli.

Double Strand Break (D S B) Repair Through Homologous Recombination (H R)
Figure 23.13 A pathway for homologous recombination (HR) to repair D N A double-strand breaks. RNAPII is R N A polymerase II. For description see text.
Genetic defects involving DSB repair in the BRCA1 and BRCA2 genes are risk factors for breast or ovarian cancer

Daughter-Strand Gap Repair (1 of 2)
depends on the multi-functional protein, RecA
In daughter-strand gap repair, the damaged site (a thymine dimer) is not repaired, but the replicated portion of an incompletely replicated chromosome is used as a repair template
Figure 23.14 Daughter-strand gap repair.

Daughter-Strand Gap Repair (2 of 2)
This process allows replication to continue and an excision system comes in later to repair the damage
Figure 23.14 Daughter-strand gap repair.
23.2 Recombination

Site-Specific Recombination
This type of recombination establishes lysogeny in bacteriophage λ. The enzyme integrase inserts the circular viral genome into attB site.
Figure 23.15 Site-specific recombination establishes lysogeny in bacteriophage λ.

Holliday Model for Homologous Recombination (1 of 2)
Figure 23.16 The Holliday model for homologous recombination.
First, D N A is nicked at the same site on two paired chromosomes
Next partial unwinding of the D N A allows strand invasion
Enzymatic ligation generates a crossed-strand intermediate (Holliday junction)
The cross-strand structure can move along the duplex

Holliday Model for Homologous Recombination (2 of 2)
The Holliday junction isomerizes (6–8 or 9–11); the strand breakage generates a recombinant or nonrecombinant heteroduplex
Figure 23.16 The Holliday model for homologous recombination.

Holliday Junction (H J) Visualized by Electron Microscopy
Figure 23.17 Electron microscopic visualization of a olliday junction.

RecA-Mediated Strand Exchange
RecA uses A T P to promote the pairing of homologous D N A sequences

RecA Recombination Sites Are Recognized by the RecBCD Complex (1 of 2)
In E. coli, recombination is favored at or near a specific DNA sequence 5′-GCTGGTCC, called Chi (χ)
Figure 23.20 A model of the E. coli RecBCD protein in complex with D N A, based on the crystal structure.

RecA Recombination Sites Are Recognized by the RecBCD Complex (2 of 2)
The RecBCD complex recognizes the χ sequence at the 3′ end of the unwound ssD N A
Figure 23.20 A model of the E. coli RecBCD protein in complex with D N A, based on the crystal structure.

Branch Migration after Formation of a Holliday Junction
The branch migration step is catalyzed by the ruvA, ruvB, and ruvC gene products. RuvA protein recognizes the Holliday junction. RuvB is an ATP-driven motor protein, rotating the two arms in opposite directions, drawing the upper and lower arms in and pushing the left and right arms out. RuvC nicks the two strands
Figure 23.21 A model for the RuvA-RuvB-Holliday junction structure.
23.3 Gene Rearrangements

Generation of Antibody Diversity Through Gene Rearrangements (1 of 3)
In the differentiation of one antibody-forming clone of cells, one of the 300 V sequences and one of the four J sequences are combined as the sequence in between is spliced out
Figure 23.22 Gene rearrangements in antibody gene maturation.

Generation of Antibody Diversity Through Gene Rearrangements (2 of 3)
The removed sequence disappears from all progeny from that cell line
Figure 23.22 Gene rearrangements in antibody gene maturation.

Generation of Antibody Diversity Through Gene Rearrangements (3 of 3)
The upstream V and downstream J sequences are retained, but are not part of the m R N A transcript and therefore are not involved in antibody synthesis
Figure 23.22 Gene rearrangements in antibody gene maturation.

V-J Joining to Achieve Additional Diversity (1 of 2)
The proteins R A G 1 and R A G 2 catalyze double strand breaks, initiating recombination of V and J sequences
Figure 23.24 Site-specific recombination event catalyzed by R A G 1 and R A G 2.

V-J Joining to Achieve Additional Diversity (2 of 2)
Cutting and splicing may occur anywhere within the terminal trinucleotide, and can generate four different recombinant forms
Figure 23.23 Generation of diversity by variability in V-J joining.

Transposable Genetic Elements and Genome Rearrangements (1 of 3)
Genome rearrangements can be promoted by H R between two copies of the same transposable element
Figure 23.25 Genome rearrangements that can be promoted by homologous recombination between two copies of the same transposable element.

Transposable Genetic Elements and Genome Rearrangements (2 of 3)
A transposon is a bacterial transposable element flanked by short repeated sequences
Figure 23.25 Genome rearrangements that can be promoted by homologous recombination between two copies of the same transposable element.

Transposable Genetic Elements and Genome Rearrangements (3 of 3)
Recombination can yield insertions or deletions depending on the orientation of the repeats
Transposable elements occur in bacteria and many eukaryotes
Figure 23.25 Genome rearrangements that can be promoted by homologous recombination between two copies of the same transposable element.

Transposase Function
Figure 23.26 Action of transposase to generate direct repeats during the insertion of a transposon or an insertion sequence.
In a simple transposon, the D N A between the flanking sequence encodes only for the transposase; a complex transposon encodes for the transposase and other proteins such as antibiotic resistance

Retroviruses Use Long Terminal Repeats (L T R s) for Integration Into the Host Chromosome
Figure 23.27 Structure of retroviral genomes in the integrated state.

Two Modes of Gene Amplification Can Lead to Drug Resistance (1 of 3)
Cells can become resistant to the drug methotrexate, an inhibitor of the enzyme dihydrofolate reducatase (D H F R)
Figure 23.28 Two modes of gene amplification leading to drug resistance.

Two Modes of Gene Amplification Can Lead to Drug Resistance (2 of 3)
Tandem duplication of the DHFR gene generates a chromosome with multiple copies of the gene in a homogeneously staining region (H S R)
Figure 23.28 Two modes of gene amplification leading to drug resistance.

Two Modes of Gene Amplification Can Lead to Drug Resistance (3 of 3)
A D N A segment may be excised to form minichromosomes called double-minute chromosomes
In both cases, resistance is conferred by overproduction of the D H F R enzyme
Figure 23.28 Two modes of gene amplification leading to drug resistance.

Electron Micrograph Showing Single Copy Regions and H S R s of the D H F R Gene
Chromosomes are stained orange, the D H F R gene is stained yellow. Single copies are indicated by arrows
Figure 23.29 Structural changes to the chromosome that accompany dihydrofolate reductase gene amplification.

Gene Amplification May Occur via Unequal Sister-Chromatid Exchange (Unequal Crossover)
Figure 23.30 Unequal crossing over, as a mechanism to explain early steps in gene amplification.
23.4 Tools of Biochemistry

Targeted Insertion of Genes into a Genome
Figure 23A.1 Targeted insertion of the
gene into the
chromosomal hprt gene.
Here, the
gene replaced (and “knocked-out”) the hprt
gene

The C R I S P R-Cas9 System
The C R I S P R technology is based on Cas9 activity, catalyzing a site-specific double-stranded D N A cleavage. This technology can be used for gene knockout, for site-directed mutagenesis, or for insertion of new D N A into a chromosome
Figure 23A.2 C R I S P R -Cas9 system from Streptococcus thermophilus.

Chapter 23 Summary (1 of 3)
Repair of damaged cellular D N A can be accomplished in various ways, including
photoreactivation, in which light energy is captured to reverse pyrimidine dimer formation
the removal of alkylguanine by alkyltransferases that only function once
nucleotide excision repair systems that recognize damaged nucleotides, cleave them out and replace them with nucleotide patches
base excision repair systems that recognize and cleave 8-oxoguanine (an abundant oxidative stress product) using D N A glycosylase activity

Chapter 23 Summary (2 of 3)
Mismatch repair systems correct occasional errors arising during replication
Double-strand D N A breaks are repaired either by homologous recombination or by nonhomologous end-joining
Genetic recombination between homologous D N A segments involves helicase-catalyzed duplex unwinding, site-specific D N A cutting, duplex-strand invasion by single-stranded 3′-hydroxyl termini, chain extension, branch migration, and resolution of Holliday structures

Chapter 23 Summary (3 of 3)
Gene arrangements can be performed by processes such as
joining reactions in differentiation of the immune system
gene transposition
retroviral genome integration
gene amplification by recombination mechanisms

24.1 D N A as the Template for R N A Synthesis

Information Transfer in the Cell
D N A replication – D N A template-directed duplication of the genome prior to cell division
Transcription – D N A template-directed biosynthesis of R N A
Reverse transcription – R N A template-directed biosynthesis of D N A
Translation – m R N A template-directed biosynthesis of proteins

Regulation of Transcription by D N A- Protein Interactions
Gene expression is controlled by proteins that recognize particular D N A sequences and bind at those sites. These D N A-binding protein can either activate or repress the expression of certain (downstream) genes
Figure 24.1 Regulation of transcription by D N A–protein interactions.

The Operon Model (Jacob and Monod, 1961)

Inhibitor Studies Reveal Three Distinct R N A Polymerases in Eukaryotes (2 of 2)
Eukaryotes have three R N A polymerases, identified from their different sensitivities to α-amanitin (a toxin from the Amanita mushroom)

Initiation of Transcription (1 of 2)
R N A polymerase first forms a closed-complex in which D N A is not unwound
Next, the stable, open complex forms where the −10 region is unwound
Figure 24.8 D N A bending in an open-promoter complex.

Initiation of Transcription (2 of 2)
Lastly, an
dependent isomerization further unwinds the D N A from −12 to +2
Figure 24.8 D N A bending in an open-promoter complex.

Elongation – The Transcription Bubble (1 of 2)
R N A chain growth begins by binding the template-specific r N T P, followed by binding of the second
Nucleophilic attack by the 3′-O H of the first nucleotide on the α-phosphate of the second nucleotide forms a phosphodiester bond in a manner analogous to D N A replication

Elongation – The Transcription Bubble (2 of 2)
As this process continues, the D N A is continuously unwound in a “transcription bubble”
σ-factor dissociates during incorporation of the first 10 nucleotides
Figure 24.9 The transcription bubble.

R N A Polymerase Advances Discontinuously, Often Pausing and Backtracking
The 3′-tail in the backtracked polymerase complex may be cleaved or move forward, repositioning the R N A 3′ nucleotide back into the active site
Pausing and backtracking control rate and fidelity
Figure 24.10 Backtracking in an elongation complex.

Termination of Transcription (1 of 3)
Termination of transcription in bacteria can occur in factor-independent and factor-dependent events
In factor-dependent termination, additional proteins (e.g., ρ protein and NusA) are involved in facilitating the release of the R N A transcript from R N A polymerase
Figure 24.11 A model for factor-independent termination of transcription.

Termination of Transcription (2 of 3)
In factor-independent termination, two symmetrical G C-rich segments form a stem-loop structure weakening the protein-D N A-R N A complex (G C rich segments slow transcription, allowing the stem-loop time to form)
Figure 24.11 A model for factor-independent termination of transcription.

Termination of Transcription (3 of 3)
The A-rich segment that follows further weakens these interactions, causing release of the transcript and termination of transcription
Figure 24.11 A model for factor-independent termination of transcription.

R N A polymerase III (pol III) – transcription of small R N A genes

Termination of Transcription in Eukaryotes (1 of 2)
R N A polymerase II usually transcribes well past the end of the gene, passing through one or more T T A T T T signals
The pre-m R N A carrying the A A U A A A signal is cleaved 11 to 30 residues downstream of these sites

After Capping, Pre-m R N A Undergoes Splicing (1 of 3)
In the splicing process small nuclear ribonucleoproteins (s n R N P s) such as U1 and U2 aid in pre-m R N A loop formation
Figure 24.24 A schematic view of the mechanism of m R N A splicing.

After Capping, Pre-m R N A Undergoes Splicing (2 of 3)
The 2′-O H of the branchpoint (in this case A) performs a nucleophilic attack on the phosphate of a base at the 5′ splice site (here, G) forming a lariat and releasing the 5′ exon
Figure 24.24 A schematic view of the mechanism of m R N A splicing.

After Capping, Pre-m R N A Undergoes Splicing (3 of 3)
The 3′-O H of the 5′ exon then attacks the phosphate of the first nucleotide of the 3′ exon forming the spliced m R N A
The s n R N P-pre–m R N A complex is called the spliceosome

Chapter 24 Summary (1 of 3)
All classes of R N A are synthesized by the template-dependent copying of one D N A strand within a gene, catalyzed by R N A polymerase(s)
R N A polymerase(s) transcribe in 5′ → 3′ direction, using 5′-ribonucleotide triphosphates as substrates
Bacteria synthesize all classes of R N A with one polymerase, whereas eukaryotic cells have three different polymerases (I, II, and III) to produce ribosomal (r R N A), messenger (m R N A), and transfer (t R N A) R N A species, respectively

Chapter 24 Summary (2 of 3)
D N A strand selection and duplex unwinding and rewinding are carried by R N A polymerase(s) in conjunction with other proteins
R N A polymerases bind at promoter sites by formation of specific D N A-protein contacts to initiate and then elongate R N A synthesis
The process of R N A elongation is terminated by specific D N A sequences, sometimes facilitated by other proteins

Chapter 24 Summary (3 of 3)
Posttranscriptional processing includes trimming t R N A and r R N A precursors as well as (in eukaryotes) cutting and splicing m R N A precursors
While bacterial m R N A undergoes little processing, eukaryotic m R N A is extensively modified, with polyadenylylation at the 3′-end, capping at the 5′-end, and splicing throughout the gene

25.1 An Overview of Translation

Information Transfer in the Cell
D N A replication – D N A template-directed duplication of the genome prior to cell division
Transcription – D N A template-directed biosynthesis of RNA
Reverse transcription – R N A template-directed biosynthesis of D N A
Translation – m R N A template-directed biosynthesis of proteins

Translation – from m R N A to Protein
In the process of translation, the ribosome uses an mRNA template and aminoacyl-tRNAs to synthesize a polypeptide
Messenger R N A is read 5′ → 3′ and the polypeptide is synthesized from the N- to the C-terminus
Figure 25.1 Translation of an RNA message into a protein.

Activation of Amino Acids for Incorporation into Proteins by Attachment to a t R N A
Figure 25.2 Activation of amino acids for incorporation into proteins.
The anticodon loop contains a trinucleotide sequence called the anticodon that is complementary to the appropriate trinucleotide codon in the m R N A

In the first step in initiation in bacteria, initiation factors IF1 and IF3 facilitate the dissociation of the 70S ribosome
Figure 25.20 Initiation of protein biosynthesis in bacteria.

Stage 1 – Initiation (2 of 3)
m R N A and the initiator t R N A bind the 30S subunit with the help of initiation factor I F2
Figure 25.20 Initiation of protein biosynthesis in bacteria

Stage 1 – Initiation (3 of 3)
The 50S subunit binds to the 30S initiation complex, and the initiator t R N A is aligned in the P site
Figure 25.20 Initiation of protein biosynthesis in bacteria.

Stage 2 – Protein Chain Elongation   (1 of 2)
At the beginning of each cycle, the peptide chains are attached to the P site, and the E and A sites are empty
The recognized aminoacyl-t R N A then binds in the A site with the help of the E F-T u elongation factor
Figure 25.21 Chain elongation in bacterial translation.

Stage 2 – Protein Chain Elongation (2 of 2)
The peptide bond is formed by the peptidyl-transferase region
The ribosome translocates to move the P site t R N A to the E site, and the newly added peptidyl-t R N A from the A site to the P site
Figure 25.21 Chain elongation in bacterial translation

Peptidyltransferase Mechanism Involving A2451 (Escherichia coli Numbering) as a General Base
Figure 25.22 A mechanism for peptidyltransferase involving A2451 (E. coli) as a general base.

Schematic View of the “Ratcheting” of the Ribosome along m R N A
Figure 25.24 A schematic view of ribosome subunit rotational motions, based on crystal structures of ribosomes in intermediate states.

Stage 3 – Termination (1 of 3)
When the ribosome encounters a stop codon, there is no t R N A com-plementary to the codon
Instead, a release factor (RF1 recognizes the UA A stop codon, and RF2 recognizes U A G and U G A stop codons) binds the A site
Figure 25.26 Termination of translation in bacteria.

Stage 3 – Termination (2 of 3)
The peptidyl-t R N A is attacked by a water molecule, releasing the peptide
Release factor R F3 stimulates the release process through G T P binding and hydrolysis
Figure 25.26 Termination of translation in bacteria.

Stage 3 – Termination (3 of 3)
The ribosome then dissociates from the mR N A; most likely the 50S subunit leaves first, stimulated by IF1 and IF3
Figure 25.26 Termination of translation in bacteria.

Structure of the 70S Ribosome in Complex with Release Factor RF1
Figure 25.25 Interaction of RF1 with a stop codon in the A site.
25.5 Inhibition of Translation by Antibiotics

A Variety of Antibiotics Function by Inhibiting Translation in Prokaryotes
Figure 25.27 Some antibiotics that act by interfering with protein biosynthesis.
25.6 Translation in Eukaryotes

Structure of the Yeast 80S Ribosome (1 of 2)
Eukaryotic translation is more complex than in bacteria, requiring more protein factors
Figure 25.28 The yeast 80S ribosome.

Structure of the Yeast 80S Ribosome (2 of 2)
The most significant differences are in initiation: 5′-end of m R N A is sensed by the 7-methylguano-sine cap (not the S D sequence) and the initiator A U G encodes Met, (not formyl-Met)
Figure 25.28 The yeast 80S ribosome.

Cycloheximide and Diphtheria Toxin Inhibit Eukaryotic Translation
Diphtheria toxin catalyzes the addition of an A  D  P-ribose group to a specifically modified histidine in the translocation factor E F 2, irreversibly blocking translation
Figure 25.29 A D P-ribosylated diphthamide derivative of histidine in e E F2.
25.7 Rate of Translation; Polyribosomes

Translation Rate and Coupling (1 of 2)
Translation in E. coli is rapid (synthesizing a 300-residue protein in approximately 20 sec)
Figure 25.30 Coupling of transcription to translation in E. coli via the interaction between N u s E and N u s G.

Translation Rate and Coupling (2 of 2)
This rate matches that of transcription; unsurprising since in bacteria transcription and translation are coupled (impossible in eukaryotes because these processes take place in different cellular compartments)
Figure 25.30 Coupling of transcription to translation in E. coli via the interaction between N u s E and N u s G.

Polyribosomes (1 of 3)
As soon as one ribosome clears the 5′ region of m R N A, another ribosome can attach
Figure 25.31 Polyribosomes.

Polyribosomes (2 of 3)
Multiple ribosomes attached to a single m R N A is called a polyribosome
Figure 25.3 Polyribosomes.

Polyribosomes (3 of 3)
As many as 50 ribosomes may be attached to one m R N A
Figure 25.31 Polyribosomes.
25.8 The Final Stages in Protein Synthesis: Folding and Covalent Modification

Model of Bacterial Protein Secretion (1 of 3)
Bacterial proteins destined for secretion for insertion into the membrane or secretion possess N-terminal signal sequences (leader sequences)
Figure 25.32 A model for protein secretion by bacteria.

Model of Bacterial Protein Secretion (2 of 3)
Chaperones, such as SecB, prevent the protein from folding prematurely
SecA drives the protein through the SecY E G translocon
Figure 25.32 A model for protein secretion by bacteria.

Model of Bacterial Protein Secretion (3 of 3)
The protein folds after passing through the translocon
A periplasmic peptidase cleaves off the leader sequence, completing the process
Figure 25.32 A model for protein secretion by bacteria.
25.9 Protein Targeting in Eukaryotes

In Eukaryotes, Proteins Synthesized in the Cytoplasm Are Targeted to Different Organelles
Figure 25.33 Transport of newly synthesized mitochondrial proteins into the matrix and other mitochondrial sites.

Membrane-Bound Proteins Are Synthesized on the Rough Endoplasmic Reticulum (R E R)
Figure 25.34 The sequence of events in the synthesis of proteins on the rough endoplasmic reticulum (R E R).

Protein-Carrying Vesicles Bud Off the R E R and Move to the Golgi Complex for Final Sorting to the Correct Destination
Figure 25.35 Transfer from the rough endoplasmic reticulum (R E R) to the Golgi complex.

Vesicles Are Marked by Specific Membrane Proteins Called S N A R E S Which Interact and Cause Membrane Fusion (1 of 2)
Figure 25.36 A schematic view of S N A R E–pin fusion.

Vesicles Are Marked by Specific Membrane Proteins Called S N A R E S Which Interact and Cause Membrane Fusion (2 of 2)
Figure 25.36 A schematic view of S N A R E–pin fusion.

Chapter 25 Summary (1 of 2)
Translation occurs via the movement of a ribosome in a 5′-to-3′ direction along messenger R N A, with amino acids selected by pairing of t R N A anticodons with m R N A codons
The link between m R N A and protein sequences is dictated by the genetic code, which is highly conserved among organisms
The code is redundant, which means, multiple codons are used for most amino acids
The code also includes start and stop signals

Chapter 25 Summary (2 of 2)
The anticodon for each amino acid is a trinucleotide sequence, located on each t R N A molecule
The ribosome moves along m R N A with sequential pairing between codon and anticodon
An R N A component of the large ribosomal subunit provides the catalytic site for the formation of peptide bonds
Simultaneously with translation, nascent polypeptide chains undergo folding, posttranslational modification, and transport through membranes en route to their final intracellular or extracellular destinations

26.1 Regulation of Transcription in Bacteria

The lac Operon Is a Model System for Investigating Transcriptional Regulation (1 of 3)
Figure 26.1 A map of the lactose operon
Lactose utilization in E. coli is controlled by three contiguous
genes – lacZ (b-galactosidase), lacY (a
-galactoside
permease), and lacA (thiogalacto-side transacetylase)

The lac Operon Is a Model System for Investigating Transcriptional Regulation (2 of 3)
Figure 26.1 A map of the lactose operon.
These three genes and the promoter region make up the lac operon
When transcribed, the operon yields a polycistronic
m R N A, one in which multiple coding sequences exist

The lac Operon Is a Model System for Investigating Transcriptional Regulation (3 of 3)
Distinct phenotypes may arise from mutations in these genes or regulatory regions
Constitutive – all three genes are synthesized at high levels, even in the absence of inducer
Noninducible – all three gene activities remain low, even after the addition of inducer
Mutations involving these phenotypes mapped to two locations – lacO (the operator) and lacI (a repressor protein)

Structures of lac Operon Inducers
All three compounds are inducers of the lac operon; allolactose is the true intracellular inducer
I P T G is a synthetic inducer, often used in the laboratory; it
is not cleaved by
-galactosidase so its concentration in
the cell remains high

Repression in the lac Operon
Figure 26.2 Configurations of the lactose operon.
The repressor protein synthesized by the lacI gene binds a specific sequence in the operator and blocks transcription by preventing R N A polymerase from binding

Derepression in the lac Operon
Figure 26.2 Configurations of the lactose operon.
The repressor has an inducer binding site. When repressor binds the inducer, the affinity of the repressor for the operator D N A is greatly reduced, derepressing transcription. This repressor-inducer system provides negative control of the lac operon

Repressor Binding Site in the Operator
The operator is a 35-bp region of D N A, 28 of which are symmetrical
The transcription start site is located within the 24-bp region protected by repressor

The 122-bp Regulatory Region of the lac Operon
Figure 26.3 The 122-base lac regulatory region.
mutations are constitutive

Structure of the lac Repressor in Complex with lac Operator (1 of 2)
This structure shows the dimeric repressor; the complete tetrameric protein would bind the +11 and −82 sites forming a DNA loop
Figure 26.4 Structure of the lac repressor.

Structure of the lac Repressor in Complex with lac Operator (2 of 2)
The transparent regions show how the D N A-binding regions move apart upon binding inducer, causing a significant reduction in operator affinitya
Figure 26.4 Structure of the lac repressor.

Transcriptional Activation of the lac Operon When Glucose Levels Are Low (1 of 2)
In E. coli, when glucose levels drop, c A M P levels rise (catabolite activation)
C A M P interacts with c A M P receptor protein (C R P), activating the lac operon
Figure 26.5 Positive and negative control in the lac operon.

Transcriptional Activation of the lac Operon When Glucose Levels Are Low (2 of 2)
The binding of c A M P to C R P causes a conformational change, increasing affinity for D N A
C R P helps recruit R N A polymerase, stimulating transcription
Figure 26.5 Positive and negative control in the lac operon

Structure of the C R P- c A M P- D N A Complex
Figure 26.6 A transcriptional regulatory protein.
C R P binds D N A though a helix-turn-helix motif (dark purple)

Ribbon Diagrams Showing the Conserved Helix-Turn-Helix (H T H) Motif in Repressors
Figure 26.8 D N A-binding faces of
repressor, and CRP, showing the helix–turn–helix motif.
Amino acid homologies are similar, but not identical in the H T
H region.
is the recognition helix (forms interactions
with specific D N A bases).
contributes to D N A binding
but not specificity (forms interactions with the phosphate backbone)

Conserved Residues in the D N A-Binding Helices of cI and Cro
Figure 26.9 Conserved residues in the D N A-binding helices of
cI repressor and Cro
In both proteins, the Ala in
contacts a residue in
which helps to position the helices

Specific Protein-D N A Contacts for the cI and Cro Repressors
Both proteins use a glutamine residue to interact with an A-T base pair through hydrogen bonding interactions
Figure 26.10 Specific amino acid–nucleotide contacts for cI and Cro repressors.

The S O S Regulon – D N A Damage Response (1 of 3)
The lytic stage is initiated by various types of D N A damage
Unlike the lac system, cI is not inactivated by a small molecule but cleaved by a protease
Figure 26.11 The S O S regulon.

The S O S Regulon – D N A Damage Response (2 of 3)
LexA and RecA are the major control elements of the SOS response
When bound to ssD N A, LexA stimulates proteo-lysis of the cI, lexA, and umuD gene products
Figure 26.11 The S O S regulon.

The S O S Regulon – D N A Damage Response (3 of 3)
In λ lysogen, cleavage of cI activates prophage excision and phage reproduction
Figure 26.11 The S O S regulon.

Transcriptional Regulation in the t r p Operon
The t r p operon is involved in the biosynthesis of tryptophan, a process that requires energy
Figure 26.12 The trp operon.
The t r p repressor inhibits tryptophan synthesis by binding of the repressor-ligand complex to the t r p operator, blocking transcription

R N A Base Sequence of the t r p Leader Region
Figure 26.13 R N A base sequence of the trp leader region.

Mechanism of Attenuation in the t r p Operon (1 of 3)
Attenuation is a second mechanism of regulation of the trp operon
Figure 26.14 Mechanism of attenuation in the trp operon.

Mechanism of Attenuation in the t r p Operon (2 of 3)
In attenuation, early termination of the t r p operon transcription is caused by 3–4-stem-loop terminator formation (c)
Figure 26.14 Mechanism of attenuation in the t r p operon.

Mechanism of Attenuation in the t r p Operon (3 of 3)
When tryptophan levels are low, formation of the 3–4-stem-loop is inhibited, allowing full transcription
Figure 26.14 Mechanism of attenuation in the t r p operon.
26.2 Transcriptional Regulation in Eukaryotes

Transcriptional Regulation in Eukaryotes Is More Complex Than in Prokaryotes
The eukaryotic genome contains a large number of transcription factors (about 3000 in humans)
Regulation by transcription factors is combinatorial
The complexity associated with multicellularity demands higher orders of regulation
Eukaryotes use chromatin, not naked D N A as the template
Additional complexity is brought about by small regulatory R N A molecules

Representations of Transcriptionally Repressed (Top) and Active (bottom) Chromatin (1 of 5)
Many eukaryotic transcriptional regulators function to clear the D N A of nucleosomes
Figure 26.15 Schematic representations of transcriptionally repressed (top) and active (bottom) chromatin.

Representations of Transcriptionally Repressed (Top) and Active (bottom) Chromatin (2 of 5)
In the repressed state, methylation of histone 3 at lysine 9 (H3K9) recruits the compaction protein heterochromatin protein 1 (H P1)
Figure 26.15 Schematic representations of transcriptionally repressed (top) and active (bottom) chromatin.

Representations of Transcriptionally Repressed (Top) and Active (bottom) Chromatin (3 of 5)
Polycomb repressive complex (P R C) recruits histone deacetylases (H D A C s), which remove acetyl groups
Figure 26.15 Schematic representations of transcriptionally repressed (top) and active (bottom) chromatin.

Representations of Transcriptionally Repressed (Top) and Active (bottom) Chromatin (4 of 5)
Removal of acetyl groups strengthen the ionic interactions between D N A and histones
Figure 26.15 Schematic representations of transcriptionally repressed (top) and active (bottom) chromatin.

Representations of Transcriptionally Repressed (Top) and Active (bottom) Chromatin (5 of 5)
H3K4 methylation is a transcriptional activating signal (euchromatin formation)
Figure 26.15 Schematic representations of transcriptionally repressed (top) and active (bottom) chromatin.

Mediator Links Upstream Regulatory Sequences with R N A Polymerase II and Transcription Factors at the Promoter Site (1 of 3)
In the repressed state (bottom), Srb8-11 binds Mediator, preventing interaction with RNA polymerase
Figure 26.17 Mediator as a bridge between gene-specific regulatory factors and the general transcription machinery at the pol

Mediator Links Upstream Regulatory Sequences with RNA Polymerase II and Transcription Factors at the Promoter Site (2 of 3)
In the active state (top) activator protein stimulates a conformational change in Mediator and the release of the Srb8-11 complex
Figure 26.17 Mediator as a bridge between gene-specific regulatory factors and the general transcription machinery at the pol

Mediator Links Upstream Regulatory Sequences with R N A Polymerase II and Transcription Factors at the Promoter Site (3 of 3)
The release of the Srb8-11 complex allows the interaction with RNA polymerase II and transcription factors at the promoter
Figure 26.17 Mediator as a bridge between gene-specific regulatory factors and the general transcription machinery at the pol

Model of Mediator in Complex with R N A Polymerase roman numeral two
Figure 26.18 The structure of yeast Mediator in complex with R N A

Chromatin Remodeling Complexes Couple A T P Hydrolysis to Changes in Chromatin Structure (1 of 2)
Figure 26.19 Four chromatin remodeler families and conserved domains of the A T Pase-containing subunit.
The ATPase domain is conserved in the structures of these four families

Chromatin Remodeling Complexes Couple A T P Hydrolysis to Changes in Chromatin Structure (2 of 2)
Figure 26.19 Four chromatin remodeler families and conserved domains of the A T Pase-containing subunit.
Bromodomains interact with acetylated lysine residues
Chromodomains interact with methylated histones (especially at H3K4)

Histone Modifications and Phosphorylation of the C-Terminal Domain (C T D) of the R N A Pol II (1 of 2)
Nucleosome distribution is shown in gray
Phosphorylation of the C T D changes from Ser-5 (blue line) to Ser-2 (red line) as termination of transcription nears; histone modifications are also altered as a consequence of C T D phosphorylation changes

Histone Modifications and Phosphorylation of the C-Terminal Domain (C T D) of the R N A Pol II (2 of 2)
The specific consequences of these modifications are active areas of research
The relationship between histone modification patterns and activation or repression of genes is called epigenetics (lit. above genetics)
Figure 26.20 Histone and pol II CTD modifications as a function of gene position.
26.3 D N A Methylation, Gene Silencing, and Epigenetics

D N A Methylation in Eukaryotes (1 of 5)
D N A methylation patterns are altered in cancer; analyses of these alterations should enhance our understanding of carcinogenesis
D N A methylation occurs at cytosine residues; these methylated Cs are upstream from Gs in a CpG dinucleotide
Figure 26.21 De novo and maintenance methylation of D N A and the effect of 5-azacytidine on D N A methylation.

D N A Methylation in Eukaryotes (2 of 5)
C p G regions are typically underrepresented in eukaryotes, but regions of expected representation occur and are called C p G islands
D N A methylation patterns are heritable
Figure 26.21 De novo and maintenance methylation of D N A and the effect of 5-azacytidine on D N A methylation.

D N A Methylation in Eukaryotes (3 of 5)
Mammalian cells possess three different D N A methyltransferases (D N M T s); Dnmt1 is responsible for maintenance of methylation patterns, Dnmt3 a and 3b are de novo methyltransferases
Figure 26.21 De novo and maintenance methylation of D N A and the effect of 5-azacytidine on D N A methylation.

D N A Methylation in Eukaryotes (4 of 5)
D N A methylation can lead to permanent gene inactivation
A natural role of D N A demethylation may be the activation of repressed genes
There is no one single mechanism accounting for gene repression as a function of methylation

D N A Methylation in Eukaryotes (5 of 5)
D N A CpG methylation is responsible for at least two known developmental gene inactivation processes – X chromosome inactivation and gene imprinting
In females, one of the two X-chromosomes must be permanently inactivated by D N A methylation
In imprinting, a single parental allele for a gene is expressed while the other imprinted gene is deactivated by D N A methylation
5-Hydroxymethylcytosine May Be an Intermediate in the Oxidative Pathway for D N A Demethylation
26.4 Regulation of Translation

Regulation of Translation in Bacteria (1 of 2)
Figure 26.22 Inactivation of ompF mRNA by pairing with antisense RNA from the micF gene.
Regulation occurs by at least three mechanisms: ribosome occlusion due to mRNA tertiary structure, translational repression by protein binding to mRNA, and regulatory R N A molecules base-pairing with mRNA

Regulation of Translation in Bacteria (2 of 2)
Figure 26.22 Inactivation of ompF mR N A by pairing with antisense R N A from the micF gene.
Shown here is the antisense R N A micF gene which base pairs to the ompF gene; this interaction prevents either R N A from being used as a template for translation

Translational Regulation by Phosphorylation of Eukaryotic Initiation Factors
Figure 26.23 Regulation of translation in erythropoietic cells by heme levels
26.5 R N A Interference

RNA Interference: m i R N A and s i R N A (1 of 3)
Figure 26.24 Biogenesis of m i R N A.
MicroRNAs (m i R N A s) are often involved in gene regulation
The primary transcript (pri-m i R N A) is converted to pre-m i R N A via cleavage by the Drosha enzyme complex

R N A Interference: miR N A and siR N A (2 of 3)
Figure 26.24 Biogenesis of m i R N A.
Pre-m i R N A is exported to the cytoplasm where Dicer, in conjunction with Argonaute, further process the pre-m i R N A to form mature m i R N A
The mature m i R N A and Argonaute are transferred to the R I S C complex

R N A Interference: miR N A and siR N A (3 of 3)
Figure 26.24 Biogenesis of miR N A
The R I S C complex uses complementarity to the miR N A to identify substrates for degradation
Small interfering R N As (siR N As) are formed primarily as a defense mechanism, targeted against viral R N A molecules (very effective in plants)
26.6 Riboswitches

Riboswitches Are mR N A Molecules Whose Translation Is Controlled by Ligand Binding (1 of 2)
Riboswitches contain aptamers – a specific binding site created from an oligo- or poly-nucleotide sequence
These aptamers are specific for a given metabolite such as B12 cofactor or S-adeno-sylmethionine
Figure 26.25 Structure of the thiamine pyrophosphate riboswitch from Arabidopsis thaliana.

Riboswitches Are mR N A Molecules Whose Translation Is Controlled by Ligand Binding (2 of 2)
In the structure of the thiamin pyrophosphate (T P P) riboswitch (shown here), binding of T P P shuts off gene expression of T P P biosynthesis genes
Figure 26.25 Structure of the thiamine pyrophosphate riboswitch from Arabidopsis thaliana.
26.7 RNA Editing

R N A Editing of the Lipoprotein B Gene Transcript (1 of 2)
Figure 26.26 R N A editing of the lipoprotein B gene transcript.
In R N A editing in bacteria, mR N A sequences can be modified by the posttranscriptional addition or removal of U M P

R N A Editing of the Lipoprotein B Gene Transcript (2 of 2)
Figure 26.26 RNA editing of the lipoprotein B gene transcript.
In mammalian cells, R N A editing involves the deamination of A M P or C M P R N A nucleotides (deamination of C M P yields U M P)

Chapter 26 Summary (1 of 3)
In bacteria, gene expression is regulated primarily at the level of transcription
Transcription is controlled by repressors or activators, which affect the binding of R N A polymerase to promoter sites or the elongation process
Many bacterial genes are expressed as operons, in which multiple proteins can be translated from the same multicistronic mR N A

Chapter 26 Summary (2 of 3)
In eukaryotes, gene expression can also be controlled at the level of transcription, but the regulatory mechanisms are more complex
Many different transcription factors have to assemble at a promoter and upstream enhancer, in order for R N A polymerase II to bind and transcribe a gene
Chromatin remodeling complexes play also a role in clearing transcription start regions that are needed for association with R N A polymerase II and general transcription factors

Chapter 26 Summary (3 of 3)
Methylation of D N A cytosine residues leads to gene silencing; D N A methylation patterns are heritable
Many R N A-based processes such as riboswitches, R N A interference, and R N A editing are important translational regulatory mechanisms

3.1 Free Energy

Thermodynamic Systems (1 of 2)
A system is any part of the universe that we choose to study and everything else is the surroundings
Systems are defined in terms of composition, temperature, pressure, and volume

Thermodynamic Systems (2 of 2)
Systems may be open, closed, or isolated
Closed systems - can exchange energy (e.g., heat or work) but not matter with surroundings
Open systems - can exchange matter and energy with surroundings
Isolated systems - can exchange neither matter nor energy with surroundings
Living systems generally operate at constant temperature and pressure

The First Law of Thermodynamics and Enthalpy
The first law of thermodynamics: Energy is conserved
In a closed system, energy can be exchanged with the surroundings in the form of work or heat
Enthalpy (H) is the heat exchanged between the system and the surroundings at constant pressure (which is the typical condition of living systems)
Measuring the Heat of a Reaction

Reversible and Irreversible Processes (1 of 2)
A reversible process is near a state of equilibrium
A process is at equilibrium when:
It is the lowest energy state of the system
The forward and reverse rates of the process are equal
Example: ice melting at

Reversible and Irreversible Processes (2 of 2)
Irreversible processes are set up far from an equilibrium state, and then proceed towards that state
Example: burning paper

The Second Law of Thermodynamics and Entropy (1 of 3)
The second law of thermodynamics: The entropy of an isolated system will tend to increase to a maximum value
The degree of randomness or disorder of a system is measured by a function of state called the entropy (S)

The Second Law of Thermodynamics and Entropy (2 of 3)
High entropy in the initial state (left), low entropy due to changing the system conditions (middle), and higher entropy in the final state (right) when a new equilibrium is reached

4.6 The Biological Functions of Nucleic Acids: A Preview of Genetic Biochemistry

Storage of Genetic Information, Replication, Transcription, and Translation (1 of 5)
D N A is used to store the total genetic information of an organism (genome)
Replication passes on the genetic information from cell to cell and from generation to generation
Transcription converts D N A-based information of genes into messenger R N A (m R N A) molecules
Translation produces proteins based on information in m R N A at ribosomes

Chapter 4 Summary (1 of 3)
There are two types of biologically relevant nucleic acid: D N A and R N A
R N A has the sugar ribose, while D N A has deoxyribose
R N A uses the pyrimidine uracil, while D N A uses thymine
Both, R N A and D N A, are polymers of four kinds of nucleoside 5′-phosphates connected by links between
3′-hydroxyls and 5′-phosphates
Each nucleic acid has a defined sequence, the primary structure

Chapter 4 Summary (2 of 3)
D N A is the polymer of genetic information
D N A has a secondary structure of a right-handed double helix
The purine adenine basepairs via two hydrogen bonds with the pyrimidine thymine, while the purine guanine basepairs via three hydrogen bonds with the pyrimidine cytosine
The replication mechanism is semiconservative
Energy needs to be invested to separate the double helical D N A structure into single strands

Chapter 4 Summary (3 of 3)
Both, D N A and R N A, form various tertiary structures such as supercoils, hairpins, or cruciform
Nucleic acids have several important biological functions:
D N A contains stored genetic information
D N A is transcribed into messenger R N A (m R N A)
m R N A is translated into proteins at the ribosome
Several tools and techniques have been developed to manipulate D N A such as gene cloning, automated oligonucleotide synthesis, dideoxynucleotide sequencing, site-directed mutagenesis, and X-ray diffraction

5.3 Proteins: Polypeptides of Defined Sequence

The Amino Acid Sequences of Human and Whale Myoglobin (1 of 2)

The Amino Acid Sequences of Human and Whale Myoglobin (2 of 2)
Identical amino acids are the same in both sequences

Evolutionary relationships are inferred from protein amino acid sequences

Biosynthesis of insulin involves a variety of post-translational modifications
5.4 From Gene to Protein

The Genetic Code
The standard genetic code shows the codons, or base triplets, that correspond to each amino acid residue
The A U G start codon places a Met at the beginning of eukaryotic proteins,
Three codons, U A A, U G A, and U A G, are stop codons, which signal termination of translation
Figure 5.17 The genetic code.

Transcription and Translation
Figure 5.19 Relationships between D N A encoding a gene, the transcribed m R N A, and the translated polypeptide chain.

Insulin Processing (1 of 2)
Figure 5.20 Structure of preproinsulin and its conversion to insulin.

Insulin Processing (2 of 2)
Insulin is synthesized as an inactive precursor, preproinsulin, which undergoes enzymatic cleavage to proinsulin, followed by folding and disulfide bond formation, followed by additional enzymatic proteolysis
This allows insulin, like other peptide or protein hormones, to be synthesized and stored as an inactive precursor, available for rapid mobilization on demand

Chapter 5 Summary (1 of 2)
Proteins are polymers of
twenty common amino acids (and two rare), distinguished by their side chains (R groups), are incorporated into proteins
Proteins are produced by condensation of amino acids via peptide bond formation
The unique defined sequence of amino acids constitutes the primary structure of proteins
In cells, genes are transcribed into messenger R N A (m R N A), which is then translated into a polypeptide strand at the ribosomes

Chapter 5 Summary (2 of 2)
Using recombinant expression techniques, proteins can be produced in non-native organisms at very high yields
Genes of interest are incorporated into these non-native organisms, and then transcribed and translated by these organisms
Depending on the certain properties of the produced proteins, they can be purified at high purity by various chromatographic techniques, and the proteins’ masses and sequences can be determined

6.1 Secondary Structure: Regular Ways to Fold the Polypeptide Chain

Pauling’s Rules for Secondary Structure
In the early 1950s, Pauling and collaborators postulated several principles of secondary protein structures that any such structure must obey:
Bond angles and lengths should be like those for respective free amino acids
No atoms should approach one another more closely than allowed by their van der Waals radii
The amide group must remain planar
Noncovalent bonds (particularly hydrogen bonds) stabilize folding processes and products

Four Levels of Protein Structure        (1 of 3)
Primary (1°) structure refers to the amino acid sequence that makes up the protein
Secondary (2°) structure refers to local areas of repeating main chain structure
Tertiary (3°) structure refers to the spatial arrangement of the secondary structural elements in the polypeptide chain
Quaternary (4°) structure refers to the spatial arrangement of multiple polypeptide chains to form multisubunit complexes

Common Secondary Structure Elements (1 of 7)
α-helix: side chains radiate out from helix axis; H bonds are nearly parallel to the helix axis; often with distinct hydrophilic and hydrophobic faces
β-sheet: stabilized by interchain H bonds; side chains alternate sides of the sheet; strands in β sheets can be either parallel (both strands N→C) or antiparallel (one strand N→C, the other strand C→N)
The
helix meets the Pauling criteria but is not
frequently found in proteins

6.2 Fibrous Proteins: Structural Materials of Cells and Tissues

Fibrous Proteins as Structural Materials (1 of 4)
Fibrous proteins are elongated molecules with well-defined secondary structures
Examples include:
keratin—hair, fingernails, feathers, scales, or intermediate filaments (intracellular)
fibroin—silk cocoons
collagen—abundant connective tissue protein; matrix material in bone on which mineral components precipitate

α-Keratin

In α-keratin, large hydrophobic residues repeat every four positions. The α-helix has 3.6 residues per turn, giving each helix a hydrophobic side, which defines the interface between two long helices in the coiled-coil structure typical of keratin.

Silk Fibroin
Figure 6.13 Theoretical model for the structure of silk fibroin.
The extensive close-packed β sheet of fibroin is interrupted by compact folded regions which provide some elasticity.

Collagen (1 of 3)
It is an abundant connective tissue protein; matrix material in bone, on which mineral components precipitate; triple-strand left-handed helix
It contains hydroxyproline (Hyp) and hydroxylysine

Collagen (2 of 3)
G-X-Y tripeptide motif, where X is Pro and Y is Pro or Hyp, lends itself to triple-strand structure
Polypeptide chains are crosslinked and glycosylated
Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) is a cofactor required for proline hydroxylation; vitamin C deficiency (scurvy) leads to collagen degeneration

6.3 Globular Proteins : Tertiary Structure and Functional Diversity

Representations of 3D Structures (1 of 2)
Figure 6.15 The structure of human ubiquitin.

Representations of 3D Structures (2 of 2)
Figure 6.15 The structure of human ubiquitin.

Folding into Defined Structures with Diverse Functions (1 of 2)
Proteins have diverse structures, with varying amounts of helix, sheet, and loop regions
Larger proteins often contain two or more distinct “domains” of compact folded structure
A typical protein “domain” is ~200 amino acids and will fold independently
A domain frequently possesses some defined function (e.g., D N A recognition, oligomerization, cofactor binding, etc.)

Folding into Defined Structures with Diverse Functions (2 of 2)
Figure 6.16 Examples of diversity in protein tertiary structures.

Secondary Structure Motifs, Domains, and Supersecondary Structures
Figure 6.17 Two examples of repeated supersecondary structures within a protein domain.

Classification of Protein Structure (1 of 3)
Protein tertiary structure is characterized by the content of helix and sheet secondary structures, and by defined turns that link these secondary structures
Some proteins are predominantly helix or sheet, others possess a mixture of helix and sheet, or very little defined secondary structure
Not all parts of a globular protein structure can be categorized as helix, sheet, or turn. Such regions are often called “random coil” or, more properly, “irregularly structured regions.”

Information for Protein Folding (1 of 2)

Most of the information specifying the tertiary structure is carried in the amino acid sequence, as shown by Anfinsen’s experiment (carried out in the 1950s and 1960s) with bovine ribonuclease A

Information for Protein Folding (2 of 2)
Thermal denaturation of R N Ase A
Denaturation was measured by
viscosity (☐)
optical rotation (O)

Thermal Denaturation of R N Ase A
The “thermodynamic hypothesis” of protein folding (favorable process under physiological conditions) is supported by the reversibility of folding shown by renaturation followed by repeat denaturation (closed triangles)
Figure 6.22 The denaturation and refolding of ribonuclease A.

Thermodynamics, Folding and Stability of Proteins (1 of 7)
Favorable intramolecular enthalpic interactions
charge-charge interactions (e.g., ionic bonds)
intermolecular hydrogen bonds
van der Waals interactions; proteins are densely packed
Unfavorable loss of conformational entropy
Unfolded state has many conformations (high entropy); folded state has only a few closely related conformations (low entropy)

Thermodynamics, Folding and Stability of Proteins (2 of 7)
Favorable gain of solvent entropy from burying hydrophobic groups (the “hydrophobic effect”)
As hydrophobic side chains cluster in the interior they release ordered solvent molecules from cage-like clathrate structures

Kinetics of Protein Folding (1 of 2)
Levinthal’s paradox (postulated in 1968): If protein samples all possible conformations, rejecting each until it reaches the most stable conformation, folding a protein
could take
years!

6.6 Prediction of Protein Secondary and Tertiary Structure

Predicting Secondary Structures from Amino Acid Sequences
Empirical methods are about 80% accurate
Based on observed distributions of amino acids in helix vs. sheet conformations (e.g., Ala prefers helix, Val prefers sheet, etc.)
Amphiphilic α-helix shows repeating patterns of side chain polarity every 3–4 residues
Amphiphilic β strand shows repeating patterns of side chain polarity every other residue

Predicting Tertiary Structures from Amino Acid Sequences (1 of 2)
Critical need for accurate prediction from sequence, since structures are known for only about 1% of all known sequences
Prediction of tertiary structure is difficult due to the need to correctly predict interactions between residues that are far apart in the primary structure
Current computational methods are about 60% accurate
