6.7 Tools of Biochemistry

Determining Molecular Masses of Native Protein Structures
Molecular masses of native protein structures can be determined using various techniques such as size exclusion chromatography (comparing protein structures to reference proteins eluting through a resin-filled column), analytical ultracentrifugation (examining the sedimentation equilibrium of a protein structures), or mass spectrometry (identifying to mass-to-charge ratios of all components of a protein structure)

Determining the Number and Approximate Molecular Masses of Protein Structure Subunits (1 of 3)
The number and approximate molecular masses of protein structure subunits can be determined using polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (P A G E) under denaturing conditions (e.g., high heat) in the presence of sodium dodecylsulfate (S D S) and a reducing agent
Subunit molecular masses are estimated by comparison to reference proteins simultaneously separated in the gel

Determining the Number and Approximate Molecular Masses of Protein Structure Subunits (2 of 3)
Figure 6B.2 S D S-P A G E analysis of an antibody.

Determining the Number and Approximate Molecular Masses of Protein Structure Subunits (3 of 3)
Figure 6B.1 Estimation of protein molecular weight by S D S gel electrophoresis.
Figure 6B.2 S D S-P A G E analysis of an antibody.

Chapter 6 Summary (1 of 3)
All proteins have at least three levels of organization
Genes dictate the primary level, the amino acid sequences, which then dictates the other levels (secondary, tertiary and, in some cases, quaternary structures)
The peptide bond limits the secondary structures that can be formed
Ramachandran plots visualize clusters of these
sterically allowed secondary structures (α-helix, β-
sheet,
helix, β-turn, γ-turn, etc.)

Chapter 6 Summary (2 of 3)
Proteins can be broadly distinguished into fibrous and globular
Fibrous proteins are often elongated, performing structural roles in cells, while globular proteins have more complex tertiary structures and fold into compact shapes
The folding of globular proteins occurs rapidly and spontaneously under standard biochemical conditions
Misfolding is prevented or corrected by chaperones
Many proteins form functional multisubunit assemblies (quaternary structures), for example exhibiting helical symmetry or point-group symmetry

Chapter 6 Summary (3 of 3)
Various biochemical tools and techniques can be used to monitor protein folding processes into certain structures or changes in the conformation of specific structures. Examples are U V/Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, circular dichroism, and nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy
Molecular masses of native protein structures can be studied, for example, by size exclusion chromatography, analytical ultracentrifugation, and mass spectrometry, while the number and approximate molar masses of protein structure subunits can be determined, for example, by denaturing polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis

7.1 Binding a Specific Target: Antibody Structure and Function

Immunoglobulin Structure and Function (1 of 3)
The immune response is the body’s primary defense against invaders (e.g., pathogens causing infection) and cancer
The immune response system is able to distinguish between “self” and “foreign”
Two types of immune response: innate and adaptive
The adaptive immune response includes humoral and cellular components

Immunoglobulin Structure and Function (2 of 3)
During the humoral response, each B lymphocyte synthesizes an antibody, or immunoglobulin (I  g  G), to a particular antigen
B cells are stimulated to proliferate and secrete the antibody, which binds the antigen, marking it for destruction by macrophages
During cellular response, T lymphocytes, bearing antibody-like structures on their surface, recognize and kill infected or cancerous cells

Immunoglobulin Structure and Function (3 of 3)
Antibodies recognize specific epitopes on antigens
Figure 7.1 Antigenic determinants.
Myoglobin with three different antigenic segments shown in cyan, purple, and orange

Antibodies recognize specific epitopes on antigens
Figure 7.1 Antigenic determinants

7.2 The Adaptive Immune Response

Developmental Paths for Stimulated B Lymphocytes (1 of 2)
An immature B lymphocyte displays on its surface an antibody to a particular antigen
Initial exposure to that antigen stimulates the B cell to proliferate and form effector cells, which secrete a soluble form of the antibody, and memory cells, which mount a larger response upon subsequent exposure to the antigen
This is the basis for the protective effects of vaccines

7.13 Mechanisms of Protein Mutation

Most mutations occur in somatic cells and are not carried forth to the next generation
Evolutionary relationships are defined by considering rates of mutation in genomes and differences in amino acid sequences for homologous proteins
For example, there are 25 amino acid differences between human and sperm whale myoglobins, suggesting that the two species diverged about 100 million years ago

The span of functional protein units reaches from monomers to multi-subunit structures
Motor proteins carrying out the mechanical work of motion within cells, tissues or entire organisms are large complexes with many single polypeptides and other components involved for proper function
One set of motor proteins used in muscles and some contractile systems is actin and myosin
Actin and myosin convert the energy of A T P hydrolysis into mechanical work

Chapter 7 Summary (1 of 3)
The innate and adaptive immune response are the main body defenses against “foreign” invaders
Antibodies or immunoglobulins are humoral components of the adaptive immune response
Antibodies bind specific antigens that are secreted by B cells
Each antibody is composed of two heavy and two light chains, and has two identical antigen-binding sites with complementarity-determining regions (C D R)
E L I S A and Western Blotting are immunological techniques to detect immunoreactive proteins (antigens) in mixtures of cellular extract

Chapter 7 Summary (2 of 3)
Hemoglobin and myoglobin are heme-containing oxygen-binding proteins that are used by vertebrates for oxygen transport and storage
Hemoglobin undergoes a transition between the T (tense) and R (relaxed) state, which is influenced by various allosteric effectors such
(positive homotropic),
and 2,3-bisphosphoglycerate (negative heterotropic)
Allosteric effectors increase binding or release of oxygen from   hemoglobin

Chapter 7 Summary (3 of 3)
Various hemoglobin mutants are found in human populations some are deleterious
A number of multi-subunit protein complexes such as the actin-myosin contractile system are able to convert the free energy of A T P hydrolysis into mechanical work
Coordinated (and regulated) molecular motion of actin-myosin protein complexes result in muscle contraction on the macroscopic level

8.1 Enzymes as Biological Catalysts

What is a Catalyst?
A catalyst is a substance that increases the rate or velocity of a chemical reaction without itself being changed in the overall process
A catalyst accelerates the approach to equilibrium for a given reaction without changing the thermodynamic favorability of the reaction
A catalyst lowers the energy barrier (activation energy of the transition state) in the process of converting a substrate into a product

Transition State and Reaction Rate (2 of 5)
However, to determine the rate of a chemical reaction, not only its initial state and its final state have to be considered, but also its transition state
The transition state is an additional energy barrier to convert reactants into products (and vice versa)
The transition state has more free energy in comparison to that of the reactant or product; thus, it is the least stable state during the progress of a chemical reaction

Multisubstrate Reactions (1 of 4)
Many enzymes catalyze chemical reactions that convert more than one substrate (reactant) into more than one product
For such reactions, the order of the binding and releasing steps becomes an important feature of the enzymatic mechanism
There are at least three types of reactions:
Random substrate binding
Ordered substrate binding
Ping-pong (double displacement) mechanism

Multisubstrate Reactions (2 of 4)
Random substrate binding
Either one of the substrates can be bound first for a reaction to occur (Example: binding of A T P and glucose by hexokinase in the glycolytic pathway)

Multisubstrate Reactions (3 of 4)
Ordered substrate binding
One substrate must bind first before the other(s) can bind significantly. A reaction only occurs when substrates are bound in this order (Examples: dehydrogenase reactions where it is
necessary that the cofactor, i.e.,
or N A D H, binds first)

Multisubstrate Reactions (4 of 4)
Ping-pong (double displacement) mechanism
One substrate is bound and reacts, releasing the first product. Then, a second substrate is bound and reacts, releasing a second product (Examples: serine proteases such as trypsin or chymotrypsin)
8.7 Enzyme Inhibition

Drugs, Toxins, and Enzymatic Activity (1 of 2)
Many prescription drugs are enzyme inhibitors
The development of some drugs has been advanced by kinetic studies, as well as structural studies of the target enzymes
Also, the effects of some natural and synthetic toxins are the result of enzyme inhibition or inactivation
There are two classes of enzyme inhibitors:
reversible inhibitors (noncovalently bound)
irreversible inhibitors (covalently bound)

Drugs, Toxins, and Enzymatic Activity (2 of 2)
Reversible inhibition
While in a reversible inhibition the inhibitor interacts with an enzyme noncovalently, in an irreversible reaction the inhibitor is bound covalently
There are at least three different mode of reversible inhibition:
competitive inhibition
uncompetitive inhibition
mixed or noncompetitive inhibition

8.8 The Regulation of Enzyme Activity

Controlling Enzyme Functions in the Cellular Context (1 of 4)
There are various ways to control and regulate the activities of enzymes within cells. Some important example are:
Substrate level control
reaction rate increases with increasing [S]
in general, a large change in [S] would be required to change the activity of an enzyme significantly (thus, this is a crude means of regulation)

Controlling Enzyme Functions in the Cellular Context (2 of 4)
Regulation at committed steps of pathways
feedback inhibition or activation at control points
often mediated by allosteric enzymes
efficient means to maintain homeostasis
Covalent modification
reversible (e.g., many cell signaling processes)
irreversible (e.g., zymogen activation, blood clotting)

Controlling Enzyme Functions in the Cellular Context (3 of 4)
Feedback inhibition

Controlling Enzyme Functions in the Cellular Context (4 of 4)
Allostery
Allosteric enzymes are usually multisubunit proteins that change their conformations upon binding of substrates or effector molecules
In homoallostery, enzymes exhibit cooperativity in substrate binding
In heteroallostery, enzymes are regulated by (nonsubstrate) effector molecules

8.9 Covalent Modifications Used to Regulate Enzyme Activity

Reversible and Irreversible Modifications (1 of 3)
Many enzymes are covalently modified to activate or deactivate them
in most cases, covalent modification serve as functional on or off switches
Such modifications can be reversible or irreversible:
phosphorylation (carried out by protein kinases) can be reversed by dephosphorylation (performed by protein phosphatases)
zymogens are converted to active enzymes by (irreversible) proteolytic cleavage

Reversible and Irreversible Modifications (2 of 3)
Reversible phosphorylation and dephosphorylation by kinases and phosphatases, respectively

8.10 Nonprotein Biocatalysts : Catalytic Nucleic Acids

Ribonucleic Acids as Enzymes
Ribonucleic acids can catalyze chemical reactions
This type of ribonucleic acids (R N A molecules) are called ribozymes
Many biochemists believe that ancient cells had already R N A-based self-replication and catalytic mechanisms (“R N A World”) before D N A evolved as the main molecule of genetic information and protein became the main molecule of catalytic activity in the cell
8.11 Tools of Biochemistry

Analysis in the Steady State (1 of 6)
Most enzymatic reactions can be measured under steady-state conditions
–  The reaction rate (ν), the
the Michaelis constant
and the turnover number
for a reaction can
be determined when the Michaelis–Menten equation applies
Figure 8.20 The steady state in enzyme kinetics.

Analysis in the Steady State (2 of 6)
Measurements under steady-state conditions (range of seconds to hours) can be carried out using:
Spectrophotometry: substrate or product must absorb light and absorbance must change during the progress of the reaction (substrate and product must absorb light differently)
Fluorescence: similar to spectrophotometry, distinct emission spectra of substrate and product are necessary

Analysis in the Steady State (3 of 6)
Radioactivity assay: substrate is labeled with a radioactive isotope and changes during the conversion to a product are measured. However, separation of isotope in the (initial) substrate state and the (final) product state is necessary
Reactions that are extremely fast require specific techniques, such as:
Stopped flow: Enzyme and substrate are measured while mixed in a flow rate-controlled device

Analysis in the Steady State (4 of 6)
A stopped-flow device
Figure 8A.2 Typical stopped-flow apparatus.

Analysis in the Steady State (5 of 6)
The temperature jump method
Temperature jump: A reaction mixture is at equilibrium at temperature T1. When the temperature is “jumped” to T2, the equilibrium of the reaction will shift, which can be measured
Figure 8A.3 The temperature jump method.

Chapter 8 Summary (1 of 3)
An enzyme is a biological catalyst that enhances the rate of a chemical reaction by lowering its activation energy without affecting its equilibrium
Most enzymes in living systems are proteins, some are R N A
An enzyme binds at least one substrate at its active site, forming an enzyme-substrate complex that converts the substrate into a product, which is then released
The enzyme brings the substrate close to the reaction’s transition state (induced fit hypothesis)

Chapter 8 Summary (2 of 3)
Many enzymes require coenzymes or metal ions for their diverse functions
Most simple enzymatic reactions can be described by the Michaelis–Menten equation:
Enzymes can be inhibited reversibly (competitive, uncompetitive, or mixed/noncompetitive) or irreversibly (covalent binding of an inhibiting molecule to the enzyme’s active site)

Chapter 8 Summary (3 of 3)
Activities of enzymes can be regulated in various ways by: e.g., substrate level control, feedback inhibition/activation, or reversible/irreversible covalent protein modification
An example of reversible modification is the phosphorylation and dephosphorylation of enzymes by kinases and phosphatases, respectively, while the proteolytic activation of zymogens is an example of irreversible modification

9.1 Monosaccharides

Diverse Functions of Carbohydrates
Carbohydrates have diverse functions:
Storage and generation of energy (e.g., glucose, glycogen, starch)
Molecular recognition (e.g., immune system)
Cellular protection (e.g., bacterial and plant cell walls)
Cell adhesion (e.g., glycoproteins)
Biological lubrication (e.g., glycosaminoglycans)
Building and maintaining biological structure (e.g., cellulose, chitin)

Carbohydrate Terminology (1 of 3)
Monosaccharide: Simple sugars and derivatives with 3 to 9 carbon atoms
Oligosaccharide: Compound formed by linking several monosaccharides together (e.g., disaccharide, with 2)
Polysaccharide: Polymer formed from multiple saccharide units; may be homopolysaccharide or heteropolysaccharide
Glycan: Generic term for oligosaccharides and polysaccharides

Enantiomers (1 of 3)
Monosaccharides are chiral
for example, the second carbon of glyceraldehyde carries four different substituents
Enantiomers are optical isomers that are (nonsuperimposable) mirror images
glyceraldehyde has two enantiomers designated as D and L (or alternatively, R and S)

Diastereomers (1 of 3)
Compounds with more than one asymmetric carbon may be enantiomers (mirror images) or diastereomers
diastereomers are optical isomers that are not mirror images
enantiomers have three or more carbons, while diastereomers have four or more carbons
By convention, D and L refer to configuration about the asymmetric carbon farthest from the carbonyl carbon

Aldotetroses

Homopolysaccharides and Heteropolysaccharides
Polysaccharides can be distinguished into homopolysaccharides and heterosaccharides:
Homopolysaccharides are made of one type of monomer, while heterosaccharides are made of more than one type of monomer
Functionally, polysaccharides can be divided into
energy storage polysaccharides (e.g., starch and glycogen)
structural polysaccharides (e.g., cellulose)
lubricants (e.g., some glycoaminoglycans)

Glycosaminoglycans  (1 of 2)
are polymers of repeating disaccharide units
serve structural and nonstructural purposes invertebrates
are important parts of connective, epithelial and neural tissues, as well as the skin
form matrices to hold together certain proteins in skin and connective tissues
act as viscosity-increasing agents or lubricants (in certain body fluids)

Peptidoglycan (1 of 3)
Bacterial (in particular, gram-positive) cell walls have cross-linked, multilayered polysaccharide-peptide complexes called peptidoglycan at the surface
Long chains of strictly alternating copolymers of N-acetylglucosamine (N A G) and N-acetylmuramic acid (N A M) are crosslinked through short peptides
Peptidoglycan synthesis is the site of action of the earliest antibiotics (such as penicillin)

9.5 Glycoproteins

Linking Saccharide Chains to Proteins (1 of 2)
More than half of all eukaryotic proteins are glycoproteins, that is, they carry covalently attached oligosaccharide or polysaccharide chains
These chains can be N-linked (to the side chain amide group of an asparagine residue) or O-linked (to the side chain of the hydroxyl groups of threonine or serine residues)
Glycoproteins have a variety of different functions such as in cellular protein distribution, cell adhesion, or cell recognition

Linking Saccharide Chains to Proteins (2 of 2)
N-linked and O-linked glycoproteins

N-Linked and O-Linked Glycoproteins
Blood-group antigens are O-linked glycoproteins
Type A has GalNAc glycosyltransferase activity,while type B has Gal glycosyltransferase activity
Type AB has both, while type O has neither
The ABO is one of (at least) 14 genetically characterized blood group systems, with more than 100 different antigens, many found in tissues other than blood

Blood-Group Antigens are O-Linked Glycoproteins
The A B O blood types

The ABO Blood Types
Table 9.3 Transfusion relationships among ABO blood types
principle, this relationship is true. However, Abs are not given donations
from other types because the donor’s antibodies could react with the recipient’s antigens, unless the red blood cells are first separated from the plasma.

Erythropoetin A (EPO)
is a glycoprotein with both O- and N-linked oligosaccharides, which help stabilizing the protein core in the blood
Hormone is synthesized in kidney that stimulates the production of red blood cells
EPO is administered during cancer chemotherapy to counteract anemia
Recombinant EPO is misused by some athletes to improve performance

Influenza Virus Neuraminidase (1 of 2)
Release of influenza virus particles at the conclusion of a cycle of infection requires neuraminidase action to cleave sialic acid (or N-acetylneuraminic acid) from the remainder of N-linked oligosaccharide chains

Influenza Virus Neuraminidase (2 of 2)
Oseltamivir, marketed as Tamiflu, is an analog of the sialic acid substrate
It inhibits neuraminidase activity and thus blocks the release of newly formed virus particles from infected cells
9.6 Tools of Biochemistry

Glycomics
Because of the large variety of monomers and branching possibilities, structural analysis of polysaccharides is difficult
In addition to the development of improved analytical techniques, the field of glycomics has been facilitated by the characterization of enzymes that digest specific links in polysaccharides

Chapter 9 Summary (1 of 2)
Carbohydrates (or saccharides) are compounds with the
general stoichiometric formula
They have one or more chiral centers and exist as enantiomers or diastereomers
Monosaccharides may be aldoses or ketoses
When containing five or more carbons, they usually form rings with five (furanose) or six (pyranose) atoms, which
exist as
anomers and exhibit multiple
conformations
Derivatives of monosaccharides include phosphate esters, sugar acids, amino sugars, and glycosides

Chapter 9 Summary (2 of 2)
Monosaccharides can be linked by glycosidic bonds to form di-, oligo-, or polysaccharides
The condensation reaction linking monosaccharides is thermodynamically unfavored under physiological conditions and requires “activated” (i.e., phosphorylated) substrates
Polysaccharides are used for various purposes, including energy storage (starch and glycogen) and cellular structures (cellulose and chitin)
Oligo- and polysaccharides can also be linked to proteins to form glycoproteins

Dissociation of Weak Electrolytes
Consider a weak acid, HA
What is the acid dissociation constant equation?
HA ⇄ H + + A -
HA: conjugate acid (protonated form)
A - : conjugate base (de-protonated form)

The Henderson-Hasselbalch Equation
Know this! You'll use it constantly .
For any acid HA, the relationship between the pK a , the concentrations existing at equilibrium and the solution pH is given by:
HA ⇄ H + + A -
The pH is related to the pKa of the acid and the ratio of conj. base to conj. acid

Example
What is the pH of a solution containing 0.36 M sodium acetate and 0.72 M acetic acid? Note: the pKa of acetic acid is 4.8
Acetic acid: CH3COOH		 (conj. acid)
Sodium acetate: CH3COO- Na+  (conj. base)
pH = 4.8 + log (0.36/0.72) = 4.5
Even having twice as much acid as base changed the pH by 0.3 points!

Example
What is the pH of a solution containing the same concentration of conjugate acid and conjugate base?
[A - ] = [HA]
[A - ]/[HA] = 1
pH = pKa + log (1)      what’s log (1)?
pH = pKa
pKa is the pH when [HA] = [A-]
Zero!

Acid-Base Behavior of Amino Acids

High pH = more base = COOH will be COO- &
–NH3+ will be –NH2

Chapter 6 Proteins: Secondary, Tertiary, and Quaternary Structure

Secondary Structure
The secondary structure is the arrangement of localized regions of a protein.
These regions arise due to hydrogen bonding between the N—H group of one amide with the C═O group of another.
Two stable arrangements are the a -helix and the b -pleated sheet .
Most proteins have regions of a -helix and b -pleated sheet, and other regions that are random arrangements.

Secondary Structure
The a -helix:
H-bonds: along the axis of the helix

Alpha helix
Stabilized by main chain H-bonds  (NH and C=O)
Typically right handed with 3.6 amino acids per turn
Note: Side chains are NOT involved in helix H-bonding
Side chains point outward

Secondary Structure
The b -pleated sheet:
H-bonds: across the sheet, between different strands of the sheet
β-strand
β-strand
Multiple strands form one sheet

Beta sheets
Beta sheets can be parallel or anti-parralel
N to C line up, or alternate
Side chains point above and below the plane in an alternating fashion

Secondary Structure
Shorthand symbols on a protein ribbon diagram:
ribbon diagrams simplify the way we view protein structure

ribbon diagrams simplify the way we view protein structure

Why are secondary structures favored?
Even through peptide chains are flexible, bond rotation can be limited
There is a limited amount of “favorable” arrangements due to
Stabilization by intra-chain interactions (mainly H-bonds)
Destabilization by steric clashes
Limited rotation around the peptide bond

Phi and Psi angles
Dihedral angles play an important part in determining protein structure
Phi and Psi angles provide rotational degrees of freedom to the peptide chain
Omega is the angle around the peptide bond (constant ~ 180)
Phi: N-C α , Psi: C α -C

Phi and Psi angles
Dihedral angles range from -180 to 180 degrees
Example: Phi at 180 and at 45 degrees
Combinations of phi and psi angles give rise to specific protein structure elements

Protein domains are nature ’ s modular strategy for protein design
Proteins composed of about 250 amino acids or less often have a simple, compact globular shape

Subunits have very high affinity for each other

Rotational and translational entropy

Protein structure
Proteins adopt 3 dimensional structures stabilized by weak forces
H-bonds, ionic interactions, etc
Protein can be “unfolded” in a process known as denaturation

Denaturation Leads to Loss of Protein Structure and Function
The cellular environment is suited to maintaining the weak forces that preserve protein structure and function
External stresses – heat, chemical treatment, etc. – can disrupt these forces in a process termed denaturation – the loss of structure and function
The cooking of an egg is an everyday example
Ovalbumin, the principal protein in egg white, remains in its native structure up to a characteristic melting temperature, T m
Above this temperature, the structure unfolds and function is lost

Denaturation Leads to Loss of Protein Structure and Function
Protein unfolding often leads to aggregation:
Clear egg white (soluble proteins) -> white insoluble aggregated protein

Denaturation Leads to Loss of Protein Structure and Function
Unfolding is “cooperative”
Showing a sigmoidal curve
Under thermal denaturation, the midpoint is known as the Tm (melting temp)
Where ½ is folded, ½ is unfolded
Tm

Chemical denaturation
Figure 6.31 Proteins can be denatured (unfolded) by high concentrations of guanidine- HCl or urea.  The denaturation of chymotrypsin is plotted here.
These so-called chaotropic solvents change the way the protein interacts with water and favor the unfolded form of the protein

Protein folding
So, how do proteins fold in the first place?

Is There a Single Mechanism for Protein Folding?
How a protein achieves its stable, folded state is a complex question
Levinthal ’ s paradox demonstrates that proteins cannot fold by sampling all possible conformations
This implies that proteins actually fold via specific “ folding pathways ”
What factors play a role in protein folding processes?

Postulated Themes of Protein Folding
Secondary structures – helices, sheets, and turns – probably form first
Nonpolar residues may aggregate or coalesce in a process termed a hydrophobic collapse
Subsequent steps probably involve formation of long-range interactions between secondary structures or involving other hydrophobic interactions
The folding process may involve one or more intermediate states, including transition states and what have become known as molten globules

The Protein Folding Energy Landscape
Figure 6.34  A model for the steps involved in the folding of globular proteins.
Ken Dill has suggested that the folding process can be pictured as a funnel of free energies.  The rim at the top represents the many unfolded states.  Polypeptides ‘ fall down the wall of the funnel ’ to ever fewer possibilities and lower energies as they fold.
Part 2C Proteins: Isolation and characterization

Studying proteins
To study protein of interest, it must be isolated from other cellular proteins
Humans		~40,000 genes
Yeast		~6000 genes
Bacteria		~3000 genes
How can we isolate our protein of interest?
chromatography

1. Protein expression (production)
If human protein, can use human cells (can be grown in the lab)
Human proteins can be “expressed” in another organism, most commonly bacteria
The gene that codes for the protein of interest is “cloned” in bacterial host
Bacteria are “induced” to produce the protein
Bacteria are easier to grow in large cultures
Human cells growing in culture

2. Harvesting
In this step, the cells are broken down to release all cellular components:
Detergents: disrupt cell membrane
Enzymes: breakdown the cell wall of bacteria
Example: lysozyme (found in saliva)
Ultrasonic waves: “sonication”
Disrupt cell membrane
Mechanical: forcing cells through a small opening under high pressure

3. Purification by chromatography
Separation of molecules based on their different interactions with a stationary phase and a mobile phase
Molecules that interact more with the solid phase will move “slower” (will be retarded)
Molecules that interact more with the mobile phase will move faster

Types of chromatography
Size exclusion (Gel filtration):
Separating proteins base on size (Molecular weight)
Ion exchange:
Separating proteins based on charge
Affinity chromatography:
Separating proteins based on specific interaction

Size exclusion chromatography
Proteins are separated based on size (molecular weight)
AKA: gel filtration chromatography
Mixture is added to the column
Larger proteins move “faster” than smaller proteins

Size exclusion chromatography
Small proteins are held back as they pass through the small openings within the beads on the column
Large proteins pass around the beads on the column
The longer the column, the better the separation

Ion exchange chromatography
Separation based on charge
The beads on the column have either a negative or a positive charge
Proteins with opposite charge will bind to the beads
Proteins with similar charge will be repelled and move through the column
Increasing salt concentration in a gradient sequentially removes the bound proteins as the salt competes with the proteins
More charged proteins will be retained longer

Ion exchange chromatography
Binding of opposite charged molecules
Elution: when the protein comes out of the column!

Ion exchange chromatography
Each protein has a specific net charge at any given pH
The pH at which the net charge of a protein is zero is known as the pI (isoelectric pH)
Above the pI , the protein is negatively charged (deprotonated)
Below the pI , the protein is positively charger (protonated)
Separation of proteins on ion exchange chromatography can be accomplished by changing the pH of the mobile phase

Ion exchange chromatography
Example: your protein of interest has a pI of 7
To purify the protein, use positively charged beads
Start with high pH (> pH 9)
The protein will be negatively charged, will bind to the beads
Slowly lower the pH of the mobile phase
Around pH 7, the protein will be neutral, and will start to elute from the column
Other proteins will elute at earlier pH values or will be retained longer

Affinity chromatography
Purifying protein based on specific interaction with the stationary phase
Protein mixture is loaded on the column
The stationary phase contains an immobilized receptor for our protein of interest
All other proteins wash away
Elution is performed by breaking the interactions with the stationary phase (Changing pH or salt)

Affinity chromatography
Example: Purifying growth hormone using growth hormone receptor column
After all other proteins are washed away, a low pH solution is used to elute growth hormone from the column

Affinity chromatography
Advantages:
Typically provides the most efficient way to purify proteins
High purity due to high specificity of molecular interaction
Affinity chromatography can be limited
It is not always the case that there is an available receptor (or binding partner) for your protein
Protein of interest may not have a known function at all

IMAC
A specific type of affinity chromatography known as Immobilized-metal affinity chromatography
Relies on the interaction between the side chains of a stretch of histidines with a metal (typically Nickel or Zinc)
Requires that the protein has a 6-10 histidine tag either at the C- or N-terminus
This sequence can be added at the gene level

IMAC
Protein of interest has a “His-tag”
Cellular proteins are added to the column:
Beads are modified with NTA ( nitrilotriacetic acid)
Proteins without His-tag do not bind
Elution:
High [Imidazole]
Competes with
his-tagged protein
What’s another way for elution?

Chromatography
Typically, more than one type of chromatography is used to purify a protein of interest
Must balance purity with yield
Every purification step: higher purity, lower yield
IMAC is the most common
Can be followed by size exclusion or ion exchange to get an extra pure sample, depending on application

Protein characterization: PAGE
How to assess purity and yield from a protein purification experiment?
PAGE: P oly a crylamide G el E lectrophoresis
A gel matrix through which the proteins travel using an electric field
Small proteins move faster, larger proteins move slower through the pores in the gel matrix
Negative electrode
Positive Electrode
Mobility

Gel electrophoresis
separate proteins according to their electrophoretic mobility (a function of length of polypeptide chain or molecular weight).
separate proteins according to their size, and no other physical feature.
Proteins move from negative to positive electrode through the gel
All must have a net negative charge

SDS: provides negative charge
To give all proteins an “even” net negative charge, SDS is used
Sodium dodecylsulfate
Larger proteins = More SDS
Relatively similar mass/charge ratio

SDS PAGE
SDS (the detergent soap) breaks up hydrophobic areas and coats proteins with negative charges thus overwhelming positive charges in the protein.
The detergent binds to hydrophobic regions in a constant ratio of about 1.4 g of SDS per gram of protein.
Larger proteins = More SDS
Relatively similar mass/charge ratio
SDS Makes the Rate of Protein Migration Proportional to Molecular Weight

PAGE: polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis
The gel is a polymer of acrylamide, which forms long chains
Bis -acrylamide is a crosslinker which connects chains of polyacrylamide
This forms the gel matrix in which proteins will travel
High % of acrylamide makes a more dense gel matrix
For separating small proteins, do we need high or low % acrylamide?

SDS PAGE
Samples are loaded in wells side-by-side
Always use a molecular weight standard
Proteins appear as bands
Protein bands can be identified based on molecular weight

Chromatography + electrophoresis
After protein purification, typically gel electrophoresis is used for analysis
Example: gel from IMAC purification
M: MW standard
Lysate contains all cellular proteins
Flow through: unbound proteins
His-tagged protein elutes using imidazole

2D electrophoresis
AKA: isoelectric focusing
2 steps:
pH gradient: separate proteins by pI
SDS PAGE: separate proteins by MW
A protein will stop migrating at pH corresponding to its pI

What do proteins do?

Catalysis: Enzymes
Enzymes are proteins that catalyze a chemical reaction
Convert substrate(s) into product(s)
Enzymes are catalysts: (what is a catalyst?)
Accelerate the rate of a chemical reaction
Enzymes are not changed in the process
Reaction must be thermodynamically favored (spontaneous)

Thermodynamics and kinetics of chemical reactions
Take notes…

Virtually All Reactions in Cells Are Mediated by Enzymes
Enzymes catalyze thermodynamically favorable reactions, causing them to proceed at extraordinarily rapid rates
Enzymes provide cells with the ability to exert kinetic control over thermodynamic potentiality
Living systems use enzymes to accelerate and control the rates of vitally important biochemical reactions

Enzymes
How do enzymes accelerate the rate of a chemical reactions?
By lowering the activation energy (∆ G ‡ )
Enzymes DO NOT change the overall ∆G of a reaction

The Turnover Number Defines the Activity of One Enzyme Molecule

The Ratio k cat /K m Defines the Catalytic Efficiency of an Enzyme
The catalytic efficiency: k cat /K m
An estimate of "how perfect" the enzyme is
k cat / K m measures how well the enzyme performs when S is low
The upper limit for k cat / K m is the diffusion limit - the rate at which E and S diffuse together
The Ratio k cat / K m Defines the Catalytic Efficiency of an Enzyme

Transition state stabilization by enzymes
All reactions pass through a transition state (X ‡ ) on the reaction pathway
The active sites of enzymes bind the transition state of the reaction more tightly than the substrate
By doing so, enzymes stabilize the transition state and lower the activation energy of the reaction

Transition state stabilization by enzymes
The structure of the active site is well-suited for the transition state binding
Enzymes have an extremely high affinity for the transition state
This forces the substrate to adopt the transition state structure
Increasing the likelihood of product formation
The Kd of the enzyme for the transition state may be up to 10 -20 to 10 -26 M !

14.2 What Role Does Transition-State Stabilization Play in Enzyme Catalysis?
The binding of S to E must be favorable
But not too favorable!
K m cannot be "too tight" - goal is to make the energy barrier between ES and EX ‡ small

Enzymes as catalysts
Binding:
High affinity of the enzyme for the transition state
Modest affinity for the substrate
Low affinity for the product
Structure/function:
active site side chains are precisely located to allow TS stabilization
De-solvation of Substrate: prevent water from interfering with intermediates
In some cases, allowing water to react in a controlled manner

Protein Motions Are Essential to Enzyme Catalysis
Proteins are constantly moving – bonds vibrate, side chains bend and rotate, backbone loops wiggle and sway, and whole domains move as a unit
Enzymes depend on such motions to provoke and direct catalytic events
Protein motions support catalysis in several ways.  Active site conformation changes can:
Assist substrate binding
Bring catalytic groups into position
Assist in bond making and bond breaking
Facilitate conversion of substrate to product

How do enzymes carryout chemical reactions?
Example: Proteases (Proteinase)
Enzymes that breakdown proteins
Must break the highly stable peptide bond

14.5 What Can Be Learned From Typical Enzyme Mechanisms?
First Example: the serine proteases
Covalent catalysis : Enzyme and substrate become linked in a covalent bond at one or more points in the reaction pathway
The formation of the covalent bond provides chemistry that speeds the reaction
Serine proteases also employ general acid-base catalysis (amino acid side chains act as acid or base)
Specific acid/base catalysis : water as acid/base

The Serine Proteases
All involve a serine in catalysis - thus the name
Ser is part of a "catalytic triad" of Ser , His, Asp
Serine proteases are homologous, but locations of the three crucial residues differ somewhat
Examples:
Trypsin, chymotrypsin: digestive enzymes
Elastase : remodeling of extracellular matrix
Thrombin, plasmin: involved in coaggulation
Subtilisin : from bacteria, used in detergents

Types of enzyme inhibition
Irreversible inhibition
Typically involves an inhibitor molecule binding covalently (irreversibly) to the enzyme
Usually binding to a catalytic side chain, preventing catalysis

Suicide inhibitor: Penicillin
Penicillin is an antibiotic, inhibits the enzyme transpeptidase

Types of reversible inhibition
Competitive inhibition:

Non-competitive inhibition:
Inhibitor binds away from active site
Same Km, lower Vmax
Uncompetitive inhibition:
Inhibitor binds to ES complex
Lower Km, lower Vmax

Adenosine deaminase

Cofactors

Metal catalysis
Zn 2+ ion can allow a base ( Histidine ) to abstract a proton from water, forming OH -
OH - is a powerful nucleophile, resulting in addition of a –OH to the ring (tetrahedral intermediate forms)
Glu396 acts as a general acid, then a general base

What about “non-spontaneous” reactions?
While enzymes can speed up the reaction by lowering the activation energy, they are unable to change the overall ∆G of the reaction
In many cases, a non-spontaneous reaction is required, how do enzymes overcome this problem?
Solution: coupling

Example
First step in metabolizing glucose is phosphorylation
Glucose + PO 4  Glucose-6-Phosphate + H 2 O (∆G = 13.8 kJ/ mol )
Adding a phosphate group to glucose is non-spontaneous
Enzyme uses ATP as a source of PO 4
Breakdown of ATP is highly favorable
ATP + H 2 O  ADP + PO 4 (∆G = -30.5 kJ/ mol )
Coupling ATP hydrolysis with phosphorylation gives an overall spontaneous reaction

ATP as a source of energy
Adenosine triphosphate (ATP)
∆G = -30.5 kJ/ mol
ATP hydrolysis releases 30.5 kJ/ mol of energy
This energy can be harnessed by enzymes to carryout reactions with positive ∆G
Energy

Coupling
Using Hess’s law, the sum of ∆G values from two reactions = the ∆G of the net reaction
Can you write down the individual reactions and the net reaction?
Net reaction

Coupling
All that matters in the net reaction
Glucose + PO 4  glucose-6-phos. + H 2 O  (13.8 kJ/ mol )
ATP + H 2 O        ADP + PO 4 (-30.5 kJ/ mol )
______________________________________________
Net:     Glucose + ATP  glucose-6-phos. + ADP (-16.7 kJ/ mol )
The reason why this works is because ∆G is a state function
Only depends on the difference between the initial and final states

Can enzymes be designed?
Enzymes catalyze chemical reactions by stabilizing transition states
Enzymes have the highest affinity for the transition state
If we can “design” a protein that binds to a transition state at high affinity, it may carryout the desired chemical reactions
A class of designer enzymes is known as Catalytic Antibodies

Antibodies
Antibodies ( immunoglobulins )
Produced by immune cells (B-lymphocytes)
Bind to and neutralize foreign molecule (antigens)
Antibodies have very high affinity for the antigen
The antigen binding site is well suited to fit the antigen
So, what if we use a transition state analog as the antigen?

Catalytic antibodies
“Immunizing” an animal with a TS analog as a foreign antigen
Result: antibodies that have high affinity for TS
Given the substrate, some of these antibodies may catalyze the reaction by forcing the S to adopt a TS
Catalytic antibodies are often called “ Abzymes ”
Advantage: can catalyze “non-natural” reactions
Disadvantage: typically slow kinetics, high Km values, not as efficient as “natural” enzymes
Antibody antigen interactions are not nearly as high as natural enzymes’ affinity for a TS
( Kd : 10 -9 -10 -12 M vs 10 -20 -10 -26 M)

Computational enzyme design
In few examples, enzymes can be designed by computational modeling
A computer model of the TS is used to “place” catalytic groups
Computer algorithm finds best positions to place groups in a protein “scaffold”
Designs are purified and tested for activity in the lab

Computational enzyme design
If you are interested here are some links:
First reported in 2004: http:// science.sciencemag.org /content/304/5679/1967
Then retracted for lack of reproducibility in 2008
First successful case reported in 2008

For reviews:

Computationally designed enzymes are still very slow, field is still new

Are all enzymes proteins?
Vast majority of chemical reactions in biology are catalyzed by enzymes
Few chemical reactions are carried out by RNA (ribozymes)
Ribozymes - segments of RNA that display enzyme activity
Examples:
RNase P: breaks RNA
Peptidyl transferase : creates peptide bonds during protein synthesis at the ribosome

How does the ribosome function?
mRNA codes for protein sequence
Each 3 nucleotides code for a single amino acid
Amino acids are brought to the ribosome by tRNA
Peptide bond is formed between the incoming a.a . and the growing protein chain
Reaction is catalyzed by rRNA

How does the ribosome function?
The ribosome is a collection of proteins and rRNA associated tightly together to form a large 3D structure
The ribosome is a machine for protein synthesis
The rRNA within the ribosome acts as a catalyst to for peptide bond formation
Atomic structure of one subunit of a ribosome. Blue: proteins, orange: RNA, Green: active site
Cool animation here [https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ribosome#/media/File:Protein_translation.gif]

What’s the mechanism
Peptide bond formation is catalyzed by an adenosine (A76), using the ribose sugar hydroxyl groups
C-term of the peptide chain is attached to the tRNA via an ester bond
Proton exchange between the two hydroxyls of the ribose allows amino group of a.a . to attack the carbonyl
Peptide bond is formed

RNA world theory
Hypothesis since the 1980’s that RNA molecules can act as enzymes (ribozymes)
Some examples were well characterized (such as RNA processing)
In 2000: Discovery that peptide bond formation in the ribosome
Reinforced the “RNA world theory”
Early life relied on RNA for catalysis
Result: formation of peptides  proteins  enzymes
Enzymes are much more proficient that ribozymes

Summary
Enzyme inhibitors as drugs:
More potent = low Ki
Km/Ki is a measure of potency
Some enzymes require cofactors
Metals or vitamins
Coupling: catalyze “non-spontaneous” reactions
Enzymes can be designed:
Cat. Antibodies or computational design
Not all enzymes are proteins
RNA (ribozymes)

Myoglobin: oxygen storage
Single peptide chain
Single oxygen binding site

Myoglobin vs. Hemoglobin
Function:
Mb: Oxygen storage in the muscle
Hb : oxygen transport from lungs to tissue, CO 2 transport from tissue to lungs
Structure:
Mb: one subunit, one heme
Hb : 4 subunits (2α,2β), each with 1 heme , total 4 oxygen binding sites

Fe 2+ is coordinated by His F8
Iron is coordinated by 6 groups:
4 Nitrogens from porphyrin
Histidine F8 (#8 in helix F)
1 Oxygen molecule

O 2 -Binding Curves of Myoglobin
Binding depends on partial pressure of oxygen ( p O 2 )
Kd ~ 1 torr
At high p O 2 , oxygen is bound
When p O 2 is low (e.g. workout), Oxygen is released
Mb: single ligand, single binding site
Result: hyperbolic curve

Hb : the “plot” thickens
Hb has 4 binding sites, up to 4 oxygens can bind
Instead of a hyperbolic curve  sigmoidal curve

Oxygen binding to Hb is cooperative
The binding of O 2 to Hb is cooperative – binding of oxygen to the first subunit makes binding to the other subunits more favorable
Binding at one site affects binding at the next site

Oxygen binding to Hb is cooperative
binding of oxygen to the first subunit makes binding to the other subunits more favorable:
Positive cooperativity : sigmoidal plot

The Hill coefficient: O 2 -Binding Curve for Hb
Sigmoidal behavior fits the Hill equation, were H is the Hill Coefficient
Equation requires an exponential term, which is the power of [L]

Hill Coefficient
Hill Coefficient describes cooperative binding behavior
H > 1: positive cooperativity
Binding at one site  higher affinity at next site
H< 1: negative cooperativity
Binding at one site  lower affinity at next site
H = 1: no cooperativity
Non-interacting binding sites

For Hemoglobin

Cooperativity
What the molecular basis for cooperativity ?
How does binding at one subunit affect binding at another subunit?
Answer: conformational changes
Oxygen binding changes the protein conformation
Without oxygen bound, Fe 2+ is out of heme plane
Oxygen binding pulls the Fe 2+ into the heme plane
Fe 2+ pulls its His F8 side chain along with it
The F helix moves when oxygen binds
Total movement of Fe 2+ is 0.029 nm – i.e ., 0.29 Å
See it here [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1OorAAFe5oQ] and here [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pM2cdisL2Sw]

Oxygen Binding by Hb Induces a Quaternary Structure Change
Total movement of Fe 2+ is 0.029 nm – i.e ., 0.29 Å triggers a large global conformation change
One alpha-beta pair moves relative to the other by 15 degrees upon oxygen binding
This massive change is induced by movement of Fe by 0.029 nm when oxygen binds

Oxygen binding to Hb results in a 15° rotation of one αβ pair relative to the other
Figure 15.26  Subunit motion in hemoglobin when the molecule goes from the (a) deoxy form to the (b) oxy form.

The Physiological Significance of the Hb:O 2 Interaction
Hb must be able to bind oxygen in the lungs
High p O 2 (~100 torr ) in alveoli (air sacks)
Hb must be able to release oxygen in capillaries
Low p O 2 (~50 torr ) in venous blood
If Hb behaved like Mb, very little oxygen would be released in capillaries
The sigmoid, cooperative oxygen-binding curve of Hb makes its physiological actions possible!

H + Promotes Dissociation of Oxygen from Hemoglobin: The Bohr effect
Figure 15.29 The oxygen saturation curves for myoglobin and for hemoglobin at five different pH values: 7.6, 7.4,7.2, 7.0, 6.8.
 [H + ]

Decreasing O 2 Binding by H + is Termed the Bohr Effect
The effect of H + on O 2 binding was discovered by Christian Bohr (the father of Neils Bohr, the atomic physicist)
Binding of protons diminishes oxygen binding
Binding of oxygen diminishes proton binding

CO 2 has a double effect
CO2 can shift the Hb curve to the right (low affinity)
CO 2 can decrease the affinity of Hb for O 2 in two ways:
CO 2 binds directly to Hb forming “ Carbaminohemoglobin ”  O 2 release
CO 2 reacts with H 2 O to form Carbonic acid, which dissociates to release H+, enhancing the Bohr effect  O 2 release
CO 2 + H 2 O ⇄ H 2 CO 3 ⇄ H + + HCO 3 –

Cl - also reduces the affinity for O 2
The chloride ion also has a negative effect of Oxygen affinity
High [ Cl -]  low oxygen affinity  O 2 release
What is the physiological significance in all of this?
High [H + ], [ Cl - ] and p CO 2 all are results of metabolism
Tissue with the highest levels of these compounds promote the release of the most O 2

HCO 3 - is exchanged with Cl -

Allostery
So how do these molecules promote oxygen release?
H + , Cl - and CO 2 DO NOT compete with the O 2 binding site
Instead, they bind far away from the heme center
Binding of H + , Cl - and CO 2 changes the conformation of Hb to a low affinity conformation
This is known as allosteric modulation
Binding at one site, affects binding at a far away site

Allostery
Binding at one site, affects binding at a far away site
Negative allostery :
Binding at one site, decreases affinity at a far away site
Positive allostery :
Binding at one site, increases affinity at a far away site
H + , Cl - and CO 2 are all negative allosteric effectors
they decrease the affinity of Hb for O 2

Negative allostery
Binding at one site, release at another site
Effect is transferred through the protein due to a conformation change

Hb mutation and sickle cell disease
Sickle cell anemia (disease)
Mutation in beta chain of Hb
Position 6: Glu  Val (E6V mutation)
Mutant Hb is known as HbS
Change from polar charged amino acid to non-polar (hydrophobic amino acid)
There is a hydrophobic pocket exposed only in the dexy-Hb form
Valine side chain fits into that pocket
Deoxy-Hb is prone to polymerization

Sickle-Cell Anemia is a Molecular Disease
Figure 15.34 The polymerization of Hb S molecules arises because Val is on the surface of β - chains These can insert into hydrophobic pockets in neighboring Hb S molecules.
Valine side chain
Hydrophobic pocket

Sickle-Cell Anemia is a Molecular Disease
Figure 15.34 Polymerization of HB S.

Sickle-Cell Anemia is a Molecular Disease
Figure 15.34 Structure of the polymerized Hb S filament. Val at position 6 of the β -chains is shown in blue. Hemes are red.

Sickle-Cell Anemia is a Molecular Disease
Hb S molecules aggregate into long, chainlike polymeric structures
Sickle-cell anemia patients have abnormally-shaped red blood cells
The erythrocytes are crescent-shaped instead of disc-shaped
The sickle cells pass less freely through the capillaries, impairing circulation and causing tissue damage
Sickle cell (left), normal RBC (right)

Sickle cell disease epidemiology
Sickle cell mutation can provide protection against malaria (endemic in sub-Saharan regions)
Even heterozygous individuals (only one “bad” copy of the HbS ) are protected
Sickle cell disease is very common in sub-Saharan Africa due to natural selection
Mechanism of protection is still not clear

Carbon monoxide poisoning
CO is a powerful “antagonist” of oxygen binding to Hb
Unlike CO 2 , CO binds to the SAME SITE where oxygen binds
CO affinity is 200X higher for Fe center than O 2
Result: inhibition of O 2 binding and transport
If not treated  loss of consciousness and death

CO poisoning
CO is a non-covalent competitive binder
200X tighter than O 2
Curve is shifted to the left
CO enhances binding
Prevents full O 2 saturation
Hb bound to CO is known as Caboxyhemoglobin

The G-Protein-Coupled Receptors Are 7-TMS Integral Membrane Proteins
The G-protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) have 7 transmembrane helices.

Phospholipase C (PLC): breaks down phopsholipid

Synthesis or Release of a Second Messenger is Followed Quickly by Degradation or Removal

Two Stages of Amplification Occur in the G-Protein-Mediated Hormone Reponse
Figure 32.22 Adenylyl cyclase activity is modulated by the interplay of stimulatory ( G s ) and inhibitory ( G i ) proteins. Hormone binding to β 1 - and β 2 - receptors activates adenylyl cyclase , whereas hormone binding to α 2 -receptors leads to inhibition of adenylyl cyclase .

What is a second messenger?
The hormone is the "first messenger"
The second messenger - Ca 2+ , cAMP or other - is released when the hormone binds to its (extracellular) receptor
The second messenger then activates (or inhibits) processes in the cytosol or nucleus
Degradation and/or clearance of the second messenger is also (obviously) important

GPCR restoration
At some point, the G-protein will convert GTP to GDP
This will cause the G-protein to bind to the GPCR again (if there is no ligand)
“Inactive” form will be restored

Two Stages of Amplification Occur in the G-Protein-Mediated Hormone Reponse
First stage: a single hormone-receptor complex can activate many G proteins before the hormone dissociates from the receptor
Second stage: G α -activated adenylyl cyclase synthesizes many cAMP molecules
Thus binding of a hormone to a receptor stimulates a large increase in concentration of cAMP in the cell

Two Stages of Amplification Occur in the G-Protein-Mediated Hormone Reponse
The hormone receptor, G protein, and cyclase constitute a hormone signal transduction unit
G protein regulation may either be stimulatory or inhibitory – each receptor interacts both with stimulatory G s or inhibitory G i
G βγ also acts as a cell signal

IP3 as a Second Messenger: produced by phospholipid breakdown
A diverse array of second messengers is generated by breakdown of membrane phospholipids
Binding of some hormones to their receptors triggers activation of specific phospholipase C
Results in the breakdown of Inositol phospholipids to yield IP 3 and DAG ( diacylglycerol )

Neuronal Signaling and Pharmacodynamics

Ligand binding and neurosignaling
Neurotransmitters:
Small molecule ligands that mediate signaling
Bind to cell surface receptor, trigger desired effect
Communication between neurons or from neuron to “effector organ”

Neurotransmitters
A neurotransmitter is a chemical messenger that transmits nerve impulses from one neuron to another.
The space between the two neurons is called a synapse (AKA synaptic cleft) .
The presynaptic neuron releases the neuro -transmitter.
The postsynaptic neuron contains the receptors that bind the neurotransmitter.

Neurotransmitters
Once the neurotransmitter is bound to the receptor, the chemical message is delivered.
The transmitter is then degraded or returned to the presynaptic neuron to begin the process again.

Neurotransmitters : Acetylcholine
Acetylcholine is a quaternary ammonium ion
that serves as a neurotransmitter between neurons
and muscle cells .
Effect: excitation (nerves) or contraction (in muscles)
Activates cholinergic receptors

Acetylcholine degradation
Acetylcholine is quickly degraded by the enzyme acetylcholinesterase
Acetylcholinesterase serves a very important function: too much acetylcholine =  nerves/muscles unable to rebound from activation

Acetylcholinesterase mechanism
Acetylcholinesterase
active site contains a reactive serine that carries out neucleophilic attack on the carbonyl.
Histidine is important in removing H from serine -OH
Can you guess the mechanism?

18.8	Neurotransmitters C. Acetylcholine and Nicotine Addiction
At low concentrations, nicotine binds to acetyl-choline receptors (agonist), resulting in feelings of wellbeing and alertness .
This triggers other nerve cells to release dopamine , which increases the pleasure sensation.
A person becomes addicted to the pleasure sensation gained from taking in nicotine.
As the dopamine receptors deplete over time, a person will crave more nicotine to reproduce the “ high ” feelings.

Functional selectivity
“Whereas most endogenous agonists, such as serotonin, activate both G-protein and [other] pathways, some compounds can stabilize distinct receptor conformations, thereby preferentially activating select signal transduction pathways. This phenomenon has been termed ‘functional selectivity’ or ‘biased agonism ’ ”
from: Cell 168, 377–389, January 26, 2017
Instead of 2 states: (ligand-free: off and ligand-bound: on), there are multiple degrees of activation of a receptor
Each ligand induces a slightly different conformation, altering the interaction of the receptor with the intracellular components, resulting in drastically different physiological outputs

The plot thickens: olfactory receptors
Olfactory (smell) receptors:
A few hundred GPCRs
How can a limited number of receptors distinguish between a vast number of odors?
Key: specificity and cross reactivity
Each receptor can bind a large number of molecules with different affinities
Each molecule can bind to a number of receptors with different affinities
Result: multiple neurons triggered at different degrees in response for each odor

Olfactory sensing

Olfactory sensing
Additionally, different receptors have different affinities for the same odor molecule
At low concentration, the odor only triggers a few receptors
At high concentrations, more receptors are triggered
The same molecule can be perceived differently at different concentrations!
Humans: ~900 ORs, ~60% are pseudogenes
~ 9 million expressed in the olfactory bulb
Dogs: ~1500 ORs, ~18% are pseudogenes
~250 million expressed in the olfactory bulb

Part 5A: Proteomics Dr. Shahir Rizk

Proteomics
Proteomics is a term that refers to the large-scale study of proteins.
Typically how sequence relates to function
How proteins relate to each other within the same organism and across different species

Identification of proteins
Often times, an isolated protein can have unknown sequence, structure and/or function
Task of a researcher is to determine:
Sequence: can be compared to other proteins
Function: by comparison to proteins with similar sequence, followed by functional assays
Structure: more difficult, usually involves:
X-ray crystallography
NMR
Cryo-electron microscopy

Protein sequence
Each protein has a unique sequence
Sequence homology:
Proteins that serve the same function in different organisms can have similar sequence
Traditionally, proteins were isolated from organisms without knowledge of their sequence
Example: insulin was known to regulate blood sugar, but sequence was not known
Chemical and enzymatic methods can be used to sequence proteins

Protein sequencing
Older method: Edman degradation:
Chemical digestion of amino acids from the N-terminal, one-by-one
Each cleaved amino acids is analyzed to determine the sequence
Newer methods:
Fragmentation, followed by Mass spectrometry
DNA sequencing (if the gene is known)

Protein sequencing: Mass spec
Fragmentation:
Using proteases to digest the protein of interest
Most proteases have a preference on where to cut
Trypsin - cleavage on the C-side of Lys, Arg
Result is fragments of the protein ending with Lys or Arg
Chymotrypsin - C-side of Phe, Tyr, Trp
Result is fragments of the protein ending with Phe, Tyr or Trp

Enzymatic Fragmentation
Figure 5.10 (a) Trypsin is a protease that cleaves peptide bonds in which Arg or Lys contributes the carbonyl function.

Enzymatic Fragmentation
Figure 5.10 (b) The products of the reaction with trypsin are a mixture of peptide fragments with C-terminal Arg or Lys residues and a single peptide derived from the C-terminal end of the polypeptide.

Fragmentation
The result of fragmentation of the protein by trypsin is a set of peptide fragments that end with Lys or Arg
Each will have a unique sequence
Each will have a unique mass
Each protein has a unique combination of fragments with specific masses
Mass spectrometry can be used to determine the mass of the fragments very precisely

Amino Acid Sequence Can Be Determined by Mass Spectrometry
Mass spectrometry (MS) separates particles on the basis of mass-to-charge ratio
Two types of MS:
Electrospray ionization MS (ESI)
MALDI-TOF:
Matrix-assisted laser desorption, ionization, time of flight

MALDI-TOF
Sample of peptides is deposited on a surface
A powerful laser is used to ionize the sample (now it has a charge, typically +1
The charged samples travel through a vaccum tube towards the negative electrode
Smaller molecules travel faster
“time of flight” is
related to the
Mass/charge (M/z)

MALDI-TOF
Data obtained as peaks, each corresponding to a specific fragment
Use database to identify your protein based on the peptide fragment signature
Each protein has a unique MS profile

ESI MS
Sample of peptide fragments is sprayed to make very small droplets under a strong electric field
Fragments pick up positive charges and a mass detector is used to identify the M/z of each fragment
The data look like a set of sharp peaks, each corresponding to a mass/charge value for each fragment
Fragments are compared to a database to determine the identity of the protein based on its MS signature
Amino Acid Sequence Can Be Determined by Mass Spectrometry

Different M/z values for the same peptide
More charges = smaller M/z value

MS data analysis
All human genes have been sequenced as well as many mammals, prokaryotes, plants, fungi etc
Based on the genetic information (sequence), a computer can predict the MS signature of each protein when digested by a specific protease
By comparing the MS data for an unknown protein with the database of that organism, the protein can be identified based on its MS signature

MS: practical applications
Identifying disease causing mutations
2D gel electrophoresis of normal vs. patient serum
Note: one band is missing in the patient
Cut out the band from the gel
Use protease for fragmentation, MS analysis for identification
Normal
Patient

MS: practical applications
Identifying binding partners for your protein
What proteins interact with your protein of interest
Immobilize protein of interest on solid surface
Mix with cellular extract, or serum etc.
Proteins that bind protein of interest will be captured, identified by MS
MS
Pull-down Assay

5.5  What is the Nature of Amino Acid Sequences?
Sequences and composition reflect the function of the protein
Membrane proteins have stretches of hydrophobic residues, whereas fibrous proteins may have atypical sequences
Homologous proteins from different organisms have homologous sequences
e.g., cytochrome c is highly conserved

Phylogeny of Cytochrome c
The number of amino acid differences between two cytochrome c sequences is proportional to the phylogenetic difference between the species from which they are derived
This observation can be used to build phylogenetic trees of proteins
This is the basis for studies of molecular evolution

Related Proteins Show a Common Evolutionary Origin

Part 5B: Protein structure determination Dr. Shahir Rizk

The scale of things
Many techniques to view small objects
Proteins are in the 5-100 nm range
Individual bonds are in the 0.15 nm range (1.5 Å )

How can we see small objects?
Microscopy:
Light hits a sample, causing diffraction
A lens is used to refocus the light, producing a magnified image

The resolution limit
Light microscopy can be used to “resolve” objects within ~200 nm
Useful for Eukaryotic cells and organelles
Resolution is the ability to distinguish between 2 objects within a certain distance from each other
Why can’t light microscopy resolve anything below ~200 nm?
Resolution is limited by the wavelength of light used to resolve objects
d ≈ λ /2	d = distance between 2 objects (resolution)
Low d = high resolution

The resolution limit
Light microscopy is limited to ~ 200 nm, ideal for large cells, organelles
Unable to resolve smaller structures
Bacteria

Electron microscopy
Electron microscopy can resolve much smaller objects
Uses much shorter wavelength (~2 pm)
Higher resolution than light microscopy
Can see far more cellular detail
Resolution limit ~1nm

Electron microscopy
Instead of visible light, EM uses and electron beam
The beam diffracts when it hits the sample
Unlike light microscopy, beam is refocused by magnets
This limits the resolution to ~ 1 nm (10 Å )

EM
Here is an image of dividing bacteria using EM

Cryo -electron micrsocopy
EM can be used to view molecules as small as proteins with resolution down to ~ 5 Å
Cryo -EM is carried out at very low temperatures
Electron beam scans a very large number of several protein molecule on a surface
Each molecule is “seen” from a different angle
Computational analysis provides a 3D reconstruction of the shape of the molecule
Useful in looking at small viruses, large multi-protein complexes, not individual chemical bonds
Not “atomic resolution”

Cryo -EM
The obtained images show the general structure of proteins but without atomic detail
Can help show the conformation of a protein, but not enough to show individual bonds, amino acid side chains, or secondary structure elements

Cryo -EM
Example: changes in the structure of the dengue virus at different pH

Atomic resolution
To get the full structure of a protein, we need to get to atomic resolution: ~ 1.5 A (carbon-carbon bond)

X-ray crystallography
A protein crystal is shot with an x-ray beam, causing the beam to diffract
A diffraction pattern is unique to the protein structure
A technique that allows atomic resolution
Most protein structures have been determined using x-ray crystallography

X-ray crystallography
Advantages:
X-ray beam wavelength ~ 1.5 Å
Can have resolution down to ~ 0.7 Å
Can provide atomic resolution, resolve individual bonds
Provides the “electron density” around atoms

X-ray crystallography limitations
Crystallization:
Not enough data can be obtained from a single protein molecule
Protein sample must be in crystal form. Proteins are arranged in repeating units to provide useful signal
Crystallization can be difficult, time consuming to find the right conditions
Some proteins don’t crystallize!

X-ray crystallography limitations
Scientists must screen a large number of conditions to force a protein into crystal formation
Protein must be extremely pure, highly concentrated
Risk: precipitation
Crystallization conditions may disrupt protein structure (different pH, salt concentration, etc )

X-ray crystallography limitations
2. Data processing (phasing)
Once a crystal forms, and x-ray beam is used to obtain a diffraction pattern
Unlike light microscopy, there is no material known that can act as a lens to refocus the x-rays
Computational analysis must be used to generate a model of the molecule that fits the diffraction pattern
Instead of an image of the molecule, we obtain a model of the molecule
Light microscopy vs. x-ray crystallography

Results of x-ray crystallography
Electron density of the atoms in the molecule
Since the amino acid sequence of the protein is known, the sequence is “threaded” into the electron density

Resolution
X-ray crystallography can resolve down to 0.7Å
Not all crystals give such high resolution
Imperfections in crystal lattice arrangements
Small movements within the crystals (blurry)
Example: phenylalanine ring

Cryo -EM and crystal structures
Cryo -EM can image relatively large mutliprotein complexes
These can be very difficult to crystallize
Combining crystal structures of individual proteins with cryo -EM data can give information on how proteins come together to form large structures
Example: Dengue virus coat structure

Cryo -EM and crystal structures
Combining data from x-ray crystallography and cryo -EM can give information on conformational changes
Example: ATP synthase
Optional:
Read the article here [https://elifesciences.org/articles/10180]

Protein NMR
NMR spectroscopy:
how the nuclei of certain atoms interact with the magnetic component of electromagnetic waves
Chemical shits indicate the environment around the nucleus of an atom, connectivity to other atoms
1 H NMR: useful for identifying small organic molecules
Proteins are very large
Contain thousands of
protons
Hard to decipher

2D NMR
15 N is an NMR active isotope of Nitrogen (not 14 N)
2D NMR combines the spectrum from 1 H & 15 N
This can simplify the assignment of the individual peaks to the individual amino acids
Based on the combined chemical shifts, a model of the structure of the protein can be built

2D NMR advantages
Can provide atomic resolution
Does not require crystallization
Molecules are free in solution
This provides “solution structure”
Capture different conformations (as the molecule “wiggles” in solution, not locked in crystal)

2D NMR limitations
Limited to small proteins
Small proteins or fragments of proteins
Largest structure is ~ 50kDa
The larger the magnet, the larger the molecular resolution
Typically large facilities are needed
Protein must be “labeled” with 15 N isotope
Express protein is bacteria supplemented with 15 N labeled amino acids (expensive)

Protein structure determination
Each technique has its own advantages and disadvantages
To study a system, often many techniques are used to tackle to problem from different angles
Each technique can provide a unique “view” of a molecular system
In combination, can provide a fuller picture of biological molecules

Carbohydrates

Carbohydrates
Carbohydrates are synthesized in green plants by photosynthesis , whereby energy from the sun is stored as chemical energy in carbohydrates.
In the body, they are used for bursts of energy needed during exercise in the form of glucose.

Monosaccharides
Monosaccharides , the simplest carbohydrates, generally have 3 to 6 C atoms in a chain with an aldehyde or ketone ending and many –OH groups.
Aldehyde monosaccharides are aldoses ; ketone monosaccharides are ketoses .

Monosaccharides
The simplest aldose is glyceraldehyde .
The simplest ketose is dihydroxyacetone .
They are constitutional isomers of each other, sharing the formula C 3 H 6 O 3 .
They are intermediate products of the breakdown of glucose

Monosaccharides
A monosaccharide is characterized by the number
of C atoms in its chain:
A triose has 3 C’ s.
A tetrose has 4 C’ s.
A pentose has 5 C’ s.
A hexose has 6 C’ s.
The terms are then combined with the words aldose
and ketose:
Glyceraldehyde is an aldotriose .
Dihydroxyacetone is a ketotriose .

Glyceraldehyde, the simplest aldose, has one chirality center, and has two possible enantiomers .

The prefix L is used when the –OH group is drawn on the left side of the carbon chain.

Monosaccharides B. More than One Chirality Center
All naturally occurring sugars are D sugars.

The Cyclic Forms of Monosaccharides
The C atom that is part of the hemiacetal is a new chirality center , called the anomeric carbon.

The Cyclic Forms of Monosaccharides A. The Cyclic Forms of D -Glucose
To cyclize D -glucose, first we must determine which alcohol to use to make a six-membered ring.

The Cyclic Forms of Monosaccharides The Cyclic Forms of D -Glucose
The first step in cyclization is to rotate glucose 90 o .

The Cyclic Forms of Monosaccharides A. The Cyclic Forms of D -Glucose
Next, the chain must be twisted around, forming a six-membered ring:

The Cyclic Forms of Monosaccharides A. The Cyclic Forms of D -Glucose
As the reaction occurs, there are two cyclic forms of D -glucose, an a anomer and a b anomer .
These rings are called Haworth projections .

The cyclization reaction exists in an equilibrium called mutarotation .

A new chirality center is created ( anomeric Carbon )

Anomeric Carbon can be α or β
The cyclization reaction exists in an equilibrium called mutarotation .

Focus on Health & Medicine A. Lactose Intolerance
Lactose is the disaccharide in milk; it consists of 1 galactose ring and 1 glucose ring joined by a 1 4- b -glycosidic bond .

Focus on Health & Medicine A. Lactose Intolerance
The disaccharide bond is cleaved by the enzyme lactase in the body.
Individuals who are lactose intolerant have low levels of this enzyme.
Without the enzyme , lactose cannot be digested , causing abdominal cramps and diarrhea.

Lactase mechanism
Lactase catalysis the hydrolysis of the disaccharide
Initial nucleophilic attack carried out by carboxylic acid side chain, resulting in covalent-enzyme intermediate known as galactosyl -enzyme intermed .
Water breaks the intermediate to release galactose

Focus on Health & Medicine B. Sucrose and Artificial Sweeteners
Sucrose (table sugar) is a disaccharide consisting of 1 glucose ring and 1 fructose ring .
Sucrose is very sweet, but contains many calories .
To reduce caloric intake, many artificial sweeteners have been developed.
Is the glycosidic bond in sucrose alpha or beta?
Since both anomeric carbons are connected, it’s alpha-1 on the glucose and beta-2 on the fructose

Focus on Health & Medicine B. Sucrose and Artificial Sweeteners
Aspartame (sold as Equal): Not actually a sugar, but has a very sweet taste

Focus on Health & Medicine B. Sucrose and Artificial Sweeteners
Saccharine (sold at Sweet’ n Low) was used extensively during World War I.

Focus on Health & Medicine B. Sucrose and Artificial Sweeteners
Sucralose (sold as Splenda ) has a very similar structure to sucrose.
500X sweeter than sucrose. Cannot be broken down by the body.

Polysaccharides A. Cellulose
Polysaccharides contain three or more monosaccharides joined together.
Cellulose is an unbranched polymer made up of repeating glucose units joined by 1 4- b -glycosidic linkages .
Cellulose is found in the cell walls of all plants, where it gives support and rigidity to wood, plant stems, and grass.
Humans do not posses the enzyme to hydrolyze cellulose ( b -glycosidase) and cannot digest it .

Polysaccharides A. Cellulose
Cellulose makes up the insoluble fiber .

Polysaccharides B. Starch
Starch is a polymer made up of repeating glucose units joined by - glycosodic linkages .
Starch is present in corn , rice , wheat , and potatoes .
The first main type of starch is amylose :

Polysaccharides B. Starch
The second type of starch is amylopectin .

Polysaccharides B. Starch
Amylose is an unbranched polymer linked by 1 4- a -glycosidic linkages .
Amylopectin is a branched polymer linked by 1 4- a and 16- a -glycosidic linkages .
Both starch molecules can be digested by humans using the enzyme amylase .

Polysaccharides C. Glycogen
Glycogen is the major form of polysaccharide storage in animals , similar in structure to amylopectin.
It is stored mainly in the liver and in muscle cells.
When glucose is needed for energy , glucose units are hydrolyzed from the ends of the glycogen polymer.
Because glycogen is highly branched , there are many ends available for hydrolysis.

Fed state: (right after a meal)
Liver takes up glucose from the blood to prevent a spike in glucose level
Glucose is converted into glycogen in the muscle and in the liver for short-term storage
Fasting state: (no food for ~ 6-12 hrs )
Muscle glycogen is used locally by the muscles
Liver glycogen in broken down into glucose, which is released into the blood to maintain normal glucose levels

Reducing sugars
A reducing sugar is a sugar than can reduce another molecule, while in the process becoming oxidized
Only sugars with a free anomeric carbon are reducing sugars
Reducing sugars are sugars with free anomeric carbons - they will reduce oxidizing agents, such as peroxide and some metals (Cu 2+ and Ag + ).
The sugar is oxidized in the process

Glucose is a reducing sugar
Glucose can reduce Cu 2+ to Cu +
The reaction produces red precipitate, can be used to detect the presence of glucose (reducing sugars)

Polysaccharides as reducing sugars
Amylose and cellulose are both reducing polysaccharides
Each contains a “reducing end”: exposed anomeric Carbon

Disaccharides
Not all disaccharides are reducing sugars
Lactose, maltose and cellobiose are all reducing
Sucrose is non-reducing: does not contain a free anomeric carbon

Endergonic vs. Exergonic
Endergonic:
Free energy absorbed during the process
Free energy: (ΔG) is positive
Exergonic:
Free energy released during the process
Free energy: (ΔG) is negative
ΔG = -RT ln K eq

Endergonic vs. Exergonic
Endergonic:
Process is non-spontaneous
Exergonic:
Process is spontaneous
The more negative the free energy, the more spontaneous the process will be.

Endergonic vs. Exergonic
Question:
Reaction L has a ΔG of -2.5 kcal/ mol , whereas reaction M has a ΔG of +2.5 kcal/mol.
L is spontaneous, energy is absorbed
M is spontaneous, energy is released
L is non-spontaneous, energy is released
M is spontaneous, energy is absorbed
L is spontaneous, energy is released

Endergonic vs. Exergonic
Question:
Reaction L has a ΔG of -5 kcal/ mol , whereas reaction M has a ΔG of -2.5 kcal/mol. Which reaction will have the faster rate?
There is not enough information to know about the rate. ΔG does not tell you anything about the reaction rate

Endothermic vs. Exothermic
Endothermic:
Heat energy absorbed during the process
Enthalpy: (ΔH) is positive
Exothermic:
Heat energy released during the process
Enthalpy: (ΔH) is negative
ΔG = ΔH - TΔS
Are all exothermic processes exergonic?
No, must consider entropy

Enthalpy vs. Entropy
ΔH: the amount of heat added to a system at constant pressure
This is useful, since pressure changes are not common in biological systems
If heat is added, ΔH is positive, process is endothermic
Entropy (S): the number of degrees of freedom within a system
If the number of degrees of freedom increases, ΔS is positive, process: “ entropically favorable ”
entropy is constantly increasing in the universe

The Pathways of Catabolism Converge to a Few End Products
Figure 17.7 The three stages of catabolism. Stage 1 : Proteins, polysaccharides, and lipids are broken down into their component building blocks. Stage 2 : The building blocks are degraded into the common product, the acetyl groups of acetyl-CoA. Stage 3 : Catabolism converges to three principal end products: water, carbon dioxide, and ammonia.

ATP Serves in a Cellular Energy Cycle
ATP is the energy currency of cells
Phototrophs transform light energy into the chemical energy of ATP
In heterotrophs, catabolism produces ATP, which drives activities of cells
ATP cycle carries energy from photosynthesis or catabolism to the energy-requiring processes of cells
Non-spontaneous reactions (positive ΔG) can be carried out by coupling to ATP hydrolysis
ATP and Energy Production

ATP and Energy Production A. General Features of ATP Hydrolysis
Hydrolysis of ATP cleaves 1 phosphate group.
This forms ADP and phosphate ( HPO 4 2− ),
releasing 7.3 kcal/mol of energy.
ENERGY

ATP and Energy Production B. General Features of ATP Phosphorylation
Phosphorylation is the reverse reaction, where a phosphate group is added to ADP , forming ATP requiring 7.3 kcal/mol of energy.
ENERGY

ATP and Energy Production A. General Features of ATP Phosphorylation
Any process (walking, running, breathing) is fueled by the release of energy when ATP is hydrolyzed to ADP.
Energy is absorbed and stored in ATP when it is synthesized from ADP.
