Coenzymes in Metabolism A. Coenzymes NAD + and NADH
NAD is a coenzyme acting as an oxidizing agent causes an oxidation reaction to occur, so the coenzyme is reduced .
NADH is a coenzyme acting as a reducing agent causes a reduction reaction to occur, so the coenzyme is oxidized
When a coenzyme acts as an oxidizing agent, it gains e − in the form of Hydrogen (NAD  NADH )
When a coenzyme acts as a reducing agent, it loses e − in the form of Hydrogen ( NADH  NAD)

NADPH Provides the Reducing Power for Anabolic Processes
Whereas catabolism is oxidative, anabolism is reductive
Biosynthesis typically relies on reducing equivalents  from NADPH
NADPH can be viewed as the carrier of electrons from catabolic reactions to anabolic reactions
In photosynthesis, light energy is used to pull electrons from water and transfer them to NADP +
O 2 is a by-product of this process

Reaction 7: Phosphoglycerate Kinase
ATP synthesis from a high-energy phosphate
Phosphoglycerate kinase transfers a phosphoryl group from 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate to ADP to form an ATP.
This is referred to as “ substrate-level phosphorylation ”
This reaction “ pays off ” the ATP debt created by the priming reactions in the first phase
2,3-BPG (for hemoglobin) is made by circumventing the PGK reaction (Figure 18.15)

Reaction 7: Phosphoglycerate Kinase
Phosphoglycerate kinase transfers a phosphoryl group from 1,3-bisphosphoglycerate to ADP to form ATP.  This type of ATP-synthesizing reaction is referred to as a “ substrate-level phosphorylation ”

G3P-DH  is the site of action of arsenate
Arsenate is a substrate for the G3P-DH reaction, forming
1-arseno-3-phosphoglycerate.  This product breaks down to
3-phosphoglycerate, essentially bypassing the phosphoglycerate kinase reaction.  The result is that glycolysis in the presence of arsenate produces no net ATP.

2,3-BPG is made by reactions that detour around the phosphoglycerate kinase reaction
2,3-bisphosphoglycerate is an important regulator of hemoglobin
2,3-BPG is formed from 1,3-BPG by bisphosphoglycerate mutase
3-phosphoglycerate is then formed by 2,3-bisphosphoglycerate phosphatase
Most cells contain only a trace of 2,3-BPG, but erythrocytes typically contain 4-5 mM 2,3-BPG

Reaction 10: Pyruvate Kinase
Conversion of PEP to pyruvate makes ATP

PK is allosterically inhibited by ATP (high energy) and acetyl-CoA (feedback inhibition)

Reaction 10: Pyruvate Kinase
The pyruvate kinase reaction converts PEP to pyruvate, driving synthesis of ATP.  Note that two ATP are produced here, since two PEP are formed from one glucose.  These two ATP are the “ payoff ” of glycolysis.

Citrate Synthase

C α of the acetyl group in acetyl-CoA is acidic and can be deprotonated to form a carbanion
This carbanion is a strong nucleophile that can attack the α -carbonyl of oxaloacetate, yielding citryl-CoA
Thioester hydrolysis then produces citrate

The citrate synthase synthase reaction initiates the TCA cycle

19.4  How Is Oxaloacetate Regenerated to Complete the TCA Cycle?
Hydrolysis of succinyl-CoA (a CoA ester) drives the phosphorylation of GDP to produce GTP

19.4  How Is Oxaloacetate Regenerated to Complete the TCA Cycle?
Succinyl-CoA Synthetase Catalyzes
a Substrate-Level Phosphorylation
A nucleoside triphosphate is made
This is possible because succinyl-CoA is a high-energy intermediate
Its hydrolysis (the hydrolysis of a CoA ester) drives the phosphorylation of GDP to produce GTP

Succinyl-CoA Syn. mechanism
The mechanism of the succinyl -CoA synthetase reaction.
Phosphate anion carries out a nucleophilic attack on the carbonyl C of succinyl -CoA, forming succinyl phosphate ( phosphorolysis )
Subsequent phosphoryl transfer to an active-site His forms a phosphohistidine intermediate, releasing the product succinate
The phosphoryl group is then transferred to GDP to produce GTP

Completion of the TCA Cycle – Oxidation of Succinate to Oxaloacetate
This process involves a series of three reactions that recycles oxaloacetate to restart the cycle
Rearranging one 4C unit into another
Succinate to oxaloacetate
These reactions include:
Oxidation of a single bond to a double bond
FAD is reduced to FADH2
Hydration across the double bond
Oxidation of the resulting alcohol to a ketone
NAD reduced to NADH

Final 3 steps
1. Succinate conversion to Fumarate is used to reduce FAD to FADH2
2. Water is added to fumarate (alkene) to form Malate
3. Malate conversion to oxaloacetate is used to reduce NAD to NADH
Oxaloacetate is recycled, NADH, FADH2 are produced (more energy)

Succinate Dehydrogenase Is FAD-Dependent
An FAD-dependent oxidation of a single bond to a double bond
The mechanism involves hydride removal by FAD and a deprotonation
This enzyme is actually part of the electron transport pathway in the inner mitochondrial membrane
The electrons transferred from succinate to FAD (to form FADH 2 ) are passed directly to ubiquinone (UQ) in the electron transport pathway
The Succinate Dehydrogenase Reaction

Fumarase Catalyzes the trans -Hydration of Fumarate to Form L -Malate
Hydration occurs across the newly formed double bond
Hydration involves trans -addition of the elements of water across the double bond
The actual mechanism is not known for certain

Electron Transfer Energy Stored in a Proton Gradient: The Mitchell Hypothesis
The coupling between oxidation and phosphorylation was a mystery for many years
Many biochemists squandered careers searching for the elusive "high energy intermediate"
Peter Mitchell proposed a novel idea - a proton gradient across the inner membrane could be used to drive ATP synthesis
The proton gradient is created by the proteins of the electron-transport pathway
Mitchell was ridiculed, but the chemiosmotic hypothesis eventually won him a Nobel prize

Electron Transfer Energy Stored in a Proton Gradient: The Mitchell Hypothesis

ATP Synthase is Composed of F 1 and F 0
Figure 20.20 The ATP synthase, a rotating molecular motor.  The c , γ and ε subunits constitute the rotating portion (the rotor) of the motor. The b, d and h subunits form a long, slender stalk that connects F 0 in the membrane and F 1 . Flow of protons from the a -subunit through the c -subunits turns the rotor and drives the cycle of conformation changes in the α - and β -subunits of F 1 that synthesize ATP.

The Catalytic Sites of ATP Synthase Adopt Three Different Conformations
Alternating α and β subunits with 1 γ subunit (purple) extends through the center of the hexamer .
As the γ subunit rotates, each α / β dimer adopts a different confomration
Each conformation is a different step in ATP fromation

Staphylococcal Enterotoxin C3
Steven Frye
ESQPDPMPDDLHKSSEFTGTMGNMKYLYDDHYVSATKVKSVDKFLAHDLIYNINDKKLNNYDKVKTELLNEDLANKYKDEVVDVYGSNYYVNCYFSSKDNVGKVTSGKTCMY GGITKHEGNHFD NGNLQNVLIRVYENKRNTISFEVQTDKKSVTAQELDIKARNFLINKKNLYEFNSSPYETGYIKFIESNGNTFWYDMMPAPGDKFDQSKYLMIYKDNKMVDSKSVKIEVHLTTKNG
Staphylococcal enterotoxin C (SEC) contains roughly six variants or subgroups which include SEC3 (PDB: 1CK1) [2 ]. SEC’s cause a variety of diseases and are one of the most routine causes of food poisoning [2] . SEC3 contains two domains, one large and one small. These domains work together to bind Zinc via interactions of the side groups including two Histidine residues and an Aspartic acid residue [1] . Zinc is also responsible for mediating homodimerization of SEC3 via interactions with an aspartic acid residue on another monomer unit [1] . Zinc’s role in SECs appears to be minimal. Zinc does force the protein into a specific conformation which reflects its receptor binding capabilities, but Zinc is thought to exist as a non-functional heavy atom, except for dimerization which also appears to have little to no effect on functionality [1]. SECs are quite potent when referring to their toxicity. 10µL/kg causes emesis in 100% of animals tested [1 ]. Even nanogram quantities cause symptoms such as nausea, emesis, and headaches.

Endergonic vs. Exergonic
Endergonic:
Free energy absorbed during the process
Free energy: (ΔG) is positive
Exergonic:
Free energy released during the process
Free energy: (ΔG) is negative
ΔG = -RT ln (Q)
What is Q?
What is Q at equilibrium?

Endergonic vs. Exergonic
Endergonic:
Process is non-spontaneous
Exergonic:
Process is spontaneous
The more negative the free energy, the more spontaneous the process will be.

Endothermic vs. Exothermic
Endothermic:
Heat energy absorbed during the process
Enthalpy: (ΔH) is positive
Exothermic:
Heat energy released during the process
Enthalpy: (ΔH) is negative
ΔG = ΔH - TΔS
Are all exothermic processes exergonic?
No, must consider entropy

Enthalpy vs. Entropy
ΔH: the amount of heat added to a system at constant pressure
This is useful, since pressure changes are not common in biological systems
If heat is added, ΔH is positive, process is endothermic
Entropy (S): the number of degrees of freedom within a system
If the number of degrees of freedom increases, ΔS is positive, process: “ entropically favorable ”
entropy is constantly increasing in the universe

The Pathways of Catabolism Converge to a Few End Products
Figure 17.7 The three stages of catabolism. Stage 1 : Proteins, polysaccharides, and lipids are broken down into their component building blocks. Stage 2 : The building blocks are degraded into the common product, the acetyl groups of acetyl-CoA. Stage 3 : Catabolism converges to three principal end products: water, carbon dioxide, and ammonia.

ATP Serves in a Cellular Energy Cycle
ATP is the energy currency of cells
Phototrophs transform light energy into the chemical energy of ATP
In heterotrophs, catabolism produces ATP, which drives activities of cells
ATP cycle carries energy from photosynthesis or catabolism to the energy-requiring processes of cells
Non-spontaneous reactions (positive ΔG) can be carried out by coupling to ATP hydrolysis

Electron carriers FAD and NAD are reduced to FADH2 and NADH, shuttle electrons to the ETC
NADH and FADH2 donate electrons to oxygen to make H 2 O

The Urea Cycle (nitrogen excretion)
Metabolic Pathways are Compartmentalized Within Cells

Locations of biochemical processes in the cell
The location in the cell | The biochemical process
Cytosol | Glycolysis, pentose phosphate shunt, FA synthesis and glycogen synthesis
Mitochondrial Matrix | β-oxidation of FAs and TCA cycle
Inner mitochondrial membrane | Oxidative phosphorylation
Both cytosol and mitochondria | Gluconeogenesis, urea cycle and heme synthesis

17.6  What Food Substances Form the Basis of Human Nutrition?
Protein is a rich source of nitrogen and also provides essential amino acids
Carbohydrates provide needed energy and essential components for nucleotides and nucleic acids
Lipids provide essential fatty acids that are key components of membranes and also important signal molecules
Fiber – whether soluble or insoluble – can be a beneficial component in the human diet
Part 5A: Proteomics Dr. Shahir Rizk

Proteomics
Proteomics is a term that refers to the large-scale study of proteins.
Typically how sequence relates to function
How proteins relate to each other within the same organism and across different species

Identification of proteins
Often times, an isolated protein can have unknown sequence, structure and/or function
Task of a researcher is to determine:
Sequence: can be compared to other proteins
Function: by comparison to proteins with similar sequence, followed by functional assays
Structure: more difficult, usually involves:
X-ray crystallography
NMR
Cryo-electron microscopy

Protein sequence
Each protein has a unique sequence
Sequence homology:
Proteins that serve the same function in different organisms can have similar sequence
Traditionally, proteins were isolated from organisms without knowledge of their sequence
Example: insulin was known to regulate blood sugar, but sequence was not known
Chemical and enzymatic methods can be used to sequence proteins

Protein sequencing
Older method: Edman degradation:
Chemical digestion of amino acids from the N-terminal, one-by-one
Each cleaved amino acids is analyzed to determine the sequence
Newer methods:
Fragmentation, followed by Mass spectrometry
DNA sequencing (if the gene is known)

Protein sequencing: Mass spec
Fragmentation:
Using proteases to digest the protein of interest
Most proteases have a preference on where to cut
Trypsin - cleavage on the C-side of Lys, Arg
Result is fragments of the protein ending with Lys or Arg
Chymotrypsin - C-side of Phe, Tyr, Trp
Result is fragments of the protein ending with Phe, Tyr or Trp

Enzymatic Fragmentation
Figure 5.10 (a) Trypsin is a protease that cleaves peptide bonds in which Arg or Lys contributes the carbonyl function.

Enzymatic Fragmentation
Figure 5.10 (b) The products of the reaction with trypsin are a mixture of peptide fragments with C-terminal Arg or Lys residues and a single peptide derived from the C-terminal end of the polypeptide.

Fragmentation
The result of fragmentation of the protein by trypsin is a set of peptide fragments that end with Lys or Arg
Each will have a unique sequence
Each will have a unique mass
Each protein has a unique combination of fragments with specific masses
Mass spectrometry can be used to determine the mass of the fragments very precisely

Amino Acid Sequence Can Be Determined by Mass Spectrometry
Mass spectrometry (MS) separates particles on the basis of mass-to-charge ratio
Two types of MS:
Electrospray ionization MS (ESI)
MALDI-TOF:
Matrix-assisted laser desorption, ionization, time of flight

MALDI-TOF
Sample of peptides is deposited on a surface
A powerful laser is used to ionize the sample (now it has a charge, typically +1
The charged samples travel through a vaccum tube towards the negative electrode
Smaller molecules travel faster
“time of flight” is
related to the
Mass/charge (M/z)

MALDI-TOF
Data obtained as peaks, each corresponding to a specific fragment
Use database to identify your protein based on the peptide fragment signature
Each protein has a unique MS profile

ESI MS
Sample of peptide fragments is sprayed to make very small droplets under a strong electric field
Fragments pick up positive charges and a mass detector is used to identify the M/z of each fragment
The data look like a set of sharp peaks, each corresponding to a mass/charge value for each fragment
Fragments are compared to a database to determine the identity of the protein based on its MS signature
Amino Acid Sequence Can Be Determined by Mass Spectrometry

Different M/z values for the same peptide
More charges = smaller M/z value

MS data analysis
All human genes have been sequenced as well as many mammals, prokaryotes, plants, fungi etc
Based on the genetic information (sequence), a computer can predict the MS signature of each protein when digested by a specific protease
By comparing the MS data for an unknown protein with the database of that organism, the protein can be identified based on its MS signature

MS: practical applications
Identifying disease causing mutations
2D gel electrophoresis of normal vs. patient serum
Note: one band is missing in the patient
Cut out the band from the gel
Use protease for fragmentation, MS analysis for identification
Normal
Patient

MS: practical applications
Identifying binding partners for your protein
What proteins interact with your protein of interest
Immobilize protein of interest on solid surface
Mix with cellular extract, or serum etc.
Proteins that bind protein of interest will be captured, identified by MS
MS
Pull-down Assay

5.5  What is the Nature of Amino Acid Sequences?
Sequences and composition reflect the function of the protein
Membrane proteins have stretches of hydrophobic residues, whereas fibrous proteins may have atypical sequences
Homologous proteins from different organisms have homologous sequences
e.g., cytochrome c is highly conserved

Phylogeny of Cytochrome c
The number of amino acid differences between two cytochrome c sequences is proportional to the phylogenetic difference between the species from which they are derived
This observation can be used to build phylogenetic trees of proteins
This is the basis for studies of molecular evolution

Related Proteins Show a Common Evolutionary Origin
Figure 5.20  This phylogenetic tree depicts the evolutionary relationships among organisms as determined by the similarity of their cytochrome c sequences.

conserved amino acids

We can use data bases to search for similar proteins
If the similar proteins have a known function, that provides a starting point for guessing the function of our protein

Part 1B: Enzyme regulation

Chapter 15
Metabolic regulation is achieved through an exquisitely balanced interplay among enzymes and small molecules.
The name of the game is “Regulation”

15.1 – What Factors Influence Enzymatic Activity?
The availability of substrates and cofactors usually determines how fast the reaction goes
As product accumulates, the apparent rate of the enzymatic reaction will decrease
Genetic regulation of enzyme synthesis and decay determines the amount of enzyme present at any moment
Enzyme activity can be regulated allosterically
Enzyme activity can be regulated through covalent modification
Zymogens, isozymes, and modulator proteins may play a role

15.1 – What Factors Influence Enzymatic Activity?
Enzyme regulation by reversible covalent modification:
Example: phosphorylation
Usually activates a protein
Carried out by kinase
Dephosphorylation (typically deactivating) by phosphatase

15.4 What Kinds of Covalent Modification Regulate the Activity of Enzymes?
Enzyme activity can be regulated through reversible phosphorylation
This is the most prominent form of covalent modification in cellular regulation
Phosphorylation is accomplished by protein kinases
Each protein kinase targets specific proteins for phosphorylation
Phosphoprotein phosphatases catalyze the reverse reaction – removing phosphoryl groups from proteins
Kinases and phosphatases themselves are targets of regulation
Phosphorylation Cascade: hormone response

15.1 – Covalent modification of enzymes
Acetylation:
Addition of acetyl group, usually at lysine
Can you predict what the product looks like?

Acetylation in Enzyme Regulation
Acetylation is a prominent modification for the regulation of metabolic enzymes
Acetylation of an ε-NH 3 + group on a Lys residue changes it from a positively charged amino group to a neutral amide
This change may have consequences for protein structure and thus function
The acetylating enzyme is termed an acetyl-CoA-dependent lysine acetyltransferase or KAT
More than 30 KATs are known in mammals
Deacetylation by KDACs (lysine deacetylases) reverse the effects of acetylation

15.1 – Protein processing: cleavage
Figure 15.2  Proinsulin is an 86-residue precursor to insulin
Zymogens are inactive precursors of enzymes.  Typically, proteolytic cleavage produces the active enzyme.
Figure 15.3 The proteolytic activation of chymotrypsinogen
Proteolytic Enzymes of the Digestive Tract

Isozymes Are Enzymes With Slightly Different Subunits
lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) has 2 isozymes: A and B
A is more active, B is less active
By mixing different proportions of A and B, result in a range of activities

Regulation by small molecules
Product inhibition:
Accumulation of the product of an enzyme inhibits the reaction
Usually the product has some affinity for the active site
At high [product], can act as competitive inhibitor

Regulation by small molecules
Feedforward activation:
A substrate of an early stage activates an enzyme at a later stage
Feedback inhibition:
A product several steps later in a process inhibits an early step in the process

Regulation by small molecules
Cooperativity:
Substrate binding within multiple interacting subunits of an enzyme
Allostery:
A regulatory molecule binds far away from the active site to modulate enzyme activity

Cooperativity
Enzymes with multiple interacting subunits
The binding of substrate at one subunit affects the binding of the substrate at another subunit
If binding at site 1 enhances binding at site 2:
Positive cooperativity
If binding at site 1 decreases binding at site 2:
Negative cooperativity
Result: Non-Machaelis-Menton Kinetics

15.2 – Cooperativity
The shape of the curve is different based on the type of cooperativity
Positive cooperativity is sigmoidal
Negative and No cooperativity are hyperbolic

15.2 –Allosteric Regulation
Action at "another site"
Enzymes situated at key steps in metabolic pathways are modulated by allosteric effectors
Allosteric effectors are non-substrate molecules that bind far away from the active site (at a regulatory site)

15.2 –Allosteric Regulation
Action at "another site"
These effectors are usually produced elsewhere in the pathway
Effectors may be feed-forward activators or feedback inhibitors

Allosteric Regulation
allosteric effector changes the conformation (structure) of the enzyme
Shifting the equilibrium to a different form
Different structure = different function
Example: glycogen phosphorylase
Allosteric
Activator
T-state (Taut):
Less active
R-state (relaxed):
More active

GP converts glycogen into readily usable fuel in the form of glucose-1-phosphate
Do you expect GP to be active after a meal or before a meal?
Before a meal (fasting state): low glucose, low insulin, low ATP
High AMP

Glycogen Phosphorylase Activity is Regulated Allosterically
Muscle glycogen phosphorylase shows cooperativity in substrate binding
ATP and glucose-6-P are allosteric inhibitors of glycogen phosphorylase
AMP is an allosteric activator of glycogen phosphorylase
When ATP and glucose-6-P are abundant, glycogen breakdown is inhibited
When cellular energy reserves are low ( i.e ., high [AMP] and low [ATP] and [G-6-P]) glycogen catabolism is stimulated

Glycogen Phosphorylase Activity is Regulated Allosterically
rate versus S curves for glycogen phosphorylase.
The response to the concentration of the substrate phosphate (P i ).
ATP is a feedback inhibitor.
AMP is a positive effector.  It binds at the same site as ATP.
Note that the allosteric effector shifts the curve to the right
(inhibitor) or the left (activator)

Glycogen Phosphorylase Activity is Regulated Allosterically
What is the effect of AMP (activator) on the apparent Km of the enzyme for the substrate?
Lower Km = higher activity at lower [S]

Glycogen phosphorylase conforms to the MWC model
The active form of the enzyme is designated the R state
The inactive form of the enzyme is denoted the T state
AMP promotes the conversion to the active state
ATP, glucose-6-P, and caffeine favor conversion to the inactive T state
A significant conformation change occurs at the subunit interface between the T and R state
This conformational change at the interface is linked to a structural change at the active site that affects catalysis

Glycogen Phosphorylase is Controlled by Both Allosteric Regulation and Covalent Modification
Figure 15.16  The mechanism of covalent modification and allosteric regulation of glycogen phosphorylase.

Regulation of enzymes (review)
The very catalytic proficiency for which enzymes have evolved means that their activity must not be allowed to run amok
Activity is regulated in many ways:
Genetically
Substrate availability
Allostery
Post-translational modification
Protein-protein interactions

Kinetics of allosteric enzymes
Generally these don ’ t obey Michaelis-Menten kinetics
Homotropic positive effectors produce sigmoidal (S-shaped) kinetics curves rather than hyperbolae
Homotropic : same molecule regulation
e. g. the fact that the binding of the first substrate accelerates binding of second and later ones

T  R State transitions
Many allosteric effectors influence the equilibrium between two conformations
One is typically more rigid and inactive, the other is more flexible and active
The rigid one is typically called the “ tight ” or “ T ” state; the flexible one is called the “ relaxed ” or “ R ” state
Allosteric effectors shift the equilibrium toward R (positive) or toward T (negative)

Models for cooperativity
MWC model:
Monod, Wyman, Changeux
“Concerted” model (all or nothing)
KNF model:
Koshland , Nemethy , Filmer
“sequential” model (ligand-induced conformation change)

MWC model
Assumptions:
2 or more identical subunits, each with 1 binding site
2 conformational states (R and T) with different affinity for S
Conformation change occurs in a “concerted” manner, meaning all subunits will be R or T at any given time
Equilibrium shifts to R form as [S] goes up
Can only explain positive cooperativity

KNF model
Assumptions:
At least 2 conformational states per subunit
Only the subunit bound to the ligand changes its conformation
Conformation change at one subunit alters interactions with other subunits
The change in interaction can induce stronger or weaker ligand binding
Can explain positive and negative cooperativity

Part II: Regulation of metabolism A. Glycolysis

Regulation of Glycolysis
It is the main pathway for carbohydrate metabolism (glucose, fructose, galactose )
All the reactions of glycolysis occur in the cytoplasm
Glycolysis is an anaerobic process and can produce ATP in the absence of O 2
The first step is transporting glucose into the cell from the blood stream

Glycolysis
Glucose transport into the cell is mediated by glucose transport proteins (GLUT-1 to GLUT-5)
GLUT-1 : RBCs, Brain, testicles and retina
GLUT-2 : liver, kidney, pancreas, intestine
In pancreas, glucose binding  insulin release
GLUT-3 : Brain
GLUT-4 : Fatty tissue, skeletal and cardiac
GLUT-5 : (fructose) intestine and sperms
Know that glucose is transported into the cell by facilitated diffusion a form of passive transport facilitated by transport proteins.
NO ENERGY NEEDED

Insulin stimulates glucose uptake by translocating GLUT- 4 protein to the plasma membrane (in muscles and adipose tissues)

Part 3A: Gluconeogenesis and its regulation Chapter 22

The Substrates for Gluconeogenesis Include Pyruvate, Lactate, and Amino Acids
Pyruvate, lactate, glycerol, amino acids and all TCA intermediates can be utilized
Fatty acids cannot
Why?
Most fatty acids yield only acetyl-CoA
Acetyl-CoA is not a substrate for synthesis of sugars

Features of Gluconeogenesis
Occurs mainly in liver and kidneys
Not the mere reversal of glycolysis for 2 reasons:
1)  The Δ G of glycolysis is -74 kJ/mol
If gluconeogenesis were just the reverse of glycolysis, its Δ G would be positive
Energetics must change to make gluconeogenesis favorable
2)  Reciprocal regulation of glycolysis and  gluconeogenesis:
When glycolysis is active, gluconeogenesis is turned off, and when gluconeogenesis is proceeding, glycolysis is turned off.

Reverse of glycolysis

1. Pyruvate to PEP
Intermediate: oxaloacetate
Energy from GTP produced in TCA can be used here
2. Fruc 1,6 bis-P → Fruc 6-P
3. Glucose 6 P to Glucose

Gluconeogenesis - Something Borrowed, Something New
Three steps are replaced:
Pyruvate carboxylase and PEP carboxykinase replace the pyruvate kinase reaction of glycolysis
Fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase replaces the phosphofructokinase reaction of glycolysis
Glucose-6-phosphatase replaces the hexokinase reaction of glycolysis
The new reactions provide for a spontaneous pathway ( D G negative in the direction of sugar synthesis), and they provide new mechanisms of regulation

Pyruvate Carboxylase is a Biotin-Dependent Enzyme
Pyruvate is converted to oxaloacetate
The reaction requires ATP and bicarbonate as substrates
Acetyl-CoA is an allosteric activator
Regulation: when ATP or acetyl-CoA are high, pyruvate enters gluconeogenesis
Note this takes place in the mitochondria

Pyruvate Carboxylase is a Biotin-Dependent Enzyme
The pyruvate carboxylase reaction is the initiation of the gluconeogenesis pathway
Carboxylations that use bicarbonate as the carbon source require biotin as a coenzyme

Pyruvate Carboxylase is a Biotin-Dependent Enzyme
In most organisms, pyruvate carboxylase is a homotetramer of 130 kD subunits, with each subunit composed of three functional domains. This is the biotin carboxylase domain of pyruvate carboxylase from B. thermodenitrificans.

Pyruvate carboxylase is a compartmentalized reaction.
Figure 22.4  Pyruvate is converted to oxaloacetate in the mitochondria.  Because oxaloacetate cannot be transported across the mitochondrial membrane, it must be reduced to malate, which is then transported to the cytosol and oxidized back to oxaloacetate before gluconeogenesis can continue.

Types of Glycogen
Liver glycogen:
For regulation of blood glucose level during fasting state (4 – 6 hrs after meal)
Muscle glycogen
Reserve for muscle activity not released into blood (used locally)
Both types are chemically identical

Metabolism of Tissue Glycogen is Regulated
Digestive breakdown of starch is unregulated -  nearly 100% of ingested food is absorbed and metabolized
But tissue glycogen is an important energy reservoir - its breakdown is carefully controlled
Glycogen phosphorylase cleaves glucose from the nonreducing ends of glycogen molecules
This is a phosphorolysis , not a hydrolysis: product is Glucose-phosphate, plugs directly into glycolysis

22.5 How Is Glycogen Metabolism Controlled?
A highly regulated process, involving reciprocal control of glycogen phosphorylase and glycogen synthase
GP allosterically activated by AMP and inhibited by ATP, glucose-6-P and caffeine
GS is stimulated by glucose-6-P
Both enzymes are regulated by covalent modification - phosphorylation

Glycogen Synthase is Regulated by Covalent Modification
Glycogen synthase exists in two distinct forms
Active, dephosphorylated GS-I
Less active, phosphorylated GS-D
The phosphorylated form is allosterically activated by glucose-6-P
At least 9 serine residues are phosphorylated
Four different protein kinases are involved
Dephosphorylation is carried out by phosphoprotein phosphatase-1 (PP1)
PP1 inactivates glycogen phosphorylase and activates glycogen synthase

Insulin Modulates the Action of Glycogen Synthase in Several Ways
Binding of insulin to plasma membrane receptors in the liver and muscles triggers protein kinase cascades that stimulate glycogen synthesis
Insulin ’ s effect include stimulation of lipid synthesis, glycogen synthesis, protein synthesis, glycolysis, and active transport, and inhibition of gluconeogenesis and lipid breakdown
Glucose uptake provides substrate for glycogen synthesis and glucose-6-P, which allosterically activates the otherwise inactive form of glycogen synthase

Utilization of Glucose-6-P Depends on the Cell ’ s Need for ATP, NADPH, and Rib-5-P
Glucose can be a substrate either for glycolysis or for the pentose phosphate pathway

Part 4A: Fatty Acid Catabolism

Why Are Fatty Acids Used by Organisms for Energy Storage?
Two reasons:
The carbon in fatty acids (mostly -CH 2 -) is reduced (so its oxidation yields the most energy possible).
Fatty acids are not hydrated (as mono- and polysaccharides are), so they can pack more closely in storage tissues

23.1 How Are Fats Mobilized from  Dietary Intake and Adipose Tissue?
Most of the “ fats ” in diet and in adipose tissue are triglycerides (triacylglycerols)
Triglycerides represent the major energy input in the modern American diet
Triglycerides are also the major form of stored energy in the body
Hormones (glucagon, epinephrine) trigger the release of fatty acids from adipose tissue

Triacylglycerols
Triacylglycerols (triglycerides) are three esters formed from glycerol and three molecules of fatty acids .

FA breakdown

CoA activates FAs for oxidation
Acyl-CoA synthetase condenses fatty acids with CoA, with simultaneous hydrolysis of ATP to AMP and PP i
Formation of a CoA ester is expensive energetically
Reaction just barely breaks even with ATP hydrolysis
But subsequent hydrolysis of PP i drives the reaction strongly forward

Carnitine as a Carrier
Carnitine carries fatty acyl groups across the mitochondrial membranes
Short-chain fatty acids are carried directly into the mitochondrial matrix
Long-chain fatty acids cannot be directly transported into the matrix
Instead, long-chain FAs are converted to acyl-carnitines and are then transported into the mitochondria
Acyl-CoA esters are formed inside the inner mito membrane from transported acylcarnitines

Carnitine Carries Acyl Groups Across the Inner Mitochondrial Membrane
The process involves the coordinated actions of carnitine acyltransferases on both the inner and outer mitochondrial membranes and a translocase that shuttles O-acylcarnitines across the inner membrane.

23.2 How Are Fatty Acids Broken Down?
Fatty Acid-CoA is degraded by removal of 2-C units at a time
this occurs in the mitochondria
the 2-C unit released is acetyl-CoA, not free acetate
The process begins with oxidation of the carbon that is " β " to the carboxyl carbon, so the process is called " β -oxidation"

 -Oxidation of Fatty Acids
A Repeated Sequence of 4 Reactions
Strategy: create a carbonyl group at the b -C
First 3 reactions do that; fourth cleaves the 	“ b -keto ester" in a reverse Claisen 	condensation
Products: an acetyl-CoA and a fatty acid two carbons shorter
The first three reactions are crucial and classic - we will see them again in other pathways
Figure 23.9 The β -oxidation of saturated fatty acids involves a cycle of four enzyme-catalyzed reactions.

23.2 How Are Fatty Acids Broken Down?
Figure 23.13 The mechanism of acyl-CoA dehydrogenase. Removal of a proton from the α -carbon is followed by hydride transfer from the β -carbon to FAD.

23.2 How Are Fatty Acids Broken Down?
Enoyl-CoA Hydratase Adds Water Across the Double Bond
The next step is the addition of the elements of H 2 O across the new double bond in a stereospecific manner, yielding the corresponding hydoxyacyl-CoA.  The reaction is catalyzed by enoyl-CoA hydratase , also called crotonase . These enzymes convert trans -enoyl-CoA derivatives to L - β -hydroxyacyl-CoA.

23.2 How Are Fatty Acids Broken Down?
L -Hydroxyacyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Oxidizes the β -Hydroxyl Group
The third reaction of this cycle is the oxidation of the hydroxyl group at the β -position to produce a β -ketoacyl-CoA derivative.  This second oxidation reaction is catalyzed by L -hydroxyacyl-CoA dehydrogenase , an enzyme that requires NAD + as a coenzyme.  NADH produced in this reaction represents metabolic energy.  Each NADH produced by this reaction in mammalian mitochondria drives the synthesis of 2.7 ATP.

The Fourth Reaction of β -Oxidation : Thiolase
Aka b -ketothiolase
Cysteine thiolate on enzyme attacks the b -carbonyl group
Thiol group of a new CoA attacks the shortened chain, forming a new, shorter acyl-CoA

23.2 How Are Fatty Acids Broken Down?
Figure 23.15  Attack by an enzyme Cys thiolate group at the β -carbonyl carbon produces a tetrahedral intermediate, which decomposes with departure of acetyl-CoA, leaving an enzyme thioester intermediate. Attack by the thiol group of a second CoA yields a new acyl-CoA.

Summary of β -Oxidation
Repetition of the cycle yields a succession of acetate units
For example: palmitic acid (16 C long) yields eight acetyl-CoAs
Complete b -oxidation of one palmitic acid yields 106 molecules of ATP
Large energy yield is a consequence of the highly reduced state of the carbon in fatty acids
This (and their low density) makes fatty acids the fuel of choice for migratory birds and many other animals

Regulation of FA degradation
Hormone-sensitive lipase (pancreatic lipase):
Activated by glucagon and epinephrin
General activators:
Low energy levels (AMP, glucagon)
General inhibitors:
High energy levels (ATP)
Insulin
Remember: glycerol is glucogenic, Acetyl-CoA is not

Exercise Can Reverse the Consequences of Metabolic Syndrome
Metabolic syndrome is a combination of disorders that increase the risk of diabetes and cardiovascular disease
Insights into how the body deals with high-fat and sugar diets are emerging from a variety of studies
Endurance training increases the mass of slow-twitch muscle, whereas resistance-training builds fast-twitch muscle fibers
Slow-twitch muscles depend on fatty acid oxidation and TCA cycle activity and are “ oxidative ”
Fast-twitch muscles are adapted for short bursts of energy from glycolysis and are “ glycolytic ”

Formation of Malonyl-CoA Activates Acetate Units for Fatty Acid Synthesis
The design strategy for fatty acid synthesis:
First acetyl - CoA is transported to the cytoplasm through the citrate shuttle
the acetate (2C) units are activated by addition of CO2 to form malonyl -CoA (3C) by acetyl CoA carboxylase (ACC)

Fatty Acid Synthesis
in bacteria catalyzed by 6 different enzymes plus a separate acyl carrier protein (ACP)
in mammals catalyzed by individual domains of a very large polypeptide that includes an ACP domain.
Evolution of the mammalian Fatty Acid Synthase apparently has involved gene fusion .
The condensation reaction (step 3) involves decarboxylation of the malonyl moiety, followed by attack of the resultant carbanion on the carbonyl carbon of the acetyl (or acyl) moiety .

The b -ketone is reduced to an alcohol by e - transfer from NADPH.
Dehydration yields a trans double bond.
Reduction by NADPH yields a saturated chain.
Following transfer of the growing fatty acid from phosphopantetheine to the Condensing Enzyme's cysteine sulfhydryl, the cycle begins again , with another malonyl-CoA.

Product release:
When the fatty acid is 16 carbon atoms long, a Thioesterase domain catalyzes hydrolysis of the thioester linking the fatty acid to phosphopantetheine .
The 16-C saturated fatty acid palmitate is the final product of the Fatty Acid Synthase complex.

Regulation of HMG-CoA Reductase
Three different mechanisms contribute to the regulation of HMG- CoA reductase
Phosphorylation by kinase inactivates HMG-CoA reductase .  This inactivation can be reversed by phosphatases
Degradation of HMG- CoA reductase .  This enzyme has a half-life of only 3 hours, and the half-life itself depends on cholesterol levels:  High [cholesterol] means a short half-life for HMG- CoA reductase
Gene expression.  Cholesterol levels control the amount of mRNA.  If [cholesterol] is high, levels of mRNA coding for the reductase are reduced

The conversion of mevalonate to squalene
Don ’ t worry about the details of the reactions
Remember that squalene is an important intermediate

Squalene Is Synthesized from Mevalonate
Squalene is a long branched Carbon chain with a precise arrangement of double bonds
The double bonds can be used to form new C-C bonds

Cholesterol is Formed from Squalene – After Many Steps
At the endoplasmic reticulum membrane
Squalene monooxygenase converts squalene to squalene-2,3-epoxide
A cyclase converts the epoxide to lanosterol
20 more steps are required to form cholesterol
All at/in the endoplasmic reticulum membrane

Steroid Hormones
A hormone is a molecule that is synthesized in one part of an organism, which then elicits a response at a different site.
Two important classes of steroid hormones include sex hormones and adrenal cortical steroids .
The female sex hormones are estrogens and progestins .
The male sex hormones are called androgens .

The estrogens estradiol and estrone control development of secondary sex characteristics, regulate the menstrual cycle, and are made in the ovaries.
Steroid Hormones Female Sex Hormones: Estrogens

Steroid Hormones Female Sex Hormones: Progestin
The progestin progesterone is called the “ pregnancy hormone ” ; it is responsible for the  preparation of the uterus for implantation of a  fertilized egg.

Steroid Hormones Male Sex Hormones: Androgens
Testosterone and androsterone are androgens made in the testes.
They control the development of secondary sex characteristics in males .

Synthetic androgens
Synthetic androgen analogues, called anabolic steroids , promote muscle growth.
They have the same effect as testosterone , but are more stable, so they are not metabolized as quickly.
They have come to be used by athletes and body builders , but are not permitted in competitive  sports.
Prolonged use of anabolic steroids can cause physical and psychological problems .
Synthetic steroids

aldosterone
cortisone
cortisol

Steroid Hormones Adrenal Cortical Steroids
Aldosterone regulates blood pressure and volume by controlling the concentration of Na + and K + in body fluids.
Cortisone and cortisol serve as anti-inflammatory agents, which also regulate carbohydrate  metabolism.
Prednisone , a synthetic alternative, has similar anti-inflammatory properties.

Part 5: Nitrogen Metabolism

How Do the Various Organic Forms of Nitrogen Arise?
Nitrogen in organic compounds is predominantly in a reduced state
But the prevalent forms of nitrogen in the environment are inorganic and oxidized:
N 2 (dinitrogen gas)
NO 3 - (nitrate anion)
The two principal routes for nitrogen acquisition are nitrate assimilation and nitrogen fixation
Both these lead to formation of ammonium ions
Reactions that incorporate ammonium ion into organic molecules follow

25.1 Which Metabolic Pathways Allow Organisms to Live on Inorganic Forms of Nitrogen?
Nitrogen is cycled between organisms and the inanimate environment
Nitrate assimilation – the reduction of nitrate to NH 4 + in plants, various fungi, and certain bacteria, in a two-step metabolic pathway
Nitrogen fixation – the formation of NH 4 + from N 2 gas
Organisms release nitrogen either as NH 4 + or as organic compounds such as urea
Bacteria return these to the environment by oxidizing them

Nitrate Assimilation
The two-electron reduction of nitrate to nitrite, catalyzed by nitrate reductase
The six-electron reduction of nitrite to ammonium, catalyzed by nitrite reductase
Nitrate assimilation is the predominant means by which green plants, algae, and many microorganisms acquire nitrogen
Nitrate assimilation accounts for more than 99% of the inorganic nitrogen assimilated into organisms
Nitrate (fertilizer/manure) comes from the breakdown of organic matter in the soil

Nitrogen fixation (N 2 from thin air)
Soybean plants can meet their nitrogen requirements both by assimilating nitrate and, in symbiosis with bacteria, fixing N 2 .

Nitrogen Fixation
Only occurs in certain prokaryotes
Rhizobia fix nitrogen in symbiotic association with leguminous plants
Rhizobia fix N for the plant and the plant provides Rhizobia with carbon substrates
Nitrogen-fixing systems require:
nitrogenase , ATP, O 2 -free conditions
Regulatory controls:
ADP inhibits nitrogenase
NH 4 + inhibits expression of nif genes

Nitrogen Fixation
Nitrogen-fixing by Nitrogenase
Process is spontaneous but has a very high activation energy
Reduction of one Nitrogen molecule to 2 ammonia molecules requires 12 ATPs
Once ammonia is produced, it can be incorporated into organic molecules

21.1 What Are the General Properties of Photosynthesis?
General Aspects
Photosynthesis occurs in thylakoid membranes of chloroplasts - structures involving paired folds (lamellae) that stack to form " grana "
The soluble portion of the chloroplast is the " stroma "
The interior of the thylakoid vesicles is the " thylakoid space " or "thylakoid lumen"
Chloroplasts possess DNA, RNA and ribosomes

Photosynthesis Consists of Both Light Reactions and Dark Reactions
The light reactions , associated with the thylakoid membranes, capture light energy and convert it to chemical energy in the form of reducing potential (NADPH) and ATP, with evolution of oxygen
The dark reactions , located in the stroma , use NADPH and ATP to drive the endergonic process of hexose sugar formation from CO 2 in a series of reactions in the stroma

Photosynthesis Consists of Both Light Reactions and Dark Reactions

A More Generalized Equation for Photosynthesis :
CO 2 +    2H 2 A →      (CH 2 O)   +    2A       +     H 2 O
Hydrogen  Hydrogen          Reduced    Oxidized
acceptor    donor                 acceptor     donor
In photosynthetic bacteria,H 2 A can be H 2 S or other oxidizable substrates, like isopropanol:

21.2 How Is Solar Energy Captured by Chlorophyll?
Chlorophyll is a photoreactive , isoprene-based pigment

21.2 How Is Solar Energy Captured by Chlorophyll?
Chlorophyll is a photoreactive, isoprene-based pigment
A planar, conjugated ring system - similar to porphyrins
Mg 2+ is coordinated in the center of the planar conjugated ring structure
A long chain alcohol, phytol , group confers membrane solubility
Aromaticity makes chlorophyll an efficient absorber of light
Light absorption promotes an electron to a higher orbital, enhancing the potential for transfer of this electron to a suitable acceptor

21.2 How Is Solar Energy Captured by Chlorophyll?
Figure 21.5 Structures (a) and absorption spectra (b) of chlorophyll a and b . The phytyl side chain of ring IV provides a hydrophobic tail to anchor the chlorophyll in membrane protein complexes.
Since the absorption spectra for a and b differ, plants that possess both can harvest a wider spectrum of incident energy.

21.2 How Is Solar Energy Captured by Chlorophyll?
Figure 21.5 (a)
Space-filling model of chlorophyll a

The Light Energy Absorbed by Photosynthetic Pigments Has Several Possible Fates
Figure 21.7 Possible fates of the quantum of light energy absorbed by photosynthetic pigments.

The Light Energy Absorbed by Photosynthetic Pigments Has Several Possible Fates
Each photon represents a quantum of energy
Each quantum of light energy has 4 possible fates:
1) Loss as heat .  Energy can be dissipated as heat through redistribution into atomic vibrations within the pigment molecule.
2) Loss as light . A light-excited electron can return to a lower orbital, emitting a photon of fluorescence.
3) Resonance energy transfer . Excitation energy can be transferred to a neighboring molecule.
4) Energy transduction . The excitation energy changes the reduction potential of the pigment, making it an effective electron donor.

Transduction of Light Energy into Chemical Energy Involves Oxidation-Reduction
The basis of photosynthesis is transduction of light energy into chemical energy (of oxidation-reduction).
Photon absorption raises chlorophyll (Chl) to Chl*
Electron transfer from Chl* to an adjacent molecule A, producing oxidized Chl (Chl • + ) and reduced A (A - )
Oxidation of A - eventually culminates in reduction of NADP + to NADPH
The system is restored to its original state once NADPH is formed and water is oxidized

Transduction of Light Energy into Chemical Energy Involves Oxidation-Reduction
Figure 21.8 Model for light absorption by chlorophyll and transduction of light energy into an oxidation-reduction reaction. The process culminates in production of NADPH and oxidation of water.

Photosynthetic Units Consist of Many Chlorophyll Molecules but Only a Single Reaction Center
The photosynthetic unit consists of several hundred light-capturing chlorophylls plus a pair of special chlorophylls in the reaction center
Light is captured by one of the "antenna chlorophylls" and routed from one to the other until it reaches the reaction center chlorophyll dimer that is photochemically active

Photosynthetic Units Consist of Many Chlorophyll Molecules But Only a Single Reaction Center
Figure 21.9 Schematic diagram of a photosynthetic unit.

21.3 What Kinds of Photosystems Are Used to Capture Light Energy?
Oxygenic phototrophs have two distinct photosystems: PSI (P700) and PSII (P680)
PSI has a maximal red light absorption at 700 nm and use ferredoxins as terminal electron acceptors
PSII has a maximal red absorption at 680 nm and use quinones as terminal electron acceptors
Chloroplasts given light at 680 and 700 nm simultaneously yield more O 2 than the sum of amounts when each is used alone
All chlorophyll is protein-bound – as part of either PSI or PSII or light-harvesting complexes (LHCs)

PSI and PSII Participate in the Overall Process of Photosynthesis
What do PSI and PSII do?
PSI provides reducing power in the form of NADPH
PSII splits water, producing O 2 , and feeds the electrons released into an electron transport chain that couples PSII to PSI via cytochrome b 6 f
Electron transfer between PSII and PSI pumps protons for chemiosmotic ATP synthesis
Essentially, electrons flow from H 2 O to NADP + , driven by light energy absorbed at the reaction centers
Light-driven phosphorylation of ADP to make ATP is termed photophosphorylation

The Pathway of Photosynthetic Electron Transfer Is Called the Z Scheme
The electron carriers are arranged as a chain, according to their standard reduction potentials
Such an arrangement resembles the letter “ Z ”
Thus the pathway name – the Z scheme
Its components:
PQ = plastoquinone
PC = plastocyanin
"F"s = ferredoxins
A o = a special chlorophyll a
A 1 = a special PSI quinone
Cytochrome b 6 /cytochrome f complex is a proton pump

The Z Scheme
Figure 21.11 The Z scheme of photosynthesis. (a) A diagrammatic representation. (b) The functional relationships among PSI, PSII, the cyt b 6 f complex, and the ATP synthase in the thylakoid membrane.
The Z Scheme
The Z Scheme

21.6 How Does Light Drive the Synthesis of ATP?
Photophosphorylation
The transduction of the electrochemical gradient into the chemical energy of ATP is carried out by the chloroplast ATP synthase, more properly called the CF 1 CF 0 -ATP synthase
Electron transfer through the proteins of the Z scheme drives the generation of a proton gradient across the thylakoid membrane
Protons pumped into the lumen of the thylakoids flow back out, driving the synthesis of ATP
CF 1 -CF o ATP synthase is similar to the mitochondrial ATP synthase

c -ring of CF 1 -CF 0 -ATP synthase
Figure 21.21
The c-subunit assembly of spinach chloroplast ATP synthase consists of 14 identical subunits.

How Does PSII Generate O 2 From H 2 O?
The oxidation of H 2 O to O 2 is chemically difficult
The oxygen-evolving complex (OEC) is a large globular protein domain on the lumenal side of PSII
The active site of the OEC contains a cube-like metal cluster of four Mn ions, one Ca ion, and five O atoms bridging the Mn atoms.
Electron removal from H 2 O molecules at the cluster (one from each Mn ) facilitates O 2 formation

How Does PSII Generate O 2 From H 2 O?
Figure 21.17 Structure of the PSII oxygen-evolving complex (OEC).  Four Mn atoms (red, A-D) and a Ca atom (green) form the water-splitting metal cluster of the OEC.  Bridging O atoms are purple.

21.5 What Is the Quantum Yield of Photosynthesis?
The quantum yield of photosynthesis is defined as the amount of product formed per equivalent of light input
i.e., the amount of O 2 evolved per photon
Four photons per reaction center - 8 total - drive the evolution of 1 O 2 , reduction of 2 NADP + , and the translocation of 14 H +
Chloroplast ATP synthase has 14 c -subunits, so 3 ATPs are formed for every 14 H + translocated
So 3 ATP should be synthesized from an input of 8 photons of light
Part 7B: Photosynthesis The Dark Reaction

21.7 How Is Carbon Dioxide Used to Make Organic Molecules?
Fixation of CO 2 in plants, algae, etc.
Melvin Calvin at Berkeley in 1945 showed that Chlorella could take up 14 CO 2 and produce 3-phosphoglycerate
What was actually happening was that CO 2 was combining with a 5-C sugar to form a 6-C intermediate
The 6-C intermediate breaks down to two 3-P-glycerates
Ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate is the CO 2 acceptor in CO 2 fixation

Ribulose-1,5-Bisphosphate
The CO 2 Acceptor
Fixation is accomplished by ribulose bisphosphate carboxylase ( oxygenase ), aka rubisco
Probably the world's most abundant protein

The Calvin-Benson Cycle
aka The Calvin Cycle
CO2 + NADPH + ATP  3-P-glycerate
The set of reactions that transform 3-P-glycerate into hexose sugar
The principal CO 2 fixation pathway in nature
A large part of this pathway is really a disguised gluconeogenesis pathway!
With some pentose phosphate pathway reactions thrown in

The free energy of cleavage of ~P bonds of ATP , and reducing power of NADPH , are used to fix and reduce CO 2 to form carbohydrate .
Enzymes & intermediates of the Calvin Cycle are located in the chloroplast stroma , a compartment somewhat analogous to the mitochondrial matrix.
The Calvin cycle

Ribulose Bisphosphate Carboxylase ( RuBP Carboxylase), catalyzes CO 2 fixation:
ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate + CO 2  2 X 3- phosphoglycerate
Rubisco

Ribulose-1,5-Bisphosphate is the CO 2 Acceptor in CO 2 Fixation
Figure 21.23 Molecular graphic of ribulose bisphosphate carboxylase. The enzyme consists of 8 equivalents each of two subunits, designated L (large) and S (small).  Clusters of four S subunits (orange and red) are located at each end of the symmetric octamer formed by the L subunits (light and dark green).  The active sites are revealed in the ribbon diagram by bound ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate (yellow).

Ribulose-1,5-Bisphosphate is the CO 2 Acceptor in CO 2 Fixation
Figure 21.24 The ribulose bisphosphate carboxylase reaction.  Mg 2+ at the active site aids in stabilizing the 2,3-enediol transition state (I) for CO 2 addition and in facilitating the carbon-carbon bond cleavage that leads to product formation.

Ribulose-1,5-Bisphosphate is the CO 2 Acceptor in CO 2 Fixation
Figure 21.24 The ribulose bisphosphate carboxylase reaction.  Mg 2+ at the active site aids in stabilizing the 2,3-enediol transition state (I) for CO 2 addition and in facilitating the carbon-carbon bond cleavage that leads to product formation.

RuBP Carboxylase - postulated mechanism:
Extraction of H + from C3 of ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate promotes formation of an enediolate intermediate .
Nucleophilic attack on CO 2 leads to formation of a b - keto acid intermediate , that reacts with water and cleaves to form 2 molecules of 3-phosphoglycerate .

Calvin cycle: step 1 CO 2 fixation
3 CO2 + 3 RuBP  6 3-PG

Calvin cycle step 2: reduction
6 3-PG + 6NADPH + 6ATP  6 G3P + 6NADP + 6ADP

Step 2: 3PG to G3P
1 st enzyme makes 1,3 bisphosphoglycerate
(requires ATP)
2 nd enzyme requires NADPH
(comes from the light reaction)
The result is glyceraldehyde 3 phopshate , which is a substrate for gluconeogenesis

Calvin cycle step 3: remaking RuBP
Step 2 generates 6 X 3C units (6XG3P)
Only 5 are needed to make 3 Ribulose5-P
3 ATPs needed to convert R-5P to RuBP
1 G3P molecule is left, can be used to make glucose

Calvin cycle step 3: remaking RuBP
Each 6 CO2 molecules can generate a net of 2X G3P molecules, which combine to make 1 glucose molecule

Regulation of Calvin Cycle
Although it’s called the dark reaction
Regulation prevents the Calvin Cycle from being active in the dark , when it might function in a futile cycle with Glycolysis & Pentose Phosphate Pathway, wasting ATP & NADPH.
Light activates , or dark inhibits, the Calvin Cycle in several ways.

Light-activated e - transfer is linked to pumping of H + into thylakoid disks. pH in the stroma increases to about 8.
Alkaline pH activates stromal Calvin Cycle enzymes
Regulation by Light.

The Carbon Dioxide Fixation Pathway Is Indirectly Activated by Light

Part 9A: Introduction to signal transduction

32.1 What Are Hormones?
A molecule produced by one organ (a gland), released into the bloodstream
Binds to another organ (effector organ) to regulate physiology or behavior
U ltimately hormones regulate gene expression
A neurotransmitter is a molecule released from a Neuron , received by another neuron or an effector organ (muscle or gland, etc.)

32.1 What Are Hormones?
Many different chemical species act as hormones
Steroid hormones , derived from cholesterol, regulate metabolism, salt and water balance, inflammatory responses, and sexual function
Amino acid derivatives , such as epinephrine, which regulates smooth muscle function, blood pressure, cardiac rate, and more, and the thyroid hormones (which stimulate metabolism)
Peptide hormones regulate processes in all tissues, including release of yet other hormones
Hormones bind with very high affinity to their receptors – with K D values of 10 -12 to 10 -6 M

Steroid Hormones Act in Two Ways
The steroid hormones exert their effects in two ways :
By entering cells and migrating to the nucleus, steroid hormones act as transcription factors, modulating gene expression. These effects occur on time scales of hours and involve synthesis of new proteins
Remember that Steroids can go right through the 	membrane

Steroid Hormones Act in Two Ways
The steroid hormones exert their effects in two ways :
2. By acting at the membrane (either inside or outside of the cell), steroid hormones regulate ligand -gated ion channels and perhaps other processes . These processes occur very rapidly, on time scales of seconds and minutes
Nonsteroid hormones bind exclusively to plasma membrane receptors

Steroid Hormones Act in Two Ways
Figure 32.2 Nonsteroid hormones act exclusively at the plasma membrane. Steroid hormones may act either at the nucleus or at intracellular or extracellular plasma membrane receptors.

Steroid hormone example:
Estrogen receptor is intracellular
Estrogen goes through the membrane
Binding to receptor causes translocation into the nucleus, where
the estrogen-estrogen receptor complex acts as a transcription factor for gene expression

Polypeptide Hormones Share Similarities of Synthesis and Processing
Polypeptide hormones are typically synthesized from mRNA as inactive precursors (termed preprohormones ) and then activated by proteolysis
A single preprohormone may produce several  different peptide hormones

Polypeptide Hormones Share Similarities of Synthesis and Processing
An example of many products from a single precursor is prepro-opiomelanocortin , a 250-residue precursor polypeptide synthesized in the pituitary gland
A cascade of steps produces a natural opiate ( endorphin ) and several other hormones
Endorphins and other opiate-like hormones are produced by the body in response to stress and may contribute to the “ runner ’ s high ”

Polypeptide Hormones Share Similarities of Synthesis and Processing
Figure 32.3 The conversion of prepro-opiomelanocortin to a family of peptide hormones, including corticotropin, β - and γ -lipotropin, α - and β -MSH, and endorphin. Numbers of residues in each segment are shown.

Insulin processing
Produced as pre- proinsulin
Signal peptide is cleaved to make proinsulin
C-peptide is removed to make active form
Structure is stabilized by disulfide bonds

32.2 What is Signal Transduction?
Signal transduction consists of a stepwise progression of signaling stages:
receptor → transducers → effectors
The receptor perceives the signal, transducers relay the signal, and effectors convert the signal into an intracellular response

Signaling Pathways Depend on Multiple Molecular Interactions
Each step in a signaling pathway depends on one or more molecular interactions
A signaling protein may bind to a small molecule, a peptide, or another protein
Many signaling proteins are constructed in a cassette-like fashion with several distinct modules, termed protein interaction domains (PIDs) , each of which mediates a particular interaction
A protein with multiple modules can interact with several binding targets at once, to create a complex called a signalsome , with multiple signaling capabilities

Signaling Pathways Depend on Multiple Molecular Interactions
Figure 32.6 (a) Many signaling proteins consist of combinations of  protein modules, each with a specific binding or enzymatic function.
This is most likely the result of gene fusion. The result is a receptor can “react” to many different “signals” and produce different outcomes.
Figure 32.5 Protein modules in cell signaling pathways.

Signaling Pathways Depend on Multiple Molecular Interactions
Figure 32.6 (b) Such multifunctional proteins can act as scaffolds that direct the assembly of large signaling complexes, termed signalsomes.

32.4 How Are Receptor Signals Transduced?
Receptor signals are transduced in three ways:
Receptor -mediated activation of phosphorylation cascades that trigger activation of various enzymes
Exchange of GDP for GTP by GTP-binding proteins (G proteins), which leads to generation of second messengers ( cAMP , lipid breakdown products, Ca 2+ )
Conformation changes that open ion channels or recruit proteins into nuclear transcription complexes
The Membrane Receptor Superfamilies

Single transmembrane receptors
Receptor tyrosine kinases/ guanylyl cyclases comprise a “super-family”
Composition:
Extracellular domain (can be composed of multiple ligand binding domains)
A single transmembrane domain
Intracellular domain , typically having a kinase or guanylyl cyclase enzymatic function
Figure 32.4 A complete signal transduction pathway. (top part)
Figure 32.4 A complete signal transduction pathway. (bottom part)

Single TMS Receptors Are Guanylyl Cyclases or Tyrosine Kinases
Single TMS receptors possess an external ligand recognition site and an internal enzymatic domain – either a receptor tyrosine kinase (RTK) or a receptor guanylyl cyclase (RGC)
Each of these activities is found in two forms
Guanylyl cyclase activity is found in both membrane-bound receptors and in soluble, cytoplasmic proteins
Tyrosine kinase activity is exhibited by two different types of membrane protein
RTKs are integral membrane proteins
Non-RTKs are peripheral, lipid-anchored proteins

Single TMS Receptors Are Guanylyl Cyclases or Tyrosine Kinases
As shown in Figure 32.9, the EGF receptor and the insulin receptor are receptor tyrosine kinases
EGF receptors are activated by ligand-induced dimerization
The insulin receptor is an α 2 β 2 tetramer stabilized by disulfide bonds
receptor Ret is a receptor tyrosine kinase that mediates the effects of neuronal growth factors known as neurotrophins .

Ligand-Induced Receptor Oligomerization can Occur in Several Ways
Figure 32.10 (a) EGF receptor dimerization and activation involves binding of hormone to the extracellular domains of two receptor molecules.

EGF Receptor is Activated by Ligand-Induced Dimerization
Human EGF is a 53-amino acid polypeptide that stimulates proliferation of epithelial cells
Human EGF receptor is an 1186-amino acid RTK
The extracellular domain is divided into 4 domains
Prior to EGF binding, EGF receptors exist as inactive monomers in the plasma membrane
EGF binding induces a conformation change that rotates domains I and II
Dimerization of the extracellular domains brings the intracellular domains together, activating the tyrosine kinase activity

The EGF Tyrosine Kinase is Regulated by Phosphorylation
Figure 32.13 Hormone-induced dimerization of the EGF receptor promotes autophosphorylation of five tyrosine residues near the C-terminus of each EGF receptor.

Ligand-Induced Receptor Oligomerization can Occur in Several Ways
Figure 32.10 (b) The insulin receptor is a preexisting tetramer.  Two insulin-binding sites are created by association of the extracellular domains.

The Insulin Receptor Mediates Several Signaling Pathways
Insulin, a small heterodimeric peptide, is the most potent anabolic hormone known
The insulin receptor is an RTK that catalyzes the phosphorylation of tyrosine residues of several intracellular substrates
Each of these phosphorylated substrates binds a particular family of proteins containing SH2 domains
Each of these signaling pathways can be confined to distinct cellular locations and can proceed with a different time course, thus providing spatial and temporal dimensions to insulin action in cells

The Insulin Receptor Mediates Several Signaling Pathways
Note: the insulin receptor is not made of one continuous polypeptide chain like EGFR
Two subunits (X2) are linked via disulfide bonds

The Insulin Receptor Adopts a Folded Dimeric Structure in the Membrane
Figure 32.15  Each of two insulin-binding sites is created by the L1 and L2 domains of one α -chain and the Fn2 domain of the other α / β chain pair

Autophosphorylation of the Insulin Receptor Kinase Opens the Active Site
Binding of insulin to its receptor activates the tyrosine kinase activity of the intracellular domains
IN THE OFF POSITION: The insulin receptor kinase (intracellular domain) contains an activation loop that lies across the kinase active site, excluding substrate peptides and inhibiting the kinase
UPON INSULIN BINDING: Phosphorylation of three tyrosine residues on the activation loop ( autophosphorylation ) causes the activation loop to move away from the active site
This opens the active site so that target proteins are bound and phosphorylated by the kinase, triggering appropriate signaling pathways

Autophosphorylation of the Insulin Receptor Kinase Opens the Active Site
Figure 32.16 In its native state, the insulin receptor tyrosine kinase is inhibited by an activation loop (yellow and red) which prevents substrate access to the active site.

Ligand-Induced Receptor Oligomerization can Occur in Several Ways
Figure 32.10 (d) Neutotrophin activation of Ret involves association of two molecules of the Ret kinase and two molecules of a GPI-anchored coreceptor protein.

GPI-anchor
Proteins with no transmembrane domains can still be associated with the membrane by covalent attachment of phospholipid
Example: GPI anchoring of coreceptors
GPI: Glycophosphatidylinositol
Part 9B: GPCRs

32.4 How Are Receptor Signals Transduced?
Receptor signals are transduced in three ways:
Receptor-mediated activation of phosphorylation cascades that trigger activation of various enzymes
Exchange of GDP for GTP by GTP-binding proteins (G proteins), which leads to generation of second messengers ( cAMP , lipid breakdown products, Ca 2+ )
Conformation changes that open ion channels or recruit proteins into nuclear transcription complexes

The Membrane Receptor Superfamilies
Figure 32.7 The G-protein-coupled receptors are named for the GTP-binding proteins that mediate some of their effects.

The G-Protein-Coupled Receptors Are 7-TMS Integral Membrane Proteins
The G-protein-coupled receptors (GPCRs) have 7 transmembrane helices.
Binding of hormone to a GPCR induces a conformation change that activates a GTP-binding protein, also known as a G protein
Activated G proteins trigger a variety of cellular effects, including activation of adenylyl and guanylyl cyclases , activation of phospholipases, and activation of ion channels

The G-Protein-Coupled Receptors Are 7-TMS Integral Membrane Proteins
Figure 32.8 (a) The 7-transmembrane segment arrangement of the β 2 -adrenergic receptor in the membrane .
Binding of ligand activates a bound “G-protein” resulting in signal propagation
G-protein

The G-Protein-Coupled Receptors Are 7-TMS Integral Membrane Proteins
Figure 32.8 (b) The structure of a β 2 -adrenergic receptor

GPCR Signals are Transduced by G Proteins
The signals of GPCRs are transduced by GTP-binding proteins (G proteins)
Two kinds: " heterotrimeric G proteins" and "small G proteins"
The heterotrimeric G proteins consist of α -, β - and γ subunits
α -subunit binds GDP or GTP and has an intrinsic GTPase activity

GPCR Signals are Transduced by G Proteins
Figure 32.21 A heterotrimeric G protein ( α – pink, β – yellow, γ – blue) docked with a β 2 -adrenergic receptor (green).
GPCR
G-protein

GPCR activation
Binding of hormone to receptor stimulates GTP-GDP exchange, causing G α to dissociate from G βγ and to associate with an effector protein
Effector proteins produce a Second Messenger
Effector proteins

GPCR activation
In the absence of ligand, GPCR is bond to G-protein
G α subunit is bound to GDP (inactive)

GPCR activation
When ligand binds, G-protein dissociates
G α subunit exchanges GDP for GTP (active)
Activates Effector proteins in one or more ways

GPCR activation
Two main effector proteins
Adenylate cyclase : ATP  cAMP + PPi
Phospholipase: breaks down phopsholipid
Both cases: A second messenger is produced

Adenylate cyclase produces cAMP (2 nd messenger)
Cyclic AMP is synthesized by membrane-bound adenylyl cyclase and degraded by soluble phosphodiesterase
cAMP can activate downstream effectors to propagate signal
Synthesis or Release of a Second Messenger is Followed Quickly by Degradation or Removal
Activation of some GPCRs is inhibitory: result in low cAMP

Two Stages of Amplification Occur in the G-Protein-Mediated Hormone Reponse
Figure 32.22 Adenylyl cyclase activity is modulated by the interplay of stimulatory ( G s ) and inhibitory ( G i ) proteins. Hormone binding to β 1 - and β 2 - receptors activates adenylyl cyclase , whereas hormone binding to α 2 -receptors leads to inhibition of adenylyl cyclase .

What is a second messenger?
The hormone is the "first messenger"
The second messenger - Ca 2+ , cAMP or other - is released when the hormone binds to its (extracellular) receptor
The second messenger then activates (or inhibits) processes in the cytosol or nucleus
Degradation and/or clearance of the second messenger is also (obviously) important

GPCR restoration
At some point, the G-protein will convert GTP to GDP
This will cause the G-protein to bind to the GPCR again (if there is no ligand)
“Inactive” form will be restored

Two Stages of Amplification Occur in the G-Protein-Mediated Hormone Reponse
First stage: a single hormone-receptor complex can activate many G proteins before the hormone dissociates from the receptor
Second stage: G α -activated adenylyl cyclase synthesizes many cAMP molecules
Thus binding of a hormone to a receptor stimulates a large increase in concentration of cAMP in the cell

Two Stages of Amplification Occur in the G-Protein-Mediated Hormone Reponse
The hormone receptor, G protein, and cyclase constitute a hormone signal transduction unit
G protein regulation may either be stimulatory or inhibitory – each receptor interacts both with stimulatory G s or inhibitory G i
G βγ also acts as a cell signal

IP3 as a Second Messenger: produced by phospholipid breakdown
A diverse array of second messengers is generated by breakdown of membrane phospholipids
Binding of some hormones to their receptors triggers activation of specific phospholipase C
Results in the breakdown of Inositol phospholipids to yield IP 3 and DAG

IP3: inositol-1,4,5-triphosphate
And DAG: diacylglycerol

sequence in the alignment

-Modeling program may CHANGE the alignment!

Project Mode
- Allows user to “tweek” the alignment by 
hand

• Incorrect sequence alignment
- Use multiple sequence alignment
- Use more sophisticated alignment programs
- Compare results of different alignment programs
• Incorrectly built loop regions
Loops are often missing from X-ray structures and are usually built automatically by the server; 
different servers use different methods for building loops. 
- Compare results for loop regions from different servers
- Be aware that that loop structures are at best educated guesses
• Poor choice of template 
- Compare results from different templates
- Avoid templates with low resolution (> ~2.8 Angstroms)
• Errors present in the template
- Difficult to eliminate! 
- Pay attention to the quality of the template
https://proteinstructures.com/Modeling/Modeling/homology-modeling.html

How do you assess models?
Classic approach: Ramachandran plots!
1) Only a small set of Phi-Psi angles are sterically allowed for AAs other than glycine
2) If find disallowed angles, something is probably wrong!
Ramachandran plots

2. Go to Favorites -> Model Panel
3. In Model panel, scroll down to find the Ramachandran Plot button
4. Clicking on the Ramachandran button should pop a live graph
Note that clicking on a dot will select the AA!

How to interpret QMEAN Z-scores:
• QMEAN Z-scores around zero indicate good agreement between the model structure and 
experimental structures of similar size.
• Scores of -4.0 or below are an indication of models with low quality. 
• Positive values indicate that the model scores higher than experimental structures on average. 
Qualitative Model Energy Analysis (QMEAN): 
“Composite scoring function describing the major geometrical aspects of protein structures.”
Features include torsion angles,  solvent accessibility.
Benkert P, Tosatto SC, Schomburg D. QMEAN: A comprehensive scoring functionfor model quality assessment. Proteins. 
2008 Apr;71(1):261-77.
“QMEAN shows a statistically significant improvement over nearly all quality measures describing the ability of 
the scoring function to identify the native structure and to discriminate good from bad models. ”
QMEAN and GMQE
GMQE (Global Model Quality Estimation):
Additional quality estimation in SwissModel that combines properties from the target–template 
alignment and the template search method. 
• GMQE score is expressed as a number between 0 and 1, reflecting the expected accuracy of a 
model built with that alignment and template and the coverage of the target. 
• Higher numbers indicate higher reliability. 
“A method for estimating the absolute quality of a single protein structure, i.e. without 
including additional information from other models or alternative template structures.” 
https://omictools.com/qmean-tool

Building phylogenetic trees
➤Three basic methods
1. Distance
a)  Measure pairwise distance between sequences
b)  Use matrix of distances to build tree
- Group most closely related sequences, followed by successively more distant…
“Neighbor Joining” (ClustalX)
- Find tree that minimizes difference between observed distances in matrix
and distances measured along branches
various names “Fitch-Margoliash”
Note:  collapses all differences between pair of sequences into one number
2.  Parsimony
- Find tree that minimizes # evolutionary changes
PAUP
PHYLIP “PROTPARS”
3. Likelihood
- Find most likely tree (based on explicit model of evolution)
Bayesian (MrBayes)
Maximum likelihood (ML)
Note:  2 and 3 are SLOW!!!  Inexact with with large # sequences
3. Combined methods
- Use neighbor joining for first tree, then use ML (“FastTree”, PASTA)

Searching protein sequence databases
BLAST and PSI-BLAST:
Questions for today
• What is “the database?”
•  How does BLAST work?
•  How do you run BLAST and interpret the results?
1)
Computers align your sequence to every sequence in “the database”
(or a subset as you dictate)
2)
Computer analyzes each alignment
3)
Computer presents to you the sequences that look most closely related
4)
You decide whether or not the “hits” looks real
Regular BLAST:
Questions for Wednesday
•  What is the difference between BLAST and PSI-BLAST?
•  How do you use and interpret PSI-BLAST?
• Other ways to search the databases?
3

Sequence Databases
“GenBank:” the NIH sequence database contains:
> 200 million gene sequences!  
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/genbank/statistics/
Series of subdatabases, including: 
• Protein sequences
• Full genomes of many (!) organisms
• Partial but annotated genomes of many others
• Enormous amounts of sequence form whole genome shotgun, 
transcriptome, environmental DNA, etc…. 
Part of the International Nucleotide Sequence Database Collaboration
“Genbank” has all sequences submitted to:
- GenBank itself (NCBI). 
- DNA DataBank of Japan (DDBJ), 
- the European Molecular Biology Laboratory (EMBL)
These three organizations exchange data on a daily basis.
New release: every two months. 
Genbank does includes proteins submitted to 
protein databases, PDB
Does not include many partial sequences from 
genome projects
4

Genbank  has many sub-databases
Protein databases:
• Model Organisms:  genomes of 27 model organisms (wide taxonomic range).  
Good place to start!
• RefSeq = “Reference sequences”: curated dataset 
current knowledge of known genes. 
• NR = “non-redundant”: set of non-identical sequences from many databases
- proteins predicted from DNA/RNA sequences in Genbank
- proteins submitted as protein sequences to protein databases
still has a lot of redundant information: mutants, errors, etc.
• PDB = database of structurally characterized proteins
- find out if there is a structure for your protein or a relative!
➤What is tBLASTn?
Notes:  
1) Some genome projects have not tried to predict proteins
➥may need search DNA database viaTBLASTN
2) Some genome projects have not yet submitted data to GENBANK
➥may need to go to their databases directly
5

BLAST
BLAST = “Basic Local Alignment Search Tool”
http://blast.ncbi.nlm.nih.go
➥a set of algorithms that find regions of similarity between biological 
sequences.
- Designed for database searching; VERY fast!
- not exact: 
➥other programs give more accurate searches, better pairwise alignments
e.g. “Smith-Waterman” for database searching
see “http://www.clcbio.com/index.php?id=1098 for BLAST vs Smith-Waterman
- for large databases, BLAST is the preferred initial search method
Reasons to find related sequences:
• to infer function of an unknown protein 
➥find characterized relatives, conserved domains
• to perform structure/function analysis
➥analyze conservation across members of family (orthologs and paralogs)
• analyze evolutionary relationships between species 
➥analyze phylogenetic relationships between a set of orthologs
• map DNA to a known chromosome or source
6

How BLAST works
A nontechnical explanation…
Text modified from www.mssm.edu/library/reference/pdfs/BLAST%20Handout.pdf [page is no longer active, but I still have to cite it!]
BLAST does the following:
1)
It breaks a query sequence into a series of “words” of a set number of letters. 
Sequence: 
GESGAGKT
3-letter words:
GES, ESG, SGA  
word length can be adjusted in “algorithm parameters”, but this is rarely necessary
2) These words are compared to words from sequences in the database. 
3) When a match is found, the sequences are aligned locally by extending from this 
initial word match and scored using a “scoring matrix.”
aligned locally:  penalty for gaps; if gap is too big, regions are considered separately
4) “Hits” are presented to you in the order of the highest score/lowest E value
E value:  how likely the similarity between your sequence and the one in the database is due to chance

Flavors of BLAST:
* blastp: search protein database using a protein query sequence
Powerful variation: *psi-blast
* blastn: search nucleotide database using nucleotide query sequence
Variations:  megablast, discontiguous megablast
used in analysis of large DNA sequences/genomes
Search programs that involve translation of the query and/or database:
blastx: search protein database using translated nucleotide query sequence
➥you provide DNA/RNA sequence, BLAST translates it in all 6 frames then searches the protein database
time-consuming
* tblastn: search translated nucleotide database using protein query sequence
➥you provide protein sequence, it dynamically translates the nucleotide database in all 6 frames 
then searches this translated database
more time-consuming
tblastx: search translated nucleotide database using translated nucleotide query
➥you provide DNA/RNA sequence, it translates your sequence and the database in all 6 frames
then searches this translated database with the six translated probe sequences.
VERY time-consuming
8

• Scoring of matches between AAs is performed using a “scoring matrix”
(next page)
• Gaps are assigned a certain scoring “gap penalty” for their existence, length. 
➥Scoring matrices and gap penalties can be changed from their defaults by using 
the “Algorithm parameters” link on the BLAST screen.
• Default scoring matrices and gap penalties usually work fine!
➥If searching for divergent sequences, these may require tweeking
Scoring:

Three common scoring matrices:
1) Identity
2) PAM
3) BLOSUM
