• Numbers: derived from comparing aligned sequences of proteins with known homology and 
determining the number of "accepted point mutations" (PAM) observed. 
Details: "Log odds-matrix: Mij = 10(log10Rij), where Mij is the matrix element and Rij is the probability of that 
substitution as observed in the database, divided by the normalized frequency of occurence for amino acid i. 
All of the number are rounded to the nearest integer. Higher order PAM matrices extraopolated from PAM1 by 
multiplying the PAM1 matrix by itself 
1 PAM = 1 mutation/100 AAs
250 PAMs = 250mutations/100AAs 
11

Problems with the PAM matrices:
1)
Based on small subset of largely globular proteins known at the 
time (1978)
2)
Based on set of closely-related proteins -- PAM250 extrapolated 
from PAM1
Solution:  BLOSUM matrices (Henikoff and Henikoff, 1992)
“BLocksSUbstitution Matrix”
• Made separate BLOSUM matrices for closely related and 
distantly related proteins based on separate alignments (not 
extrapolations)
• Based on “blocks” of highly conserved regions in protein families

PAM160 ~BLOSUM60
PAM200 ~BLOSUM52
PAM250 ~BLOSUM45

Closer sequences
Comparison of Protein scoring matrices
•BLOSUMn is based on sequences that shared at 
least n percent identity, generally:
BLOSUM62 for general use
BLOSUM80 for close relations
BLOSUM45 for distant relations

How BLAST works
A nontechnical explanation…
Text modified from www.mssm.edu/library/reference/pdfs/BLAST%20Handout.pdf [page is no longer active, but I still have to cite it!]
BLAST does the following:
1)
It breaks a query sequence into a series of “words” of a set number of letters. 
Sequence: 
GESGAGKT
3-letter words:
GES, ESG, SGA  
word length can be adjusted in “algorithm parameters”, but this is rarely necessary
2) These words are compared to words from sequences in the database. 
3) When a match is found, the sequences are aligned locally by extending from this 
initial word match and scored using a “scoring matrix.”
aligned locally:  penalty for gaps; if gap is too big, regions are considered separately
4) “Hits” are presented to you in the order of the highest score/lowest E value
E value:  how likely the similarity between your sequence and the one in the database is due to chance

8
Possible to improve on BLAST?
Position-specific scoring matrix!  “PSSM”

9
PSI-BLAST
Step 1:
1.
Start with a standard protein-protein BLAST search 
that uses a standard substitution matrix  (e.g. BLOSUM62)
2.
Use the results to build a position specific scoring 
matrix (PSSM) according to alignment of all the hits 
with  low E-value.
Step 2:
1.
Perform a BLAST search using the PSSM to evaluate 
the pairwise alignments.  
➜PSSM vs. DB  instead of seq vs. DB  
2.
Update the PSSM according  to the new result
3.
Go back to the beginning of step two (iterate!) or stop.

10
BLOSUM62 Protein Scoring Matix
11/08/2016
10
R
K
K
P

Differences between BLAST and PSI-BLAST??
1)
BLAST scores are based on generic substitution matrix
Default= BLOSUM 62
2)
PSI-BLAST runs a regular blast search, then makes a multiple-sequence 
alignment (MSA) of all hits above a threshold, then  calculates a 
“position-specific scoring matrix”
“PSSM”
3)
PSI-BLAST uses this PSSM to conduct a more sensitive search of the 
database
4)
PSSM can be optimized by running multiple iterations
➥powerful but can be dangerous!
- Sometimes converges to a clear set of homologs
- Sometimes diverges to include clearly irrelevant sequences!
Advantages of PSI-BLAST:
• much more sensitive scoring system: each position has its own matrix!
• much better at finding distant relatives
Disadvantages of PSI-BLAST:
• If irrelevant sequences get included in the PSSM, PSI-BLAST returns to you a 
mixture of related and unrelated sequences!  
• Depending on the frequency of these unrelated sequences in the database, this 
can get worse each iteration!
12

Some practical “Rules of Thumb:”
• Sequences with >25% identity are usually homologous
longer aligned regions are more likely real!
• Sequences with < 25% identity are in the “twilight zone”
➥10% identity can be meaningful it is the “right” 10%!
➥some proteins can be aligned structurally but not by 1° sequence!
➤How do you tell whether “the right 10% are conserved?
• Regular BLAST E values of 10-4 are usually significant
• PSI-BLAST E values can be difficult to interpret 
➥if irrelevant sequences have been included, the results will be 
misleading
• BEWARE COILED-COIL!
• Searching for short sequences or low complexity sequences? 
➥usually require alterations to the default parameters. 
13

14
BLAST and PSI-BLAST
Lets sum up…
-
Blast is a fast way to find homologues
-
No analytic theory that estimates the statistical 
significance of gapped alignments
-
Gap scores have been selected by trial and error.
applying different scoring matrix è No 
guarantee for gap scores 
-
PSI-BLAST finds weak homologues fast

15
1.
A much more sensitive scoring system .
each position has its own pattern probabilities .
2.
More weight to conserved positions.
3.
Lowers the influence of random sequence noise.
➥Good at finding distant relatives.
The power of PSI-BLAST:

Bioinformatics Lecture 4: 
Multiple sequence alignments!

Do, Katoh 
Methods Mol 
Biol. 2008
Progressive Alignment steps:
1.
Perform pairwise alignments (similar to BLAST)
2.
Use results of pairwise alignment to build a distance matrix
3.
Use the distance matrix to build a guide tree
4.
Use the guide tree to perform the progressive alignment
a.
Align closely related sequences first
b.
Align groups of more distantly related sequences against each other

Problems with progressive alignments:
1. Not guaranteed to be “optimal”
I.
Not mathematically optimal – too many possibiliies!
II.
Not necessarily biologically correct
•
Best score may not correspond to the actual structural 
alignment
•
Machine will align any set of sequences you give it.  But 
there may be no actual alignment!
(Problems with any alignment approach)
2. If guide tree is bad, wrong sequences will be aligned 
against each other initially
3. Need good “dynamic 
range” of diversity to give a 
good alignment for full set of 
sequences

http://www.clustal.org/clustal2/
Be sure to download clustalX (not clustalW)

Progressive Alignment steps:
1.
Perform pairwise alignments (similar to BLAST)
2.
Use results of pairwise alignment to build a distance matrix
3.
Use the distance matrix to build a guide tree
4.
Use the guide tree to perform the progressive alignment
a.
Align closely related sequences first
b.
Align groups of more distantly related sequences against each other
Bioinformatics Lecture 5: 
Multiple sequence alignments!
Recap…

Problems with progressive alignments:
1.
Not guaranteed to be “optimal”
I.
Not mathematically optimal – too many possibiliies!
II.
Not necessarily biologically correct
•
Best score may not correspond to the actual structural 
alignment
•
Machine will align any set of sequences you give it.  But there 
may be no actual alignment!
(Problems with any alignment approach)
2.
If guide tree is bad, wrong sequences will be aligned against 
each other initially
3.
Need good “dynamic range” of 
diversity to give a good alignment 
for full set of sequences
Result: Any aligment will have errors!
Our job:
• Identify problematic regions and/or sequences
• Figure out how to fix them as much as possible

How to identify poorly aligned sequences/regions?
1) See if there is anything obvious
•
If it looks like a frameshift….
• If a frameshift in the original: 
➞Delete or replace the sequence!

2) Look for patterns involving unusual or significant AAs….
C, F, Y, W.  Also G,P,  patterns of hydrophobic and charge.
Which sequence is misaligned?
Pays to get to know your sequence!
• If a region or a sequence is misaligned: 
➞Realign region OR realign sequence OR iterate alignment 
OR new program
- If a sequence is missing something that is everywhere else, 
it is either: 
a) misaligned
b) unrecognizable
c) missing that part
Realignment by hand: bad idea (!)

Not sure?
One useful approach: Dotplot!
Compare one sequence to another in sliding window
1. Matches
A match between sequences 
looks like a diagonal line on the 
dotplot graphic, representing the 
continuous match (or repeat).
2. Frame shifts
a. Mutations
Mutations are distinctions 
between sequences. On the 
graphic they are represented by 
gaps in diagonal lines. They 
interrupt matches.
b. Insertions
Insertions are parts of one 
sequence that are missed in the 
another, while the surrounding 
parts match. In other words, an 
insertion is a subsequence that 
was inserted into a sequence.
Graphically, insertions are 
represented by gaps which lie 
only on one axis. A little shift 
towards the other axis indicates 
a mutation involved.
c. Deletions
A deletion is a subsequence that 
was deleted from a sequence.
A deletion from sequence A 
found in sequence B can be 
considered as an insertion into 
sequence B and contained in 
sequence A.
Image and text: https://ugene.net/wiki/pages/viewpage.action?pageId=4227426

Assignment for Monday Sept. 16
Instructions
Please upload your homework by submitting to Sakai “Assignments” two files:
a) Your Clustal-generated ".aln" file that contains the alignment you made of your present set of sequences.
For most 
people, this will be the set of sequences you identified by PSI-BLAST in the model organism database, but for a few of you 
interested in non-human proteins, you might need to pull your proteins from a different database.
Whatever database you use, please don't try to submit here anything with more than 500 proteins - if your set is too large, 
please let me know ASAP, and we will do some work together to figure out how to whittle it down in a logical way.
b) A document (PPT or Word) that contains the following information:
i) The common name, accession number, and sequence of your probe protein (i.e., your protein of interest)
ii) Screenshots of what you find when you use your probe protein to search the NCBI Conserved Domain Database 
AND Interpro (see the class notes from Wednesday for links).
iii) A page (or so) that summarizes your analysis of your protein family as it now stands. Note that this will be 
unavoidably preliminary! The point is just to get you thinking about this.
For (iii), please include the following:
a) A summary of what domains exist in your protein, and why they might be functionally significant
b) An estimate of how many paralogs of your protein exist in the human genome (or your organism of primary 
interest). In making this estimate, please try to avoid including splice isoforms, and also avoid including proteins that 
simply have only a small part (e.g., one domain) in common with your protein. Note: for some protein families, this 
will be trivial, but for others, it will be quite challenging. Just give it a try, and if you have caveats, please list them.
c) An estimate of how far back in evolution your protein goes. In other words, in what range of organisms if your 
protein found? When and where did it appear? Again, for some proteins answering this will be trivial, but for others, 
it will be challenging because it is hard to tell what is the "same" protein in different organisms. To do this, one 
reasonable approach is to compare the domain organization of PSI-BLAST hits across different organisms. As with 
(b), just give it a try, and if you have caveats, please list them.
Please post to Piazza if you have problems!

Phylogenetic trees: Graphs that 
represent evolutionary relationships
One utility: classify sequences!
Many of today’s images from:

How to read trees:     Tree: graph that represents evolutionary relationships
Key points:
1)
Trees can be based on:
• Species
• Genes or proteins
• Individual organisms
2)
Time runs from the root to tips, not across
• Tips are most recent; ”root” (or center) is 
oldest
• Nodes indicate points of divergence
• horizontal distance has no meaning
• Branchlength may or may not have meaning
Varied ways to draw trees!
Rooted, unrooted….

The same tree can look different:
• if some branches are flipped (rotated at nodes)
Remember: horizontal distance has no meaning!
• if some branches are collapsed 
Which taxon is most 
distant from the oval?

Rooted vs. unrooted trees
https://slideplayer.com/slide/8111803/
These two trees are exactly the same!
(except for the root)
Rooted tree: assume you know where the bottom is!
Unrooted tree: like a tumbleweed!
No assumption about where the root attaches.
Are these two trees the same?
Hint: there are two additional differences 
Unrooted trees generally safer
➙Less likely to be misinterpreted
➙Much more appropriate if you don’t 
know the root
IF you know the root, unrooted trees 
can be easier to interpret

• Orientation can vary. 
• Format can vary.
Remember: Time goes from:
- Root to tip (rooted trees)
- In to out (unrooted trees)
• Be careful about misinterpreting branch length: 
Is it supposed to have meaning?
Additional points about tree interpretation
Demonstration with tree 
building software:
“Archaeopteryx”

Interpreting Phylogenetic Trees
• Branches: individual sequences
Branchlength: sequence nonidentity
• Tree is “unrooted”
position of ancestral sequence 
is not assumed
• Nodes - inferred points of sequence divergence
1) organism divergence
2) gene duplication
• Positive relationship between time and branchlength
but:
- not defined unless carefully calibrated 
- varies over different parts of tree

How to build a phylogenetic tree
1) Identify, align all sequences
• Find sequences via BLAST or PSI-BLAST
• Align via standard software 
ClustalX?
More sophisticated: Clustal Omega, MUSCLE, PASTA….
• See if the alignment looks good. If not, use your judgement!
Options include:
- Remove poorly aligned sequences
- Ask machine to realign poorly aligned sequences
- Cut off N- or C- terminal extensions (confuse Clustal)
- Use a more sophisticated program
2) Determine phylogenetic relationships
• use PROTEIN SEQUENCE!!!
• use only conserved core of alignment
Trim final alignment using Jalview
• best to use more than one approach
3) Estimate confidence in observed groupings:  
Bootstrapping   10-1000 trials (choose based on size of sequence set!)
Other statistical approaches

Building phylogenetic trees
➤Three basic methods
1. Distance
a)  Measure pairwise distance between sequences
b)  Use matrix of distances to build tree
- Group most closely related sequences, followed by successively more distant…
Neighbor Joining (ClustalX)
- Find tree that minimizes difference between observed distances in matrix
and distances measured along branches
various names Fitch-Margoliash
Note:  Distance appraoches collapse all differences between each pair of sequences into one number
2.  Parsimony
- Find tree that minimizes # evolutionary changes
PAUP
PHYLIP PROTPARS
3. Likelihood
- Find most likely tree (based on explicit model of evolution)
Bayesian (MrBayes)
Maximum likelihood (ML)
Note:  2 and 3 are SLOW!!!  Inexact with with large # sequences
4. Combined methods
- Use neighbor joining for first tree, then use ML (“FastTree”, PASTA)

List of useful phylogenetic analysis software programs
Most user-friendly: ClustalX – NJ algorithm
Tree-building
Pros:
• Easy to use 
• Easy bootstrapping
• FAST
• Works reasonably well for 
protein sequences
Cons:
• Uses an identity matrix (why bad?)
• NJ is frowned upon by the evolution 
community
• Bogs down if have huge numbers of 
sequences
More sophisticated NJ:
• PHYLIP package: “Protdist”
• MEGA package NJ 
Parsimony: PHYLIP ”Protpars”; PAUP
Multi-program packages:
• PHYLIP (classic)
• MEGA (the new thing…)
To find: Google…
Baysian, ML: MrBAYES, RaxML
Combined methods:  Good for large datasets!
• FASTTREE

CELL DISCOVERY
Robert Hooke published Micrographia (1665)
Used early microscope to see the units that make up cork
Named these units “cells”
Anton van Leeuwenhoek first to see live cells (1670s)

EARLY CELL BIOLOGY
Theodor Schwann, Matthias Schleiden, and Rudolf Virchow develop first cell theory in mid 1800s
All organisms made of cells
Cell is the smallest living unit
Cells come from other cells (Omnis cellula e cellula)

MODERN CELL BIOLOGY
Keith Porter, George Palade, Albert Claude, and Christian de Duve used cell fractionation and electron microscopy to describe internal structures (1940s-1950s)
Cells are NOT just bags of DNA and proteins

TYPES OF CELLS
Prokaryotes
DNA NOT contained in a nucleus
Includes bacteria and archaea
“small”
Eukaryotes
DNA IS contained in a nucleus
Includes protists, fungi, plants, and animals
“large”

COMPLETED PROTEINS ARE NOT TRANSLOCATED INTO THE RER
Co-translational translocation – translation and translocation occur at the same time

HOW ARE PROTEINS TARGETED TO RER?
“All” proteins start synthesis in cytosol
Elongating peptide exits through groove in ribosome (N-terminus first)
~ 40 aa not exposed to cytosol during synthesis

SECRETORY PROTEIN SIGNAL SEQUENCE
Signal sequence – hydrophobic rich 15-30 aa (at least 6 consecutive hydrophobic aa) at N-terminus
Signal recognition particle (SRP) binds signal sequence once exposed and pauses translation

CO-TRANSLATIONAL TRANSLOCATION
SRP binds SRP receptor (SR) on ER membrane
Both SRP and SR hydrolyze GTP
Ribosome “passed” to translocon
Translation resumes
SRP and SR released

CO-TRANSLATIONAL TRANSLOCATION
Signal peptidase cleaves (removes) signal sequence
Releases mature soluble protein into ER

HOW WAS CO-TRANSLATIONAL TRANSLOCATION MECHANISM DISCOVERED?
Gunter Blobel et al.
1970s-1980s
1999 Nobel Prize
Reconstitution experiments
disassemble process
reassemble with various combinations of components

codes for 25 kD protein (preprolactin)
but secreted protein = 22 kD (prolactin)

ACTICE QUESTION 12 KEY
You are investigating the uptake of LDL particles in a cell brought back from the latest Mars mission.  Preliminary studies show that the Martian cells contain cholesterol in their membranes just as Earth cells do.  Luckily, the Martian cells are able to incorporate cholesterol from Earth LDL particles into their membranes which makes experiments much easier.  You perform the following experiments.
A. You perform a pulse-chase experiment to define the pathway that ApoB follows after uptake of LDL particles by the Martian cells.  You find that the ApoB follows the same path as in Earth cells.  What are these localizations in order?
Clathrin-coated vesicles > uncoated vesicles > late endosome > lysosome
B. You perform in vitro binding studies with purified Earth LDL particles and Martian LDL receptors.  You find that the LDL particle binds to the receptor equally well at both pH 7 and pH 5.  Are these binding results the same or different from what you would expect with LDL particles and LDL receptors from Earth cells?  Explain.
These results are different than what occurs with Earth LDL particles and receptors.  Earth LDL particles do not bind to Earth LDL receptors at pH 5.
C. Propose a model for how receptor mediated endocytosis of LDL works in the Martian cells.
LDL particles bind to LDL receptors at the cell surface.  The receptors with particles bound are internalized in clathrin-coated vesicles.  The vesicles uncoat and fuse with the late endosome.  The lumen of the Martian late endosome is pH 3.  The low pH causes the LDL particle to be released from the receptor.  The particle is sent to the lysosome to be degraded and the receptor is returned to the cell surface.

PRACTICE QUESTION 10 KEY
You are using reconstitution experiments to study the formation of COPII-coated vesicles.  Specific components that you have available are shown in the table below.  The “ER membrane” is an artificial membrane that contains the Sec12 protein and V-SNAREs.  GTPgS is an analog of GTP that is functionally identical in every way except that it cannot be hydrolyzed.  You perform the following experiments.  After incubating the components indicated in experiment 1 you observe normal formation of COPII-coated vesicles that are able to fuse at a target membrane.
A Describe the steps that would occur in the process of vesicular transport to the target membrane with the components in experiment 2.  Explain the role(s) for each component in these steps.
Without Sar1 present, no steps of the process can occur.
B. Describe the steps that would occur in the process of vesicular transport to the target membrane with the components in experiment 3.  Explain the role(s) for each component in these steps.
Sec12 at the ER membrane activates Sar1 by causing Sar1 to replace GDP with GTP.  Sar1-GTP extends its hydrophobic tail and inserts into the ER membrane.  Sar1 binds to Sec23 of the Sec23/24 complex.  Sec23/24 curve the membrane and recruit Sec13/31 complexes.  Sec23/24 complexes form the inner coat of the vesicle and Sec13/31 form the outer coat.  The vesicle pinches off and is released into the cytosol.  Sar1 hydrolyzes its GTP, releases from the membrane, and the coat disassembles.  Without a Rab, the vesicle is not targeted to a membrane and will not fuse at any target.
C. Describe the steps that would occur in the process of vesicular
transport to the target membrane with the components in experiment 4.
Explain the role(s) for each component in these steps.
Sec12 at the ER membrane activates Sar1 by causing Sar1 to replace
GDP with GTPgS.  Sar1-GTPgS extends its hydrophobic tail and inserts
into the ER membrane.  Sar1 binds to Sec23 of the Sec23/24 complex.
Sec23/24 curve the membrane and recruit Sec13/31 complexes.
Sec23/24 complexes form the inner coat of the vesicle and Sec13/31
form the outer coat.  The vesicle pinches off and is released into the
cytosol.  Because GTPgS cannot be hydrolyzed by Sar1 the coat is not
released.  With the coat present, Rab and V-SNARE are not able to
target and fuse the vesicle to the target membrane.

Antibiotics
Antibiotics are naturally produced microbial metabolites (or their derivatives) that kill or inhibit growth of other microorganisms
an example of species competition
[Speaker Notes] Image from: Fleming A (1929) On the antibacterial action of cultures of a Penicillium, with special reference to their use in the isolation of B. influenza. British Journal of Experimental Pathology; 10.

Humans take antibiotics to treat bacterial infections
Used in treating infections (diseases) caused by bacterial pathogens (useless against viral infections)
Antibiotics kill susceptible bacterial cells (pathogens and non-pathogens) in the body
If bacterial cells can survive an antibiotic, then the bacteria are resistant to this antibiotic, which can no longer be used to treat this infection caused by this bacterial pathogen

How do antibiotics work?
Kapoor et al (2017) DOI: 10.4103/joacp.JOACP_349_15 [http://dx.doi.org/10.4103/joacp.JOACP_349_15]
1. Affect envelope of bacterial cells (cell wall, cell membrane)
e.g. ampicillin causes the cell to make a defective cell wall  bacterial cell lyses and dies
Nalidixic acid
tetracycline
ampicillin

How do antibiotics work?
Kapoor et al (2017) DOI: 10.4103/joacp.JOACP_349_15 [http://dx.doi.org/10.4103/joacp.JOACP_349_15]
2. Inhibit microbial metabolism or nucleic acid synthesis
e.g. nalidixic acid blocks DNA gyrase which affects nucleic acid synthesis.  Makes it difficult for bacterial cell to replicate.
Nalidixic acid
tetracycline
ampicillin

How do antibiotics work?
Kapoor et al (2017) DOI: 10.4103/joacp.JOACP_349_15 [http://dx.doi.org/10.4103/joacp.JOACP_349_15]
3. Inhibit protein synthesis
e.g. tetracycline inhibits bacterial ribosomes  bacteria cannot make proteins and die
Nalidixic acid
tetracycline
ampicillin

Bacteria can become resistant to antibiotics by acquiring antibiotic resistance genes
Antibiotic resistance genes
1. Code for enzyme that inactivates the drug
2. Modify drug target so that cell is not sensitive to the drug
3. Limit uptake of drug
4. “Efflux pumps” that pump drug out of cell

Each time the antibiotic is used, resistant bacteria multiply and prevail because they are the only ones able to do so under those conditions  the population becomes more resistant
The evolution of antibiotic resistance in a bacterial population

Transfer of antibiotic resistance genes between natural  and clinical environments
Martínez (2008) Science. 321: 365-367.
DOI: 10.1126/science.1159483
[Speaker Notes] Most antibiotic resistance genes acquired through horizontal gene transfer have been originated in environmental microorganisms. In their original host, these determinants (red dot) form part of integrated regulatory and metabolic networks. After their dissemination (circle), their integration in the novel metabolic networks would be difficult, and their only role will be resistance. The reintroduction of plasmid-encoded antibiotic resistance determinants in natural environments, together with the changes they suffer as the consequence of human activities, might be relevant for the future evolution and dissemination of antibiotic resistance determinants in bacterial pathogens.

What is known about drug resistance in E. coli?
A gram negative bacterium (have two membranes)
Efflux pumps
Normal function is to keep out the toxic compounds in the environment, to send molecules out to interact with the host
In the presence of antibiotics, can be selected to become drug or multidrug efflux pumps
The surface channels can also decrease in numbers to keep the drug out

Critical chi-square value
Probability value or Significance level
Example 1: Our data has a chi-square value of 0.5 and df = 1
Probability value would be 0.5 < P > 0.4
p value of 0.5 means 50% chance that the deviation of the observed from the expected is due to chance alone (no other forces acting)
Example 2: Our data has a chi-square value of 5.2 and df = 1
P value would be less than 0.05 (P < 0.05)
This means that there is less than 5% chance that the differences between observed and expected values are due to chance alone.

For 1 degree of freedom:
If your chi-square value is smaller than the critical chi-square value of 3.84 , then accept null hypothesis that observed values match expected values
If your chi-square value is larger than the critical chi-square value of 3.84 , then reject null hypothesis that differences between observed and expected values are due to chance or sampling error.
P> 0.05 Null hypothesis accepted
(no significant difference from a set  value)
P<0.05 Null hypothesis rejected , alternative hypothesis should be considered

Example
(Topic slide)
Mutualistic interaction between yucca plant and yucca moth
Presenter Name
[Speaker Notes] Yucca is a perennial shrub found in warm and dry parts of North and Central America. They are studied for their unique pollinator – the yucca moth. It is a 40 million year mutualistic relationship. The yucca moth lays an egg in the flower and then pollinate the flower. The larvae will feed on the seeds, but only a fraction of the seeds, leaving the rest to perpetuate the yucca species. 

For more than 40 million years there has been a relationship between yucca plants and yucca moths. It’s a particularly important one because neither the yucca or the moth can survive without the other. The moth’s larvae depend on the seeds of the yucca plant for food, and the yucca plant can only be pollinated by the yucca moth.

Yucca plant ( Yucca filamentosa ) and yucca moth ( Tegeticula yuccasella ) are involved in obligate plant-pollinator mutualism
Use photos and simple text to introduce your study system
(Background slide)
(unique stigma anatomy of yucca flowers; requires yucca moth for pollination)
recessed stigma
A perennial shrub
[Speaker Notes] Nursery pollination, in which insects use as hosts the very plants they pollinate, ranges from obligate mutualism to parasitism.
http://sp11symbiosis.providence.wikispaces.net/The+Yucca+Plant+and+Its+Moth
The yucca moth, otherwise known as the Tegeticula yuccasella, is an insect that exemplifies coevolution with its symbiont, the yucca plant, Yucca elata. These two organisms are most often found in dry regions within the eastern hemisphere, mainly the southwestern United States and Mexico (2). 
Every spring both male and female yucca moths emerge from the ground from their cocoons that have been subterranean for the winter season. After mating, the female with the fertilized eggs will locate a nearby yucca flower, use a specialized appendage behind her mouth to collect a ball of pollen. 
A specific moth that is genetically programmed for stuffing a little ball of pollen into the cup-shaped stigma of each flower. 
The pistil of each flower terminates in a three-lobed stigma, the lobes in some species with glistening, feathery branches. The stigma lobes surround a central orifice that leads to a recessed receptive stigma. In order for pollination to occur, masses of pollen must be forced down into this central stigmatic depression. Herein lies the adaptive advantage and marvelous genetic programming of a little moth that is absolutely vital for the survival and perpetuation of yucca plants.

When a female is ready to lay eggs, she first goes to a yucca flower to collect pollen.  Unlike most moth species, yucca moths have two short tentacles near their mouth that they use to scrape pollen from the anthers of the flower.  As she collects the sticky pollen, the yucca moth packs it into a ball and sticks it under her head.  She then flies off to another yucca flower.
When she arrives at the second yucca flower, usually one that has very recently opened, she goes straight to the bottom to find the ovary.  She opens a small hole in the ovary and lays her eggs inside.  Once the eggs are laid, she scrapes a small amount of pollen from her sticky ball with her tentacles, walks to the stigma of the flower, and packs the pollen into tiny depressions within the style.  She may then return to the ovary of the same flower to lay more eggs or fly to another flower.  Either way, before she leaves the flower, she marks it with a pheromone (a chemical other moths can sense).  The scent marker will tell later visitors that they’re not the first to reach the flower, and they will either lay fewer eggs than the first moth, or none at all, depending upon how many moths have left their scent already.  This helps moderate the number of larva that hatch within each flower, and prevents the plant from aborting the flower altogether, which it will do if too many eggs are laid.

Developing larvae consume a small percentage of seeds, leaving the rest to perpetuate the species
(More background, but leading towards the question)
Oviposition by female T. yuccasella and pollination of Y. filamentosa
female yucca moth inserts eggs into ovary wall then moves to the stigma, deposits pollen ball to ensure the pollination of the ovules and seed formation for her larva
ovipositor
Flowers with heavy egg load tend to abort before eggs hatch
[Speaker Notes] The inherent conflict of interests between the interacting species and mechanisms by the interacting species that prevent over-exploitation of each other

At this point the female is ready for egg laying. It is presumed that the moths fly to another plant, as in the well-documented behavior of another yucca moth Tegeticula yuccasella. The female moth crawls into a flower and positions herself on the side of the ovary, head outward, and inserts her egg-laying device (called an ovipositor) into the ovary wall near the partition between adjacent ovary sections (carpels). The ovary wall is thinnest near the partition between carpels. A single, slender egg is inserted into the ovule chamber (locule). Now she is ready for pollination--the crucial event that enables the perpetuation of all yuccas in the wild.
After inserting her egg into the flower ovary, the female moth (still carrying a pollen mass in her coiled palpi) climbs to the top of the ovary. Uncoiling her palpi from the pollen mass, she draws them back and forth over the stigma, pressing pollen into the central stigmatic depression. This insures pollination of the flower in which she has deposited an egg. Germinating pollen grains send hundreds of sperm-bearing pollen tubes into the ovary, resulting in the fertilization of hundreds of ovules (immature seeds) inside, some of which provide food for the hungry moth larva.
The yucca moth larva hatches inside the green developing ovary of the flower during late spring and summer and begins to feed on the maturing seeds. It remains inside the ovary (seed capsule) through the summer and fall, high on a branch of the flower stalk. The seed capsule is composed of three sections or carpels, each with two columns of seeds. At maturity during the fall, up to 38 flattened black seeds lie in tightly packed tiers within each column, resembling coins stacked in a dispenser. In the column containing the moth larva, six to 14 of the seeds in the lower portion of the tier are fastened together with silk, and a robust, pinkish larva occupies a cylindrical feeding cavity within these joined seeds. According to Powell and Mackie (1966), yucca capsules may be occupied by more than one larva, but the average number is usually one or two. Although the larva is a seed predator, it only consumes a small percentage of the hundreds of seeds within the capsule. Since the larva develops into a moth that pollinates the yucca plant, the relationship is clearly beneficial to both partners. By comparison, the relationship between the Mexican jumping bean (Sebastiana pavoniana) and its symbiotic moth (Laspeyresia saltitans) is clearly one-sided. The moth is a seed predator but plays no role in the pollination of its host shrub.

For many years cell biologists watched the puppet show of DNA synthesis, mitosis, and cytokinesis but had no idea of what lay behind the curtain controlling these events.  The cell-cycle control system was simply a black box inside the cell.  It was not even clear whether there was a separate control system, or whether the processes of DNA synthesis, mitosis and cytokinesis somehow controlled themselves.    A major breakthrough came in the late 1980’s with the identification of the key proteins of the control system, along with the realization that they are distinct from the proteins that perform the processes of DNA replication, chromosome segregation, and so on.
-Alberts et al., 2002

46)   The Law of Independent Assortment states
a. that during gamete formation the genes will segregate independently from one another if they are not linked
b. that the independence of specific alleles will be translocated and then assorted
c.  the segregation of two alleles of different gametic pairs will assort
d.  that synapses will occur in the first division of meiosis resulting in the shuffling of chromosomes
e.  that two homologs will assort independently during meiosis
47) The Law of Segregation describes
a.  How genes on different pairs of chromosomes assort
b.  How alleles on homologous chromosomes separate during mitosis
c.  How homologous chromosomes can code for similar alleles
d.  How genes can form homologous pairs during meiosis
e.  How alleles on homologous chromosomes separate during meiosis

Vocabulary: dominant, recessove, homozygous, heterozygous hemizygous, genotype, phenotype

Phenotype
The observable properties of an organism that are produced by the genotype and its interaction with the environment.

Dominant
A phenotype that is expressed in either the homozygous or heterozygous state.  Usually represented with a capital letter.

Recessive
A phenotype that is expressed only in the homozygous state.  Usually represented with a lower case letter.

Genotype
The specific allelic composition of a cell-either of the entire cell or, more commonly, for a certain gene or a set of genes.
Genotypes

Homozygous
A term describing a diploid organism having the same alleles at one or more genes and therefore producing gametes of identical phenotypes

Heterozygous
A term describing a diploid organism having different alleles of one or more genes and therefore producing gametes of different genotypes
Heterozygous

Hemizygous
Describes the genotype for genes present in only one copy in an otherwise diploid organism, such as X-linked genes in a male.

Lethal genes

General rules in your thinking should be:
If one is dealing with a monohybrid cross, you’ll generally see the ratios as quarters, such as the classic 3:1 ratio. A common modification is  1:2:1, where there is incomplete or co-dominance.  A 2:1 ratio is almost always a lethal where the missing quarter of the progeny die in utero.

General rules in your thinking should be:
-If one is dealing with a dihybrid cross, the ratios will generally be in 16ths.  These would include the classic 9:3:3:1 ratio.  Weird modifications of this that still involve 16ths, such as 9:7, or 13:3, or a 12:3:1, usually involve 2 genes interacting.

Pleiotropy
The production, by one particular mutant gene, of apparently unrelated multiple effects at the phenotypic level.
Pleiotropic Mutation

Genotype		    Phenotype
X
Penetrance The proportion of individuals with a specific genotype who manifest that genotype at the phenotypic level.
Expressivity:The degree to which a particular genotype is expressed in the phenotype.

The genetic distance between genes can be determined by measuring the percentage of recombinants.
Sturtevant and Morgan proposed that genes that are close together will have fewer opportunities for recombination, than those that are far away.
The distance between genes can defined as the % recombinants.

Tumor Suppressor Genes
Genes which cause cancer when both copies of gene are mutated in a diploid and lead to loss of function
Are recessive

p53
Tumor suppressor gene which is mutated in over 50% of all cases of human cancer
p53 was named by virtue of the mass of the protein, 53 kd

p53
is stabilized (by phosphorylation) upon DNA damage
Is a transcription factor

p53 target genes involved in cell cycle control:p21, WAF1/CIP1, GADD45, WIP1, mdm-2, EGFR, PCNA, CyclinD1, CyclinG, TGFalpha and 14-3-3sigma
p53 target genes involved in DNA repair:GADD45, PCNA, and p21 WAF1/CIP1
p53 target genes involved in apoptosis:BAX, Bcl-L, FAS1, FASL, IGF-BP3, PAG608 and DR5/KILLER, GML, P2XM
p53 target genes involved in angiogenesis:TSP-1, BAI1
p53 target genes involved in cellular stress response:TP53TG1, CSR, PIG3
Examples of genes targeted by p53:

p53
p21 is also known as cyclin dependent kinase inhibitor CDKN1A

p53 also induces transcription of genes involved in Programmed Cell Death (apoptosis)
-Turns on gene CD95 (APO-1/FAS) receptor
-can also activate pathway directly

The loss of p53 activity can thus be trebly dangerous in relation to cancer.  First, it allows faulty mutant cells to continue through the cell cycle.  Secondly, it allows them to escape apoptosis.  Third, it leads to the genetic instability characteristic of cancer cells, allowing further cancer-promoting mutations to accumulate as they divide.    Many other mutations can contribute to each of these types of misbehavior, but p53 mutations contribute to them all.
Alberts et al., 2002

Summary of steps for determining linkage order and map distances
1)	Look at the cross, and at the offspring
2)	Establish which class of the offspring is the parentals, and the double crossovers
3)	If the order of genes is not specified, establish the correct linear order of the genes by model building
Group the single crossovers into regions
5)	Determine distances,  being sure to include the double crossovers in calculations

Mathematical Treatment of Population Genetics
Genotypic Frequency
Allele Frequency
Hardy-Weinberg Equilibrium
Assumptions for HW Equilibrium

Genotypic Frequency
Genotypic frequencies are a  measure of each allele configuration found in a population. For example, AA, Aa, and aa may have frequencies of X, Y, and Z.

Genotypic Frequencies
Are calculated by determining the number of individuals in a sample population with a specific genotype and dividing by the total population.
For instance, if the alleles A and a exist in a population penguins as:
Genotype:		AA		Aa		aa
Number of Individuals: 	886		224		350
then one can easily determine the frequencies as:
Total number of individuals:886+224+350 = 1460
Genotype frequencies:	AA		Aa		aa
886/1460	224/1460	350/1460
0.607 		0.153 		0.240
Note that the sum of the genotypic frequencies will equal 1.0
0.607	+	0.153	+	0.240       =     1.0
The same principles apply to populations with multiple alleles.

Example Problem
Given the number of individuals with their respective genotypes, please determine the genotypic frequencies
322  RR	225  Rr		97    rr

Solution
322  RR	225  Rr	97    rr
322 	+ 	225 	+ 	97 	=644 total individuals
Proportion of each genotypic class:
322/644	225/644	97/644
Genotypic Freq. =0.50 RR  	0.35 Rr	0.15 rr

Allele Frequency
A measure of the commonness of an allele in a population; the proportion of all alleles of that gene in the population that are of this specific type.  For example A and a may have allele frequencies of p and q.

Allele frequency sum equals 1.0
Can have allele frequencies for multiple alleles.

Allele frequencies can be calculated from the genotypes:
322  RR		225  Rr		97    rr
(322 + 322) + 225 	=  869  =   R=   0.67
644+644	  	   1288
225   +    (97  + 97) =   419	    =   r =	0.33
644 + 644             1288

224   +    (350  + 350) =   924=   a =  0.32
1460+1460 	     2920

Hardy-Weinberg Law
Both allelic and genotypic frequencies in a population remain constant—that is, they are in equilibrium—from generation to generation unless specific disturbing influences are introduced.

Tissue-Specific Knockout Technology
Why make tissue specific knockouts?
Many genes will cause death if knocked out in all tissues.  Tissue specific knockouts will allow you address the function of a given gene, in a given tissue.
How can you knock out a gene in a specific tissue?
Flank the gene with signals that allow it to be cut out, when a tissue-specific “scissors” are expressed

Tissue-Specific Knockout Technology
Cre/lox
Phage P1 enzyme Cre recombinase catalyzes recombination between 34 bp loxP recognition sequences.
The P1 genome is ~93kb and contains at least 117 genes

Infective virions of P1 contain linear, double stranded,  cyclically permuted, with a terminal redundancy of 10-15 kb.
After infection, the viral DNA circularizes by recombination between redundant sequences to enter a lytic or lysogenic path

Flox
To flank a gene of interest with loxP recognition sequences so that it can be cut out.

Tissue specific knockout:	Step 1
Construct mouse expressing cre-recombinase in the desired tissue

Tissue specific knockout:	Step 2
Construct a mouse that has the tissue specific gene of interest floxed

Tissue specific knockout:	Step 3
Mate the mouse with the tissue specific cre-recombinase to the mouse with the floxed gene of interest
Tissue specific knockout:	Results

Stem Cell (Definition)
A stem cell is an unspecialized cell that is capable of replicating or self renewing itself and developing into specialized cells of a variety of cell types.

Stem cells are described depending on the range of cell types that they can generate.
Totipotent- can give rise to any of the cell types in the body, including germ cells.
Pluripotent- Pluripotent stem cells can give rise to any fetal or adult cell type. However, alone they cannot develop into a fetus or adult organism because they lack the potential to contribute to extra-embryonic tissue, such as the placenta.
Multipotent-having the potential to give rise to cells from multiple, but a limited number of lineages.
Unipotent-having the capacity to differentiate into only one cell type

Embryonic Stem Cells
Cells derived from the inner cell mass of the early mammalian embryo that can give rise to all the cells in the body. Embryonic stem cells are totipotent.

Vaccines

What is a vaccine?
A biological product that can be used to safely induce an immune response that confers protection against infection and/or disease on subsequent exposure to a pathogen (Pollard & Bijker, 2021, p. 83).​
​Does not cause disease ​
Artificially mimics infection ​
Generates primary immune response = creation of memory B cells
Vaccine Categories

Types of Vaccines
Live active
No modification
Small amount = mild disease
No longer used
Example: smallpox
Live attenuated
Modified – weaken or inactivate
Pathogenic part removed
Widely used
Examples: measles mumps, rubella, chickenpox, influenza, oral polio….
Whole killed
Whole cell used – killed or nonviable
Example: whole-cell pertussis, influenza, polio, hepatitis A, rabies, 1896 - typhoid
Toxoid
Modification of toxin from bacteria
Anti-toxoid antibody produced
Short lived – 10 years
Example: tetanus, 1923 - diphtheria

Types of Vaccines
Virus-like particles
Looks like the virus…. But isn’t
Example: human papillomavirus
Subunit
Protein, polysaccharide, or peptide
Small amount – just enough for immune response
Example: pertussis, hepatitis A & B, meningococcus, pneumococcus, 1970 - anthrax
Outer membrane vesicle
Gram negative bacteria
Piece of cell wall membrane with antigen
Example: 1987 – Haemophilus influenzae type B
Protein-polysaccharide conjugate
Polysaccharide bound to carrier protein
Example: Haemophilus influenzae type B, pneumococcus, typhoid, 1987 - meningogoccus

Vaccines and Disease

Hepatitis B virus (HBV)
Family: Hepadinoviridae
Long recognized as significant cause of liver damage associated with morbidity and mortality
Pleomorphic, partially dsDNA, icosahedral capsule with envelop
Transmissions: humans are reservoir and vector; direct contact – body secretions, recipient of contaminated blood products, percutaneous injection of virus, perinatal exposure
Incubation: 1-3 months
Disease: acute infection with resolution (90%); fulminant hepatitis (1%); chronic hepatitis (9%)
400 million cases worldwide
Diagnosis: serology, viral antigen detection, and PCR
Treatment: antivirals and liver transplant
Prevention: HBV vaccine; hepatitis B immunoglobulins
Vaccine - 3 part series; 2nd dose at least 4 weeks after 1st, 3rd dose at least 8 weeks after 2nd and 16 weeks after 1st

Hepatitis B antigens and antibodies
HBsAg – surface antigen
Most reliable marker
Found weeks before evidence of liver damage
IgM anti-HBc – core antigen
Appears early in the course of disease
Anti-HBs – 6 months after acute infection
Indicates recovery and immunity
Best indication of active viral replication – HBV DNA
Quantitation predicts patient response to treatment

Distinguishing Features of Progenitor/Precursor Cells and Stem Cells. A stem cell is an unspecialized cell that is capable of replicating or self renewing itself and developing into specialized cells of a variety of cell types. The product of a stem cell undergoing division is at least one additional stem cell that has the same capabilities of the originating cell. Shown here is an example of a hematopoietic stem cell producing a second generation stem cell and a neuron. A progenitor cell (also known as a precursor cell) is unspecialized or has partial characteristics of a specialized cell that is capable of undergoing cell division and yielding two specialized cells. Shown here is an example of a myeloid progenitor/precursor undergoing cell division to yield two specialized cells (a neutrophil and a red blood cell).

Eosinophils swarming a worm
Macrophage

Monocytes (mononuclear phagocytes)
-once they leave the bloodstream, they mature into macrophages
-along with neutrophils are the main “professional phagocytes”
-macrophages contain specialized lysosomes that fuse with phagosomes and expose microorganisms to a barrage of enzymatically produced nasties like superoxide O2- and hypochlorite HOCl (the active ingredient in bleach), and a mixture of lysosomal hydrolases.
-much larger and longer lived than neutrophils.
-remove senescent, dead, and damaged cells, unique in being able to devour large microorganisms such as protozoa.
-macrophages have different names, depending on the tissue in which they are found

Ca10(PO4)6 (OH)2
Consists of Ca2+ ions surrounded by PO42- and OH- ions
Review Article
The role of hydroxyl channel in defining selected physicochemical peculiarities exhibited by hydroxyapatite
Vuk Uskoković*a
Show Affiliations
RSC Adv., 2015,5, 36614-36633
DOI: 10.1039/C4RA17180BReceived 28 Dec 2014, Accepted 27 Mar 2015First published online 27 Mar 2015
Mysteries surrounding the most important mineral for vertebrate biology, hydroxyapatite, are many. Perhaps the Greek root of its name, απαταo, meaning ‘to deceive’ and given to its mineral form by the early gem collectors who confused it with more precious stones, is still applicable today, though in a different connotation, descriptive of a number of physicochemical peculiarities exhibited by it. Comparable to water as the epitome of peculiarities in the realm of liquids, hydroxyapatite can serve as a paradigm of peculiarities in the world of solids. Ten of the peculiar properties of hydroxyapatite are sketched in this study, ranging from (i) the crystal lattice flexibility to (ii) notorious surface layer instability, (iii) finite piezoelectricity, pyroelectricity and conductivity to protons, (iv) accelerated growth and improved osteoconductivity in electromagnetic fields, (v) a high nucleation rate at low supersaturation and low crystal growth rate at high supersaturation, (vi) higher bioactivity and resorbability of biological apatite compared to those of the synthetic ones, and beyond. An attempt has been made to explain this array of curious characteristics by referring to a particular element of the crystal structure of hydroxyapatite: the hydroxyl ion channel extending in the direction of the c-axis, through a crystallographic column created by the overlapping calcium ion triangles.

A network of vessels and nodes that conveys lymph.  It returns plasma-derived intersitial fluids to the bloodstream and plays an important role in the integration of the immune system.

Important Points
Functional antibody genes do not exist as such in embryonic DNA, but are created during development
During development, cells of the immune system rearrange genes that they need to function.
Rearranged genes can be joined in a sloppy fashion, further increasing diversity
Somatic Mutation of immunoglobulin genes even further enhances diversity

Human Immunodiversity
Total number of
different genes in human       ~21,000
Number of
different antibody Molecules
~1,000,000,000,000
(2.14 vs 1012)

Antibody diversity is generated in part by combinations of different heavy and light chains.
Pretend
1000 different heavy chain genes
1000 different light chain genes
Would yield 106 different antibodies
-nowhere near enough
Any heavy chain can bind to any light chain

Myeloma cell
Cancerous plasma cell that still makes antibody.  Because the cell constantly divides, but still only produces one type of antibody, they are extremely useful for obtaining large amounts of a specific antibody and for studying individual antibody gene arrangement.

Humoral immune response
Host defenses that are mediated by antibody present in the plasma, lymph, and tissue fluids.  It protects against extracellular bacteria and foreign macromolecules.  Transfer of antibodies confers this type of immunity on the recipient.

Cell- mediated immune response
Host defenses that are mediated by antigen-specific T cells and various nonspecific cells of the immune system.  It protects against intracellular bacteria, viruses, and cancer and is responsible for graft rejection. Transfer of primed T cells confers this type of immunity on the recipient.

Kuby:
Understanding T-cell development has been a challenge for students and immunologists alike, but it helps to keep in mind the central purpose of the process:
to generate a large population of T cells that express a diverse array of receptor specificities that can interact with self-MHC, but do not respond to self proteins (self-restrictive to MHC, and self-tolerant).

The precise identity of the bone marrow cell precursor that gives rise to T cells is still debated.  However it is clear that this precursor, which is directed to the thymus via chemokine receptors, retains the potential to give rise to NK cells, Dendritic Cells, B cells, and even myeloid cells.

Science 30 April 1999:Vol. 284. no. 5415, pp. 770 - 776DOI: 10.1126/science.284.5415.770
Review
Notch Signaling: Cell Fate Control and Signal Integration in Development
Spyros Artavanis-Tsakonas, * Matthew D. Rand, Robert J. Lake
Notch signaling defines an evolutionarily ancient cell interaction mechanism, which plays a fundamental role in metazoan development. Signals exchanged between neighboring cells through the Notch receptor can amplify and consolidate molecular differences, which eventually dictate cell fates. Thus, Notch signals control how cells respond to intrinsic or extrinsic developmental cues that are necessary to unfold specific developmental programs. Notch activity affects the implementation of differentiation, proliferation, and apoptotic programs, providing a general developmental tool to influence organ formation and morphogenesis.
Massachusetts General Hospital Cancer Center, Department of Cell Biology, Harvard Medical School, Building 149, 13th Street, Charlestown, MA 02129, USA.

The vast majority of DP thymocytes (~98%) never meet the selection criteria and die by apoptosis within the thymus.
The bulk of DP thymocyte death (~95%) occurs among cells that fail positive selection because their receptors do not specifically recognize self-MHC molecules.  These cells do not receive survival signals through their TCRs and die by a process known as “death by neglect.”  A small percentage of cells  (~2%-5%) are eliminated by negative selection.  Only 2%-5% actually exit the thymus as mature T-Cells.

Positive Selection

Anergy
A state if immune unresponsiveness.  Induced when the T cell’s antigen receptor is stimulated, effectively freezing T cell responses pending a “second signal” from the antigen-presenting cell.  The delivery of the second signal by the APC cell rescues the activated T cell from anergy, allowing it to produce the lymphokines necessary for the growth of additional T cells.

Superantigens
are viral or bacterial proteins that bind simultaneously to the Vb domain of a T-cell receptor and to the a chain of a class II MHC molecule.

Because superantigens bind many Vb sequences, then activation is polyclonal and can affect a significant percentage of the total Th population.  Massive activations that follow result in overproduction of Th cell cytokines, leading to systemic toxicity like toxic shock syndrome and food poisoning.

Secondary Response

The hypothalamus is the thermostat of the body, in that it alters the temperature set point during temperature feedback and fevers. During a fever, the set point is raised, which causes the body to increase its temperature through both actively generating and retaining heat (vasoconstriction). If these measures are insufficient to make the blood temperature in the brain match the new setting in the hypothalamus, then shivering begins so those muscle movements produce more heat. When the fever stops (when PGE2 release ends), the temperature set point is lowered to normal, and the reverse of these processes (vasodilation, end of shivering, and nonshivering heat production) as well as sweating are used to cool the body to the new, lower setting.

End of Smallpox Vaccine Use
Routine vaccination in the US halted in 1972.
The last naturally occurring case was in 1977.
In 1978, there was an escape of smallpox from containment in a research laboratory in Birmingham, England. A medical photographer, Janet Parker died from the disease itself, after which the Professor responsible for the unit, Professor Henry Bedson, committed suicide.
The US has enough vaccine stockpiled to immunize everyone in the US.

MMR Vaccine Controversy
-case of scientific misconduct triggered a health problem
-1998 The Lancet presented apparent evidence that autism could be caused by the MMR vaccine.  The lead author of the article, Andrew Wakefield, had multiple undeclared conflicts of interest,  manipulated evidence, and had broke other ethical codes.
-The Lancet paper was partially retracted in 2004 and fully retracted in 2010, and Wakefield was found guilty by the General Medical Council of serious professional misconduct in May 2010 and was struck off the Medical Register, meaning he could no longer practice as a doctor.
-After vaccination rates dropped, the incidence of two of the three diseases increased greatly in the UK. In 1998 there were 56 confirmed cases of measles in the UK; in 2006 there were 449 in the first five months of the year, with the first death since 1992; cases occurred in inadequately vaccinated children.  Mumps cases began rising in 1999 after years of very few cases, and by 2005 the United Kingdom was in a mumps epidemic with almost 5000 notifications in the first month of 2005 alone.

Secondary Immunodeficiency
- an acquired defect
Malnutrition. Inhibits lymphocyte maturation and function.
Some viruses, e.g., HIV. Depletes T4-lymphocytes.
Irradiation - exposure to X-rays and gamma rays. Causes a decreased production of lymphocyte precursors in the bone marrow.
Cytotoxic drugs such as many used in cancer chemotherapy. Causes a decreased production of lymphocyte precursors in the bone marrow.
Corticosteroids – anti-inflammatory steroids. Damages lymphocytes.
Leukemias, cancers of the lymphoid system, metastases. Reduces areas for lymphocyte development.
Aging. Adaptive immunity, especially cell-mediated immunity, tends to diminish with aging.
Removal of the spleen. Decreased ability to remove microbes that enter the blood.

Why would anyone want to make antibodies?
If you are trying to understand the function of a gene by deleting it, you could prove the gene was gone if your protein disappeared in the mutant.
If you are trying to understand the function of a gene, determining its location in a tissue, or even better, its sub-cellular distribution is very important.
If you want to determine what proteins interact with a protein you are interested in, antibodies can be used to precipitate your protein and other proteins that stick to your protein of interest.
If you are testing for the presence of a bacteria or virus in a sample, antibodies are very sensitive detectors.
There are many antibodies already for sale…….

Examples of possible ways to organize a lecture summary
As text paragraphs:
A membrane forms the border or division between the contents of a cell or vesicle and its surroundings. Membranes are composed of a phospholipid bilayer with embedded and associated proteins. A phospholipid has a 3-carbon backbone with a charged or polar “head group” attached to one carbon and fatty acid hydrocarbon chains attached to the other two. The fatty acids form a hydrophobic cluster, while the head groups interact with the aqueous solutions inside and outside the cell.
Lipids differ in both the nature of their head groups, although they always contain a phosphate group, and in the fatty acids they incorporate. Phospholipids with saturated hydrocarbon chains will pack close together, while those with unsaturated fatty acids will have small gaps between them. In animal cells only, those gaps can be filled by cholesterol. Membranes in which a large fraction of the phospholipids have unsaturated fatty acid tails will be more fluid than those with primarily saturated fatty acid tails – this is important if the cell needs to be able to change shape easily, such as in ameboid movement. It’s also easier for molecules to move through the membrane by diffusion if the phospholipid tails are less tightly packed. Phospholipids can move within their layer, but very rarely change layers.
Integral membrane proteins are embedded in the membranes; hydrophobic amino acid side groups cluster with the fatty acid tails and polar or charged side groups form the intracellular and extracellular domains.  Peripheral membrane proteins can be inside or outside the cell. They associate with the polar head groups by non-covalent interactions (primarily hydrogen bonds), but are not embedded in the hydrophobic portion of the bilayer. Proteins also change their relative position in or adjacent to a membrane, but once situated seldom move from one side of a membrane to the other.

i. mostly H-bonds between side groups and polar head groups
3. move around, but don't cross membrane

Comparison of RNA Synthesis and DNA Synthesis
Similarities
1) Synthesis only occurs in the  5’  3’ direction. Nucleotides are only added
to a free 3’ –OH group.
2) The nucleotide to be added to the growing strand must be a nucleotide
triphosphate. The polymerization reaction releases an inorganic
pyrophosphate (PPi) and a nucleotide monophosphate is incorporated into
the growing nucleic acid strand.
3) There are multiple polymerase enzymes capable of catalyzing the polymerization
reaction.
4) The nucleotide sequence of the strand being synthesized is complementary to
that of the template strand.
+ H2O

Comparison of RNA Synthesis and DNA Synthesis
Differences
1) The nucleotides incorporated into the growing strand are ribonucleotides
rather than deoxyribonucleotides (NTPS or rNTPs rather than dNTPs).
2) RNA polymerase enzymes are able initiate synthesis de novo – they don’t
require a primer.
3) RNA polymerase enzymes have limited exonuclease/proofreading activity.
4) Only ONE strand of the DNA is used as the template for RNA synthesis.
5) The newly synthesized strand does NOT remain hydrogen bonded to the template
strand.
6) In RNA synthesis, only a small portion of the DNA (one gene) is used as template,
while in DNA synthesis the entire genome is usually used as template/replicated.

Molecular Biology and the COVID-19 Pandemic
1. isolate SARS-CoV-2 coronavirus RNA
generate cDNAs
determine DNA sequence
2. compare SARS-CoV-2 sequence to other coronavirus genomes
identify unique regions
design PCR primers
determine optimal conditions for RT-PCR of viral RNA
this is “the COVID test” – PCR to determine if template (viral RNA) is present in sample
3. identify sequences in viral genome that code for specific proteins
identify proteins required for successful infection (slower mutation rate)
identify proteins on surface of virus
4. used cloned cDNAs from viral genes to generate vaccines
SARS-CoV-2, the causative agent of COVID-19

cleaved into multiple
viral proteins

Two approaches to a vaccine
1. clone portion of viral genome for spike protein into adenovirus
inject modified adenoviral vector
infected cells produce adenovirus with COVID virus spike protein
immune system recognizes “non-self” proteins, responds
similar to flu vaccine – primes immune system by exposure to non-virulent
form of virus
the Oxford – AstraZeneca vaccine was produced this way
2. produce an mRNA that codes for the spike protein
in vitro transcription of cloned cDNA – add cap and poly-A tail
introduce the mRNA into liposomes
inject the liposomes – transfection of mRNA into cells
cells translate spike protein, target it in cell membrane
immune system recognizes “non-self” proteins, responds
the Moderna and Pfizer-BioNTech vaccines were produced this way

Differences between Eukaryotic and Prokaryotic Cells
Prokaryotic cells are quite simple in structure. They have no nucleus, no organelles and a small amount of DNA in the form of a single, circular chromosome.
Eukaryotic cells on the other hand, have a nucleus, multiple organelles and more DNA arranged in multiple, linear chromosomes.

Figure 9.6
A eukaryote contains a well-defined nucleus, whereas in prokaryotes, the chromosome lies in the cytoplasm in an area called the nucleoid.

I n order to pack large chromosomes into a small nucleus, an elaborate packing procedure is needed.
DNA attaches to proteins called histones an amino acids bear positive charges and form an ionic bond with the negative phosphate group of the DNA helix.
This protein-DNA structure is called chromatin.

Figure 9.7
These figures illustrate the compaction of the eukaryotic chromosome.

DNA Replication
Replication = DNA copies itself exactly
(Occurs within the nucleus)
Any mistake in copying = mutation
DNA mutation = chromosomal mutation
Most are corrected by mismatch repair or nucleotide excision repair

DNA Polymerases
The DNA polymerases are enzymes that create DNA molecules by assembling nucleotides, the building blocks of DNA . These enzymes are essential to DNA replication and usually work in pairs to create two identical DNA strands from a single original DNA molecule.

Primer
A primer is a strand of short nucleic acid sequences (generally about 10 base pairs) that serves as a starting point for DNA synthesis. It is required for DNA replication because the enzymes that catalyze this process, DNA polymerases, can only add new nucleotides to an existing strand of DNA .

Okazaki Fragments
Strand of DNA being replicated continuously. In DNA replication, the strand that is made in the 5' to 3' direction by continuous polymerization at the 3' growing tip. See also lagging strand .

Replication summarry
1) Helicase uncoils the DNA
2) RNA primase adds short sequences of RNA to both strands (the primer )The 3) primer allows DNA polymerase III to bind and start replication
4) DNA polymerase III adds nucleotides to each template strand in a 5'→3' direction
5) These nucleotides are initially deoxyribonucleoside triphosphates but they lose two phosphate groups during the replication process to release energy
6) One strand is replicated in a continuous manner in the same direction as the replication fork (leading strand)
7) The other strand is replicated in fragments (Okazaki fragments) in the opposite direction (lagging strand)
8) DNA polymerase I removes the RNA primers and replaces them with DNA
9) DNA ligase then joins the Okazaki fragments together to form a continuous strand

Telomere repair
What are they?
Ends of linear chromosomes
Repetitive DNA sequence: TTAGGG in vertebrates
Specialized proteins
Form a "capped" end s
So telomeres allow cells to divide without losing genes. Cell division is necessary for growing new skin, blood, bone, and other cells. Without telomeres , chromosome ends could fuse together and corrupt the cell's genetic blueprint, possibly causing malfunction, cancer, or cell death .

DNA Repair
DNA repair is a collection of processes by which cell identifies and corrects damage to the DNA molecules that encode its genome
Most mistakes are corrected during replication
mismatch repair is a system for recognizing and repairing erroneous insertion, deletion, and mis -incorporation of bases that can arise during DNA replication and recombination

DNA repair
Nucleotide excision repair (NER) is a particularly important excision mechanism that removes DNA damage induced by ultraviolet light (UV) .
But … Mutation is a natural process that changes a DNA sequence. And it is more common than you may think .
Cancer!
CELL death.

The C entral Dogma
The central dogma of molecular biology explains that DNA codes for RNA, which codes for proteins. DNA is the molecule of heredity that passes from parents to offspring. It contains the instructions for building RNA and proteins, which make up the structure of the body and carry out most of its functions.

Figure 9.14
The central dogma states that DNA encodes RNA, which in turn encodes protein .

As the DNA double-helix is unwound, bases on the single strand of DNA pair up with complimentary ribonucleotide bases
Except, A’s located on the DNA molecule pair up with U’s on the RNA strand
A Closer Look at Transcription

Each grouping of 3 ribonucleotides encodes for a single amino acid
Each coding triplet of mRNA bases is called a codon
Which codon corresponds to what amino acid has been cracked
Genetic code :  the inventory of linkages between nucleotide triplets and the amino acids they code for
14.3 A Closer Look at Transcription

A Closer Look at Transcription
Transcription: the process by which the genetic information encoded in DNA is copied into messenger RNA (mRNA)
Translation: the process by which information encoded in messenger RNA (mRNA) is used to assemble a protein at a ribosome

DNA never leaves nucleus
DNA creates a messenger molecule to deliver its information outside of the nucleus: mRNA Messenger RNA

Protein Synthesis in Overview
The first step in generating a protein is unwinding the DNA to expose all of the A’s, T’s, C’s and G’s
The DNA is then rewritten using a different type of nucleotides ( deoxyribonucleotides -> ribonucleotides )
The resulting product is a molecule called messenger RNA (mRNA)
The mRNA then docks onto an organelle called the ribosome where the mRNA sequence is read to form a polypeptide chain
TRANSCRIPTION
TRANSLATION

Amino acids are ferried to the ribosome by another type of RNA molecule called tRNA
Transfer RNA ( tRNA ) : a form of RNA that, in protein synthesis, binds with amino acids, transfers them to ribosomes and then binds with messenger RNA (mRNA)
Each tRNA molecule
14.4 A Closer Look at Translation
links with a specific amino acid
The tRNA features a triplet code of its own – anticodon – which recognizes the specific placing of an amino acid molecule based on the mRNA sequence

The ribosome – the workbench of protein synthesis ( ie ., translation) – is composed of two parts (subunits)
A large subunit
A small subunit
The subunits can exist separately in the cytoplasm until translation is needed
Don’t worry about “The steps of translation ”

Figure 9.20
This figure shows the genetic code for translating each nucleotide triplet, or codon, in mRNA into an amino acid or a termination signal in a nascent protein . (credit: modification of work by NIH)
14.5 Genetic Regulation

Gene Regulation
Gene regulation is the process of turning genes on and off. During early development, cells begin to take on specific functions. Gene regulation ensures that the appropriate genes are expressed at the proper times. Gene regulation can also help an organism respond to its environment.

Why is control of gene expression different in eukaryotes than in prokaryotes?
Eukaryote gene structure and function differ from prokaryote gene structure and function in several important ways .
Eukaryotes generally have many more genes and these genes are spread across multiple chromosomes
Prokaryotes have fewer genes and these genes are all located on one chromosome.
Groups of genes producing proteins with related functions are often organized into operons in prokaryotes but not in eukaryotes.
Eukaryotes also have mRNA that must have its introns excised and the mRNA transported out of the nucleus to the ribosomes .
The greater complexity of the eukaryote genome means that a greater variety and complexity of control mechanisms is necessary. There are also more steps in the transcription and translation process at which control of expression can occur in eukaryotes.

Out of the 3.2 billion base pairs that make up the human genome, there exists only 20,000 – 25,000 genes
If we were to spell the genome out, letter-by-letter, it would fill 1,000 phonebooks, each 1000pp long (1 million pages total!)
Each of the letters has to copied exactly (without error) each time a cell divides
Some of the proteins that are produced from the genome function to control the expression of other proteins
Taken together, we understand that human genetics is amazingly complex with layer upon layer of regulation and control
The most simple of activities – thinking about this lecture or tapping your foot – cause changes in gene expression which allow these actions to occur in the first place

Pandemics

1889-1890: Flu Pandemic
Originally called the “Asiatic Flu” or “Russian Flu”
Influenza A virus subtype H3N8 .
The first cases were observed in May 1889 in three separate locations
Bukhara in Central Asia
Athabasca in NW Canada
Greenland.
Rapid population growth of the 19th century, specifically in urban areas, helped the flu spread, and before long the outbreak had spread across the globe.
Though it was the first true epidemic in the era of bacteriology and much was learned from it.
Death Toll: 1 million
US – 10,481 deaths

1918-1920: Flu Pandemic
Wire service reports of a flu outbreak in Madrid in the spring of 1918 led to the pandemic being called the “Spanish flu.”
The pandemic was caused by Influenza A subtype H1N1
Over 1/3 of the world’s population was infected.
The 1918 flu pandemic was different from other influenza outbreaks because it was notorious for striking down hardy and completely healthy young adults, while leaving children and those with weaker immune systems still alive.
Death toll: 20 – 50 million
Mortality rate estimated at 10% - 20%
Up to 25 million deaths in first 25 weeks alone

1956-1958: Asian Flu
Asian Flu was a pandemic outbreak of Influenza A subtype H2N2 , that originated in China in 1956 and lasted until 1958.
In its two-year spree, Asian Flu traveled from the Chinese province of Guizhou to Singapore, Hong Kong, and the United States.
Death toll: 2 million
69,800 of those in the US alone.

1968: Flu Pandemic
A category 2 Flu pandemic sometimes referred to as “the Hong Kong Flu,” the 1968 flu pandemic was caused by Influenza A subtype H3N2, a genetic offshoot of the H2N2 subtype.
From the first reported case on July 13, 1968 in Hong Kong, it took only 17 days before outbreaks of the virus were reported in Singapore and Vietnam, and within three months had spread to The Philippines, India, Australia, Europe, and the United States.
Death toll: 1 million
Low mortality rate = 0.5%
500,000 residents of Hong Kong, approximately 15% of its population at the time.

2009-2010: Influenza Pandemic
The virus appeared to be a new strain of Influenza A subtype H1N1 that resulted from a previous triple reassortment of bird, swine, and human flu viruses and that further combined with a Eurasian pig flu virus.
Nearly 1/3 of adults over 60 had antibodies because of historic exposure to 1918 Spanish flu.
Most young people didn’t
Vaccine produced, but not available until 11/2009
Death toll: 151,700-575,400
US (4/12/09-4/10/10) – 60.8 million cases, 274,304 hospitalizations, 12,469 deaths

Complications of pneumococcal pneumonia include:
Infection of space between membranes that surround lungs and chest cavity (empyema)
Inflammation of sac surrounding heart (pericarditis)
Blockage of airway allowing air into the lungs with collapse within the lungs and collection of pus in the lungs
Pneumococcal pneumonia kills about 1 in 20 who get it

Blood infection
Sepsis

Symptoms of pneumococcal bacteremia include:

Chills
Low alertness
About 1 in 30 children and 1 in 7 adults die of pneumococcal bacteremia

Sepsis is an extreme response to a blood infection. Symptoms include:
Confusion or disorientation
Shortness of breath
High heart rate
Fever, shivering, or feeling very cold
Extreme pain or discomfort
Clammy or sweaty skin
Complications include kidney failure and damage to brain, lungs, or heart

Meningitis
Symptoms
Symptoms of pneumococcal meningitis include:
Stiff neck
Fever
Headache
Photophobia
Confusion
In babies, meningitis may cause poor eating and drinking, low alertness and vomiting
About 1 in 12 children and 1 in 7 adults die of pneumococcal meningitis
Survivors may have long-term problems – hearing loss or developmental delays

Major Lines of Defense
O r gans of the Immune System

Organs and Tissue
Skin

Bone Marrow
Where immune cells are created

Located in the upper chest

Largest internal organ of immune system
25% of blood from heart go through spleen on every beat

Organs and Tissue
Bloodstream

Lymphatic System
