Cells of the Immune System
Types of Immunity
Innate Immunity

Innate Immunity
An antigen is any substance, living (bacteria, virus, etc.) or inactivated (protein), that elicits a response from the immune system.
Innate immunity = natural immunity
Does not require prior exposure to an antigen
Can respond immediately to an invader
Recognizes broadly distributed antigen molecules rather than specific ones
Key players = white blood cells (leukocytes)
Come from myeloid progenitor cell
Collectively referred to as phagocytes or phagocytic cells = eat things
Granulocytes
Have granules containing substances to destroy invaders
Plymophonuclear lymphocytes
Because the nucleus has multiple lobes

Neutrophils
40% to 70% of total circulating white blood cells
F irst line of defense against infection – bacteria
Mature neutrophils have a half-life of about 2 to 3 days
During acute inflammatory responses (such as infection)  leave the peripheral blood circulation and enter tissues where the problem is occurring
Purpose = engulf and digest pathogens (phagocyte)

Eosinophils
U p to 5% of circulating white blood cells
Target organisms too large to be engulfed
K ill by secreting toxic substances ( eg , reactive oxygen compounds similar to those produced in neutrophils), major basic protein (which is toxic to parasites), eosinophil cationic protein, and several enzymes
Major source of inflammatory mediators = allergies

Basophils
< 5% of circulating white blood cells
Basophils have high-affinity receptors for IgE (a type of antibody) = triggers release of granules when bound by antigen
Release histimine = "allergy feeling" we have with seasonal allergies

Monocytes
Patrol and respond to problems
Macrophages, "big eater" in Greek, ingest and degrade bacteria
Monocytes and macrophages coordinate an immune response by notifying other immune cells of the problem.
Housekeepers = recycle dead cells, like red blood cells, and clearing away cellular debris
Monocyte in blood
Macrophage in tissue

Dendritic cells
Phagocytic leukocyte that circulates in the blood or takes up residence in the peripheral tissues
E ssential for immune surveillance and activation of adaptive immune cells
A ntigen presenting cells = engulf antigens and display them as small fragments on their surface to activate T lymphocytes

Natural Killer Cells
Subset of lymphocytes
I mportant for tumor surveillance
E xpress both activating and inhibitory receptors
Healthy cells – have markers that inhibit NK cells from killing
Virus infected cells and cancer cells – do not have markers = NK cells at tack!
Adaptive Immunity

Acquired Immunity
Natural acquired immunity
Naturally encounter a foreign invader  develop antibodies
Artificial acquired immunity
Intentionally exposed to foreign elements  develop antibodies

Lymphocytes
18% to 42% of circulating cells
Lymphocytes are divided into three major groups
T cells
B cells
N atural killer (NK) cells – part of innate immunity; you saw them.
major players in adaptive immunity

T lymphocytes
Recognize and remove virus infected cells and cancer cells
Have granules containing cytotoxins that cause apoptosis = cell death
Cytotoxic T cells (CD8)
Produce cytokines (signal proteins)
Activate other immune cells against bacteria and parasites
Alert B cells to make antibodies
Prevent adverse immune actions
Helper T cells (CD4)

B lymphocytes
Develop in bone marrow
Circulate as naïve cell (no surface receptors)
Eat antigens and create receptors
Future encounter = I know you!  activation
Make antibodies
Antibodies

Proteins = immunoglobulins
Created in response to a pathogen seen by the immune system
Antibodies

Immunoglobulin G
Gamma ( γ ) heavy chain
Monomer shape = one antibody
Short name = IgG
Binding sites = 2
Most abundant – 80% of circulating antibodies
Crosses placenta
Binds to phagocytes
Main antibody of secondary response
Vaccines target the production of these antibodies

Immunoglobulin M
Mu ( µ ) heavy chain
Pentamer shaper = five antibodies attached at Fc regions in a ring
Short name = IgM
Binding sites = 10
6% of circulating antibodies
Does not cross placenta
Main antibody of primary response
Monomer of IgM = B cell receptor

Immunoglobulin A
Alpha ( α ) heavy chain
Dimer shaper = two antibodies connected at Fc region
Short name = IgA
Binding sites = 4
13% of circulating antibodies
Does not cross placenta
Secreted in mucous, tears, saliva, colostrum

Immunoglobulin E
Epsilon ( ε ) heavy chain
Monomer shaper = one antibody
Short name = IgE
Binding sites = 2
0.002% of circulating antibodies
Does not cross placenta
Binds to mast cells and basophils
Allergy and antiparasitic activity

Immunoglobulin D
Delta ( δ ) heavy chain
Monomer shaper = one antibody
Short name = IgD
Binding sites = 2
1% of circulating antibodies
Does not cross placenta
B cell receptor

How to antibodies actually work?
The very tip of the antibody (variable region) is unique to the antigen the antibody was made against
Occasionally, we seen incidences where antibodies are made against a pathogen and the antigen binding site is so close to surface markers on our own healthy organs that the antibodies unintentionally attack. One classic example of this is when someone has strep throat that is not treated, the body creates antibodies to fight the infection. Unfortunately, the antigen binding site of these antibodies also binds to markers on the surface of the heart. This causes rheumatic fever.

What do antibodies do?
Neutralization: Antibodies neutralize pathogens by binding to them and blocking or inhibiting their binding or interaction with cells that they may infect.
For example, when HIV infects a cell it binds to surface markers on the cell that allow it to enter and cause infection. Antibodies against HIV bind to the virus, preventing it from attaching to the surface markers on cells.

What do antibodies do?
Opsonization: This action occurs when antibodies coat the pathogen, tagging it for phagocytosis by innate immune cells.
Many innate immune cells (monocytes, macrophages, neutrophils) have Fc receptors that recognize the constant region of antibodies, which allows them to engulf the pathogen and destroy it.

What do antibodies do?
Complement activation: Antibodies can activate complement through the classic pathway which recruits complement proteins to the pathogen for destruction.
Complement is a complex pathway to understand and well beyond the scope of his course but essentially when all of the proteins come together they create a hole in the pathogen causing it to burst (lyse) and fall apart.

The Scientific Method

The Scientific Method
Logical, problem-solving approach used by scientists
Typically starts with an observation that leads to a question and wanting to know more
Cancer biologist – might see that a certain kind of cancer cannot be treated with chemotherapy
Marine ecologist – might notice that a coral reef in the area is bleaching (turning white)
Steps to the Scientific Method
Make an observation
Form a question or identify a problem
Research – see if there are already answers out there
Form a hypothesis
Conduct experiment to test hypothesis
Draw conclusion from experiment data
Report interpretation
Why?

Scenario:
You are getting ready for an all-day study session about vaccines, and you need a good breakfast to get you started.
You are going to make a breakfast sandwich and coffee.
You put bread in the toaster, but it won’t toast.
The Scientific Method

You are a little frustrated because your eggs are now getting cold.
You grab your cup of coffee and ask…
The Scientific Method
Why?

You try to get the toaster to work and notice the light doesn’t come on and the coils do not get hot.
You make sure it is plugged in, and it is.
This makes you think…
The Scientific Method

To determine if your prediction that the outlet is broken is true, you need to come up with a hypothesis to test it.
A hypothesis isn’t always the right answer, but it is a way to help you determine the right answer.
The Scientific Method

You have made a hypothesis and now it is time to test it out.
Let's find another outlet and plug the toaster in.
The Scientific Method

Results of your test may support or contradict your hypothesis.
Support of hypothesis cannot conclusively provide it is correct but means that it is likely correct.
If results contradict a hypothesis, it is probably not true – unless there was a flaw in the test.
The Scientific Method

First step – observation of the need
Collaborative effort – academia, small biotech companies, large vaccine manufacturers, regulatory authorities/ government
Several obstacles to think about when creating a vaccine
Vaccine Development

Antigen
Blueprint that generates an immune response – must have!
Small portion of disease-causing organism – protein or polysaccharide
Toxin
DNA/RNA
Whole organism – weakened or attenuated

Preservatives
Prevent the vaccine from becoming contaminated once opened
Used for vaccines that have multiple doses – single dose vaccines do not have preservatives
Most common = 2-phenoxyethanol
Used for many years – safe and used in baby care products

Stabilizers
Prevent chemical reactions from occurring and keep vaccine components from sticking to vial
Regularly consumed for naturally produced by humans = no safety concern
Sugars (lactose, sucrose)
Amino acids (glycine)
Gelatin
Proteins (recombinant human albumin derived from yeast)

Surfactants
Reduce the surface tension of vaccine components
Acts as emulsifier = keeps ingredients blended together
Prevent settling and clumping
Used in many applications, such as ice cream

Diluent
Liquid used to dilute vaccine to correct concentration
Most common = sterile water

Residuals
Tiny amounts of various substance used during manufacturing – not active ingredients
Vary depending on manufacturing process – egg proteins, yeast, antibiotics
Measured in parts per million or parts per billion
Occasionally cause allergic reactions

Adjuvant
Improves immune response
Act by:
Keeping vaccine at injection site longer
Stimulating local immune cells
Providing way for vaccine to be absorbed
Most common = tiny amount of aluminum salts
Does not cause long-term health problems, regularly ingested through eating and drinking
Evaluating Vaccines for Efficacy and Safety

Pre-clinical Phase
Every vaccine begins at this phase. Vaccines are tested in an organ model to answer the following.
Is the vaccine safe? Adverse immune reactions are checked for in the established model organism.
What is a safe starting dose for humans? Multiple doses are tested to estimate the safest starting dose to use in humans during future phases.
Is this vaccine effective? This phase needs to show some efficacy in the animal model before it can ethically be tested in humans.
Typically, this phase takes approximately 1.5 years to complete and can cost an average of $20 million.
Success rate: 14% of vaccines make it thorough this phase.

Phase I
Phase I begins the initial trials in human subjects. Volunteers agree to be given the vaccine to test for safety and efficacy. Since this is the very first time the vaccine is given to people, we are talking about small test groups, only in the tens. In this phase, the following questions are answered.
Is the vaccine safe? Volunteers are checked for adverse immune system reactions, side effects, and evidence of toxicity.
What is the immune response in humans? The type of immune response and the extent of the immune response are determined in humans.
Typically, this phase takes several months to complete and can cost an average of $9 million.
Success rate: Approximately 32% of vaccines make it thorough this phase, which is only 4.5% of all vaccines that begin the clinical trial process.

Phase II
In this phase, volunteers are recruited from different age, gender, and ethnic backgrounds with various health statuses. Participants are placed in larger groups, typically in the hundreds. T he following questions are answered.
Is the vaccine still safe? The vaccine is continued to be assessed for safety and potential side effects are looked for in a more diverse study group.
How does the immune response differ among different people? The immune response is monitored, especially in regard to the subjects’
age, gender, and ethnicity. Additionally, the optimal dose is determined based on participant response to various doses.
What is the best way to administer the vaccine? The vaccine preparation and route of delivery are determined. Additionally, a schedule of immunization is created, which includes the age of first administration, number of doses, sequence of or interval between doses, duration of immunity, and potential need for booster immunizations.
This phase can take up to two years to complete and can cost anywhere from $9 million to $15 million.
Success rate: 50% of vaccines make it thorough this phase, which is only 2.2% of vaccines that begin the clinical trial process.

Phase III
Phase III is considered a long-term trial to catch any unexpected or rare events and to evaluate efficacy longer than the shorter, smaller phases. This phase recruits many more volunteers, in the thousands. The goal of this phase is to evaluate the vaccine in the most diverse population available, that should represent the population who will receive the vaccine. In this phase, the following questions are answered.
Is this vaccine still safe? The safety of the vaccine continues to be assessed and potential side effects or rare events are identified. This is performed for a longer duration in a more homogeneous target population.
This phase often lasts one to four years can cost anywhere from $40 million to $100+ million.
Success rate: At this point, 100% of vaccines make it thorough this phase, resulting in only 2.2% of vaccines that begin the clinical trials process seeking regulatory approval.
Is this vaccine effective? The clinical efficacy of the vaccine is determined, usually by monitoring the relative disease incidence among the vaccinated versus unvaccinated portion of the target population.

Regulatory Approval
After making it through phases I to III, the vaccine manufacturer must go before the regulatory authorities (often the FDA) for final approval before taking it to the market. During this review, the regulatory authorities review all data collected during all phases, specifically seeking evidence of safety and efficacy.
One in ten vaccines are not approved by the regulatory authorities and must continue data collection, or possibly scrap the entire vaccine study.
This phase takes an average of 1.32 years to complete and costs approximately $1.5 million to $2 million.
Success rate: 92% of vaccines make it thorough this phase, which is a only 2% of all vaccines that start the clinical trial process.

Phase IV
After a vaccine receives approval from regulatory authorities and is widely used in the general public, it enters what is referred to as phase IV. This is not a formal clinical trial phase that we are accustomed to, rather a continuous long-term assessment of the vaccine's efficacy and safety throughout the entire population. This continued assessment confirms that the clinical efficacy and safety determined during the clinical trials transfers to the general public.
At any time during this observation, if there are any concerns that are raised, the regulatory authorities may pause or completely stop the use of the vaccine until further investigation is completed to determine the impact of the concern.

Ethical Principles in Medicine

Principles of Ethics
Autonomy
Beneficence
Non-maleficence
Justice

Autonomy
Requires respect for the decisions of adults who have the ability to sound decisions =
self-determination
How do you know someone’s decision-making ability?
Intentionality – Is it believed that the individual will follow through with a decision?
Understanding – Does the individual understand the situation and the consequences and implications of a different decision?
Absence of controlling influences that determine their actions – Is the individual being influenced somehow against choosing reasonably?
Challenge: autonomy should reach beyond the realms of healthcare
Some individuals who have responsibilities for decision and policy making either do not understand the concept of autonomy or elect to ignore it

Beneficence
Embodies the concept of moral obligation to act in the best interest of others
Providing benefits
Balancing benefits against potential risk and harm
Calls for the commitment to:
Protect and defend the rights of others
Prevent others from harm
Remove conditions that might cause harm
Help those with disabilities
Rescue others in danger
Challenges: Can become an ethical issue when the unique setting of healthcare come to light, such as introducing a vaccine during a pandemic

Non-maleficence
Requires a commitment to not harm others in any way – part of Hippocratic Oath
Obligates one not to:
Kill
Cause pain or suffering
Incapacitate anyone
Cause anyone offence
Challenge: There is not enough scientific evidence to guarantee non-maleficence from decisions made or when the patient does not recognize  that the decision was made with the commitment of non-maleficence in mind

Justice
Calls to fairly distribute benefits, risks, costs, and resources as best as possible
Ideally, justice should offer each individual an equal share:
According to need
According to effort
According to contribution
According to merit
Challenge: Differing ideas of what is the bet possible way to treat every person

Ethics and Vaccination

Ethics and Vaccination
Herd Immunity

Ethics and Herd Immunity
Ethical questions related to a desire for herd immunity include:
Who bears the burden of vaccination and who benefits from herd immunity?
Should individuals be allowed to benefit when others assume some level of risk (e.g., from vaccination) and they do not?
Is there a line to be drawn between respecting vaccine refusers’ choices and maximizing the greater good through herd immunity?
Is there a responsibility on behalf of practitioners, policymakers, and researchers to be empathetic to the rationale underlying vaccine refusers’ decisions?
Vaccine Mandates and Objections
Vaccine Research and Testing

Informed Consent
National Childhood Vaccine Injury Act of 1986 – requires doctors to give vaccine recipients, parents, or legal guardians, a Vaccine Information Statement (VIS)
Purpose: provide basic information about vaccine risk and benefits so patient or parent can make informed decision
Opposition: concern that a regulated written consent may add unnecessary fear or concern

Access Issues
Many debates center on the evidence that access to vaccination depends to some extent on socioeconomic and racial ethnic minority status
Are all lives of equal value and equally deserving of opportunities to be protected by vaccination?
Challenges for children without insurance or in low-income families
Section 317 program – federal program to vaccinate underserved children
Access disparities affect adults
After controlling for economic status, researchers found racial ethnic minority adults less likely than whites to receive vaccines

Cultural Perspectives on  Vaccination
03.

Widely recognized that public opinion about vaccination come from deep-seated beliefs that are as varied as the individuals who have them Like many differences in society, this has results in much tension between divergent cultural viewpoints
Individuals vs Public Health

Individuals vs Public Health
Controversies over the efficacy, safety, and morality of requiring immunization stem from longstanding tensions between (1) protecting individual liberties and (2) safeguarding the public's health
H ealth mandates sacrifice individual autonomy to protect communities from disease
Unvaccinated individuals pose risk to children or people with medical conditions
Individualism
Need – good public health policies that balance the individual’s rights and the needs of the community
Public health officials and policy makers must recognize and respect diverse social and cultural perspectives
Transparency and education will help support the success and acceptance of vaccines

Religious Perspectives
Some religions and belief systems promote alternative perspectives toward vaccination.
No major religion holds a ban on vaccination, rather allow that each individual has the right to decide whether or not they want to use vaccines according to their own moral conscience.
Most references to religious objections to vaccines are generally based on:
the ethical dilemmas associated with using human tissue cells to create vaccines, and
beliefs that the body is sacred, should not receive certain chemical of blood or tissues from animals, and should be healed by God or other natural means

Religious Perspectives
Catholic Church recognizes that vaccines have value and that it is important to protect the individual and the community.
Belief: Members should seek alternatives, when available, to vaccines that are made using cell lines derived from aborted fetuses.
Most widely recognized concern is rubella vaccine – embryonic cell origin
Vatican decided – until an alternative becomes available, use the current vaccine
Some conservative Christian groups are opposed to vaccinations against diseases spread by sexual contact – HPV
Belief: providing vaccine to children tells them sex is a safe practice

Religious Perspective
Religious vaccine exemptions have risen in recent years
Small portion of population – center of controversy and media attention
Infections spread through small unvaccinated social and geographic communities
Examples
1990, Philadelphia – major measles outbreak among unvaccinated children of two fundamentalist churches that relied on prayer for healing
1994 – measles outbreak in Christian Science community that objected to vaccination – began with teenager from Illinois who attended boarding school in Missouri
2005 – measles outbreak in a religious community that opposed vaccination in Indiana – unvaccinated teen returned from a trip oversees

Suspicion and Mistrust of Vaccines
Suspicion or mistrust of vaccines is not uncommon – become more prominent with development of COVID-19 vaccine
Important to recognize historic events that have influenced decisions
Several studies have found that the legacy of racism in medicine and the Tuskegee Syphilis Study, a clinical trial conducted with African American males in Alabama who were denied appropriate treatment opportunities, are key factors underlying African Americans’ distrust of medical and public health interventions, including vaccination.
In some parts of Asia and Africa, mistrust in vaccines is often tied to "Western plot" theories that suggest vaccines are a means to sterilize or infect non-Western communities.

Suspicion and Mistrust of Vaccines
In Cameroon in 1990, rumors and fears that public health official were administering a range of childhood vaccines to sterilize women disrupted immunization efforts in the country.
In Tanzania in the mid-1990s, a missionary raised concerns about tetanus immunization that sparked rumors of sterilization and halted the immunization efforts.
In 2005, suspicion to the measles vaccine lead to decrease vaccination rates and increased infections in Nigeria.
The Taliban in Pakistan and Afghanistan have called polio vaccination an American ploy to sterilize Muslim populations and an attempt to avert Allah's will. The World Health Organization (WHO) and UNICEF have attempted to resolve this distrust, as they were able to do in Nigeria, but they have been banned from these countries. Pakistan and Afghanistan are the only countries where polio is still endemic

Anti-vaccination vs Vaccination Hesitance
04.

Anti-vaccination vs Vaccine Hesitance
Opposition to vaccines has been around as long as vaccines have been around
Criticisms take a variety of positions
Adamant anti-vaccination individuals (aka anti-vaxxers) – rarely persuaded to give up fixed belief that vaccines are harmful
More common is vaccination hesitance – focus of healthcare professionals and health education specialists
Typically, not entirely against vaccines – may have questions or concerns
Most concerns are related to safety and efficacy of the vaccine
Fears of safety are understandable
Many vaccines developed in the last 30 years
Concern for risk and long-term side effects

Myth #1
Vaccines cause autism
Originated in 1997 – study published by Andrew Wakefield in The Lancet – suggested MMR vaccine increased autism in British children
Paper has been completely discredited – serious procedural errors, undisclosed financial conflicts of interest, ethical violations
Wakefield lost medical license; paper retracted
Several studies have tested this hypothesis – none found a link between any vaccine and likelihood of developing autism

Myth #2
Infant immune systems can’t handle so many vaccines
Infant immune systems are stronger than people think
Based on antibodies present in blood – ability to respond to around 10,000 vaccines at once
IF all 14 vaccines were given at once – only use slightly more than 0.1% of a baby’s immune capacity
Immune system cells are constantly being replenished
Babies exposed to countless bacteria and vaccines every day

Myth #3
Natural immunity is better than vaccine-acquired immunity
In some cases, this is true – getting sick provides stronger immunity than vaccine
Danger far outweighs relative benefit
Example: If someone wanted to gain immunity to measles naturally – 1 in 500 change of death due to symptoms
In contrast severe allergic reactions to vaccine - <1 in 1 million

Myth #4
Vaccines contain unsafe toxins
Concerns for use of formaldehyde, mercury, or aluminum in vaccines
Toxic to humans at certain levels
Only trace levels used in vaccines
Body naturally produces higher rates of formaldehyde
No scientific evidence of chemicals in vaccines are harmful

Myth #5
Better hygiene and sanitation are actually responsible for decreased infections, not vaccines
Vaccines don’t deserve all of the credit
Sanitation, nutrition, and antibiotics have helped
Example: when measles vaccine was introduced in 1963 – infections were steady at 400,000 cases each year; rates dropped to only 25,000 by 1970
Hygiene habits and sanitation did not change during this time
Example: cases dropped from 20,000 in 1990 to 1,500 in 1993 – vaccine introduced in 1990

Myth #6
Vaccines aren’t worth the risk
No credible study linking vaccines to long term health conditions
Immediate danger = allergic reaction – so rare they can’t be truly calculated
Incident rate – usually around 1 in 1-2 million injections

Myth #7
Vaccines can infect my child with the disease it’s trying to prevent
Can cause mild symptoms – response of immune system to foreign antigens
Common misconception - symptoms = infection
Only one recorded instance where vaccine caused disease
Oral polio vaccine (OPV) – no longer used in US

Myth #8
We don’t need to vaccinate because infection rates are already so low in the United States
Infectious disease rates for vaccine-preventable disease are very low due to herd immunity
Does not give disease chance to establish itself
Protects the unvaccinated – infants, pregnant women, elderly, those with weak immune system
If too many don’t get vaccinated = opens opportunity for viruses and bacteria to establish themselves and spread

A Public Health Approach to Cholera
03
Just a reminder…

London – early 1800s
Cholera (fatal intestinal disease) was rampant
Caused death to tens of thousands of people within hours of first symptoms
Before bacteria and viruses were recognized as cause of disease
Popular opinion = cholera caused by bad air coming from rotten organic matter
John Snow (British physician) had different ideas
Believed illness was spread by contaminated water supply
Sewage was routinely dumped into the Thames River and cesspools near town
Worked to trace source of cholera outbreak
Considered father of modern epidemiology – changed the way we look at disease and how they spread

Snow conducted public health surveillance
Assisted by local clergyman, Reverend Henry Whitehead
Tracked down and interviewed cholera victims and families
Recorded deaths from cholera on a map of London
Saw a large cluster of cases were occurring in a specific area
What is the problem?
These efforts highlight the benefits of linking scientific inquiry with engagement of community stakeholders and shared ownership for health.

Snow examined data to identify risk factors
Tried to determine cause of death by using patterns of where cases were occurring
Based on previous work, he believed waster was a potential cholera source
Marked all water pumps on map
Prompted him to identify where people drew their water from
What is the cause?

After identifying likely risk factors  evaluate potential interventions
Risk factor = water supply from Broad Street pump
Snow understood the interventions required control the cholera outbreak needed to
Stop exposure to the contaminated water supply (i.e. Broad Street pump)
Stop exposure to the entire supply of contaminated water in the area
What works?

Intervention implemented – removal of handle from Broad Street pump
Prevented people from drawing water
Took a long battle with politicians and water companies…
Snow convinced British government that water contaminated with sewage was the source of cholera and other diseases
Resulted in implementation of policies and laws for water sanitation
How do you do it?

Tractor-associated Fatalities
In 1982, an epidemiologist in the Georgia Department of Public Health became interested in the number of deaths associated with farm tractors
He determined he could examine this problem by using readily available data
D eath certificate records that were included in an existing surveillance system.
He obtained the death certificate records for all deaths that had occurred in Georgia during 1971 through 1981 that were associated with farm tractor incidents.

Descriptive Epidemiology
166 farm tractor-associated deaths
When was the population effected?

Descriptive Epidemiology
166 farm tractor-associated deaths
Who was the population effected?

Descriptive Epidemiology
Where was the population effected?
An Epidemiology Investigation of Legionnaires’ Disease

Background
After such a substantial number of American Legion Conference attendees with similar symptoms were admitted to hospital emergency departments or examined in doctors’ offices, an initial investigation began.

Step 1: establish the existence
Epidemiologists were able to establish the existence of the Legionnaires’ disease outbreak by reviewing records and data
Doctors’ reports, hospital reports, lab results, public health lab confirmation tests, etc.
Thought at the time to be just a pneumonia
Cases w ere indeed higher than normal from the previous weeks or months.

Step 2: prepare for fieldwork
I nvolved preparing for field work by:
researching the outbreak,
gathering necessary supplies and equipment, and
making travel arrangements.
The epidemiologists also consulted with other entities, such as health care workers and the local health department, to determine everyone’s role in the investigation and to learn who the local contacts would be after arriving on the scene.

Step 3: verify diagnosis
T he epidemiologists ensured that the problem was accurately diagnosed by speaking with patients and reviewing laboratory findings and clinical test results.

Step 4: defining and identifying cases
Case definition established to determine if a person “fits”
Four components typically are included in a case definition, including
Clinical information about the disease — What signs and symptoms have been observed?
Characteristics about the persons who are affected — Do any commonalities exist among those who have been ill?
Information about the location or place — Where are the affected persons located?
Specification of time during which the illness onset occurred — What date or time did the illness begin to occur, and what was the symptom duration?

Step 5: using descriptive epidemiology
involved using descriptive epidemiology to describe Legionnaires’ disease and orient the data by identifying:
what,
who,
where,
when,
why, and
how.
The epidemiologist proceeded to study the:
dates,
times,
places, and
persons.:
Number of Legionnaires’ Disease Cases
Rates of Illness Among Delegates

Step 6: develop hypothesis
A hypothesis is an educated guess of how and why an event occurred (i.e. the legionnaires’ outbreak)
Statists are used to test the hypothesis – doesn’t always mean that it will be right
One hypothesis stated that the illness was associated with convention attendees who were guests at or had visited one particular hotel during their time at the convention .

Step 8: refining hypothesis
Not necessary!
Five months after the first cases of Legionnaires’ disease occurred, results of the case-control study indicated that spending time in the lobby of Hotel A was a risk factor for illness.
In December 1976, a CDC laboratorian successfully located the source bacteria after continuing to test the specimens that were thought to be dead.
Investigators concluded that those who had become ill in Philadelphia had inhaled air from the air-conditioning unit at Hotel A, either as guests or while walking on the sidewalk outside the hotel.

Step 9: implementing controls
To minimize growth of the bacteria that causes Legionnaires’ disease, controls were implemented, such as chlorination of water and testing of industrial air-handling systems.

Step 10: communicate findings
I nvestigation findings were communicated to
local health authorities,
the medical community,
the general public, and
lawmakers and other leaders.
During the briefing, epidemiologists describe
what they did and how they did it,
what they learned, and
what should be done about the illness.

Measles
CLS-N390 Vaccines: Science and Society

Rubeola virus
S ingle-stranded RNA virus
Helical capsid
E nvelope
M ost closely resembles rinderpest – pathogen of cattle (eradicated)
S ame family as mumps virus
H umans are only reservior

US - 1,282
Brazil – 20,901
DRC – 82,290
2019 Data
Most cases seen in winter and early spring

Highly contagious
Lives in nose and throat mucous
Spread via coughing and sneezing
Can spread up to four days before through four days after rash appears
Lives up to two hours in airspace after infected person leaves
Infection from breathing in contaminated air or touching surface
Tough eyes, nose, mouth
R 0 = 12 – 18
Herd immunity = 94% of population must be vaccinated
Transmission

Appear 7 – 14 days after contact with virus
High fever (spike >104°F)
Cough
Runny nose
Red, watery eyes
2 -3 days after symptoms – Koplik spots
Tiny white spots that appear inside mouth
Symptoms

3 – 5 days after symptoms  rash
Flat red spots appear on face at hairline and spread downward to neck, truck, arms, legs, feet
Raised bumps may appear on top of flat red spots
Spots may become joined together as they spread
Fever may spike when rash appears
Symptoms

Complications
More likely in <5 years, >20 years, pregnant women, immunocompromised
Common complications
Ear infections – 1 in 10 children
Diarrhea - <1 in 10 people
Severe complications
Hospitalization – 1 in 5 unvaccinated people
Pneumonia – 1 in 20 children
Most common cause of death
Encephalitis (brain swelling) – 1 in 1,000 children
Lead to convulsions – deafness or intellectual disabilities
Death – 1 to 3 in 1,000 children
Die of respiratory and neurologic complications
Complications during pregnancy – premature birth or low birth weight
Vaccine

Measles Vaccine Timeline
Live attenuated and inactivated “killed” vaccines ( Edmonston strain)
Live further attenuated vaccine (Schwarz strain)
Killed vaccine withdrawn from market
Live further attenuated vaccine ( Edmondston -Enders strain)
Two dose schedule recommended
Licensure of measles-mumps-rubella vaccine
Licensure of measles-mumps-rubella-varicella vaccine
1963
1965
1967
1968
1971
1989
2005
Measles first isolated in 1954 by John Ender

Characteristics of Vaccine
Only available as combination vaccine – MMR or MMRV
Efficacy – 95% at 12 months; 98% at 15 months
Immunity – lifelong
2 dose series – 2-5% don’t respond to first dose
Given to children 12 months or older; others without immunity

Components of Vaccine
Comes as freeze-dried (lyophilized) powder
Live attenuated virus created in chicken embryo fibroblast tissue culture
Reconstituted with sterile, preservative-free water
Human albumin
Contains small amount of …
Neomycin
Sorbitol
Gelatin

Impact of Vaccine
Before 1963
3 to 4 million cases globally
I n US
500,000 cases
48,000 hospitalizations
1,000 casess with encephalitis
400 - 500 deaths

Impact of Vaccine
Measles declared eliminated in 2000
Last measles death in 2015
Concern of Measles Resurgence

Meningococcal Disease
CLS-N390 Vaccines: Science and Society

Gram negative diplococci (kidney bean shape)
Picture shows bacteria eaten by neutrophil
Same genus as N. gonorrhoeae
Six serogroups – A, B, C, W, X, and Y
Three cause most illness in US – B, C, Y
Neisseria meningitidis

Transmission
Approximately 10% of population naturally has in back of throat = carrier
Spread via respiratory and throat secretions
Requires close or lengthy contact
Not as contagious as cold or flu
Cannot get from air
At higher risk:
Children <1, adolescents, 16 – 23
Group settings – colleges
Immunocompromised individuals

Symptoms
Incubation time: 2 – 10 days (3 – 4 more often)
Meningococcal Meningitis | Meningococcal Septicemia
Most common symptoms:
Fever 
Headache 
Stiff neck 

Often additional symptoms:
Nausea
Vomiting
Photophobia (eye sensitivity to light)
Altered mental status (confusion) | Bacteria enter bloodstream, damaging walls of blood vessels. Causes bleeding into the skin and organs

Symptoms:
Fever and chills 
Fatigue
Vomiting 
Cold hands and feet 
Severe aches or pains in muscles, joints, chest, or abdomen 
Rapid beathing 
Diarrhea 
Dark purple rash
Vaccine

Meningococcal Vaccine
First licensed – 1974
Current version licensed – 1981
Polysaccharide-protein conjugate vaccine
A, C, W, Y
Typically give 11-12 with booster at 16
Or one dose at 13-15 if not given earlier

Meningococcal Vaccine
Menactra
(Sanofi Pasture)
MenACWY -D
Licensed in 2005
Single dose vial
No preservatives or adjuvants
Protein carrier = diphtheria toxoid
Approved for 9 months – 55 years
Menveo (Novartis)
MenACWY -CRM
Licensed in 2010
MenA component – lyophilized
MenC,W,Y in saline – used to dilute MenA portion
No preservatives or adjuvants
Protein carrier = cross reacting material of diphtheria toxoid
Approved for 2-55
MenHibrix (GlaxoSmithKline)
Hib- MenCY -TT
Licensed in 2012
MenCY
H. influenzae type B
Lyophilized – dilute with 0.9% saline
Protein carrier = tetanus toxoid
Approved as 4 dose series – 2, 4, 6, and 12-18 months

Mumps
CLS-N390 Vaccine: Science and Society

Mumps virus
Single-stranded RNA virus
H elical capid
E nvelope
S ame family as measles virus
H umas are only reservior

Transmission
Moderately to highly contagious
Spread though direct contact with saliva or respiratory droplets from mouth, nose, or throat
Coughing, sneezing, or talking
Sharing items that may have saliva on them  – water bottles or cups
Participating in close-contact activities with others – playing sports, dancing, and kissing
Can be spread from a few days before to five days after salivary glands swell
R o value = 10 – 12
Herd immunity = 92% of population must be vaccinated

Symptoms
16 – 18 days after infection, may range from 12 – 25 days
Some people have mild symptoms (cold), or no symptoms at all
Best known for puffy cheeks and swollen jaw – caused by swollen salivary glands under ears (parotitis)
Other symptoms may begin a few days before
Fever
Headache
Muscle aches
Tiredness
Loss of appetite

Complications
Most likely = adults
Orchitis - inflammation of the testicles
May lead to a decrease in testicular size (testicular atrophy)
May lead to temporary sterility or decreased fertility
Oophoritis - inflammation of the ovaries
Mastitis – inflammation of the breast tissue
Pancreatitis - inflammation of the pancreas
Meningitis – inflammation of tissue covering brain and spinal cord
Deafness
Vaccine

Mumps Vaccine Timeline
Inactivated vaccine developed – discontinued in mid-1970s
1948
1967
1977
1989
2005
2006
Live attenuated vaccine (Jeryl Lynn strain) licensed – still in use
Single dose vaccine recommended for routine use
T wo dose vaccine implemented in children - MMR
Licensure of measles-mumps-rubella-varicella  vaccine
Two dose vaccine added to healthcare workers and international travelers

Characteristics of Vaccine
Only available as combination vaccine – MMR or MMRV
Efficacy – 78% at 12 months; 88% at 15 months
Immunity – lifelong
2 dose series
Given to children 12 months or older; others without immunity

Components of Vaccine
Comes as freeze-dried (lyophilized) powder
Live attenuated virus c reated in chicken embryo fibroblast tissue culture
Reconstituted with sterile, preservative-free water
Human albumin
Contains small amount of …
Neomycin
Sorbitol
Gelatin

Impact of Vaccine
Most kids had mumps before adolescence
231 cases
152,209 cases
B e fore 1967
1968
2003
Outbreaks still happen in settings with close contact – college campuses, close-knit communities, large gatherings

Multi-state outbreak in college-age students – 6,500 cases
Close knit religious community in New York – 3,000 cases
Close knit community in NW Arkansas – 3,000 cases
Two large outbreaks in IA and IL – several hundred college students
Adult migrants detained in 57 facilities – 898 cases

P e rtussis
CLS-N390 Vaccines: S c ience and S o ciety

Bordetella pertussis
Gram negative bacilli
V ery small
F astidious = hard to grow
H umans are only reservior
2002 Data - WHO

Transmission
Very contagious – aerosolizes
Spread via coughing or sneezing – or being around each other and sharing air space
Bacteria attach to cilia (tiny, hair-like extensions) lining upper respiratory tract
Hairs help expel bacteria into the air
Many babies who get pertussis are infected by siblings, parents, or caregivers who don’t know they have it
R 0 value = 15 – 17; 94% vaccination for herd immunity

Symptoms
Typically develop within 5 to 10 days of exposure
Catarrhal Stage
Early stage: 1 – 2 weeks
Runny nose
Low-grade fever
Mind, occasional cough
Highly contagious
Paroxysmal Stage
1 – 6 weeks; may be as long as 10 weeks
Fits of numerous, rapid coughs followed by “whooping:” sound
Vomiting and exhaustion after coughing fits
Convalescent Stage
2 – 3 weeks
Susceptible to other respiratory diseases
Recovery is gradual
Coughing lessens, but fits may return

Complications
1 out of 4 (25%) get pneumonia
1 out of 100 (1%) have convulsions (violent, uncontrolled shaking)
3 out of 5 (61%) have apnea (slowed or stopped breathing)
1 out of 300 (0.3%) have encephalopathy (disease of brain)
1 out of 100 (1%) die
Vaccine

Pertussis Vaccine Timeline
1914
1930s
1948
2005
Whole-cell vaccine licensed
Formalin inactivated whole-cell vaccine developed
Combined with diphtheria and tetanus (DTP)
Acellular vaccine licensed

Characteristics of Vaccine
Subunit vaccine – contains purified, inactivated components of B. pertussis cells
Efficacy – 80% - 85%
Immunity – approximately 5 - 10 years
Several vaccines available – different components
<12 months 4 doses; 12+ months – 3 doses;
booster @ 4-6 years; booster for family

Vaccine Options
DTaP – pediatric diphtheria toxoid, tetanus toxoid, and acellular pertussis
<7 years old
Tdap – adult diphtheria toxoid, tetanus toxoid, and acellular pertussis
>7 years old
DTaP- HepB -IPV – combination of pediatric diphtheria toxoid, tetanus toxoid, acellular pertussis, hepatitis B, poliovirus
DTaP-IPV-Hib – combination of pediatric diphtheria toxoid, tetanus toxoid, acellular pertussis, poliovirus, and Haemophilus influenzae type B
Other
Vaccine Impact in US
Vaccine Impact in US

Pneumococcal Disease
CLS-N390 Vaccines: Science and Society

Gram positive cocci in chains -- Often hang out in pairs -- Lancet shape (peanut) Same genus as strep throat ( S. pyogenes ) and neonatal meningitis ( S. agalactiae ) 92 serotypes --10 most common cause about 62% of invasive disease worldwide
Streptococcus pneumoniae

Transmission
Found all over -- Normal bacteria in back of throat for some – carrier
Spread via direct contact with respiratory secretions – saliva and mucous
Many people have had it in nose or throat without being ill
Age and heath conditions increase risk for pneumococcal disease

At Risk
Children at risk | Adults as risk
Younger than 2 years old and those with: 
Sickle cell disease, no spleen, HIV infection, cancer, or another condition that weakens the immune system
Diabetes
Nephrotic syndrome
Chronic heart, lung, or kidney disease
Cochlear implants
Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) leaks (escape of the fluid that surrounds the brain and spinal cord) | Adults 65+

Adults with: 
Sickle cell disease, no spleen, HIV infection, cancer, or another condition that weakens the immune system
Diabetes
Nephrotic syndrome
Chronic heart, lung, kidney, or liver disease
Cochlear implants
CSF leaks
Alcoholism

Adults who smoke cigarettes or have chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), emphysema, and asthma

Symptoms
May cause many different types of infections
Middle ear infection
Sinus infection
Pneumoniae
Meningitis
Bloodstream infection
Sepsis

Sinus infection
Middle ear infection
Symptoms
Common symptoms include:
Headache
Stuffy or runny nose
Loss of sense of smell
Facial pain or pressure
Postnasal drip
Complications are rare, by may be:
Infection of tissue surrounding eye
Bone infection
Abscess (collection of pus)
Common symptoms of otitis media include:
Ear pain
A red, swollen ear drum
Fever
Sleepiness
Usually mild and more common than severe forms of disease
Children with repeated ear infections may need ear tubes

Pneumonia
Symptoms
Common symptoms include:
Fever and chills
Cough
Rapid breathing or difficulty breathing
Chest pain
Older adults may experience confusion or low alertness rather than common symptoms
Complications of pneumococcal pneumonia include:
Infection of space between membranes that surround lungs and chest cavity (empyema)
Inflammation of sac surrounding heart (pericarditis)
Blockage of airway allowing air into the lungs with collapse within the lungs and collection of pus in the lungs
Pneumococcal pneumonia kills about 1 in 20 who get it

Blood infection
Sepsis
Symptoms
Symptoms of pneumococcal bacteremia include:
Fever
Chills
Low alertness
About 1 in 30 children and 1 in 7 adults die of pneumococcal bacteremia
Survivors may have loss of limb(s)
Sepsis is an extreme response to a blood infection. Symptoms include:
Confusion or disorientation
Shortness of breath
High heart rate
Fever, shivering, or feeling very cold
Extreme pain or discomfort
Clammy or sweaty skin
Complications include kidney failure and damage to brain, lungs, or heart

Meningitis
Symptoms
Symptoms of pneumococcal meningitis include:
Stiff neck
Fever
Headache
Photophobia
Confusion
In babies, meningitis may cause poor eating and drinking, low alertness and vomiting
About 1 in 12 children and 1 in 7 adults die of pneumococcal meningitis
Survivors may have long-term problems – hearing loss or developmental delays
Vaccine

Pneumococcal Vaccine Timeline
1977
1983
2000
2010
14-valent polysaccharide vaccine licensed
23-valent polysaccharide vaccine licensed
7-valent polysaccharide conjugate vaccine licensed
13-valent polysaccharide conjugate vaccine licensed
2011
13-valent vaccine approved for 50+

Pneumococcal Vaccines
Pneumovax 23 (Merck)
23-valent polysaccharide
23 types of bacteria causing 60%-76% of invasive disease
Single vial or 5 dose vial
Phenol preservative
For 2 yrs +
60% - 70% effective
Less effective against pneumococcal pneumonia
7 valent polysaccharide conjugate
7 types of bacteria
Conjugated to nontoxic variant of diphtheria toxin (CRM197)
13-valent polysaccharide conjugate
13 types of bacteria
Conjugated to CRM197
Polysorbate 80 – surfactant
Aluminum phosphate – adjuvant
Succinate buffer
No thimerosal preservative

Rubella (German measles)
CLS-N390 Vaccines: S c ience and Society

Rubella virus
German measles
Single-stranded RNA virus
Pleomorphic nucleocapid envelope
H ost-derived lipid membrane
Same family as Eastern equine encephalitis (EEE), Western equine encephalitis (WEE), Venezuelan equine encephalitis (VEE)
Humans are only reservoir

China 2,202
Mosambique
21.6% of cases
2020 Case Numbers – 10,194
India
Nigeria
Democratic Republic of Congo
65.5% of global cases come from five countries

Transmission
Moderately contagious
Spread up to one week before rash and seen days after
25% - 50% do not develop rash – still spread
Spread via respiratory route – coughing and sneezing
Transmission from mom to baby during pregnancy
R 0 value = 10 – 12; 92% vaccination for herd immunity

Symptoms
Children and Adults
Mild, few symptoms – most common = red rash on face that spreads to rest of body – lasts 3 days
Other symptoms 1 – 5 days before rash:
Low grade fever*
Headache*
Mild pink eye*
General discomfort*
Swollen and enlarged lymph nodes
Cough
Runny nose
Up to 70% of women may experience arthritis

Complications
Most serious complication that can develop
Unvaccinated pregnant women – may lead to miscarriage or death just after birth
Congenital Rubella Syndrome (CRS)
Mom passes virus to baby – more common if infected in first trimester
Heart problems
Loss of hearing and eyesight
Intellectual disabilities
Liver or spleen damage
Vaccine

Rubella Vaccine Timeline
Three vaccines developed:
D uck embryo
D og kidney
R abit kidney
H uman diploid fibroblast strain licensed – all others discontinued
Licensure of measles-mumps-rubella (duck embryo) vaccine
Licensure of measles-mumps-rubella-varicella vaccine
1969
1971
1979
2005
Rubella first isolated in 1962 by Parkman and Weller

Characteristics of Vaccine
Only available as combination vaccine – MMR or MMRV
Efficacy – 95% at 12 months
Immunity – lifelong; 90% show at least 15 year protection
At least one dose needed; two is better
Given to children 12 months or older; others without immunity

Components of Vaccine
Comes as freeze-dried (lyophilized) powder
Live attenuated virus created in human diploid fibroblast tissue culture
Reconstituted with sterile, preservative-free water
Human albumin
Contains small amount of …
Neomycin
Sorbitol
Gelatin

Before 1969 – common and widespread
Eradicated in 2004
less than 10 cases each year (all foreign travelers)

Tetanus
CLS-N390 Vaccines: S c ience and Society

Clostridium tetani
A naerobic (no oxygen) Gram positive bacillus
C reates spores to survive
F ound all over – in dirt and dust
M ember of Clostridium genus
S ome species are not a problem, others cause gangreen ( C. perfringens ), botulism ( C. botulinum ), and diarrhea ( C. difficile )

Transmission
Spores enter through broken skin  - injury or contaminated items
More common routes of entry:
Wounds contaminated with dirt, poop, or spit
Wounds caused by puncture – nail or needle
Burns
Crush injury
Injury with dead tissue
Less common routes of entry:
Clean superficial wounds (only top layer of skin gone)
Surgical procedures
Insect bites
Dental infections
Compound fractures
Chronic sores and infections
IV drug users
Intramuscular injections

Tetanus
Incubation period – 3 – 21 days (10 day average)
May range from one day to several months
Most occur within 14 days
Produces two toxins – one is a neurotoxin
One of the most potent toxins known, based on weight
Lethal dose = 2.5 nanograms per kilogram of body weight
154 lb – 175 nanograms
1 nanogram = 1 billionth of a gram

Symptoms
Often referred to as lockjaw
Tightening of jaw muscle seen in 80% of cases
Jaw cramping
Sudden, involuntary muscle tightening (spasms) often in stomach
Painful muscle stiffness all over
Trouble swallowing
Seizures
Headache
Fever and sweating
Changes in blood pressure and heart rate

Complications
Serious health problems include:
Uncontrolled, involuntary tightening of vocal cords – laryngospasm
Broken bones
Infections while in hospital
Blockage of main artery of lung or branches by blood clots that has traveled from elsewhere in the body – pulmonary embolism
Pneumonia from breathing in foreign materials – aspiration pneumonia
Breathing difficulty, possibly leading to death – 1 to 2 in 10 cases

Intended to elicit anti-toxin antibodies Does not target bacteria
Vaccine

Tetanus toxoid first produced in 1924
Used extensively in servicemen during World War II
History of Tetanus Vaccine Use
70 cases in World War I
12 cases in World War II

Characteristics of Vaccine
Toxoid produced by growing bacteria in liquid media
Efficacy – approximately 100%; adsorbed is better
Immunity – approximately 10 years
Filtrate incubated with formaldehyde – toxin  toxoid
Adsorbed onto an aluminum salt OR liquid
<12 months 4 doses; 12+ months – 3 doses;
booster @ 4-6 years; booster @ 11-12 years

Vaccine Options
DT – pediatric diphtheria toxoid and tetanus toxoid
Given when child has pertussis contraindication
DTaP – pediatric diphtheria toxoid, tetanus toxoid, and acellular pertussis
<7 years old
Td – adult diphtheria toxoid and tetanus toxoid
Provided as booster shot every 10 years
Given if childhood series is missed and pertussis contraindication
Tdap – adult diphtheria toxoid, tetanus toxoid, and acellular pertussis
Given when childhood series is missed before 7
>7 years old
DTaP- HepB -IPV – combination of pediatric diphtheria toxoid, tetanus toxoid, acellular pertussis, hepatitis B, poliovirus
DTaP-IPV-Hib – combination of pediatric diphtheria toxoid, tetanus toxoid, acellular pertussis, poliovirus, and Haemophilus influenzae type B
Other
Tetanus Vaccine Globally
Tetanus Deaths Globally

Tetanus in US
26 cases in 2019
Case numbers declined >95%  and deaths >99% since 1947
Sporadic cases in adults who don’t get vaccinated or stay up on booster

extracts.title: The History of Vaccination and Current Recommendations.pptx

segments.text:
CLS-N390 Vaccines: S c ience a nd Society
The History of Vaccination and Current Recommendations

L e arning Objectives
Recognize and describe the contributions of individuals in the advancement of vaccination practices.
01
02
Create a timeline of major events and efforts leading to current vaccination practices.
03
Review the history of modern vaccination recommendations.
04
Reflect on the receipt of vaccines as a child and adult for you and your family.
05
Compare and contrast current vaccine recommendations for children under 6 years old, 7-18 years old, and adults.

Introduction
The history of vaccination goes all the way back to 1000 AD when soars from smallpox survivors were scratched into the skin of others to provide immunity.

Terminology
Inoculation = introduction of material into a person to provoke immunity to a disease
Vaccination = introduction of a material designed to provoke an immune response that will provide protection from a related disease agent
Smallpox = disease caused by the variola virus
Variolation = smallpox immunization technique once practiced in many locations worldwide – transfer of matter from a smallpox sore into a cut in the skin of an uninfected person

Infectious Diseases and Vaccination up to Edward Jenner
Unknown fever struck English royal family, killing Prince Henry at 18
Didn’t know what it was – now know it was Salmonella typhi = typhoid fever
1612
French Jesuits knowingly bring smallpox to North America
Entire villages of Native Americans exterminated to due disease
1625
Smallpox outbreak hits colonial settlers in MA – 20 settlers from Mayflower
Devastated Native American population
John Winthrop, governor, said it was “divine intervention”
1633
Epidemic of yellow fever in Cuba and Yucatan  quarantine was accepted in America
Boston quarantines all ships from West Indies
Outbreak of measles in 1657
1648

Infectious Diseases and Vaccination up to Edward Jenner
Death of Chinese emperor from smallpox – new emperor supported inoculation
Inoculated his children and recommended continuation for all descendants
1661
Queen Mary II died of variola hemorrhagica = internal and pustule bleeding due to smallpox
Age 32
1659
Smallpox epidemic in Boston
Zabdiel Boylston inoculated 248 people, 6 died = 3% fatality rate
Smallpox killed 844 = 14% fatality rate
First variolation in America
1721
Benjamin Franklin lost son to smallpox in 1736
Large outbreak in Charleston, SC in 1738 killed 14% infected and half of Cherokee Indian population
1736 - 1738

Explosion of science and technology beginning mid-1800s lead to a lot of discoveries and advancement in infectious diseases and vaccination
You will continue the story

Vaccination Recommendations
Resources from CDC

Introduction to Vaccine Recommendations
Before 1985 – only seven vaccines recommended
Today – children under 2 years get vaccinated for 14 potentially serious diseases
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) makes vaccination schedule based on recommendations from Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP) – approved by American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) and American Academy of Family Physicians (AAFP)
Who makes recommendations?

Provided to ACIP for Recommendation
The safety and efficacy of the vaccine when given at specific ages.
Only vaccines licensed by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) are recommended, and vaccine makers must conduct rigorous tests during clinical trials to show that a vaccine is safe and effective at specific ages.
The severity of the disease.
Vaccines recommended for children are meant to prevent diseases that can be serious for them, potentially causing long-term health problems, or even death.
How many children get the disease if there is no vaccine.
Vaccines that do not provide benefit to many children may not be recommended.
The differences in how well a vaccine works for children of different ages.
The ability of vaccines to help the body produce immunity can vary depending on the age when the vaccine is given.

The science of vaccines
CLS-N390 Vaccines: Science and S o ciety

Learning Objectives
Incorporate all components of a vaccine to build one.
Describe what a vaccine is.
Compare and contrast the type of vaccines produced and under investigation.
Diagram the process used to evaulate vaccines for safety and efficacy.
01
03
02
04
Apply the scientific method to a given scenario.
05

What is a vaccine?
A biological product that can be used to safely induce an immune response that confers protection against infection and/or disease on subsequent exposure to a pathogen (Pollard & Bijker , 2021, p. 83).
Does not cause disease
Artificially mimics infection
Generates primary immune response = creation of memory B cells
How does a vaccine work?

How have vaccines helped?
Pollard, A. J., & Bijker , E. M. (2020). A guide to vaccinology: from basic principles to new developments. Nature Reviews Immunology , 1-18.
Types of Vaccines
Vaccine Categories

Live vaccines
No modification of virus or bacteria
Given small amount of infectious agent – mild disease occurs
No longer used
Example: smallpox
Modified to a weakened or inactivated state – remove part that is pathogenic
Widely used – elicits immune response
Examples: measles, mumps, rubella, chicken pox, influenza, oral polio….
Live active
Live attenuated

Non-living vaccines
Whole cell used – killed to render nonviable
Examples: whole-cell pertussis, influenza, polio, hepatitis A, rabies, 1896 - typhoid
Modification of toxin produced by bacteria
Anti-toxoid antibody production
Short lived ~10 years
Examples: tetanus, 1923 - diphtheria
Whole killed
Toxoid

Non-living vaccines
Purified protein, recombinant protein, polysaccharide, peptide
Only uses a portion of the pathogen to create an immune response
Examples: pertussis, influenza, hepatitis B, meningococcus, pneumococcus, hepatitis A, 1970 - anthrax
Created to look like the virus, but not be the virus
Examples: human papillomavirus, 1986 – hepatitis B
Subunit
Virus-like particle

Non-living vaccines
Gram negative bacteria outer cell  membrane with antigen attached
Used for Gram negative bacteria
Example: 1987 – Haemophilus influenzae type B
Polysaccharide chain used to elicit immune response – bound to carrier protein to make it travel better
Typically seen with bacteria
Example: Haemophilus influenzae type B, pneumococcal, typhoid,  1987 - meningococcal
Outer membrane vesicle
Protein-polysaccharide conjugate

Novel Vaccines
mRNA vaccines
Viral vector vaccines
Microneedle vaccines
The Scientific Method

The Scientific Method
Logical, problem-solving approach used by scientists
Typically starts with an observation that leads to a question and wanting to know more
Cancer biologist – might see that a certain kind of cancer cannot be treated with chemotherapy
Marine ecologist – might notice that a coral reef in the area is bleaching (turning white)
Steps to the Scientific Method
Make an observation
Form a question or identify a problem
Research – see if there are already answers out there
Form a hypothesis
Conduct experiment to test hypothesis
Draw conclusion from experiment data
Report interpretation
Why?

Scenario:
You are getting ready for an all-day study session about vaccines, and you need a good breakfast to get you started.
You are going to make a breakfast sandwich and coffee.
You put bread in the toaster, but it won’t toast.
The Scientific Method

You are a little frustrated because your eggs are now getting cold.
You grab your cup of coffee and ask…
The Scientific Method
Why?

You try to get the toaster to work and notice the light doesn’t come on and the coils do not get hot.
You make sure it is plugged in, and it is.
This makes you think…
The Scientific Method

To determine if your prediction that the outlet is broken is true, you need to come up with a hypothesis to test it.
A hypothesis isn’t always the right answer, but it is a way to help you determine the right answer.
The Scientific Method

You have made a hypothesis and now it is time to test it out.
Let's find another outlet and plug the toaster in.
The Scientific Method

Results of your test may support or contradict your hypothesis.
Support of hypothesis cannot conclusively provide it is correct but means that it is likely correct.
If results contradict a hypothesis, it is probably not true – unless there was a flaw in the test.
The Scientific Method
Vaccine Development

First step – observation of the need
Collaborative effort – academia, small biotech companies, large vaccine manufacturers, regulatory authorities/ government
Several obstacles to think about when creating a vaccine
Vaccine Development

Antigen
Blueprint that generates an immune response – must have!
Small portion of disease-causing organism – protein or polysaccharide
Toxin
DNA/RNA
Whole organism – weakened or attenuated

Preservatives
Prevent the vaccine from becoming contaminated once opened
Used for vaccines that have multiple doses – single dose vaccines do not have preservatives
Most common = 2-phenoxyethanol
Used for many years – safe and used in baby care products

Stabilizers
Prevent chemical reactions from occurring and keep vaccine components from sticking to vial
Regularly consumed for naturally produced by humans = no safety concern
Sugars (lactose, sucrose)
Amino acids (glycine)
Gelatin
Proteins (recombinant human albumin derived from yeast)

Surfactants
Reduce the surface tension of vaccine components
Acts as emulsifier = keeps ingredients blended together
Prevent settling and clumping
Used in many applications, such as ice cream

Diluent
Liquid used to dilute vaccine to correct concentration
Most common = sterile water

Residuals
Tiny amounts of various substance used during manufacturing – not active ingredients
Vary depending on manufacturing process – egg proteins, yeast, antibiotics
Measured in parts per million or parts per billion
Occasionally cause allergic reactions

Adjuvant
Improves immune response
Act by:
Keeping vaccine at injection site longer
Stimulating local immune cells
Providing way for vaccine to be absorbed
Most common = tiny amount of aluminum salts
Does not cause long-term health problems, regularly ingested through eating and drinking
Evaluating Vaccines for Efficacy and Safety

Pre-clinical Phase
Every vaccine begins at this phase. Vaccines are tested in an organ model to answer the following.
Is the vaccine safe? Adverse immune reactions are checked for in the established model organism.
What is a safe starting dose for humans? Multiple doses are tested to estimate the safest starting dose to use in humans during future phases.
Is this vaccine effective? This phase needs to show some efficacy in the animal model before it can ethically be tested in humans.
Typically, this phase takes approximately 1.5 years to complete and can cost an average of $20 million.
Success rate: 14% of vaccines make it thorough this phase.

Phase I
Phase I begins the initial trials in human subjects. Volunteers agree to be given the vaccine to test for safety and efficacy. Since this is the very first time the vaccine is given to people, we are talking about small test groups, only in the tens. In this phase, the following questions are answered.
Is the vaccine safe? Volunteers are checked for adverse immune system reactions, side effects, and evidence of toxicity.
What is the immune response in humans? The type of immune response and the extent of the immune response are determined in humans.
Typically, this phase takes several months to complete and can cost an average of $9 million.
Success rate: Approximately 32% of vaccines make it thorough this phase, which is only 4.5% of all vaccines that begin the clinical trial process.

Phase II
In this phase, volunteers are recruited from different age, gender, and ethnic backgrounds with various health statuses. Participants are placed in larger groups, typically in the hundreds. T he following questions are answered.
Is the vaccine still safe? The vaccine is continued to be assessed for safety and potential side effects are looked for in a more diverse study group.
How does the immune response differ among different people? The immune response is monitored, especially in regard to the subjects’
age, gender, and ethnicity. Additionally, the optimal dose is determined based on participant response to various doses.
What is the best way to administer the vaccine? The vaccine preparation and route of delivery are determined. Additionally, a schedule of immunization is created, which includes the age of first administration, number of doses, sequence of or interval between doses, duration of immunity, and potential need for booster immunizations.
This phase can take up to two years to complete and can cost anywhere from $9 million to $15 million.
Success rate: 50% of vaccines make it thorough this phase, which is only 2.2% of vaccines that begin the clinical trial process.

Phase III
Phase III is considered a long-term trial to catch any unexpected or rare events and to evaluate efficacy longer than the shorter, smaller phases. This phase recruits many more volunteers, in the thousands. The goal of this phase is to evaluate the vaccine in the most diverse population available, that should represent the population who will receive the vaccine. In this phase, the following questions are answered.
Is this vaccine still safe? The safety of the vaccine continues to be assessed and potential side effects or rare events are identified. This is performed for a longer duration in a more homogeneous target population.
This phase often lasts one to four years can cost anywhere from $40 million to $100+ million.
Success rate: At this point, 100% of vaccines make it thorough this phase, resulting in only 2.2% of vaccines that begin the clinical trials process seeking regulatory approval.
Is this vaccine effective? The clinical efficacy of the vaccine is determined, usually by monitoring the relative disease incidence among the vaccinated versus unvaccinated portion of the target population.

Regulatory Approval
After making it through phases I to III, the vaccine manufacturer must go before the regulatory authorities (often the FDA) for final approval before taking it to the market. During this review, the regulatory authorities review all data collected during all phases, specifically seeking evidence of safety and efficacy.
One in ten vaccines are not approved by the regulatory authorities and must continue data collection, or possibly scrap the entire vaccine study.
This phase takes an average of 1.32 years to complete and costs approximately $1.5 million to $2 million.
Success rate: 92% of vaccines make it thorough this phase, which is a only 2% of all vaccines that start the clinical trial process.

Phase IV
After a vaccine receives approval from regulatory authorities and is widely used in the general public, it enters what is referred to as phase IV. This is not a formal clinical trial phase that we are accustomed to, rather a continuous long-term assessment of the vaccine's efficacy and safety throughout the entire population. This continued assessment confirms that the clinical efficacy and safety determined during the clinical trials transfers to the general public.
At any time during this observation, if there are any concerns that are raised, the regulatory authorities may pause or completely stop the use of the vaccine until further investigation is completed to determine the impact of the concern.

Chickenpox and Shingles
CLS-N390 Vaccines: Science and S o ciety

Varicella Z o ster V i rus
Double-stranded DNA virus
N ucleocapsid
C loses related to herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1)
H umans are the only reservior
Unique – causes two separate diseases

2019 cases per 100,000 people
Highest in late winter to early spring

Transmission
Highly contagious
Close personal contact
90% transmission rate in household
10% - 35% transmission rate in limited exposure (classrooms)
Enters body via conjunctiva (eyes) or nasopharyngeal mucosa of upper respiratory tract – travels to lymph nodes
R 0 = 10 – 12; 92% vaccination for herd immunity

Incubation Period – 14-16 d
Active Illness

Chickenpox - Symptoms
Also known as varicella
Itchy, blister-like rash
First on chest, back, and face  spreads all body – inside mouth, eyelids and genital area
One week blisters become scabs  scars
Other symptoms 1-2 days before rash
Fever
Tiredness
Loss of appetite
Headache

Chickenpox - complications
Not common in healthy people
Highest risk are infants, non-vaccinated, pregnant women, immunocompromised
Serious complications include
Bacterial infection of skin and soft tissue in children – Group A streptococcal infection (the one that causes strep throat)
Infection of the lungs – pneumonia
Infection or swelling of brain – encephalitis
Bloodstream infection – sepsis
Bleeding problems
Dehydration
Can also cause death, but very rare due to vaccines

Shingles – symptoms
Also known as herpes zoster
Reactivation of virus laying dormant in sensory nerve ganglia of epithelial cells – painful rash on one side of the face or body
Tingling, itching, and pain before rash develops
Usually single stripe, but may be widespread
Blisters and scabs in 7 – 10 days; clears in 2 – 4 weeks
Other symptoms include:
Fever
Headache
Chills
Upset stomach

Shingles – complications
Anyone who had chickenpox – about 99% of population born before 1980 is at risk
10% - 18% will develop postherpetic nerve pain – usually in older individuals
Shingles along nerves in face may lead to:
Blindness
Pneumonia
Hearing problems
Brain inflammation
Death

Licensed in US in 1995 – 1,350 PFU
Efficacy – 90% after one dose for  12 months to 12 years; 78% after one does and 99% after two doses in 13+
Immunity – lifelong; breakthrough infection does exist
Originally isolated by Takahashi in early 1970s from vesicle fluid from a child with varicella disease
Given to children 12 months or older; 13+ without immunity

Live attenuated virus created in human diploid cell culture
Reconstituted with sterile, preservative-free water

Cyclic Voltammetry
• The electrode potential is ramped linearly to a more positive potential, and then ramped in reverse back to the starting voltage. The forward scan produces a current peak for any analytes that can be oxidized through the range of the potential scan.
Time
V 2
V 1
Voltage

•The current will increase as the potential reaches the oxidation potential of the analyte , but then falls off as the concentration of the analyte is depleted close to the electrode surface .
 Current from an oxidation or reduction is measured as the potential of the working electrode is changed.
• The measured current is limited by analyte diffusion at the electrode surface.
 The measured current is proportional to the analyte concentration in solution.
 The current versus potential graph- voltammogram

A reversible reaction is fast enough to maintain equilibrium concentrations of reactant and product at electrode surface
 Increased separation and broadening of cathodic and anodic peaks occur in irreversible reactions that are not fast enough to maintain equilibrium concentrations at the electrode.

Potentiostat
Instrumentation
•Three electrode cell. Current flows between the working and counter electrodes while the potential of the working electrode is controlled versus the reference electrode.

Working electrode – the one at which the reaction of interest occurs.
Reference electrode – used to measure the potential of the working electrode.
Auxiliary electrode (counter electrode) – current-supporting electrode partner of the working electrode. Current flows between the working and the auxiliary electrodes

Analytical Chemistry C310
Dr. Grace W. Muna
Research Interests: Electrochemistry and Separation Science

Introduction to Analytical Chemistry
● Branch of chemistry that deals with separation, identification and  determination of components in a sample.
● Field of chemistry that deals with the use and development of tools and processes for examining and studying chemical substances.
● Seeks ever improved methods to measure chemical composition of natural and artificial materials.
● Improve the reliability of existing techniques to meet the demands for better chemical measurements which arise constantly in our society.

Examples of Analytical Chemistry Applications
• Concentrations of O 2 and CO 2 determined in millions of blood samples every day and used to diagnose and treat illness.
• Smog control is done by the measurement of quantities of hydrocarbons, nitrogen oxides, and carbon monoxide in automobile exhaust.
• Diagnosis of parathyroid diseases in humans by measuring of ionized calcium in blood serum.
• Determination of nitrogen in foods establishes their protein content and thus their nutritional value.

Important Terms
Qualitative Analysis
•  Reveals the identity of the elements and compounds in a sample….what is present
Quantitative Analysis
•  Determines the relative amount of each substance in a sample……how much is present
Sample
Portion of material taken for analysis.
Analyte
•  The component of a sample; major, minor, or trace component we’re interested in measuring.

Matrix
• All of the components in the sample containing an analyte.
Assay
•  Process of determining how much of a given sample is the material indicated by its name.
Sampling
•  Process of collecting a small mass of material whose composition accurately represents the bulk of the material being sampled.
- homogeneous
- heterogeneous

Replicates
•  Portions of a material of approximately the same size that are carried through an analytical process.
Interferences (interferents)
•  A species that causes an error in an analysis by
enhancing or attenuating the quantity being measured.
Masking → transformation of an interference into a form that’s not detected.

Common Questions addressed by Analytical Chemistry
General Approach Question Being Addressed
Quantitative analysis		How much analyte is present in the 					sample?
Qualitative analysis		Is a particular analyte present in the 					sample?
Chemical identification	What is the identity of an unknown 					chemical in a sample?
Structural analysis		What is the atomic/molecular mass, 					composition or structure of the analyte?
Property characterization	What are some chemical or physical 					properties of the analyte?
Spatial analysis		How is the analyte distributed 					throughout a sample?
Time-dependent analysis	How does the amount an analyte or a 				property of the analyte change over 					time?

Classification of Analytical Methods
• Gravimetric methods – determines the mass of the analyte or some compound chemically related to it.
• Volumetric methods – volume of a solution containing sufficient reagent to react completely with the analyte is measured.
• Electroanalytical methods – measurement of electrical properties such as potential, current, resistance, and charge.
• Spectroscopic methods – measurement of the interaction between electromagnetic radiation and analyte atoms or molecules or production of such radiation by analytes.

General Steps in a Chemical Analysis
Formulating the question : Translate general questions into specific questions to be answered through chemical measurements.
Selecting analytical  procedures/ select method : Search chemical literature, devise new procedure.
Sampling : Selecting a representative material to analyze.
Sample preparation : Converting a representative sample into a suitable form for chemical analysis.
Analysis : Measure the concentration of analyte in several identical aliquots ; replicate measurements.
Reporting and interpretation – complete report of your results. Communicate your results clearly and completely.

Chapter 6: Chemical Equilibrium Chemical Equilibrium in the Environment
North Branch of the Potomac River runs through the Appalachian Mountains
Potomac River
Acid drainage from abandoned coal mines lowers the pH of the river to 4.5.
Papermill downstream releases calcium carbonate.
Bacterial respiration at a wastewater treatment plan adds CO 2 to the system.
Systems combine to restore river pH.
Coral Reefs
Same chemistry that helps Potomac River endangers the coral reefs.
Rising CO 2 levels in oceans are lowering the pH.
Low pH dissolves CaCO 3 from coral.

Common types of reactions encountered in chemical analysis
Precipitation – takes place when the combination of two or more soluble chemicals produces an insoluble substance or precipitate .
Complex formation – involves formation of a reversible complex between two or more chemicals, such as a reaction based on a coordinate covalent bond or non covalent interactions.
Acid-base reaction – transfer of hydrogen ion from one compound (an acid) to another (base).
• Oxidation-reduction reaction –an exchange of electrons between chemicals.
• Chemical processes that involve a change in the environment of a chemical.
