Describing Chemical Reactions
Questions asked about chemical process:
- “What amount of a chemical is required in the reaction or process”.  “How much of this chemical is actually present”.
- “ How does the ability of a chemical to `react change as we alter its 	surroundings” – Chemical activity .
- “How much energy will be given off or required by this process? or  How far might the chemical process proceed?– Chemical thermodynamics .
- “How fast does a chemical process occur” - Kinetics .
Quantitative Chemical Analysis, Daniel C. Harris and Charles A. Lucy, © 2020 W. H. Freeman and Company

Chemical equilibrium
Chemical equilibrium is a dynamic state in which the rates of the forward and the reverse reactions are identical.
• Chemical reactions do not cease at equilibrium, instead, the amounts of reactants and products are constant.
 Equilibrium constant
* predict the direction and completeness of a chemical reaction.
* yields no information concerning the rate at which equilibrium is approached.

The Equilibrium Constant
a A + b B
c C + d D
Equilibrium constant, K
Reaction is favored if K > 1.
Equilibrium constants are dimensionless.
If A is a solute, [A] means [A]/(1 M).
If D is a gas, [D] means (pressure of D in bars)/(1 bar) – written as P D .
Each quantity in the ratio is given as concentration at standard state .
Concentration of solutes expressed as mol/L
Concentration of gases expressed in bars
Concentration of pure solids, pure liquids, and solvents omitted because they are unity

Manipulating Equilibrium Constants
If the direction of a reaction is reversed, the new value of K is simply the reciprocal of the original value of K .
If two reactions are added, the new K is the product of the two individual equilibrium constants.
If n reactions are added, the overall equilibrium constant is the product of n individual equilibrium constants.

Equilibrium and Thermodynamics
Enthalpy
The heat absorbed or released (enthalpy) and the dispersal of energy into molecular motions (entropy) independently contribute to the equilibrium constant.
Enthalpy ( H ): The enthalpy change, Δ H , for a reaction is the heat absorbed or released when the reaction takes place under constant applied pressure.
If Δ H is positive, heat is absorbed and the reaction is endothermic .
If Δ H is negative, heat is released and the reaction is exothermic .
The standard enthalpy change , Δ H °, refers to the heat absorbed or released when the reactants and products are in their standard states.

Entropy
Entropy ( S ): When a chemical or physical change occurs in a reversible manner at a constant temperature, the entropy change, Δ S , is equal to the heat absorbed ( q rev ) divided by the temperature ( T ).
A gas has higher entropy than a liquid, and a liquid has higher entropy than a solid.
Δ S ° is the change in entropy when all species are in their standard state.
If Δ S is positive, the products have greater entropy than the reactants.
If Δ S is negative, the products have lower entropy than the reactants.

Free Energy
The change in Gibbs free energy ( Δ G ) is the arbiter between opposing tendencies of Δ H and Δ S . At constant temperature ( T ):
A reaction is favored if Δ G is negative.
Free energy relates the equilibrium constant to energetics of a reaction.
Δ G ° = (+) K < 1
Reaction is disfavored
Δ G ° = (−) K > 1
Reaction is favored
Satisfy yourself that K > 1 if Δ G ° is negative.

Le Châtelier’s Principle (1 of 2)
If a system at equilibrium is subjected to a change or disturbance:
The direction in which the system proceeds back to equilibrium will attempt to offset the change.
If the concentration of Cr 3+ is increased, a stress is placed on the system.
The reaction quotient , Q < K
So the system shifts to form more products until Q = K

Le Châtelier’s Principle (2 of 2)
If a reaction is at equilibrium and products are added (or reactants removed), the reaction goes to the left.
If a reaction is at equilibrium and reactants are added (or products removed), the reaction goes to the right.
When the temperature of a system is changed, so is the equilibrium constant.
The equilibrium constant of an endothermic reaction ( Δ H ° = +) increases if the temperature is raised.
Heat + reactants
products
The equilibrium constant of an exothermic reaction ( Δ H ° = −) decreases if the temperature is raised.
Reactants
products + heat

Equilibrium Problems
Equilibrium problems make thermodynamic predictions, not kinetic predictions.
Calculate what must happen for a system to reach equilibrium, not how long it will take.
Some reactions are over in an instant; others take a million years.
The size of an equilibrium constant tells us nothing about the rate (kinetics) of the reaction.
A large equilibrium constant does not imply that a reaction is fast.

Protic Acids and Bases
Understanding the behavior of acids and bases is essential to every branch of science related to chemistry.
In aqueous chemistry:
An acid increases the concentration of H 3 O + when added to water.
A base decreases the concentration of H 3 O + when added to water.
As H 3 O + decreases in solution, OH − increases. K W = [H 3 O + ][OH − ]
Protic refers to chemistry involving transfer of an H + from one molecule to another.

Brønsted -Lowry Acids and Bases
An acid is a proton donor.
A base is a proton acceptor.
A salt contains cations and anions.
Strong electrolytes dissociate nearly completely into ions in dilute aqueous solutions.

Strong Acids and Bases
In aqueous solutions, acids and bases are commonly classified in one of two ways.
Strong acids/bases react nearly “completely” to produce H + /OH − .
Weak acids/bases react only “partially” to produce H + /OH − .
There is no sharp distinction between strong and weak.
For strong acids/bases the equilibrium dissociation constants are large.
Virtually no undissociated  strong acid/base exists in solution.

Solve for all the unknown chemical species.

Diprotic Acids and Bases

How do we calculate the pH and composition of individual solutions of H 2 L + , HL, and L − ?
Use the same procedure to find the pH of diprotic H 2 A (where A is anything).
E.g., H 2 L + where HL is leucine

Acidic Form, 0.050 0 M H 2 L +
H 2 L + can dissociate twice.
H 2 L + is a weak acid ( K 1 = 4.70 × 10 −3 ); it will dissociate only partly.
HL is an even weaker acid ( K 2 = 1.80 × 10 −10 ); it will hardly dissociate at all.
Approximation: H 2 L + behaves as a monoprotic acid with K a = K 1 .
[HL] = x = 1.32 × 10 −2 M
[H 2 L + ] = F − x = 0.050 0 − 1.32 × 10 −2 = 3.68 × 10 −2 M
pH = 1.88

Basic Form, 0.050 0 M L −
L − (found in a salt such as NaL ) can undergo two hydrolysis reactions.
L − is a weak base ( K b1 = K w / K a2 =5.55 × 10 −5 ); it will not hydrolyze very much.
HL is an even weaker base ( K b2 = K w / K a1 =2.13 × 10 −12 ); it will hardly react with water at all, making very little H 2 L + .
Approximation: L − behaves as a monobasic system with K b = K b1 .
[HL] = x = 1.64 × 10 −3 M
[L − ] = F − x = 0.050 0 − 1.64 × 10 −3 = 4.84 × 10 −2 M
pH = 11.21

A solution made from leucine (HL) is more complicated because HL is both an acid and a base ( amphiprotic ).

But we cannot ignore the hydrolysis reaction ( K b2 ).
Both reactions proceed to some extent.

Box 10-2 Successive Approximations (1 of 2)
Successive approximations is a good way to deal with difficult equations that do not have simple solutions.
Equation 10-11 is not a good approximation when the concentration of the intermediate species of a diprotic acid is not close to F. This situation arises when K 1 and K 2 are nearly equal and F is small.
By estimating one value needed to complete a calculation and critically looking at the result, it is possible to repeat the calculation with better estimates.
With successive attempts, it is possible to hone in on the correct value.

Solution: Monohydrogen phthalate and phthalate were shown in the preceding example. The formula masses are KHP = C 8 H 5 O 4 K = 204.22 and Na 2 P = C 8 H 4 O 4 Na 2 = 210.10. We know [HP − ] and [P 2− ], so we use the p K 2 Henderson-Hasselbalch equation to find the pH:
K 2 is the acid dissociation constant of HP − , which appears in the denominator of the log term. The volume of solution was not needed to answer the question.

Example: Preparing a Buffer in a Diprotic System (1 of 4)
How many milliliters of 0.800 M KOH should be added to 3.38 g of oxalic acid to give a pH of 4.40 when diluted to 500 mL?
p K 1 = 1.250
p K 2 = 4.266

Example: Preparing a Buffer in a Diprotic System (2 of 4)
Solution: The desired pH is above p K 2 . We know that a 1:1 mole ratio of HOx − : Ox 2− would have pH = p K 2 = 4.266. If the pH is to be 4.40, there must be more Ox 2− than HOx − present. We must add enough base to convert all H 2 Ox into HOx − , plus enough additional base to convert the right amount of HOx − into Ox 2− .
In 3.38 g of H 2 Ox, there are 0.037 5 4 mol. The volume of 0.800 M KOH needed to react with this much H 2 Ox to make HOx − is (0.037 5 4 mol)/(0.800 M) = 46.9 3 mL.

Example: Preparing a Buffer in a Diprotic System (3 of 4)
Solution: To produce a pH of 4.40 requires an additional x mol of OH − :
The volume of KOH needed to deliver 0.021 6 6 mole is (0.021 6 4 mol)/(0.800 M) = 27.0 5 mL. The total volume of KOH needed to bring the pH to 4.40 is 46.9 3 + 27.0 5 = 73.9 8 mL.

Chapter 3-Errors in Chemical Analysis
Errors Matter ( BALCO Scandal)

Errors Matter (Zika Epidemic)
Microcephaly
Abnormally small head associated with incomplete brain development
Can be caused by infections from the mosquito-borne virus Zika during a mother’s pregnancy
CDC recommends that pregnant women who travel/live in areas with known Zika outbreaks be tested for the virus
“Botched” Testing
Technicians at a Washington DC testing lab mistakenly diluted lab reagents, resulting in 409 Zika tests all coming back “negative.”
Retesting identified pregnant women who had the Zika virus.
Standard operating procedures (SOP) outline specific procedures to ensure the quality of the chemical analysis.

• Every measurement (physical and chemical) has some uncertainty, which is called experimental error .
•  Conclusions can be expressed with a high or a low degree of confidence but never with complete certainty.
• Individual results from a set of measurements are seldom the same hence consider the “best” estimate to be the central value for the set.

Types of Errors in Experimental Data
Systematic (or determinate) error
Random (or indeterminate) error
Gross error

Systematic (or determinate) error
• Causes the mean of a data set to differ from the accepted value. * Affect measurement accuracy .
• Causes all the results to be too high or too low .
• Systematic error can be discovered and corrected although it may not be easy.
• Represented with a constant bias in measurement results.
 Have a definite value and an assignable cause , same magnitude (reproducible) for measurements made in the same way.

Sources of Systematic Errors
• Instrumental errors - Caused by nonideal instrument behavior
Other examples….
• Method errors - Arise from nonideal chemical or physical behavior of analytical systems.
Examples….
• Personal errors - Result from carelessness, inattention or personal limitation of the experimenter.
Examples….

Random (or indeterminate) error
• Causes data to scatter more or less symmetrically around a mean value.
• Arises from the effects of uncontrolled (or uncontrollable) variables in the measurement.
• Affect measurement precision.
• Random error have an equal chance of being positive or negative.

• There is always some random error superimposed on the systematic error
• Always present and cannot be corrected but might be reduced by a better experiment.

Gross error
• Differ from indeterminate and determinate error.
• Occur occasionally and are often large and may cause a result to be high or low.
• Product of human errors.
• Lead to outliers , results that appear to differ significantly for all other data in a set of replicate measurements.

Detection of Systematic Method Errors
• Difficult to detect and correct
Analysis of standard samples
• Estimate bias by analyzing standard reference materials (SRMs), materials that contain one or more analytes at known concentration levels.
• SRMs can be purchased from government and industrial sources e.g., NIST.

Independent analysis
• A second  independent and reliable analytical 	method to measure the same quantity.
- The method should differ as much as 	possible from the one under study. Why?
Blank determination
• Blank → reagents and solvents used in a determination but no analyte.
•  Whenever feasible blanks may contain added constituents to simulate sample matrix.

• All the steps of the analysis are performed in a blank determination.
• Reveals errors due to interfering contaminants from the reagents and vessels used in the analysis.
Round robin experiment
• Different people in several laboratories analyze identical samples by the same or different methods.
•Disagreement beyond the estimate of random error is systematic error.

Eliminating Systematic Errors and Personal Errors
•  Regular maintenance of equipment…periodic calibration of equipment.
• Use standard addition or internal standard to correct for matrix effects.
Good laboratory practices…have a well-kept lab notebook to note unusual trends in experiments 	and to identify sources of errors.
•  Personal errors minimized by care and self-discipline.
 Check instrument readings, notebook 	entries and calculations systematically.

Reflection Moments
Which of the following is NOT a characteristic of random (or indeterminate) error?
A. It arises from uncontrolled variables in the measurement.
B. It cannot be eliminated completely.
C. It arises from a flaw in equipment or the design of an experiment.
D. It might be reduced by a better experiment.
E. It has an equal chance to be positive or negative.
2. A systematic error can be corrected by
A. increasing the number of measurements.
B. proper calibration.
C. always employing the same person to perform the analysis.
D. using a larger amount of sample.
E. statistical evaluation.
3. True or False: Random errors can be eliminated and reduced by better techniques.
4. True or False: Reproducible measurements are always close to the “true” value.

Accuracy and Precision
• Accuracy describes closeness of the measurement to the true or accepted value.
• Precision describes reproducibility of measurements.
* The closeness of results measured in exactly the same way.
*The precision of a measurement is readily determined by carefully replicated experiments.

Low accuracy, low precision
Low accuracy, high precision
High accuracy, low precision
High accuracy, high precision
Illustrating “ accuracy ” and “ precision ”
•  Results can be precise without being accurate, and
accurate without being precise.

Some analytical data illustrating “accuracy”
and “precision”
Benzyl isothiourea
hydrochloride
Nicotinic acid
Analyst 4: imprecise, inaccurate
Analyst 3: precise, inaccurate
Analyst 2: imprecise, accurate
Analyst 1: precise, accurate

Absolute and Relative Uncertainty
• Absolute uncertainty (absolute error) → the margin of uncertainty associated with a measurement.
• Relative uncertainty is a more useful quantity than the absolute uncertainty:

The Propagation of Uncertainity from Random Errors
• Most propagation of uncertainity computations deal with random error, not systematic error. The goal is always to eliminate systematic errors.
• The overall precision of an experimental result will depend on the random errors that occur in each individual step used to obtain this result.
• Error propagation is a method that can be used to help identify the major contributions to random errors in an analysis .

Addition and Subtraction
• For addition and subtraction, use absolute uncertainty.
Example

Multiplication and Division
• For multiplication and division convert all uncertainties into percent relative uncertainties.

Example
Mixed Operations

Question
A method for the detection of morphine is used to generate a calibration curve in which the assay response ( y ) is plotted versus morphine concentration ( x , in mg/L). All Measurements Have Experimental Uncertainty
Statistics gives us tools to:
Accept conclusions that have a high probability of being correct
Reject conclusions that have a high probability of being incorrect
Statistical analysis reveals only information that already present in a data, no new information is created by statistical methods.
Scientists use statistical data analysis to evaluate the quality of experimental measurements , to test hypothesis , and to develop models to describe experimental results.

Gaussian Distribution
If an experiment is repeated a great many times and if the errors are purely random:
The results tend to cluster symmetrically about the average value.
The more times the experiment is repeated, the more closely the results approach a Gaussian distribution.
Statistical analyses are based on the assumption that random errors follow a Gaussian, or normal, distribution .
Usually we repeat an experiment 3–5 times (not 400 times).
From small data sets we can estimate properties of a hypothetical large set.

Mean (average) : the sum of a set of results divided by the number of values in the set
Standard deviation : measures how closely data are clustered about the mean

Standard Deviation
The smaller the standard deviation, s , the more closely the data are clustered about the mean.
Precision: reproducibility
Accuracy: nearness to the “truth”
Experiments with a small standard deviation are more precise than experiments with a large standard deviation.
Greater precision does not necessarily imply greater accuracy .

Statistical Tests
Two sets of measurements (on the same quantity) will generally differ in
and s . Statistical tools determine the probability of a conclusion.
Accept conclusions with a high probability of being correct.
Reject conclusions with a high probability of being incorrect.
Null hypothesis : states that two sets of data are drawn from populations with the same properties
Observed differences arise only from random variation in measurements.
Reject the null hypothesis if there is <5% probability of observing experimental results from two populations with the same value.  we have 95% chance that our conclusion is correct.

Statistical Tests: : Comparison of Standard Deviations with the F Test
The null hypothesis is used with several tests.
“Two sets of data are drawn from populations with the same properties.”
F -test compares standard deviation 
t test compares mean 

F -Test: Comparison of Standard Deviation
Are the standard deviations of two sets of measurements “statistically different”?
Put the larger standard deviation in the numerator so that F  1.
If F calculated > F table , then reject the null hypothesis.
There is <5% chance that the two data sets came from populations with the same population standard deviation.
The difference is considered significant.
Degrees of freedom for n measurements are n − 1.

For a drug’s approval, the null hypothesis is that the treatment does not cause cancer .

Grubbs Test: Check for Outliers
Should a data point that looks like an anomaly be discarded?
If you make several replicate measurements, results should fall within a Gaussian distribution about the mean. But when n is small, it can be difficult to determine if an outlying data point falls within the normal distribution.
The Grubbs test is a statistical test to decide whether to discard a datum that appears discrepant (an “outlier” ).
If G calculated > G table , then reject the null hypothesis.
There is <5% chance that the suspicious data point is a member of the same population as the other measurements.
The difference is considered significant.

Grubbs Test for an Outlier (1 of 3)
When a single data point lies far from the other data in a set of measurements:
First, check your notebook.
Are there any recorded observations about the anomalous data point (for example, a note that solution was lost during transfer)?
Any data point based on recorded faulty procedure should be discarded, no matter how well it fits the rest of the data ( “blunder” ).

Grubbs Test for an Outlier (2 of 3)
In the absence of a recorded blunder, use the Grubbs test .
If G calculated is greater than G in Table 4-6, the questionable point should be discarded.
Only one outlier may be rejected using the Grubbs test.

Grubbs Test for an Outlier (3 of 3)
In the absence of a recorded blunder, use the Grubbs test .
Volumes for replicate titrations (mL): 28.54, 28.39, 28.47, 27.68
G calculated > G table , so reject the null hypothesis.
The questionable data point is an “outlier” and should be discarded.

Errors In Hypothesis Testing
• Type 1 error (alpha error) – when you conclude the model and experimental value are not the same when they really are equivalent.
• Type 2 error (beta error) – when you conclude that a result is the same as your model, but the result is actually part of an entirely different data distribution.
How is each type of error affected by changing the CL?
• Selection of a CL for a statistical test will always represent a compromise between the size of the type 1 and type 2 errors.  For this purpose, we use 95% CL.

The Method of Least Squares
For most chemical analyses, the response obtained by the given lab procedure must be compared to known quantities (called standards ).
In this way the response from an unknown quantity can be interpreted.
Prepare a calibration curve from known standards.
Work in a region where the calibration curve is linear (usually ).
Method of least squares: used to draw the “best” straight line through experimental data points that contain some scatter
Some points will lie above and some below the line.
Equation y = mx + b can be used to quantify the unknown from its signal.

Finding the Equation of the Line
Assume:
Uncertainty in y values is much greater than uncertainty in x values ( s y >> s x ).
Uncertainties of all y values are similar.
Draw a line to minimize vertical deviations between points and line.
Figure 4-11
Vertical deviation d i = y i − y = y i − ( mx i + b )
Deviations can be positive or negative. To minimize magnitude, irrespective of sign, square the deviation
 method of least squares

Example: Finding s y , u m , and u b with a Spreadsheet (1 of 3)
Excel function LINEST returns the slope and intercept and their standard uncertainties in a table (a matrix ). As an example, enter x and y values from Table 4-7 in columns A and B. Then highlight the 3-row × 2-column region E3:F5 with your mouse. This block of cells is selected for the output of LINEST. On the Formulas ribbon, go to Insert Function. In the window that appears, in “Or select a category” select Statistical and double click LINEST. The new window asks for four inputs to the function. For y values, enter B2:B5. Then enter A2:A5 for x values. The next two entries are both “TRUE”. The first TRUE tells Excel that we want to compute the y -intercept of the line and not force the intercept to be 0. The second TRUE tells Excel to print out the uncertainties as well as the slope and intercept. The formula you have just entered is “=LINEST(B2:B5,A2:A5,TRUE,TRUE)”. Now press CONTROL+SHIFT+ENTER on a PC or CONTROL+SHIFT+RETURN on a Mac.

Example: Finding s y , u m , and u b with a Spreadsheet (2 of 3)
Excel prints out a matrix in cells E3:F5. Write labels around the block to indicate what is in each cell. The slope and intercept are on the top line. The second line contains u m and u b . Cell F5 contains s y , and cell E5 contains a quantity called R 2 , which is defined in Equation 5-3 and is a measure of the goodness of fit of the data to the line. The closer R 2 is to unity, the better the fit.

Testing the Goodness of Fit
A common measure of linearity is the square of the correlation of determination , R 2 .
R 2 , measures the fraction of the observed  variation in y that is explained by the linear relationship.
The closer R 2 is  to unity, the better the linear model explains the y variations.

Testing the Goodness of Fit
Due to presence of random errors, a correlation determination of exactly equal to 1 is almost never obtained. You can compare your value of R 2 to the critical value and determine whether it represents a real fit between x and y .

Residual Plots
A residual plot further confirms whether your data really fits with the line.
A plot of residual between experimental value for the dependent variable ( y i ) and the predicted by the best-fit line.
If there was perfect agreement btn your data and best-fit line ( y i – y i,calc ) = 0
If the best-line fit is a good description of the data, the residual plot  only random distribution of the points above and below the line ( y i – y i,calc ) = 0

Calibration Curves
A calibration curve shows the response of an analytical method to known quantities of analyte .
Standard solutions contain known concentrations of analyte.
Blank solutions contain all reagents and solvents used in the analysis, but contain no deliberately added analyte.
A spectrophotometer measures the absorbance of light ( y -axis), which is proportional to the quantity of protein analyzed ( x -axis).
Figure 4-12

Constructing a Calibration Curve (1 of 2)
Prepare known samples of analyte covering the range (0 to 150%) of concentrations expected for unknowns.
Tabulate amount of analyte in each standard and response.
Subtract the average absorbance of the blank solutions from each measured absorbance (corrected absorbance).
Blanks measure the response of the procedure when no analyte is present.
Make a graph of corrected absorbance vs. quantity of analyte.
Inspect the graph for linearity, outliers, and consistent y -uncertainty.
Figure 4-12

Constructing a Calibration Curve (2 of 2)
Use the least-squares procedure to find the best straight line through the linear portion of the data.
If you analyze an unknown at a future time, run a blank at that time .
Subtract the new blank signal from the unknown to correct.
Figure 4-12

Linear Response
The linear range of an analytical method is the analyte concentration range over which response is proportional to concentration.
Dynamic range is the concentration range over which there is a measurable response to analyte, even if the response is not linear.
Calibration procedures with a linear response are preferred.
Corrected analytical signal  quantity of analyte.
It is possible to obtain valid results beyond the linear region by fitting with a nonlinear equation.

Always make a graph of your data
Figure 4-15

Good Practice (2 of 2)
It is not reliable to extrapolate any calibration curve beyond the measured range of standards.
At least six calibration concentrations and two replicate measurements of each unknown are recommended.
Make each standard solution from a certified material.
Avoid serial dilution of a single stock solution (serial dilution propagates systematic error).
Measure calibration solutions in random order, not in consecutive order of increasing concentration.

Box 4-3 Importance of Graphs to Visualize Data
A good graph reveals key characteristics of data and guides statistical analysis.
Heights on the bar graph (a) give the mean values of two data sets.  The error bars correspond to ±standard deviation of the mean.
Data plots (b–e) show different characteristics of the data that are not evident in the bar graph.

Quality Assurance
Quality assurance is what we do to get the right answer.
Answer should have sufficient accuracy and precision to support subsequent decisions
No point in obtaining more accurate/precise answer than necessary
Data quality standards:
Get the right data.
Get the data right.
Keep the data right.
(Nancy W. Wentworth, U.S. EPA)

Pick methods to meet specifications
Consider sampling, precision, accuracy, selectivity, sensitivity, detection limit, robustness, and rate of false results
Employ blanks, fortification, calibration checks, quality control samples, and control charts to monitor performance
Write and follow standard operating procedures
Assessment
Were the specifications achieved?
Is the method fit for purpose? | Compare data and results with specifications
Document procedures and keep records suitable to meet use objectives
Verify that use objectives were met

Use Objectives
The goal of quality assurance is making sure that results meet the customer’s needs. Write clear, concise use objectives and keep in mind:
Raw data: measurements
Treated data: concentrations derived from raw data by use of calibration methods
Results: quantities reported after statistical analysis of treated data

Specifications
Specifications: state how good the numbers need to be and what precautions are required in the analytical procedure
How shall samples be taken?
How many are needed?
Are special precautions needed to avoid degradation?
What are practical restraints (cost, time, material)?
What level of accuracy will satisfy the use objectives?
What rate of false negatives or positives is acceptable?

Box 5-1 Medical Implication of False Positive Results
False positive: concentration exceeds the legal limit when, in fact, the concentration is below the limit
False negative: concentration is below the legal limit when it is actually above the limit
Even well-executed procedures produce some false conclusions due to random error.

When Choosing a Method Consider
Selectivity : ability to distinguish analyte from other species in sample (avoid interference)
Sensitivity : ability to respond reliably and measurably to change in analyte concentration (slope of the calibration curve)
Blanks: sample not intended to contain analyte
Account for interference by other species in the sample
Account for trace amount of analyte found in reagents
Frequent measures of blanks detect whether analyte from previous sample is carried into subsequent analysis by vessels or instruments

Types of Blanks
Method blank
All components except analyte
Taken through all steps of the analytical procedure
Subtract the response of the method blank from the response of sample before calculating the quantity of analyte
Reagent blank
Similar to a method blank, but it has not been subjected to all sample preparation procedures
Field blank
Indicates if analyte is inadvertently picked up by exposure to field conditions

Spike Recovery
Sometimes response to analyte can be decreased or increased by something else in the sample
Matrix : everything in the unknown, other than the analyte
Spike (or fortification): a known quantity of analyte added to a sample
Tests whether the response to the spike is the same as expected based on known calibration curve
Response is different from expectations indicates problems (contamination or loss)

Example: Spike Recovery (1 of 3)
Let C stand for concentration. One definition of spike recovery is
An unknown was found to contain 10.0 μ g of analyte per liter. A spike of 5.0 μ g /L was added to a replicate portion of unknown. Analysis of the spiked sample gave a concentration of 14.6 μ g /L. Find the percent recovery of the spike.

Example: Spike Recovery (2 of 3)
Solution: The percent of the spike found by analysis is
If the acceptable recovery is specified to be in the range of 96 to 104%, then 92% is unacceptable. Something in your method or techniques needs improvement.

Dealing with Large Numbers of Samples
Perform periodic calibration checks.
Method drift can be due to changes such as room temperature or spoilage of reagents/standards.
Calibration check solutions should be different from ones used to prepare the original calibration curve.
Performance test samples (quality control samples or blind samples) help to eliminate bias introduced by an analyst who knows the concentration of the calibration check sample.
Standard operating procedures (SOP ) outline steps to be taken for the procedure. Serve as control experiments to detect problems in the lab.

Assessment
Assessment is the process of
Collecting data to show that analytical procedures are operating within specific limits
Verifying that final results meet use objectives
Documentation is critical. Standard protocols provide direction for what must be documented and how documentation is to be done.
Control charts can be used to monitor performance.
If final results meet the use objectives, the method is fit for purpose .

Box 5-2 Control Charts
Laboratory measuring lanthanum (La) in uranium as part of international nuclear non-proliferation monitoring
Control chart: visual representation of confidence intervals for a Gaussian distribution
Warns when a property strays dangerously far from an intended target value
For a Gaussian distribution, 95.5% of all observations are within ±2 ; 99.7% within ±3 
±2  = warning lines and ±3  = action lines

Method validation : the process of proving that an analytical method is acceptable for intended purpose

Selectivity
Selectivity: extent to which an analytical method can distinguish analyte from everything else in the sample
Method is specific if it selects for only one analyte (no interferences).
Chromatogram separates drug  imidacloprid (peak 7) from other potential impurities/degradation peaks.
Figure 5-1

Linearity
Linearity: measures how well a calibration curve follows straight line  response is proportional to quantity of analyte
A common (but superficial) measure of linearity is the square of the correlation coefficient , R 2 .
A value of R 2 > 0.995–0.999 is a good fit for many purposes.
Plotting a graph shows the true nature of the calibration.

Accuracy: “Nearness to the Truth”
Accuracy can be demonstrated by:
Analyzing a certified reference material in a matrix similar to that of the unknown
Comparing results from two or more different analytical methods
Analyzing a blank sample spiked with known addition of analyte
Standard additions of analyte to the unknowns

The spike recovery for the 1.50-mg/L spike is
For the 4.50-mg/L spike, the calculated concentration is 4.67 mg/L and its recovery is 103.8%. Spike recoveries differ from 100% by more than ±2%. The method lacks the accuracy necessary to be fit for purpose.

Precision Types of Precision
Instrument precision : same quantity of one sample is repeatedly introduced into an instrument
Intra-assay precision : analysis of aliquots of a homogeneous material several times by one person in one day with the same equipment
Intermediate precision (ruggedness) : assay is performed by different people on different instruments on different days in the same lab
Interlaboratory precision : aliquots of the same sample analyzed by different people in different laboratories. Interlaboratory tests are routinely used to validate new procedures (especially if intended for regulatory use).

Range and Robustness
Range : concentration interval over which linearity, accuracy, and precision are all acceptable
Linear dynamic range : concentration range over which calibration curve is linear
Dynamic range : concentration range over which there is measurable response
Robustness: ability of an analytical method to be unaffected by small, deliberate changes in operating parameters

Limits of Detection and Quantitation
Detection limit (lower limit of detection) : smallest quantity of analyte that is significantly different from the blank
Quantitation limit (lower limit of quantitation) : smallest quantity of analyte that can be measured with reasonable accuracy

Detection Limit (1 of 2)
After estimating the detection limit from previous experience with the method, prepare a sample whose concentration is ~ 1 to 5 times the detection limit.
Measure the signal from n replicate samples ( n  7).
Compare the standard deviation ( s ) of the n measurements.
Measure the signal from n blanks (containing no analyte) and find the mean value, y blank .
The minimum detectable signal, y dl , is defined as
Signal detection limit:		y dl = y blank + 3 s
The corrected signal, y sample – y blank , is proportional to the sample concentration:
Calibration line:		y sample – y blank = m × sample concentration
where y sample is the signal observed for the sample and m is the slope of the linear calibration curve. The minimum detectable concentration is obtained by substituting y dl for y sample to get
Detection limit:

Detection Limit (2 of 2)
Another common way to define detection limit is from the least-squares equation of a calibration curve.

From previous measurements of a low concentration of analyte, the signal detection limit was estimated to be in the low nanoampere range. Signals from seven replicate samples with a concentration about 3 times the detection limit were 5.0, 5.0, 5.2, 4.2, 4.6, 6.0, and 4.9 nA. Solution: (a ) First, compute the mean for the blanks and the standard deviation of the samples. Retain extra, insignificant digits to reduce round-off errors.
Blank: Average = y blank = 1.2 6 nA
Sample: Standard deviation = s = 0.5 6 nA
The signal detection limit from Equation 5-4 is
y dl = y blank + 3 s = 1.2 6 nA + 3(0.5 6 nA ) = 2.9 4 nA
The minimum detectable concentration is obtained from Equation 5-6:

Reporting Limit
Reporting limit is the concentration below which regulations say an analyte is “not detected.”
It does not mean that analyte is not observed. It means the analyte is below the prescribed level.
The reporting limit is set at least 5 to 10 times higher than the detection limit.
Figure 5-4

Minimizing Errors in Analytical Procedures
Objective : To understand errors in calibration and how to correct them .
Possible Sources of Errors :
• Matrix effects – a change in the analytical signal caused by anything in the sample other than analyte .
•  Differences in experimental variables at the times at which blank, sample and standards are measured can also invalidate the established calibration function.
•  Contamination during sampling or sample preparation steps.
• Systematic error can occur during the calibration process.
T o avoid systematic errors in calibration:
* the standards must be accurately prepared and their chemical state must be identical to that of the analyte in the sample.
* the standards should be stable in concentration, at least during the calibration process

Minimizing Errors in Analytical Procedures
1. Separations – clean up by separation methods minimizes errors due to possible interferences in the sample matrix.
2. Saturation, Matrix Modification, and Masking
* saturation method involves adding interfering species to all the samples, standards, and the blanks so that the interference effect becomes independent of the original concentration of the interfering species in the sample.

*Matrix modifier – a species , not an interfering species, added to all samples, standard, and blanks in sufficient amount to make the analytical response to be independent of the interfering species.
Masking agent is added, which reacts selectively with the interfering species to  form a complex that does not interfere.
3. Dilution and Matrix Matching
* dilution method is used if the interfering species produces no significant effect below a certain concentration level.
* Matrix matching method attempts to duplicate the sample matrix by adding the major matrix constituents to the standards and the blanks samples.
4 . Standard Addition Method
5. Internal Standards Method

Standard Addition (1 of 3)
Standard addition: known quantities of the analyte added to the unknown
Increase in signal indicates how much analyte was in the original unknown.
This method requires a linear response to analyte concentration.
Higher precision can be achieved when standards are added by mass instead of volume.
Especially appropriate when sample composition unknown, complex, or affects the analytical signal

Standard Addition (2 of 3)
Matrix effect: change in analytical sensitivity caused by something in the sample other than analyte
Sample composition affects the analytical signal.
It is difficult to create standards and blanks whose composition matches that of the sample.
Standards and blanks do not match the composition of the unknown.
Makes traditional calibration curves unreliable

Standards
Standard addition: known amount of a compound— same substance as analyte— added to the unknown
Internal standards: known amount of a compound— different from analyte— added to the unknown
External standards: solutions with known concentrations of analyte used to prepare a calibration curve

Internal Standard Methods
Internal standards: known amount of a compound— different from analyte — added to the unknown
* An internal standard is a reference species, chemically and physically simila r to the analyte .
* Chemically stable and not react with components of the unknown.
* The response signal is the ratio of the analyte signal to the reference species signal (Response 	Factor)

Internal Standards
Especially useful for analysis when quantity of sample analyzed or instrument response varies from run-to-run
Desirable when sample loss can occur during sample preparation steps prior to analysis
Helpful for internal standard to be chemically similar to analyte so that effects of the matrix can be minimized

Prepare a known mixture of standard and analyte.
Measure the relative response of the detector to the two species.

Sampling and Sample Preparation (1 of 2)
A chemical analysis is meaningless unless you begin with a meaningful sample.
A strategy for selecting a representative sample is necessary.
Sampling: the process of collecting a representative sample for analysis
Heterogeneous: different composition from place to place in a material
Homogeneous: same composition throughout the material
Sample preparation : the series of steps that convert a representative bulk sample into a form suitable for analysis
Usually required with real-world samples
Remove substances that interfere with analysis
Convert analyte into form suitable for analysis

Sampling
Consider the concentration of nitrate in sediment beneath a lake.
Depth profile of nitrate in sediment from freshwater Lake Søbygård in Denmark
Nitrate drops by two orders of magnitude in the first 3 mm below the surface.
It makes an enormous difference whether you select a core sample that is 1 m deep or skim the top 2 mm of sediment for analysis.

Sampling and Sample Preparation (2 of 2)
Bulk Sample (or Gross Sample)
Taken from the lot for analysis or for archiving for future reference
Must be representative of the lot
Critical for producing a valid analysis
Figure 28-2
Lot: total material from which samples are taken
Laboratory Sample
Must have same composition as bulk
Uses aliquots for individual analysis

Sample Storage
Properly storing a sample requires care.
Composition may change with time after collection because of chemical changes, reaction with air, or interaction of sample with container.
Glass containers may act as an ion exchanger, altering the concentrations of trace ions and proteins in solution.
Plastic containers must be washed before use. Polyethylene containers can contribute a measurable flux of analyte even after being soaked in acid for several months.
Steel needles are an avoidable source of metal contamination in biochemical analysis.

Statistics of Sampling (1 of 3)
For random errors, the overall variance is the sum of the variance of the analytical procedure and the variance of the sampling operation.
If either s a or s s is sufficiently smaller than the other, there is little point in trying to reduce the smaller one.

Sample Preparation
• Sample preparation is the series of steps required to transform a sample so that it’s suitable for analysis .
Goals of Sample Preparation/Sample Treatment
• Sample not present in a matrix suitable for the chosen method
 extracting analyte from a complex matrix
 dissolving the sample – grinding, acid digestion or fusion, combustion (dry ashing ), wet ashing

• Eliminate substances in the sample that interfere with the measurement of the analyte .
* removing or masking interfering species
• Concentrating a dilute analyte to a level that can be measured.
place analyte at level that’s suitable for the detection.
Chemically converting analyte into a detectable form.
*place the analyte into a chemical form that can be studied by your method of choice.

Dissolving Samples for Analysis
Once a bulk sample is selected, a laboratory sample must be prepared for analysis.
Laboratory samples are usually dissolved for analysis.
It is important to dissolve the entire sample to ensure all analyte is dissolved.
If mild dissolution conditions are insufficient, acid digestion or fusion may be used.
Organic material may be destroyed by combustion ( dry ashing ) or oxidation with liquid reagents ( wet ashing ). This leaves inorganic elements in a suitable form for analysis.

Grinding
Solids are ground in a mortar and pestle .
Agate mortar (or similar porcelain, mullite, or alumina) is designed for grinding small particles into fine powder.
Less expensive mortars tend to be more porous and easily scratched.
Ceramic can be cleaned by wiping with wet tissue and washing with distilled H 2 O.
Difficult residues can be removed by grinding with 4 M HCl.
Boron carbide is five times harder than agate and less likely to contaminate sample.
Figure 28-5

Ball Mill and Shatterbox Mill
Ball mill uses steel or ceramic balls rotated inside a drum to crush sample to fine powder.
Shatterbox laboratory mill spins a puck and ring inside a grinding container, pulverizing up to 100 g of material.
Figure 28-6

Dissolving Inorganic Materials with Acid
An array of acids are commonly used for dissolving inorganic materials.
Substances that do not dissolve in nonoxidizing acids may dissolve in the oxidizing acids HNO 3 ; hot, concentrated H 2 SO 4 ; or hot, concentrated HClO 4 .
Aqua regia is a 3:1 (vol/vol) mixture of HCl:NHO 3 used to dissolve Au and Pt.
Strong oxidants can dissolve difficult materials.
Dissolving with acids can be DANGEROUS .
It is important to understand all hazards before attempting
and to follow all safety protocols.
See Table 28.5: acids for sample dissolution

Microwave Digestion
A Teflon-lined bomb (sealed vessel) heated in a microwave oven.
Complex samples can be digested.
Teflon is required because metal would absorb microwaves.
Bomb should be cooled prior to opening to prevent loss of volatile products.
Figure 28-7

Dissolving Inorganic Materials by Fusion
Substances that will not dissolve in acid can usually be dissolved in hot, molten inorganic flux .
Finely powdered unknowns are mixed with flux.
Fusion (melting) is carried out in a Pt-Au alloy crucible at 300°–1 200°C.
Disadvantage: Impurities are introduced by the large amount of solid reagent.
Figure 28-8

Decomposition of Organic Substances
Digestion of organic material can be classified as
Dry ashing : no liquid used
Halogens in coal
Metals in plant material
Wet ashing : liquid used
Metals in animal tissue
Fuming HNO 3 , H 2 SO 4 , or H 2 SO 4 and HNO 3 can be added to organic substance in glass digestion tube
Figure 28-9

Digestion with a High-Pressure Asher
Figure 28-10
The high-pressure asher uses a resistive heating element inside a sealed chamber for digestion at temperatures up to 270°C under a pressure up to 140 bar.
High pressure allows acids to be heated to high temperature without boiling.
At high temperature, HNO 3 oxidizes organic matter without assistance from H 2 SO 4 .
Hydrogen peroxide reduces nitrogen oxides generated by  digestion of organic matter.

Wet Ashing with Refluxing HNO 3 -HClO 4
Figure 28-11
Widely applicable, but hazardous, procedure.
Perchloric acid has caused numerous explosions.
First, heat the sample slowly to boiling with HNO 3 but without HClO 4 .
Boil to near dryness to destroy easily oxidized material.
Add fresh HNO 3 and repeat several times.
Cool the sample and add HClO 4 and heat again.
HNO 3 should be present during the HClO 4 treatment.

Sample Preparation Techniques Liquid Extraction
Extraction: analyte dissolved in solvent that does not necessarily dissolve the entire sample
Process does not decompose the analyte.
Microwave-assisted extraction uses temperatures 50°–100°C higher than the boiling points of solvents at atmospheric pressure.
Pesticides dissolve but soil remains behind.
Figure 28-12

Supercritical Fluid Extraction (1 of 2)
Supercritical fluid : a fluid whose temperature is above its critical temperature and whose pressure is above its critical pressure
It has properties of both a liquid and a gas.
Supercritical fluid extraction : can be used to extract compounds (usually from solids)
CO 2 is the most common supercritical fluid (inexpensive and eliminates costly disposal of organic solvent waste).
Addition of second solvent such as methanol increases solubility of polar analytes.
Nonpolar substances can be extracted with supercritical Ar.
Some chelators can extract metal ions into supercritical CO 2 .

Supercritical Fluid Extraction (2 of 2)
Figure 28-13

Liquid-Liquid Extraction
Glassware for continuous liquid-liquid extraction of nonvolatile analyte
( a ) Extraction solvent is denser than the liquid being extracted.
( b ) Extraction solvent is lighter than the liquid being extracted.
Typically extract from aqueous solution into organic solvent
Solvent boils from flask and condenses in extraction vessel
Figure 28-14

Dispersive Liquid-Liquid Microextraction
Disperser solvent miscible in both phases

Electrochemistry (1 of 2)
Branch of analytical chemistry that uses electrical measurements of chemical systems for analytical purposes

Electrochemistry (2 of 2)
Desirable attributes of electrochemistry for analytical purposes:
Relatively inexpensive equipment
Potential for miniaturization
Ability to measure some complex samples with little sample preparation

Redox Reactions (1 of 2)
Electrochemical measurements are based upon electrochemical reactions. Electrochemical reactions are commonly called redox reactions .
A redox reaction involves transfer of electrons from one reagent to another reagent.

Redox reactions involve electron transfer
The oxidizing agent , also called the oxidant , takes electrons from the reducing agent. In this process, the reducing agent is oxidized .
The reducing agent , also called the reductant , gives electrons to the oxidizing agent. In this process, the oxidizing agent is reduced .

Redox reactions (2 of 2)
Fe 3+ is the oxidizing agent because it takes an electron from V 2+ .
V 2+ is the reducing agent because it gives an electron to Fe 3+ .
As the reaction proceeds from left to right:
V 2+ is oxidized   (V 2+ → V 3+ + e − )
Fe 3+ is reduced (Fe 3+ + e − → Fe 2+ )

The electrochemical cell isolates the electrons
Michael Faraday (1791–1867) and other early scientists discovered that it was useful to separate the reducing and oxidizing agents and then to connect them with an appropriate electrical circuit that enabled the redox reaction to proceed. This type of apparatus is called an electrochemical cell .
An electrochemical cell can be readily connected to instruments that measure the electric current and potential associated with the redox reaction.
Michael Faraday

Electric Charge
Electrical charge is a measurable property of the electrons that are transferred in a redox reaction.
Electric charge ( q ) is measured in units of coulombs (C).
A single electron or proton has a charge of 1.602  10 −19 C.
The Faraday constant ( F ) gives the magnitude of the charge on a mole of electrons:
𝐹 = (1.602 × 10 −19 C) (6.022 × 10 23 mol −1 ) = 9.649 × 10 4 C/mol

Electric Current
Electric current is the quantity of charge flowing each second through a circuit.
The unit of current is the ampere , abbreviated A .
One ampere represents the flow of one coulomb of charge per second past a point in a circuit.

Voltage, Work, and Free Energy
Any electric charge creates an electric potential .
Electric potentials of opposite sign are attractive.
Positive and negative charges attract each other.
Electric potentials of the same sign are repulsive.
Positive charges repel other positive charges; negative charges repel other negative charges.
The electric potential difference, E , between two points is the work required or which can be done when a unit charge moves from one point to the other.
Potential difference is measured in units of volts (V) .

Current

Relation between work, voltage, and charge
The energy harvested from a spontaneous redox can be used to do work.
Or work can be done to store energy by recharging a redox system.
1 V = 1 J/C
q = the total amount of charge transferred in the redox reaction
E = the potential difference through which the charge moves

Sign conventions for heat and work
Heat ( q ) flow from the surroundings to the system is positive.
Heat ( q ) flow from the system to the surroundings is negative.
Work ( w ) done on a system is positive.
Work ( w ) done on the surroundings is negative.

Calculating Δ G : Gibbs Free Energy of Reaction
The Gibbs free energy change for the reaction of an electrochemical cell represents the maximum work that could be extracted from one mole of a redox reaction assuming perfect efficiency (no heat losses).
Δ G = Gibbs free energy change in joules (J)
E = potential difference in volts (V)
F = Faraday constant (C/mol)
n = unit charges per molecule
A negative Δ G corresponds to a spontaneous reaction as written.
A positive Δ G corresponds to a nonspontaneous reaction as written.
[Speaker Notes] Answer: 10.4 V

Ohm’s Law
Ohm's law states that current, I , is directly proportional to the potential difference, E , across a circuit and inversely proportional to the resistance , R , of the circuit.
The greater the potential difference ( E ), the more current ( I ) that will flow.
The greater the resistance ( R ), the less current ( I ) that will flow.

Power
Power , P , is the work done per unit time.
The SI unit of power is the watt (W) .
1 W = 1  J / s
𝑃 = 𝐸 ∙ 𝐼 = (𝐼 ∙ 𝑅) ∙ 𝐼 = 𝐼 2 ∙ 𝑅
A cell that delivers 1 A of current through a potential difference of 1 V will generate 1 W of power.

A Galvanic Cell
A galvanic cell (also called a voltaic cell ) uses a spontaneous redox reaction to generate electricity.
A battery is a galvanic cell.
In a galvanic cell (battery), the oxidizing and reducing agents are organized in a manner that allows the user to control when current flows and when it does not.
A replica of Allessandro Volta’s original battery (invented in 1799) is shown. If the top is connected to the bottom by a wire, current will flow.

A Cell in Action (1 of 2)
Figure 14-5 shows a galvanic cell with two electrodes suspended in a solution of CdCl 2
At the Cd electrode, Cd metal is oxidized to Cd 2+ ( aq ).
The electrode at which oxidation occurs is the anode.
Electrons flow through the external circuit from the Cd electrode to the Ag electrode.
At the Ag electrode, Ag + from AgCl is reduced to Ag metal.
The electrode at which reduction occurs is the cathode.

Half-Reactions and Net Reactions
The net reaction is composed of a reduction and an oxidation, each of which is called a half-reaction .
The two half-reactions are written with equal numbers of electrons before adding to obtain the net reaction .

Divided cell with a salt bridge (1 of 2)
A salt bridge is a U-shaped tube filled with a gel containing KNO 3 or other electrolyte not involved in the reaction.
Separate the reactants into two half-cells connected with a salt bridge:
The Ag + cannot diffuse to the wrong electrode.
Oxidation and reduction happen at the proper electrode.
Electrons flow through the circuit, enabling the galvanic cell to work.
To prevent charge buildup in each half-cell:

Standard Potentials Potentiometer
The instrument that is used to measure the voltage of a galvanic cell is a potentiometer (also often called a voltmeter ).
Voltage tells us how much work can be done by electrons flowing through the wire connecting the half-cells.
When electrons flow from the − to the + side of the voltmeter (as shown in the figure), the voltage is positive.
When electrons flow in the opposite direction, from the + to the − side of the voltmeter, the voltage is negative.

Practical application of galvanic cells: pH meters
When a pH probe is dipped into a solution to measure pH, a galvanic cell is created.
The pH probe constitutes one half-cell with a salt-bridge.
The solution whose pH is measured constitutes the second half-cell.
The pH meter is the potentiometer (voltmeter).
The center wire of the BNC socket is the positive input.
The outer connection of the BNC socket is the negative input.

Measured cell potential
The potentiometer in the circuit measures the difference in electric potential (voltage) between the two metal electrodes.
E = E + − E −
E + is the potential of the electrode attached to the positive terminal.
E − is the potential of the electrode attached to the negative terminal.

Standard conditions for galvanic cells
Under standard conditions:
All reactant concentrations (for dissolved substances) and pressures (for gases) are adjusted to ensure that the activity of each reactant equals one
All product concentrations (for dissolved substances) and pressures (for gases) are adjusted to ensure that the activity of each reaction product equals one
The temperature is fixed at a well-defined value (for example, T = 25°C ).

Standard cell potential
When all components of both half-cells are present at standard concentrations, pressures, and temperatures, then the measured cell potential is called the standard cell potential ( E o ) .
is the standard reduction potential of the electrode attached to the positive terminal.
is the standard reduction potential of the electrode attached to the negative terminal.

Nernst Equation for a Complete Reaction (1 of 2)
Stepwise strategy for setting up a Nernst equation for a complete reaction
Step 1 Write reduction half-reactions for both half-cells and look up
and
in a table of standard reduction potentials. In preparation for balancing the overall redox reaction, scale the half-reactions so that they contain the same number of electrons, n . When you scale a half reaction, do not scale E ° .
Step 2 Write a Nernst equation for the right half-cell, which is attached to the positive terminal of the potentiometer. This is E + . The symbol E + does not indicate whether the electrode is the anode or the cathode, only that it is attached to the positive terminal.
Step 3 Write a Nernst equation for the left half-cell, which is attached to the negative terminal of the potentiometer. This is E − . The symbol E − does not indicate whether the electrode is the anode or the cathode, only that it is attached to the negative terminal.
Step 4 Find the net cell voltage by subtraction.
Step 5 Write the net cell reaction by subtracting the left half-reaction from the right half-reaction.

Nernst equation for a complete reaction
E = E + − E −

Electrons flow spontaneously through the circuit from the more negative electrode to the more positive electrode.
If E is positive, electrons flow through the wire from the left-hand electrode to the right-hand electrode.
If E is negative, electrons flow through the wire from the right-hand electrode to the left-hand electrode.

Whenever H + appears in a redox reaction, or whenever reactants or products are acids or bases, reduction potentials are pH dependent.
The pH inside a plant or animal cell is about 7; redox potentials that apply at pH 0 are not appropriate.
The standard potential for a redox reaction is defined for a galvanic cell in which all activities are unity.
The formal potential is the reduction potential that applies under a specified set of conditions.
Biochemists call the formal potential at pH 7 E ° ′.

Relation between E ° and E °′
where
F represents the formal concentration of A and B. [A] and [B] are converted to F A and F B using the fractional composition equations in Section 10-5
To measure E °′ adjust the formal concentrations of the oxidized and reduced species so they are equal and adjust the pH to 7. The log term is zero under these conditions and the measured potential (versus S.H.E.) is E °′.

Electrodes and Potentiometry
Potentiometry uses specially-configured galvanic cells to measure voltages that provide chemical information about an analyte.

Potentiometry – What are the electrodes?
Reference Electrode *
half-cell with a fixed composition, so it has a constant potential
includes the half-cell on the left and the salt bridge (indicated by the dotted green box)
Indicator (working) Electrode*
electrode that responds to the analyte activity
the electrical conductor (the Pt wire) that exchanges electrons with the indicator half-cell
*The reference electrode can also refer only to the electrical conductor or wire that exchanges electrons with the reference half-cell. The Indicator electrode can also refer to an entire half-cell.

Where is the solution that is analyzed?
In potentiometry, one end of the salt bridge AND the indicator electrode are dipped into the solution that is analyzed.

Reference Electrode (1 of 2)
The key property of a reference electrode:
To serve as a reference electrode , a half-cell must have a potential (voltage) that remains essentially constant over the course of a measurement.
The reference electrode potential is typically held constant using a saturated solution.
In a saturated solution, the dissolving and precipitating equilibria hold the concentrations of dissolved ions constant.
saturated solution  constant ion concentrations  constant reference electrode potential

Reference Electrode (2 of 2)
The three most important reference electrodes for potentiometry are
Standard Hydrogen Electrode (S.H.E.)
Saturated Silver-Silver Chloride Electrode ( Ag|AgCl )
Saturated Calomel Electrode (S.C.E.)
All these reference electrodes use a saturated solution to hold the potential of the reference electrode constant during potentiometry measurements.

Saturated Hydrogen Electrode (S.H.E.)
The standard reference electrode for tabulated standard reduction potentials.
A standard hydrogen electrode is difficult to use because it requires:
H 2 gas
a freshly prepared catalytic Pt surface, which is easily poisoned
Because they are easier to use, the silver-silver chloride and the calomel reference electrodes are used with much greater frequency in experiments than the S.H.E. electrode.

What is a Junction Potential? (1 of 2)
A junction potential is the voltage difference present at the interface between two dissimilar electrolyte solutions.
Junction potentials develop because oppositely charged ions often have different mobilities .
Example: Cl − ions diffuse faster into water than do Na + ions, causing a charge separation at the boundary between a NaCl salt bridge and water
Figure 15-9

What is a junction potential? (2 of 2)
A junction potential is the voltage difference present at the interface between two dissimilar electrolyte solutions.
The junction potential puts a fundamental limitation on the accuracy of direct potentiometric measurements because we usually do not know the size of the junction potential.
𝐸 = 𝐸 ind − 𝐸 ref + 𝐸 junction

so the NaCl region will be depleted of Cl – faster than the NaNO 3 region will be depleted of

ceramic plug (common)

Conductive nature of glass membrane
The glass membranes in pH probes must be slightly conductive. Glass membranes in pH probes are built from amorphous silica.
Amorphous silica (Figure 15-16c) is irregularly ordered and has pores that are coated with  negatively charged oxygen atoms.
Monovalent cations like Na + can move sluggishly through the pores (Figure 15-16a), making the amorphous silica slightly conductive.
Figure 15-16
Crystalline silica (Figure 15-6b) is not conductive because it lack pores.

Cross-section of the pH sensing glass membrane
Figure 15-17
Figure 15-18
The two surfaces of the pH sensing glass membrane absorb water to form hydrated gel layers (Figure 15-17)
Metal ions in these hydrated gel regions of the membrane diffuse out of the glass and into solution.
H + can diffuse into the membrane to replace metal ions. The reaction in which H + replaces cations in the glass is an ion-exchange equilibrium (Figure 15-18).
A pH electrode responds selectively to H + because H + is the ion that diffuses most effectively into the hydrated gel layer.

Response of glass electrode
Potential difference across the glass membrane measured by the inner and outer silver-silver chloride reference electrodes
Depends on chloride concentration in each electrode compartment and potential difference across the glass membrane
[Cl − ] is fixed in each compartment and [H + ] is fixed on the inside of the glass membrane
Only variable is the pH of the analyte solution outside the membrane
𝐸 = constant − β(0.059 16)pH(outside)	at 25 °C

Calibrating a Glass pH Electrode
A glass pH electrode must be calibrated:
with a minimum of two standard buffers
one with a pH higher than the sample
one with a pH lower than the sample
at the same temperature as the unknown
The calibration should be repeated every two hours.
Figure 15-19

Storage and Care of a Glass pH Electrode
store in aqueous solution to prevent dehydration of the glass
if the electrode has dried, recondition it in dilute acid for several hours
if the electrode is to be used above pH 9, soak it in a high-pH buffer
If it responds sluggishly, or cannot be calibrated properly:
soak in 6 M HCl
soak in 20 wt % aqueous NH 4 HF 2 for 1 min as a last resort

Causes of Errors in pH Measurement
Standard buffer pH uncertainty (~0.01 pH unit)
Junction potential uncertainty (~0.01 pH unit)
Junction potential drift error
Concentrated sodium error (glass electrodes respond weakly to Na + )
Strong acid error (glass electrodes underestimate the H + concentration in strong acids)
Equilibration time error (when measurements are made too quickly)
Hydration of glass initialization error (when a dry electrode is not properly equilibrated prior to first use)
Temperature error (Difference between temperatures of standard buffers and sample)
Dirty Electrode drift error

The Sodium (or Alkaline) Error
In highly basic NaOH solutions, where [H + ] is very low and [Na + ] is high, the weak electrode response to Na + causes the pH probe to underestimate the true pH of the probe solution.
This pH underestimation problem in highly basic NaOH solutions is called the sodium or alkaline error .
Figure 15-21

Accuracy of glass electrode pH measurements
The most carefully made pH measurements made with a glass electrode have an uncertainty of ± 0.02 pH units at best, which corresponds to ~5% uncertainty in the measured activity, and concentration of H + .
Measurement of pH differences between solutions can be more accurate (uncertainty  ± 0.002 pH unit), but knowledge of the true pH will still be at least an order of magnitude more uncertain.

Selectivity Coefficient
Ion selective electrodes are not perfectly selective. Interferents can contribute to the overall ISE cell voltage.
The selectivity coefficient gives the relative response to different species with the same charge
The smaller the selectivity coefficient, the less interference by X when measuring A.

Example: Using the Selectivity Coefficient (1 of 3)
A fluoride ion-selective electrode has a selectivity coefficient
What will be the change in electrode potential when 1.0 × 10 −4 M F − at pH 5.5 is raised to pH 10.5?

Example: Using the Selectivity Coefficient (2 of 3)
Solution: From Equation 15-10, we find the potential with negligible OH – at pH 5.5:
E = constant − 0.059 16 log[1.0 × 10 −4 ] = constant + 236.6 mV
At pH 10.50, [OH − ] = 3.2 × 10 −4 M , so the electrode potential is
E = constant − 0.059 16 log[1.0 × 10 −4 + (0.1)(3.2 × 10 −4 )]
= constant + 229.5 mV
The change is 229.5 − 236.6 = −7.1 mV, which is quite significant. If you didn’t know about the pH change, you would think that the concentration of F – had increased by 32%.

Compound Electrodes (1 of 2)
Compound electrodes contain a conventional electrode surrounded by a membrane that isolates (or generates) the analyte to which the electrode responds.
The Severinghaus CO 2 sensing compound electrode
When CO 2 diffuses through the semipermeable membrane, it reacts with water to form carbonic acid.
Carbonic acid lowers the pH of the thin layer of electrolyte inside the CO 2 permeable membrane.
The glass electrode responds to the change in pH, providing a measure of the CO 2 concentration outside the electrode.

Advantages of Ion-Selective Electrodes
less expensive than competing techniques such as atomic spectroscopy and ion chromatography
linear response to log
over a wide range
nondestructive
noncontaminating
short response time
unaffected by color or turbidity

Challenges of Ion-Selective Electrodes
Precision is usually worse than 1%
Electrodes can be fouled by proteins or other organic solutes  sluggish, drifting response
Certain ions interfere with or poison particular electrodes
Some electrodes are fragile and have limited shelf life
Reliable concentration measurements require the ionic strength to be held constant in all matrices
Electrodes respond only to uncomplexed ions. Complexed ions are not seen.

Detecting Ca 2+ in human plasma
Much of the Ca 2+ in human plasma is complexed by other ligands, and thus is invisible to an ISE based sensor.
Other measurement approaches can be used to see all of the Ca 2+ (free and complexed) in the plasma.
For Example: Capillary Electrophoresis coupled to an Inductively Coupled Plasma Detector (ICP)
The largest peak is free Ca 2+ . The other peaks are proteins or small molecules to which Ca 2+ is bound.
Detection of calcium-containing species in human blood plasma by capillary electrophoresis-ICP.

Using Standard Additions with Electrodes (1 of 2)
When ion-selective electrodes are used, it is important that the composition of the standard solution closely approximates the composition of the unknown.
This can be ensured by using the method of standard additions .
In the method of standard additions, the electrode is immersed in a sample of the unknown and the potential is recorded before and after each small aliquot of standard solution is added.
A graphical procedure is used to extrapolate back to the volume of standard ( V s ) that would contain the same number of moles of analyte as the unknown.

Doping Impurities Increase Conductivity
Figure 15-39
( a ) pure silicon: electrons are locked into sigma bonds.

( c ) p -doped silicon: the impurity (e.g. Al) increases conductivity by adding a hole (o). The new hole can be occupied by an electron from a neighboring bond, effectively moving the hole to the neighboring bond.

Chapter 17_Electroanalytical Techniques Fundamentals of Electrolysis
Electrolysis is the process in which a chemical reaction is forced to occur at an electrode by an imposed voltage.
Previous chapters dealt with potentiometry , in which voltage was measured in the absence of significant current.
Electroanalytical methods that make use of electrolysis:
Electrogravimetric analysis
Coulometry
Amperometry
Voltammetry

Fundamentals of Electrolysis Using Electrolysis to Deposit Copper Metal
Dip platinum and copper electrodes into a solution of Cu 2+ .
Connect the (+) side of a variable voltage battery to the Pt electrode
Connect the (−) side of the battery to the copper electrode.
Gradually increase the applied voltage.
At some point, the voltage will be high enough to drive the following reactions:
Oxygen gas will be liberated at the Pt electrode.
Cu 2+ will be reduced to copper metal on the Cu electrode.
Figure 17-1

Working and Counter Electrodes
The electrode at which the other half-reaction occurs is called the counter electrode .
Figure 17-1
The electrode at which the half-reaction of interest occurs is called the working electrode .

Recall - Current
Current is (a) the amount of charge flowing through a circuit per unit time and (b) measured by the ammeter.

Voltage Changes when Current Flows
When electric current is negligible, the cell voltage is
E = E (cathode) – E (anode)
When electric current is not negligible, the cell voltage equation adds extra terms.
E = E (cathode) – E (anode) – IR – overpotentials
* Concentration polarization
* Ohmic Potential
* Overpotentials

Concentration Polarization Contribution to Cell Voltage
When current is flowing in an electrolysis experiment:
E = E (cathode) – E (anode) – IR – overpotentials
Concentration polarization occurs when the concentrations of reactants and products at the surface of an electrode are not the same as they are in the bulk solution. It causes E (cathode) and E (anode) to differ from predictions based on the bulk solution concentrations.
Concentration polarization is accounted for by substituting the concentrations at the electrode surfaces into the reaction quotient Q of the Nernst equation.

Ohmic Potential Contribution to Cell Voltage
When current is flowing in an electrolysis experiment:
E = E (cathode) – E (anode) – IR – overpotentials
Ohmic potential (= IR ) is the voltage needed to overcome electric resistance ( R ) of the solution in the electrochemical cell when a current is flowing.
E ohmic = IR
Resistance is measured in ohms, whose symbol is capital Greek omega, Ω .

Overpotential Contribution to Cell Voltage
The cell voltage equation when current is flowing:
E = E (cathode) – E (anode) – IR – overpotentials
Overpotential is the voltage required to overcome the activation energy for a reaction at an electrode. A greater overpotential is required to drive a reaction at a faster rate.
The cathode and anode each have their own overpotentials.
The overpotential depends on the nature of the surface and the specific half-reaction.
Example: The cell voltage required to evolve H 2 at a Hg electrode is ~1 V higher than the cell voltage required to evolve H 2 at a Pt electrode. This ~1 V overpotential on the Hg electrode arises because the activation energy for evolving H 2 is higher on Hg than on Pt.

Overpotentials: The Voltage Required to Overcome the Activation Energy for a Reaction at an Electrode
( a ) Schematic energy profile for electron transfer from a metal to H 3 O + , leading to the liberation of H 2 .
Figure 17-2
( b ) Applying a potential to the metal raises the energy of the electron in the metal and decreases the activation energy for electron transfer.
Table 17-1 Overpotential (V) for gas evolution at various current densities ( A/m 2 ) at 25°C

Voltage Required for Electrolysis Varies…
When current is flowing in an electrolysis experiment:
Concentration polarization, ohmic potential, and overpotentials make electrolysis more difficult .
Due to these terms, electrolysis requires a greater voltage ( E ) than predicted by the simplified equation, E = E (cathode) – E (anode), used in potentiometry.

Example: Effects of Ohmic Potential, Overpotential, and Concentration Polarization (1 of 5)
Suppose we wish to electrolyze I − to
in a 0.10 M KI solution containing 3.0 × 10 −5 M
(a) Find the cell voltage if no current is flowing. (b) Then suppose that electrolysis increases
to 3.0 × 10 −4 M, but other concentrations are unaffected. Suppose that the cell resistance is 2.0 Ω , the current is 63 mA, the cathode overpotential is 0.382 V, and the anode overpotential is 0.025 V. What voltage is needed to drive the reaction?

Example: Effects of Ohmic Potential, Overpotential, and Concentration Polarization (2 of 5)
Solution: (a) The open-circuit voltage is E (cathode) − E (anode):
Cathode: 2H 2 O + 2e − → H 2 ( g ) + 2OH − E ° = −0.828 V
Anode:
E ° = 0.535 V

Example: Effects of Ohmic Potential, Overpotential, and Concentration Polarization (3 of 5)
Solution:
E = E (cathode) − E (anode) = −0.591 − 0.490 = −1.081 V
We would have to apply −1.081 V to force the reaction to occur.

Example: Effects of Ohmic Potential, Overpotential, and Concentration Polarization (4 of 5)
Solution: (b) Now E (cathode) is unchanged but E (anode) changes because
is different from
in bulk solution.
E = E (cathode) − E (anode) − IR − overpotentials
= − 0.591 V − 0.520 V − (2.0 Ω )(0.063 A) − 0.382 V − 0.025 V
= − 1.644 V
Instead of −1.081 V, we need to apply −1.644 V to drive the reaction. Notice that both overpotentials contribute in a manner that increases the magnitude of the required voltage.

In Electrolysis, when E Is Constant, E (cathode) Drifts
Because analyte concentrations change as an electrolysis experiment proceeds, holding the applied voltage, E , constant, will not hold E (cathode) constant. In general, E (cathode) will gradually drift downward, leading to potentially unwanted additional electrolysis reactions.
E = E (cathode) – E (anode) – IR – overpotentials
To avoid these complications, it would be helpful to hold E (cathode), rather than E , constant.
This is achieved by using a three-electrode cell.

Controlled-Potential Electrolysis with a Three-Electrode Cell
Controlled-potential electrolysis uses a potentiostat to control the potential of the working electrode.
The reference electrode is introduced as a third electrode.
The potentiostat continuously measures the voltage between the working and reference electrodes.
The potentiostat varies the applied cell voltage ( E ) so that the working electrode potential ( E cathode or E anode ) is precisely controlled.
Figure 17-3

Why Can’t The Reference Electrode Serve as a Counter Electrode?
The metal electrodes used for the working and counter (auxiliary) electrodes can handle significant currents and are said to be polarizable because their potentials can be readily adjusted.
Reference electrodes (Ag | AgCl, calomel, etc.) are designed to work with negligible current, and are said to be nonpolarizable , because their potentials are not easily changed.
Because nonpolarizable reference electrodes cannot handle the significant currents used in electrolysis experiments, they cannot replace the counter electrode. Instead, they are added as a third electrode.
In a three-electrode cell most current flows directly from working to counter electrode. Only a tiny current flows from the working to the reference electrode.
Figure 17-3

Amperometry
In amperometry , we measure electric current between a pair of electrodes that are driving an electrolysis reaction.
One reactant is the intended analyte.
The current is measured.
Analyte concentration is proportional to the measured current.

Biosensors
Use biological components such as enzymes, antibodies, or DNA for highly selective response to one analyte
Can be based on any instrumental technology; however, biosensor based on electrochemistry or optical signals are most common
Examples of amperometry biosensors:
Lactate sensors
Hydrogen peroxide sensors
Breast cancer sensors
Blood glucose monitors (most widely used type of biosensor)
