Section 5.1
FUNCTIONAL GROUPS

Functional Groups-1
Alkanes are not generally considered to be functional groups (although C–H bonds can be reactive)
The multiple bonds in alkenes and alkynes are considered functional groups
The six-membered rings consisting of sp 2 hybridized carbons in arenes are considered functional groups
In this chapter, we will focus on alcohols (ROH) and alkyl halides (RX, X = F, Br, Cl, I)
Survey of Functional Groups
Survey of Functional Groups-1
Survey of Functional Groups-2

Section 5.2
IUPAC NOMENCLATURE OF ALKYL HALIDES

Two Types of Nomenclature
There are two distinct types of nomenclature for alkyl halides:
Functional class nomenclature: the alkyl group is treated as the “positive” portion of a halide salt

Two Types of Nomenclature-1
There are two distinct types of nomenclature for alkyl halides:
Functional class nomenclature: the alkyl group is treated as the “positive” portion of a halide salt
Substitutive nomenclature ( preferred ): the halogen is treated as a “halo-” substituent on an alkyl chain

Two Types of Nomenclature-2
When the carbon chain bears both a halogen and an alkyl substituent, the two are considered of equal rank, and the chain is numbered so as to give the lower number to the substituent nearer the end of the chain.

Section 5.3
IUPAC NOMENCLATURE OF ALCOHOLS

Two Types of Nomenclature-3
The two types of nomenclature we saw for alkyl halides apply to alcohols as well:
Functional class nomenclature: the alcohol is named as an “alkyl alcohol”; the alkyl group is treated as a substituent

Two Types of Nomenclature-4
The two types of nomenclature we saw for alkyl halides apply to alcohols as well:
Functional class nomenclature: the alcohol is named as an “alkyl alcohol”; the alkyl group is treated as a substituent
Substitutive nomenclature ( preferred ): the alcohol is named as an “alkanol,” with a number before “alkan” or “ol” to denote the position of the –OH group
Either the “ n -alkanol” or “alkan- n -ol” names are acceptable.

Substitutive Nomenclature
When using the substitutive convention, it is important that the parent chain includes the –OH or –X substituent
For the purposes of numbering, the –OH group takes precedence over halogen substituents and alkyl groups

Section 5.4
CLASSES OF ALCOHOLS AND ALKYL HALIDES

Substitution Pattern
Alcohols, alkyl halides, and other organic compounds are often classified by substitution pattern
The key question is: how many alkyl groups are attached directly to the carbon that bears the functional group?
One alkyl group: primary ( 1 degree )
Two alkyl groups: secondary ( 2 degree )
Three alkyl groups: tertiary ( 3 degree )
Substitution pattern can profoundly affect the reactivity of a carbon!
One attached alkyl group;
Two implicit hydrogens
Two attached alkyl groups;
Two implicit hydrogens

Substitution Pattern - 2
Alcohols, alkyl halides, and other organic compounds are often classified by substitution pattern
The key question is: how many alkyl groups are attached directly to the carbon that bears the functional group?
One alkyl group: primary ( 1 degree )
Two alkyl groups: secondary ( 2 degree )
Three alkyl groups: tertiary ( 3 degree )
Substitution pattern can profoundly affect the reactivity of a carbon!
Three attached alkyl groups, no hydrogens.
Three attached alkyl groups, no hydrogens.

Section 5.5
BONDING IN ALCOHOLS AND ALKYL HALIDES

Hybridization, Geometry, and Bonding
The carbon bonded to –OH or –X is sp 3 hybridized and tetrahedral
The C–O and C–X bonds are polarized toward the more electronegative O or X atom
The carbon atom bonded to O or X is partially positive

Dipole Moments
Because the C–O and C–X bonds are polarized, alcohols and alkyl halides are polar !
Dipole moments are comparable to that of water
Note the direction of the dipole vector and the implied partial charges on C, O, and X

Electrostatic Potential Maps
In both compounds, the heteroatom bears significant negative charge
In methanol, the O– H hydrogen bears most of the positive charge
In chloromethane, the nearby C– H hydrogens bear most of the positive charge

Section 5.6
PHYSICAL PROPERTIES OF ALCOHOLS AND ALKYL HALIDES: INTERMOLECULAR FORCES

Dipolar Forces
Alcohols and alkyl halides are polar . Hence, their molecules engage in dipole-dipole and dipole-induced dipole forces
These forces are much stronger than London forces, and cause the boiling and melting points of alkyl halides and alcohols to be higher than those of alkanes
Why is the boiling point of ethanol abnormally high?

Hydrogen Bonding
Alcohols contain a highly polarized O–H bond, which can engage in a special type of dipole-dipole interaction called a hydrogen bond
The partially negative O on one molecule is attracted to the partially positive H on another
Hydrogen bonding occurs only in compounds containing O–H or N–H bonds
Hydrogen bonds are directional —they involve a small degree of electron sharing
Hydrogen Bonding - 2

Boiling Points
Boiling point increases with molecular weight.
TABLE 5.2 Boiling Point of Some Alkyl Halides and Alcohols
Substituent X and boiling point, °C (1 atm)
Name of alkyl group | Formula | X = F | X = Cl | X = Br | X = I | X = OH
Methyl | CH3X | −78 | −24 | 3 | 42 | 65
Ethyl | CH3CH2X | −32 | 12 | 38 | 72 | 78
Propyl | CH3CH2CH2X | −3 | 47 | 71 | 103 | 97
Pentyl | CH3(CH2)3CH2X | 65 | 108 | 129 | 157 | 138
Hexyl | CH3(CH2)4CH2X | 92 | 134 | 155 | 180 | 157
Boiling points of alcohols are quite high due to hydrogen bonding.
As polarizability of the halogen increases, boiling point increases.

Polarizability
The ability of the electron cloud around an atom to distort its shape is called polarizability
Polarizability is greater for larger halogens; greater polarizability means a larger boiling point
Alkyl fluorides show roughly the opposite trend—fluorine is not very polarizable

Solubility
Alkyl halides are insoluble in water
Alcohols with low molecular weight (small hydrocarbon portions) are soluble in water in all proportions
Alcohols can hydrogen bond with water!
Alcohols with long alkyl chains remain insoluble due to the hydrophobic effect.

Section 5.7
PREPARATION OF ALKYL HALIDES FROM ALCOHOLS AND HYDROGEN HALIDES

Substitution Reactions
Alcohols and alkyl halides differ only in the group bound to the alkyl group (–OH or –X)
A reaction that replaces –OH with –X or vice versa is called a substitution reaction
Alkyl halides are valuable precursors to other organic functional groups—they represent a synthetic “hub”
We will study this reaction to gain insight into reaction mechanism and general principles of reactivity

Reactivity Trends
The reactivity of hydrogen halides parallels their acidity
HI > HBr > HCl > HF
too reactive				too unreactive
The reactivity of alcohols depends on substitution pattern

Reactivity Trends: Examples
Tertiary ROH react with HCl and HBr in minutes; secondary and primary require HBr and elevated temperatures.

Section 5.8
REACTIONS OF ALCOHOLS WITH HYDROGEN HALIDES: THE S N 1 MECHANISM

Reaction Mechanisms
A reaction mechanism consists of a series of steps leading from reactants to products
Each elementary step includes reactants, a single transition state , and products
Kinetic and thermodynamic data, computations, and other experiments are used to develop a mechanism
We are interested in how electrons flow and the energy progress of mechanisms

Thermodynamics of Substitution
Using heats of combustion or formation, we can determine ∆ H degree for the reaction of HX with ROH
∆ H degree = −46 kJ . This substitution is exothermic, but note the phase of HCl!
When aqueous HCl is used, ∆ H degree = +29 kJ Concentrated HCl can be used to make the reaction favorable
ROH + HX: The S N 1 Mechanism

Step 1: Proton Transfer
HCl donates a proton to the basic oxygen atom of the alcohol
p K a values confirm that this is a highly favorable process
The alcohol carbon now bears even more positive charge

Step 2: Dissociation
Water acts as a leaving group , departing with a pair of electrons to form a carbocation
This step is slow due to the formation of a relatively unstable carbocation
The alcohol carbon now has a full positive charge!
The positive carbon is electrophilic and the chloride anion is nucleophilic

Step 3: Association
The chloride anion associates with the carbocation, forming a C–Cl bond
This step forms the product and is highly favorable and rapid

The S N 1 Mechanism
The slow step is unimolecular , so this mechanism is called S N 1 .
The stability of the product of the slow step (the carbocation) is critical.

Section 5.9
STRUCTURE, BONDING, AND STABILITY OF CARBOCATIONS

Stability Trends of Carbocations
Carbocations are stabilized by attached alkyl groups
Methyl (CH 3 + ) and primary carbocations are “off limits”
Alkyl groups are electron donating
Resonance delocalization also stabilizes carbocations!

Electron Donation from Alkyl Groups
Electron density maps show how alkyl groups disperse positive charge
Non-traditional resonance forms also show this hyperconjugation effect

Electron Donation from Alkyl Groups-1
When applying hyperconjugation to carbocations more complicated than CH 3 CH 2 + , it is helpful to keep track of the various bonds.
Begin with the positively charged carbon and label the three bonds originating from it with the Greek letter α.
Proceed along the chain, labeling the bonds extending from the next carbon β, those from the next carbon γ, and so on.

Section 5.10
EFFECT OF ALCOHOL STRUCTURE ON REACTION RATE

Hammond’s Postulate
Hammond’s postulate: the transition state resembles the species to which it is closest in energy
In the slow step of S N 1 , the rate-determining TS resembles the carbocation
Structural factors that stabilize the carbocation will also stabilize the transition state and make the reaction faster
More stable carbocations form more rapidly than less stable carbocations.
Alcohol Structure and Reaction Rate

Section 5.11
Copyright © 2017 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. No reproduction or distribution without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Partial Inversion
Reactions of chiral secondary alcohols occur mostly via inversion of configuration
Note that some retention of configuration does occur due to attack on the same face as the departing leaving group.
The unstable carbocation remains close to the leaving group, blocking one face of the carbocation

Scrambling
Chiral tertiary alcohols react to give a racemic mixture of tertiary alkyl halides
Persistent tertiary cations are attacked with equal probability on both faces
The configuration at the original stereocenter is scrambled

Inequivalent Faces
Chiral tertiary alcohols react to give an unequal mixture of diastereomeric alkyl halides
The two faces of the carbocation are inequivalent and are not attacked with equal probability
Diastereomeric alcohols give the same distribution of products

Section 5.12
CARBOCATIONS REARRANGEMENTS

Unexpected Products
In some reactions of ROH with HX, unexpected products are obtained
The carbon skeleton appears to have rearranged, and the chlorine ends up bound at an unexpected site

Unexpected Products-1
In some reactions of ROH with HX, unexpected products are obtained

Carbocation Rearrangements
Shifts of hydrogen or a methyl group can occur in carbocations when such a shift:
Involves movement by only one carbon
Leads to a more stable (more substituted and/or resonance stabilized) carbocation
Note the new electrophilic carbon!
This methyl shift converts a secondary cation into a tertiary cation.
Reaction Coordinate Diagram

Hydride Shifts
Methyl shifts from quaternary carbons are common, as tertiary carbocations are the result
Tertiary carbocations also result via hydride shifts from tertiary carbons
Whenever you see a secondary carbocation in a reaction mechanism, be on the lookout for rearrangement possibilities.

Section 5.13
REACTIONS OF METHYL AND PRIMARY ALCOHOLS WITH HYDROGEN HALIDES
Methyl and Primary Halides Revisited

The first step is still protonation of the alcohol oxygen
Loss of water as a leaving group and bonding of the halide occur in a concerted manner in the second step

Section 5.14
OTHER METHODS FOR CONVERTING ALCOHOLS TO ALKYL HALIDES

Why Use Other Methods?
Alkyl halides are very important synthetic precursors, and sometimes interesting in their own right
The stereochemical course of ROH + HX can be unpredictable
Many functional groups are not stable to concentrated hydrohalic acid; milder conditions are necessary
Like ROH + HX, other methods are based on turning the hydroxyl group into a good leaving group

Thionyl Chloride
Inversion of configuration is the typical result

Thionyl Chloride-1
The key intermediate is an alkyl chlorosulfite
Good leaving group!

Section 5.15
SULFONATES AS ALKYL HALIDE SURROGATES

Sulfonates as Pseudohalides
We’ve seen that the chlorosulfite group is a good leaving group…
Good leaving group!
Halogens themselves are good leaving groups—that’s where their value lies
A functional group related to the chlorosulfite called a sulfonate is also a good leaving group
Good leaving groups are sometimes called pseudohalides for their resemblance to the halides.

Sulfonates as Pseudohalides-1
The most common sulfonates are toluenesulfonates (ROTs) and methanesulfonates (ROMs)

Preparation of Sulfonates
The reaction doesn’t involve the alcohol carbon at all, so retention of configuration is observed

Section 6.1
FUNCTIONAL-GROUP TRANSFORMATION BY NUCLEOPHILIC SUBSTITUTION

Nucleophilic Substitution
Formation of alkyl halides from alcohols is a substitution process
S N 1 :
S N 2 :
In these reactions halide serves as the nucleophile and H 2 O as the leaving group

Nucleophilic Substitution-1
Alkyl halides and pseudohalides undergo an analogous process with nucleophiles M + Y −
Because the added reagent is nucleophilic, this is called nucleophilic substitution

The Nucleophile
The nucleophile is typically a simple metal salt M + Y − , where M + is Li + , K + , or Na +
The anion Y − can be any of a variety of organic or inorganic anions. Examples :
Nucleophilicity is related in general to basicity , since nucleophiles are Lewis bases

The Electrophile
The electrophile must contain a C–LG bond polarized towards LG, the leaving group
Alkyl halides and pseudohalides (sulfonates) are most common
The partially positive carbon reacts as a Lewis acid.
The electrophilic carbon must be sp 3 hybridized

Section 6.2
RELATIVE REACTIVITY OF HALIDE LEAVING GROUPS

Leaving Group Ability
In a nucleophilic substitution reaction, the leaving group accepts electrons, just like an acid
Good leaving groups –X are associated with strong acids HX
Good leaving groups are also associated with weaker C–X bonds

Section 6.3
THE S N 2 MECHANISM OF NUCLEOPHILIC SUBSTITUTION

Kinetics of S N 2
Substitution of primary and methyl alcohols and primary and methyl halides is found to be bimolecular
The rate law depends on the concentrations of both reactants
Both reactants are involved in the rate-determining transition state
In this TS, attack of the nucleophile and loss of the leaving group occur simultaneously
Reaction Coordinate Diagram

Stereochemistry of S N 2
Studies of substitutions of optically active alkyl halides show that the nucleophile does not occupy the position where the leaving group was…
S N 2 occurs with inversion of configuration
100 %! stereospecific

Stereoelectronics of S N 2
Why does S N 2 occur with inversion of configuration stereospecifically?
Most stereospecific behavior has a stereoelectronic explanation based on molecular orbitals
In S N 2 reactions, the HOMO of the nucleophile overlaps with the LUMO of the electrophile
The most prominent lobe of the LUMO, which also lacks a node, is on the backside of the electrophilic carbon
We say that the nucleophile engages in backside attack .

Section 6.4
STERIC EFFECTS AND S N 2 REACTION RATES

Steric Hindrance in the Electrophile
Alkyl groups bound to the electrophilic carbon represent a hindrance toward backside attack of the nucleophile
We observe this in the relative rates of S N 2 for methyl, primary, secondary, and tertiary alkyl bromides
The space “occupied” by the alkyl groups is called steric hindrance
Steric Hindrance in the Electrophile-1

The Neopentyl Effect: Adjacent Alkyl Groups
Even alkyl groups adjacent to the electrophilic carbon slow the rate of S N 2
This is often called the neopentyl effect because neopentyl halides react very slowly in S N 2
neopentyl bromide

NUCLEOPHILES AND NUCLEOPHILICITY

Solvolysis
Because the nucleophile in S N 1 is typically weak and liquid, it can be used as solvent in a solvolysis reaction
Common when water, ethanol, or methanol is the nucleophile
Ensures that as soon as RX dissociates, the carbocation is captured by a nucleophilic molecule
Solvolytic conditions can also be used for S N 2 with weak nucleophiles

Nucleophiles & Nucleophilicity
Nucleophiles are “nucleus loving.” The most common type of nucleophile is a lone pair on a negative or neutral atom
Nucleophilicity is a measure of the speed (kinetics!) with which a nucleophile reacts
Nucleophilicity is related to basicity, with some quirks

Trends in Nucleophilicity
Across a row the periodic table, nucleophilicity tracks with basicity
CH 3 − > NH 2 − > OH − > F −
Down a group, nucleophilicity increases even though basicity decreases!
Larger and more polarizable anions are more nucleophilic but less basic
Solvation also plays a role:
Smaller anions are more easily solvated and shielded from acting as nucleophiles
Larger anions are more difficult to solvate and more “available” to act as nucleophiles
Ultimately, carbon is not hydrogen .

Section 6.6
THE S N 1 MECHANISM OF NUCLEOPHILIC SUBSTITUTION

Kinetics of S N 1
Substitution of tertiary alcohols and alkyl halides is found to be unimolecular
The rate law depends only on the concentration of the electrophile
Only the electrophile is involved in the rate-determining transition state
In this TS, the leaving group is separating from the electrophilic carbon and taking a pair of electrons with it

Reaction Coordinate Diagram
Loss of leaving group
Attack of weak nucleophile
Loss of proton

Section 6.7
STEREOCHEMISTRY OF S N 1 REACTIONS

Planar Intermediate of S N 1
The carbocation intermediate of S N 1 is achiral as long as no other chirality centers are present
The positive carbon and three attached atoms form a plane
Scrambling is expected .
In theory, the nucleophile should attack either side of a planar achiral cation with equal probability, affording a racemic mixture

Practical S N 1 : Partial Inversion
Many S N 1 reactions of optically active starting materials do not yield racemates!
Mostly inversion occurs, suggesting that the leaving group “hovers” near the cationic carbon during nucleophilic attack
The cationic carbon and leaving group anion form a tight ion pair .
Stereochemistry of S N 1

Section 6.8
CARBOCATION REARRANGEMENTS IN S N 1 REACTIONS

Carbocation Rearrangements
Some S N 1 reactions, especially of secondary alkyl halides, give unexpected products

Carbocation Rearrangements-1
The alkyl halide ionizes to give a carbocation and bromide ion. This is the rate-determining step.
The intermediate carbocation rapidly rearranges to form a more stable carbocation
Keep in mind that (a) the product cation must be more stable than the reactant cation and (b) only adjacent groups can migrate .

Carbocation Rearrangements-2
The tertiary carbocation is attacked by water acting as a nucleophile.
Proton transfer from the alkyloxonium ion to water completes the process .

Section 6.9
EFFECT OF SOLVENT ON THE RATE OF NUCLEOPHILIC SUBSTITUTION

Key Questions
What properties of the solvent influence the rate most?
How does the rate-determining step of the mechanism respond to a change in these properties ?

Classes of Solvents
Protic versus aprotic
Protic solvents include O–H or N–H bonds and have the ability to hydrogen bond
Aprotic solvents…don’t
Polar versus nonpolar
Polar solvents have a permanently asymmetric distribution of charge and polarized covalent bonds
Nonpolar solvents are either symmetric or include only weakly polarized bonds (C–C, C–H)
The dielectric constant is one measure of the polarity of a solvent ( dipole moment is another)
Examples of Solvents

Solvent Effects in S N 2
Solvents for S N 2 reactions must be polar (why?)
The “proticity” of the solvent exerts a strong influence on the rate of the S N 2 reaction
Protic solvents bind strongly to anionic nucleophiles via hydrogen bonding
Such binding slows S N 2 because the nucleophile is blocked from attacking the electrophile
Protic solvent molecules shield the nucleophile and slow attack on the electrophile .

Solvent Effects in S N 2 -1
Aprotic solvents cannot hydrogen bond and bind much more weakly to anionic nucleophiles
The nucleophile is thus much more “naked” and thus reacts much faster in S N 2
The “naked” nucleophile in a polar aprotic solvent reacts rapidly in S N 2 .

Solvent Effects in S N 1
Reaction rate increases dramatically as the polarity of the solvent increases
The rate-determining transition state ( in which charge separation is developing) is stabilized by Coulombic interactions
Protic solvents enhance the effect relative to aprotic solvents

Solvent | Dielectric constant ɛ | Relative rate
Acetic acid | 6 | 1
Methanol | 33 | 4
Formic acid | 58 | 5,000
Water | 78 | 150,000
*Ratio of first-order rate constant for solvolysis in indicated solvent to that for solvolysis in acetic acid at 25 degree C .

Section 6.10
NUCLEOPHILIC SUBSTITUTION OF ALKYL SULFONATES

Sulfonates in Nucleophilic Substituion
Sulfonate anions are the conjugate bases of strong acids, so they are good leaving groups
Alkyl sulfonates undergo nucleophilic substitution ( S N 1 and S N 2) just like alkyl halides; the same trends apply

The Stereochemical Advantage
Conversion of alcohols to sulfonates does not involve the C–O bond at all; hence, the enantiomeric purity of chiral alcohols is not affected by conversion to a sulfonate
Not the case for conversion of alcohols to alkyl halides!

Section 6.11
INTRODUCTION TO ORGANIC SYNTHESIS: RETROSYNTHETIC ANALYSIS

Synthetic Planning
We often want to synthesize a target compound from readily available starting materials
To do this, we must think backwards and use retrosynthetic analysis
The basic tool of retrosynthesis is the retrosynthetic arrow
We work backwards at first, making sure to use known reactions run in reverse
We then evaluate the reasonableness of the synthesis in the forward direction
This process will likely involve some false starts !

Example: Thiol from Alcohol
Imagine we wanted to prepare ( R )-2-butanethiol from ( S )-2-butanol
The secondary alkyl thiol can be made from an S N 2 reaction between an alkyl halide and HS − …

Example: Thiol from Alcohol-1
Imagine we wanted to prepare ( R )-2-butanethiol from ( S )-2-butanol
…and the alkyl bromide can be made from the alcohol via ROH + HBr substitution

Example: Thiol from Alcohol-2
Imagine we wanted to prepare ( R )-2-butanethiol from ( S )-2-butanol
However , ROH + HBr proceeds with predominant inversion of configuration—we’ll get the wrong isomer!

Example: Thiol from Alcohol-3
Imagine we wanted to prepare ( R )-2-butanethiol from ( S )-2-butanol
A better plan uses an alkyl sulfonate as an intermediate, rather than an alkyl halide

Section 6.12
SUBSTITUTION VERSUS ELIMINATION: A LOOK AHEAD

Scope and Limitations
No chemical reaction is perfectly general
Every reaction is associated with some limitations that define the scope of substrates amenable to the reaction
For example, the scope of the S N 2 reaction is limited to primary and secondary alkyl halides and pseudohalides
What does happen when we treat a tertiary alkyl halide with S N 2 conditions ?

Nucleophilic Elimination
A nucleophile ( Lewis base) can act as a Br ø nsted base as well!
Deprotonation at an atom β to a leaving group with loss of the leaving group and formation of a double bond is called elimination
We will soon learn how to predict whether substitution or elimination will occur for an alkyl halide with β -hydrogens.

Section 7.1
ALKENE NOMENCLATURE

Naming Alkenes
In the name of the corresponding alkane, - ane is replaced with – ene
The parent chain of the compound includes the double bond
The chain is numbered to give the doubly bound carbons the lowest numbers possible
Priorities of Substituents
Alkene-containing Groups

Cycloalkenes
Cycloalkenes are numbered such that the double bond connects C-1 and C-2
Number in the direction that gives the smaller numbers to substituents

Section 7.2
STRUCTURE AND BONDING IN ALKENES
The Structure of Ethylene

The Structure of Propylene
Propene (C 3 H 6 ) includes a C = C bond and a C – C bond
The C = C carbons are both sp 2 hybridized and trigonal planar; the –CH 3 carbon is sp 3 hybridized and tetrahedral

Section 7.3
ISOMERISM IN ALKENES

Alkene Isomers of C 4 H 8
There are four alkene isomers of C 4 H 8

cis - trans Isomerism
Substituents can be on the same side ( cis ) or opposite sides ( trans ) of a double bond
Not possible when one carbon of the double bond bears two identical substituents
Interconversion of cis and trans isomers via C = C rotation is extremely slow at normal temperatures

Section 7. 4
NAMING STEREOISOMERIC ALKENES BY THE E–Z NOTATIONAL SYSTEM

Extending cis - trans Isomerism
For 1,1-disubstituted alkenes, the assignment of cis or trans is straightforward
What about trisubstituted and tetrasubstituted alkenes? How do we know which groups to compare?

The E / Z System
Prioritize the two groups attached to each carbon relative to one another. Find the higher-priority group on each carbon
If the higher priority groups are cis , the geometry is Z ( zusammen , together)
If the higher priority groups are trans , the geometry is E ( entgegen , opposite )

Stereoisomerism and Alkenes
Alkenes containing chirality centers may have stereoisomers involving R / S at the chirality center and E / Z at the alkene

Section 7.5
PHYSICAL PROPERTIES OF ALKENES

Alkenes Resemble Alkanes
The physical properties of alkenes resemble those of alkanes:
Low boiling and melting points, especially for low-molecular-weight compounds
Small dipole moments (if any)
Alkenes substituted with heteroatoms do have significant dipole moments

Alkyl Groups Are Electron Donating
Alkyl groups appear to be electron donating or electron releasing on the basis of dipole moments
Resonance structures illustrate the hyperconjugative donation of electrons from an alkyl group

Section 7.6
RELATIVE STABILITIES OF ALKENES

Trends in Alkene Stability
Three factors influence the stability of alkenes:
Degree of substitution of C=C (an electronic effect)
van der Waals strain in the cis stereoisomer (a steric effect)
Chain branching
Substitution: additional alkyl groups bonded to C=C stabilize the double bond

Trends in Alkene Stability-1
Three factors influence the stability of alkenes:
Degree of substitution of C=C (an electronic effect)
van der Waals strain in the cis stereoisomer (a steric effect)
Chain branching
van der Waals strain/Steric crowding: cis isomers are destabilized by interactions between the close alkyl groups

Trends in Alkene Stability-2
Three factors influence the stability of alkenes:
Degree of substitution of C=C (an electronic effect)
van der Waals strain in the cis stereoisomer (a steric effect)
Chain branching
Chain branching: 1,1-disubstituted alkenes are more stable than cis and trans 1,2-disubstituted alkenes

Section 7.7
CYCLOALKENES

Cyclic Alkenes
Double bonds can be found in rings of all sizes. The smallest cycloalkene is cyclopropene
Cycloalkenes differ in conformation from their saturated analogues
Interconversion of these half-chair conformers occurs readily .

cis - trans Isomerism in Cycloalkenes
Small cycloalkenes universally contain cis double bonds
For cyclooctene and larger, the double bond may have the cis or the trans configuration!
The energy difference diminishes with increasing ring size

Section 7.8
PREPARATION OF ALKENES: ELIMINATION REACTIONS
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education.

Elimination in General
A β -elimination reaction involves the loss of X and Y from adjacent carbon atoms with formation of C=C
One group is electronegative (X) and the other is electron-releasing (Y)
For example, X is a good leaving group and Y is an acidic hydrogen atom
Possible for atoms other than carbon as well!

Dehydrogenation
Industrially, ethylene and propylene are prepared by the metal-assisted elimination of H 2
Enzyme-catalyzed dehydrogenations are also known

More Common Eliminations
Dehydrogenation is impractical for synthesis of most alkenes; instead, a substrate containing a good leaving group (or precursor to one) is used
Dehydrohalogenation involves elimination from H–C–C–X
Dehydration involves elimination from H–C–C–OH

Section 7.9
DEHYDRATION OF ALCOHOLS

Dehydration of Alcohols
The most common acids used are H 2 SO 4 ,H 3 PO 4 , and KHSO 4 .
Note that in both of these examples, only one elimination product is possible.

Section 7.10
REGIOSELECTIVITY IN ALCOHOL DEHYDRATION: THE ZAITSEV RULE

Regioselectivity: Which Hydrogen?
In general, alcohols may contain more than one different type of β -proton
Regioselectivity concerns the preference of the reaction to involve one particular type of β -proton
For example, a proton at C-1 or C-3 could be removed to form two different products:
In this reaction (and others), the more substituted alkene is formed preferentially

Zaitsev’s Rule
Zaitsev’s Rule: In alcohol dehydrations, the most substituted alkene is formed preferentially
Why? Alkenes are stabilized by electron-releasing alkyl substituents
Ultimately, Zaitsev’s rule reflects the idea that the major product in alcohol dehydrations is the most thermodynamically stable product
Does this idea dictate stereoselectivity as well?

Section 7.11
STEREOSELECTIVITY IN ALCOHOL DEHYDRATION

Stereoselectivity: E or Z ?
Alcohol dehydrations can often yield either ( E )- or ( Z )-alkenes
Based on the thermodynamic statement of Zaitsev’s rule, we might expect the ( E )-alkene to be formed preferentially
Experimental results support this hypothesis
In general, the more stable trans or ( E ) isomer is formed preferentially in alcohol dehydrations.

Section 7.12
THE E1 AND E2 MECHANISMS OF ALCOHOL DEHYDRATION

Reactivity Trends in Dehydrations
The reactivity of alcohols in dehydrations increases in the order primary<secondary<tertiary
This is the same reactivity trend observed for nucleophilic substitution of alcohols by hydrogen halides
Both reactions likely share a common intermediate and slow step: a carbocation!
In dehydration, loss of a proton occurs instead of attack by X −

The E1 Mechanism
The mechanism just described involves elimination and a unimolecular rate-determining step; it is called the E1 mechanism
The first two steps are identical to those of the S N 1 mechanism

The E1 Mechanism-1
The mechanism just described involves elimination and a unimolecular rate-determining step; it is called the E1 mechanism
In the third step, water removes a β -proton to form C=C
Note that the base is water , not the conjugate base of the acid (which is necessarily a weaker base than water!)

Primary Alcohols & E2
Primary alcohols do undergo elimination in strong acid, but they cannot form carbocations
Instead, β -deprotonation and loss of H 2 O are concerted and the slow step is bimolecular
This mechanism is called E2 because of the bimolecular slow step. Note that the base here is still water rather than A − .

Section 7.13
REARRANGEMENTS IN ALCOHOL DEHYDRATION

Rearrangements in Dehydrations
Because E1 reactions involve a carbocation intermediate, rearrangements should be expected
Rearrangement occurs when a more stable carbocation can be formed via migration of –H or −CH 3 by one carbon
Common for adjacent tert -butyl groups:

Rearrangements of Primary Substrates
Even primary alcohols can undergo 1,2-hydride shifts to form intermediate secondary or tertiary cations!
The intramolecular hydride shift is more rapid than bimolecular deprotonation by water.

Section 7.14
DEHYDROHALOGENATION OF ALKYL HALIDES

Dehydrohalogenation
Alkyl halides contain a good leaving group built in, and undergo elimination when a β -proton is present and reaction conditions are favorable
As one example, strong base may be used:

Selectivity of Dehydrohalogenation
The regio- and stereoselectivity of dehydrohalogenation matches the selectivity of dehydration
The most substituted alkene is formed preferentially
The more stable ( E )-isomer predominates

Section 7.15
THE E2 MECHANISM OF DEHYDROHALOGENATION OF ALKYL HALIDES
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

E2: Concerted Elimination
Most eliminations of alkyl halides occur by the E2 mechanism (E1 is rarely synthetically useful)
E2 is a single-step mechanism in which deprotonation and leaving-group departure occur simultaneously
The slow step is bimolecular; the rate depends on [ Base − ] and [RX ]
Rate = k [Alkyl halide] [Base]
A strong base (such as alkoxide) is typically used
Reaction Coordinate Diagram

Reactivity Trends in E2
Reactivity increases with leaving-group ability. Faster E2 is associated with:
Weaker C–X bond
More stable X −
Tertiary alkyl halides react most rapidly, followed by secondary and primary halides

Section 7.16
ANTI ELIMINATION IN E2 REACTIONS: STEREOELECTRONIC EFFECTS

E2 Stereochemistry: Interesting Results
An axial Br eliminates readily in the presence of strong base, but an equatorial Br does not

E2 Stereochemistry: Interesting Results-1
This result and others suggest that the deprotonated H and leaving group must be anti in E2 eliminations

Stereoelectronic Effects in E2
Why must –H and –X be anti ? The new π bond in the product arises from side-on overlap of p orbitals, which is best in the anti geometry
The C–H σ orbital overlaps ideally with the C–X σ ∗ orbital in this arrangement .
This effect, grounded in orbital overlap and the spatial arrangements of orbitals, is called a stereoelectronic effect
Backside attack in S N 2 is another example !

Stereoselectivity for ( E ) Products
Transition states with –H and –X anti also help explain the selectivity of E2 for ( E ) or trans products
The minor 2-alkene derives from the transition state in which the alkyl groups are gauche :

Stereoselectivity for ( E ) Products-1
Transition states with –H and –X anti also help explain the selectivity of E2 for ( E ) or trans products
The major 2-alkene derives from the transition state in which the alkyl groups are anti :

Section 7.17
ISOTOPE EFFECTS AND THE E2 MECHANISM

Kinetic Isotope Effects
Reactions involving cleavage of an R–H bond exhibit kinetic isotope effects
A C–D bond is slightly stronger (12 kj / mol ) than a C–H bond, so the activation energy for reaction of C–D is slightly higher than the activation energy for reaction of C–H
We observe this effect in kinetic data when C–H(D) cleavage occurs in the rate-determining step
When k C -H / k C -D ≫ 1 , we conclude that the C–H bond is involved in the slow step
What should we expect in an isotopic study of E2 elimination ?

KIEs in E2 Elimination
In the slow (and only!) step of E2, a C–H bond breaks. Thus, k C-H / k C-D should be greater than 1
In fact , k C-H / k C-D = 6.7 for the reaction below
This result supports the concerted mechanism of E2 (along with previously discussed kinetic data )
Kinetic isotope effect studies are useful for determining whether C–H cleavage occurs in the slow step of a reaction mechanism .

Section 7.18
THE E1 MECHANISM OF DEHYDROHALOGENATION OF ALKYL HALIDES

E1 Elimination
Deprotonation and leaving-group departure may not be concerted—particularly when the base is weak
In the presence of a weak base, an alkyl halide may lose the halide to form a carbocation before deprotonation

E1 Elimination-1
In the presence of a weak base, an alkyl halide may lose the halide to form a carbocation before deprotonation
In the second step, solvent deprotonates the carbocation at a β -carbon , forming the product alkene

Reactivity Trends and Selectivity
Zaitsev’s rule is followed. The most substituted alkene is formed preferentially
Tertiary alkyl halides react fastest. They form the most stable carbocations

An Important Analogy
Note that E1 dehydrohalogenation and acid-catalyzed dehydration of alcohols are very similar!
Both reactions share a common intermediate and differ only in the structure of the leaving group

Section 7.19
SUBSTITUTION AND ELIMINATION AS COMPETING REACTIONS

Competing Reactions
Substitution and elimination involve the same substrates and reaction conditions
Using previously discussed reactivity trends, we can predict whether substitution or elimination will occur given alkyl halide and Lewis base structures

General Principles
Alkyl halides generally undergo elimination in the presence of Lewis bases. For example,
Only under certain circumstances will substitution predominate:
Minimal steric hindrance in the alkyl halide and base
Weak Brønsted basicity (but substantial Lewis basicity) in the nucleophile
Brønsted or Lewis Base?

Minimal Steric Hindrance
For unhindered primary alkyl halides, S N 2 substitution predominates no matter the strength of the base
However, a sterically hindered base can tip the scales in favor of elimination !
Both the electrophile and the nucleophile must be sterically un hindered to promote S N 2 substitution .

Low Brønsted Basicity
Some nucleophiles are weak Brønsted bases despite being good Lewis bases; these nucleophiles engage in substitution selectively
Anionic nucleophiles ( A − ) whose conjugate acids (HA) have p K a <15 are the classic examples:
Thiolates ( HS − , RS − )
Cyanide ( CN − )
Azide ( N 3 − )
Neutral solvent nucleophiles such as water and alcohols engage in substitution exclusively with tertiary halides

Section 7.20
ELIMINATION REACTIONS OF SULFONATES

Eliminations of Alkyl Sulfonates
Sulfonates are pseudohalides and undergo many of the same reactions as halides
Elimination of alkyl sulfonates follows the same reactivity trends and selectivity rules as dehydrohalogenation:
The most substituted product is favored
The trans product is favored over the cis product

Addition Reactions of Alkenes

Section 8.1
HYDROGENATION OF ALKENES

Additions to Alkenes
Additions are a class of organic reactions involving formation of new bonds to an organic substrate
A and B in the reagent “add across” the ends of the π system . Often (but not always) the A–B bond is highly polarized
Note that addition is the opposite of elimination!
Addition is synthetically valuable because:
Readily available alkenes are the reactants
Two new s bonds are formed to the carbons of the alkene

Hydrogenation
The addition of H 2 across an alkene is called hydrogenation
A very useful method for preparing saturated hydrocarbons from alkenes
Hydrogenation is universally exothermic
A metal catalyst is required to activate H 2 ; typically the reaction conditions are heterogeneous

Section 8.2
STEREOCHEMISTRY OF ALKENE HYDROGENATION

Mechanism of Hydrogenation
The metal catalyst splits H 2 so that hydrogens are sticking up above the metal surface
The alkene approaches the metal surface and bonds to it
Both hydrogens add to the same face of the alkene in a syn addition

Support for syn Addition
Hydrogenation of a cyclohexene leads only to the cis product (via syn addition); the trans product (via anti addition) is not formed

Section 8.3
HEATS OF HYDROGENATION

Thermodynamic Stability of Alkenes-slide 2
Heat of hydrogenation is the heat released ( ∆ H degrees) when an alkene is hydrogenated
More stable alkenes have lower heats of hydrogenation

Thermodynamic Stability of Alkenes
Heats of hydrogenation suggest the same stability trends as heats of combustion
Substitution stabilizes alkenes
trans ( E ) isomers are more stable than cis ( Z ) isomers
Thermodynamic Stability of Alkenes-slide 3

Section 8.4
ELECTROPHILIC ADDITION OF HYDROGEN HALIDES TO ALKENES

Alkenes are Nucleophilic
The π electrons in a double bond reside in a region of high electron density
Alkenes are nucleophilic in this region—the π bond can act as a Lewis base!

Alkenes are Nucleophilic-1
The π electrons in a double bond reside in a region of high electron density
Alkenes are nucleophilic in this region—the π bond can act as a Lewis base!
Treatment with an electrophile ( Brønsted or Lewis acid) results in chemical reaction
The electron-rich region of the alkene approaches the electron-poor region of the electrophile .

Alkenes are Nucleophilic-2
The electron-rich region of the alkene approaches the electron-poor region of the electrophile .
The resulting chemical reaction is called electrophilic addition .

Electrophilic Addition of HX
Hydrogen adds to one carbon of the alkene and halogen to the other to form an alkyl halide
Reactivity trends: stronger acids and more substituted alkenes react more rapidly

Regioselectivity: Markovnikov’s Rule
Alkenes with two different sets of substituents could give two different products depending on where H and X add
Markovnikov’s rule: hydrogen ends up bound to the carbon with more hydrogens in the alkene

Mechanism of HX Addition
Why does Markovnikov’s rule work? Let’s consider the mechanism of addition of HX
Step 1 (RDS): HX protonates the alkene, forming a carbocation
Step 2: the conjugate base X − attacks the carbocation

Mechanism of HX Addition-1
Why does Markovnikov’s rule work? Let’s consider the mechanism of addition of HX
Step 1 (RDS): HX protonates the alkene, forming a carbocation
The rate of substitution is dictated by carbocation stability. Remember Hammond’s postulate!
Mechanisms involving more stable carbocations are more rapid

Carbocation Stability and Reaction Rate
HBr could protonate the central carbon :
Or HBr could protonate the end carbon :
Unstable cation ;
Slow rate of reaction
Stable cation ; relatively rapid rate

Section 8.5
CARBOCATION REARRANGEMENTS IN HYDROGEN HALIDE ADDITIONS TO ALKENES

Rearrangements in Addition of HX
Because addition of HX involves a carbocation, rearrangements are possible
For example, addition of HCl to 3-methyl-1-butene gives a mixture of products
The minor product is derived from rearrangement

Section 8.6
ACID-CATALYZED HYDRATION OF ALKENES
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Acid- catalyzed Hydration
Strong acid quantitatively forms H 3 O + in water, which we can envision as containing a highly polarized H–O bond similar to H–X
In the presence of catalytic acid, H 2 O can add across the carbons of an alkene just like HX
The product is an alcohol and the mechanism is analogous to electrophilic addition of HX

Reactivity and Selectivity
Regioselectivity: the reaction obeys Markovnikov’s rule
Stereoselectivity: chiral alcohols are produced as racemic mixtures
Reactivity trends: more substituted alkenes react more rapidly
Note that similar trends are observed in additions of HX !

Mechanism of Acid-catalyzed Hydration
Step 1: protonation of the alkene by H 3 O +
Step 2: nucleophilic attack of water on the carbocation

Mechanism of Acid-catalyzed Hydration-1
Step 3: deprotonation of the protonated alcohol intermediate by water

Section 8.7
THERMODYNAMICS OF ADDITION–ELIMINATION EQUILIBRIA

Hydration is Reversible
Both dehydration (elimination) and hydration (addition) can occur, even though they are microscopic reverses of one another!
Hydration is reversible —hydration and dehydration represent a finely balanced equilibrium
For example, K = 9 for the reaction below !

Outsmarting Equilibrium
We adjust reaction conditions to drive the reaction one way or the other, taking advantage of Le Châtelier’s principle
Adding water causes the reaction to advance to the right; an excess of water is used in hydration reactions
Removing water causes the reaction to proceed to the left; water is removed in dehydration reactions

Section 8.8
HYDROBORATION–OXIDATION OF ALKENES

Anti-Markovnikov Hydration
We sometimes want to add –H and –OH in an anti-Markovnikov fashion
In acid-catalyzed hydrations, H is electrophilic. We can achieve anti-Markovnikov hydration using a nucleophilic source of H, HBR 2
Following this hydroboration step, oxidation converts the – BR 2 group to –OH

Anti-Markovnikov Hydration-1
We sometimes want to add –H and –OH in an anti-Markovnikov fashion
Following this hydroboration step, oxidation converts the – BR 2 group to – OH

Hydroboration-Oxidation
The alkene is treated with HBR 2 to form an alkylborane :
Then the alkyl borane is oxidized with HO − and H 2 O 2 :
Boranes used include B 2 H 6 and adducts of BH 3 with Lewis bases, such as BH 3 ⋅ THF and BH 3 ⋅ OEt 2

Examples and Selectivity
Hydroboration-oxidation is two synthetic steps, but the two are typically written together
Hydroboration is syn and oxidation occurs rigorously with retention of configuration , so overall –H and –OH add in a syn fashion

Section 8.9
MECHANISM OF HYDROBORATION–OXIDATION

Mechanism of Hydroboration
HBR 2 is a nucleophilic source of H. That is, the H–B bond is polarized towards hydrogen!
Step 1: the Lewis basic π bond forms a complex with the Lewis acidic boron atom

Mechanism of Hydroboration-1
HBR 2 is a nucleophilic source of H. That is, the H–B bond is polarized towards hydrogen!
Step 2: H and B add across the π bond in a concerted, anti-Markovnikov fashion
Hydrogen forms a bond to the more substituted carbon

Mechanism of Oxidation
The boron atom in the alkylborane product remains Lewis acidic. Peroxide anion coordinates to boron
Alkyl group migration forms a C–O bond and expels hydroxide
Most importantly, the configuration at the alcohol carbon is retained throughout oxidation .

Mechanism of Oxidation-1
Attack of hydroxide on boron in an “ S N 2 -like ” step expels the conjugate base of the product
Protonation in reaction workup generates the product
Most importantly, the configuration at the alcohol carbon is retained throughout oxidation.

Section 8.10
ADDITION OF HALOGENS TO ALKENES

Halogenation of Alkenes
Elemental halogens (X 2 ) can be envisioned as electrophiles since –X is a good leaving group
In electrophilic halogenation , two X atoms add across an alkene
Only additions of Cl 2 and Br 2 are synthetically useful

Stereospecificity: Interesting Results
Additions of Cl 2 and Br 2 across alkenes are stereospecifically anti
One way to explain this result invokes a cyclic halonium ion (which has also been directly observed )
S N 2 opening of the halonium ion accounts for the observed anti stereospecificity .

Halonium Ions
Formation of the halonium ion is the rate-determining step—more substituted alkenes react more rapidly
In the TS for halonium formation, the alkene has partial positive charge and the (developing) halide partial negative charge
The cyclic halonium is more stable than an isomeric carbocation

Cohalogenation & External Nucleophiles
In nucleophilic solvents, the halonium ion is opened by solvent rather than halide
Note the regioselectivity: the nucleophile bonds to the more substituted carbon (Markovnikov)
Addition is stereospecifically anti

Section 8.11
EPOXIDATION OF ALKENES
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Epoxides
Epoxides are three-membered cyclic ethers
Can be named as n , n +1-epoxy derivatives of alkanes
Can also be named as alkene oxides

Epoxidation
The addition of a single oxygen atom across an alkene is called epoxidation
A peracid or peroxy acid is used to effect epoxidation
Note the byproduct: the corresponding carboxylic acid

Reactivity Trends and Selectivity
More substituted alkenes react more rapidly, suggesting that the alkene acts as a Lewis base
The peracid (naturally) acts as a Lewis acid
Addition of oxygen is syn ; groups cis in the alkene remain cis in the epoxide

Mechanism of Peracid Epoxidation
The syn addition and reactivity trend point to a concerted delivery of oxygen to both carbons of the alkene
Intramolecular proton transfer gives the carboxylic acid immediately

Section 8.12
OZONOLYSIS OF ALKENES

Ozonolysis: Splitting Alkenes to Carbonyls
Ozone contains both electrophilic and nucleophilic oxygens !
Addition to an alkene to form an ozonide is favorable
Each carbon of the cyclic ozonide contains two new bonds to oxygen. This is an oxidation reaction .
A subsequent reductive step with (CH 3 ) 2 S opens the ring and forms two carbonyl groups

Utility of Ozonolysis
Synthesizing carbonyl compounds from all-carbon precursors can be difficult—ozonolysis gives us a way
Ozonolysis essentially “splits” an alkene into two carbonyl compounds
Analysis of ozonolysis products (e.g., tests for ketones) can confirm the presence of an alkene in the starting material

Section 8.13
ENANTIOSELECTIVE ADDITION TO ALKENES

Enantioselective Addition Reactions
Some addition reactions (epoxidation, halogenation, etc.) are stereospecific but give racemic mixtures when chiral products are formed from achiral precursors
Selective formation of a single enantiomer requires a chiral catalyst such as an enzyme

Section 8.14
RETROSYNTHETIC ANALYSIS AND ALKENE INTERMEDIATES

Functional-group Interchange via Alkenes
Alkenes can be useful “synthetic waypoints” for functional group transformations
No reaction so far converts alcohols to epoxides directly …

Functional-group Interchange via Alkenes-1
Alkenes can be useful “synthetic waypoints” for functional group transformations
But dehydration of the alcohol followed by epoxidation gets us to the target via cyclohexene !
Retrosynthesis

Functional-group Interchange via Alkenes-2
Alkenes can be useful “synthetic waypoints” for functional group transformations
But dehydration of the alcohol followed by epoxidation gets us to the target via cyclohexene!
Forward Direction

Alkynes

Section 9.1
SOURCES OF ALKYNES

Alkynes
Alkynes contain carbon-carbon triple bonds
Alkynes with the triple bond at the end of the chain are called terminal ; they include a C sp –H group
The unique acidity of the H in terminal alkynes is very important in their reactions!
Alkynes with alkyl groups on either side of the triple bond are called internal

Sources of Alkynes
Acetylene (C 2 H 2 , ethyne) can be produced in large quantities from calcium carbide
Acetylene can also be produced via de hydrogenation of ethene

Natural Alkynes
Many fatty acids include C–C triple bonds
Natural products may also contain alkynes
Alkynes have even been found on other planets !

Section 9.2
NOMENCLATURE

Naming Alkynes
Include the triple bond in the parent chain; replace – ane in the parent alkane name with – yne
Number the parent chain such that the first multiple bond has the lowest number possible
In the event of a tie between a double and a triple bond, the double bond “wins”
The –CCH group is called an ethynyl substituent

Section 9.3
PHYSICAL PROPERTIES OF ALKYNES

Physical Properties of Alkynes
Physical properties of alkynes are similar to those of alkanes and alkenes of comparable molecular weight
Low boiling points – similar to alkanes
Low density
Low solubility in water (lacking many other polar functional groups )

Section 9.4
STRUCTURE AND BONDING IN ALKYNES: SP HYBRIDIZATION

Geometry and Bond Lengths
The C ≡ C bond is shorter and stronger than comparable C = C bonds
C ≡ C–H and C ≡ C–R bonds are also shorter
Alkynes are linear and the triply bound carbons have sp hybridization

Nucleophilicity of Alkynes
Like alkenes, alkynes have significant electron density between their carbons
Like alkenes, alkynes are nucleophilic and undergo electrophilic addition

Comparing Alkynes to Other Hydrocarbons
Note the remarkable acidity of acetylene!

Section 9.5
ACIDITY OF ACETYLENE AND TERMINAL ALKYNES

Alkynes are Relatively Acidic
Relative to alkanes and alkenes, terminal alkynes are remarkably acidic
Deprotonation of RC ≡ C–H leaves a lone pair in a hybrid orbital with high s character

Carbanion Stability
The greater % s character helps to stabilize the anion charge by allowing the negative charge to be held closer to the positively charged nucleus.
The high s character means that the lone pair is close to the nucleus and thus stabilized

Bases to Deprotonate "RC≡CH"
Alkynes are acidic relative to alkanes and alkenes, but still require fairly strong bases to be significantly deprotonated
Water is not basic enough …
…and neither is hydroxide!

Bases to Deprotonate RC ≡ CH-1
Alkynes are acidic relative to alkanes and alkenes, but still require fairly strong bases to be significantly deprotonated
Amide ion ( NH 2 − ) is usually the base of choice
Carbanions of alkanes and alkenes also work well

Section 9.6
PREPARATION OF ALKYNES BY ALKYLATION OF ACETYLENE AND TERMINAL ALKYNES
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Alkylation of Terminal Alkynes
An alkylation reaction replaces an –H or other functional group with an alkyl group
Terminal alkynes can be alkylated via a nucleophilic acetylide anion, the conjugate base of the alkyne
The strongly nucleophilic acetylide reacts with an alkyl halide via S N 2!

Alkylation of Terminal Alkynes-1
Acetylene can be subjected to two distinct alkylations or a terminal alkyne can be alkylated once
Warning: the scope of the electrophile in S N 2 still applies! It must be a methyl or primary alkyl (psuedo)halide!
The Brønsted basic acetylide anion promotes elimination in secondary and tertiary alkyl halides

Section 9.7
Preparation of alkynes by elimination reactions

Forming Alkynes via Elimination
Alkylation is a carbon-carbon bond forming reaction , but we can also form alkynes using functional group transformations
Dehydrogenation is one example
Elimination of a dihalide (two eliminations to make two π bonds) is another
Two different types of halides may be used: geminal or vicinal
These methods are typically applied only to make terminal alkynes; double eliminations of internal dihalides have regioselectivity issues.

Double Elimination of Geminal Dihalides
In a geminal dihalide, the halogen substituents have a 1,1-relationship . Both halogens are bound to the terminal carbon to prevent regioselectivity issues
Three equivalents of base are necessary because of rapid and irreversible deprotonation of the terminal alkyne product
Water returns a proton to the acetylide in workup

Double Elimination of Vicinal Dihalides
In a vicinal dihalide, the halogen substituents have a 1,2-relationship. One halogen is bound to the end carbon; the other is bound to the adjacent carbon
Three equivalents of base are again necessary
Nice route from alkenes to alkynes if dehydrogenation is not possible (or too expensive …)

Section 9.8
REACTIONS OF ALKYNES

Reactions of Alkynes
Reactions of alkynes fall into two broad categories…
Reactions of terminal alkynes via acetylide anions
Alkylation
Other S N 2 -like processes
Elimination
Nucleophilic addition to polarized π bonds (later)
Additions to one or both of the π bonds
Hydrogenation
Electrophilic addition of HX
Electrophilic halogenation
Ozonolysis
Nucleophilic addition (later )

Section 9.9
HYDROGENATION OF ALKYNES

Hydrogenation of Alkynes
Hydrogenation of an alkyne in the presence of Pt or another metal proceeds all the way to the alkane
Hydrogens add in a syn manner, just like reactions of alkenes (the second hydrogenation really just is hydrogenation of an alkene!)
Can we stop hydrogenation at the alkene stage?

Lindlar Hydrogenation
Using a “poisoned” or slowed catalyst mixture called the Lindlar catalyst , selective hydrogenation to the cis alkene is possible
Lindlar hydrogenation is very attractive because it avoids the regioselectivity and stereoselectivity issues of elimination-based methods (e.g., dehydration ).

Section 9.10
ADDITION OF HYDROGEN HALIDES TO ALKYNES

Hydrohalogenation of Alkynes
HX adds to alkynes to form alkenyl halides
Addition follows Markovnikov’s rule
Kinetic studies point to one-step termolecular process
avoids the formation of a very unstable alkenyl cation intermediate

Double Dehydrohalogenation
The reaction can be stopped at the alkenyl halide stage, or a second equivalent of HX can be used to give a geminal dihalide
Can this dihalide be re-eliminated to form the starting alkyne? No !

Section 9.11
HYDRATION OF ALKYNES
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Hydration of Alkynes
By analogy to alkenes, alkynes can also undergo hydration , addition of –H and –OH across the π bond
The product is a special kind of alkene containing an attached –OH group, an enol
As in hydration of alkenes, an acid catalyst is essential .

Hydration of Alkynes-1
By analogy to alkenes, alkynes can also undergo hydration , addition of –H and –OH across the π bond
The product is a special kind of alkene containing an attached –OH group, an enol
keto form
The enol undergoes a spontaneous isomerization reaction called tautomerization

Mechanism of Tautomerization
Tautomerization amounts to migration of an –H from O to C (or vice versa ). However , tautomerization is not intramolecular!
Acid donates a proton to C first …
Then conjugate base removes a proton from O
Tautomerization can also be catalyzed by base, in which case the steps are reversed.

Examples of Hydration
Mercury(II) is commonly used as a Lewis acid catalyst along with acid
The reaction follows Markovnikov’s rule
Be wary of hydration of asymmetric internal alkynes— regioselectivity issues!

Section 9.12
ADDITION OF HALOGENS TO ALKYNES

Halogenations of Alkynes
In the presence of 1 equivalent of X 2 , the dihaloalkene can be isolated. The halogen substituents are trans , implying anti addition (halenium ion ?!)
Use of 2 equivalents leads to a tetrahaloalkane
Only bromination and chlorination are synthetically useful

Section 9.13
OZONOLYSIS OF ALKYNES

Ozonolysis of Alkynes-1
In the presence of ozone, alkynes “split” like alkenes to form carboxylic acids
Can be a useful reaction for characterizing alkynes

Section 9.14
ALKYNES IN SYNTHESIS AND RETROSYNTHESIS

Acetylene: A Useful Two-C Fragment
C–C bonds can be formed on either end of acetylene via alkylation. It can be used to introduce a two-carbon fragment into a chain
Subsequent partial hydrogenation gives us access to all the reactions of alkenes !

Example: 1,2-epoxybutane
Design a synthesis of 1,2-epoxybutane starting from ethyl bromide and acetylene as the only carbon sources
Epoxidation needs to be involved …
…and the alkene can be made from 1-butyne !

Example: 1,2-epoxybutane-1
Design a synthesis of 1,2-epoxybutane starting from ethyl bromide and acetylene as the only carbon sources
In the forward direction ,

Introduction to Free Radicals

Section 10.1
STRUCTURE, BONDING, AND STABILITY OF ALKYL RADICALS

Free Radicals
In most stable organic species, all electrons are paired
However, some molecules must contain an unpaired electron . They are called free radicals
Some radicals are stable, but most organic radicals are not—radicals are usually intermediates in organic reactions

Alkyl Radicals
An alkyl radical contains a carbon with three single bonds and an unpaired electron
The unpaired electron resides in a 2 p atomic orbital and the carbon is sp 2 hybridized
Alkyl radicals are either planar or very nearly planar

Stability Trends of Alkyl Radicals
Like carbocations, more substituted alkyl radicals are more stable

Bond Dissociation Enthalpies
We can distinguish between heterolytic and homolytic cleavage of covalent bonds
Homolytic bond dissociation energies give insight into radical stability
The more stable the radical, the lower the bond dissociation energy (ignoring differences in starting material stability)

Bond Dissociation Energies
Homolytic bond dissociation energies give insight into radical stability

Bond Dissociation Energies-1
Homolytic bond dissociation energies give insight into radical stability
+423 kJ
+413 kJ
Bond Dissociation Energies-2
Bond Dissociation Energies-3

Section 10.2
HALOGENATION OF ALKANES

Halogenation of Alkanes
In the presence of X 2 , alkane C–H’s undergo halogenation
Chlorination and bromination are most commonly used; the reactivity of the halogens decreases in the order F 2 > Cl 2 > Br 2 > I 2
Cl 2 is reactive enough to cause polychlorination; mixtures of polychloroalkanes are common
Br 2 gives monobromination products selectively
The reaction is insensitive to solvent polarity, suggesting a radical mechanism involving neutral intermediates

Section 10.3
MECHANISM OF METHANE CHLORINATION

Radical Chain Mechanisms
Most radical mechanisms involve chain reactions : a single radical species generates new radicals in a cyclic manner
Radical chain mechanisms have three stages:
Initiation: an even-electron species splits into radicals (odd-electron species) spontaneously
Propagation: a radical reacts with an even-electron species to generate even-electron products and new radicals
Termination: radicals combine to form undesired even-electron products and terminate propagation

Initiation of Alkane Halogenation
A halogen molecule X 2 splits into two X · radicals
Substantially endothermic, but enabled by heat or light
X · radical participates in the first step of propagation and is called the propagating radical

Propagation of Alkane Halogenation
Step 1: the X · radical abstracts hydrogen from the alkane, forming HX and an alkyl radical
Step 2: the alkyl radical abstracts a halogen atom from X 2 , forming R–X and regenerating the X · radical !
The newly formed X · radical performs step 1 again on a new molecule of alkane, carrying on the chain

Termination of Alkane Halogenation
The initiation and propagation steps seem to imply that a single initiation event can lead to complete conversion of R–H to R–X!
This is not true in practice: radical intermediates can combine and terminate the radical chain
The full set of termination steps includes all possible combinations of radical intermediates from propagation

Section 10.4
HALOGENATION OF HIGHER ALKANES

Selectivity of Halogenations
Chlorine is quite reactive and tends to give mixtures of products with substitution at different C–H groups
The yield of 2-chlorobutane is slightly “better than statistical” due to greater stability of secondary radical (and the TS leading to it!)

Selectivity of Halogenations-1
A study of 2-methylpropane showed that tertiary and primary H’s are abstracted at similar rates as well
In the end, chlorination is just not very selective!

Selectivity of Halogenations-2
On the other hand, bromination is highly selective for tertiary C–H
We can thus brominate a tertiary carbon selectively

Section 10.5
FREE-RADICAL ADDITION OF HYDROGEN BROMIDE TO ALKENES AND ALKYNES
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

A Strange Result: HBr Addition to Alkenes
Although HCl and HI rigorously follow Markovnikov’s rule in reactions with alkenes, HBr sometimes does not!
In the presence of peroxides (ROOR), the anti-Markovnikov product dominates!
A change in mechanism is the most likely explanation for the change in regioselectivity

Radical Addition of HBr to Alkenes
Peroxides ( RO–OR ) contain a weak O–O bond. Homolytic cleavage of this bond generates radicals, which can act as radical initiators!
A peroxy radical then abstracts H from H–Br, generating the propagating radical Br ·

The Propagation Stage
Br · can add to an alkene to generate a β - bromoalkyl radical
Addition occurs selectively to generate the more substituted radical intermediate. This leaves Br bound to the less substituted position—anti-Markovnikov!
The alkyl radical then abstracts H from H–Br to generate product and a new Br · radical

Photochemical Activation
Ultraviolet light can also be used to initiate radical addition of HBr, with or without peroxides

Section 10.6
METAL-AMMONIA REDUCTION OF ALKYNES

Radical Hydrogenation of Alkynes
Sodium metal and liquid ammonia can be used to partially hydrogenate alkynes to trans -alkenes selectively
Step 1: sodium atoms “dissociate” into Na + and solvated electrons : electrons surrounded by ammonia ( am ). A solvated electron is transferred to the alkyne

Radical Hydrogenation of Alkynes-1
Sodium metal and liquid ammonia can be used to partially hydrogenate alkynes to trans -alkenes selectively
Step 2: the intermediate radical anion, a very strong base, deprotonates NH 3

Radical Hydrogenation of Alkynes-2
Sodium metal and liquid ammonia can be used to partially hydrogenate alkynes to trans -alkenes selectively
The resulting alkenyl radical may be cis or trans ; the radical equilibrates rapidly to form mostly the more stable trans isomer at equilibrium

Radical Hydrogenation of Alkynes-3
Sodium metal and liquid ammonia can be used to partially hydrogenate alkynes to trans -alkenes selectively
Step 3: a second single-electron transfer event occurs, yielding an alkenyl anion. Equilibration is now slow, so the anion remains mostly trans

Radical Hydrogenation of Alkynes-4
Sodium metal and liquid ammonia can be used to partially hydrogenate alkynes to trans -alkenes selectively
Step 4: a second proton transfer occurs, yielding the product alkene and a second equivalent of NH 2 −

Section 10.7
FREE RADICALS AND RETROSYNTHESIS OF ALKYL HALIDES

Radical Reactions in Retrosynthesis
Radical halogenation is a very powerful method for converting alkanes into “functionalizable” compounds
Be wary of the regioselectivity of radical halogenation! The following synthesis cannot be done in one step

Radical Reactions in Retrosynthesis-1
Anti-Markovnikov hydrobromination (with ROOR) looks like a good place to start

Radical Reactions in Retrosynthesis-2
The alkene can be generated via elimination of a tertiary alkyl bromide

Radical Reactions in Retrosynthesis-3
Making the tertiary bromide from the starting material via radical halogenation is no problem

Radical Reactions in Retrosynthesis-4
In the forward direction ,
It was critical to consider the regioselectivities of alkane halogenation and HBr addition to the alkene .

Section 11.1
THE ALLYL GROUP

Conjugation
Double bonds affect the properties of attached groups that contain unhybridized p orbitals or π bonds
Three or more adjacent p orbitals constitute a conjugated π system
The simplest conjugated system is the allylic system .

The Allyl Group
The −CH 2 CH = CH 2 unit is known as an allyl group
Allylic cations, anions, and radicals have an additional resonance structure
Resonance stabilizes allylic ions and radicals .

Valence Bond Description
All three carbons in allylic ions and radicals are sp 2 hybridized
The p orbitals of the C = C π bond interact with the p orbital on the charged or radical carbon
Overlap of three adjacent p orbitals gives a three-atom π system and delocalized bonding

Molecular Orbital Description
Allylic ions and radicals have the same orbitals, but occupancies differ .

Section 11.2
S N 1 AND S N 1 REACTIONS OF ALLYLIC HALIDES

Relative Rates of S N 1
Tertiary allylic halides react 100 times faster than tert -butyl halide!
Effects of substitution still apply (e.g., 1,1-dimethylallyl cation is more stable than allyl cation )

Products of S N 1
The allylic cation intermediate has two electrophilic positions—two products may be formed!
Substitution occurs preferentially at the carbon that better stabilizes positive charge

Products of S N 1-1
The allylic cation intermediate has two electrophilic positions—two products may be formed!
Substitution occurs preferentially at the carbon that better stabilizes positive charge
The product distribution is identical—a free allylic cation intermediate is involved!

Products of S N 2
S N 2 is also more rapid for allylic halides, although effects of substitution pattern still apply
The LUMO of allyl chloride is delocalized relative to alkyl chlorides (and more reactive as a result)

Section 11.3
ALLYLIC FREE-RADICAL HALOGENATION

Allyl Radical
Homolytic BDEs show that allyl radical is stabilized considerably relative to propyl radical
Spin density is shared by the terminal carbons (consider the SOMO)
Multiple products are possible in allylic halogenation.

Allylic Halogenation
Allyl radical is stable, so free-radical halogenation should be possible, but…
We’ve seen that X 2 engages in addition to C = C !
Allylic halogenation is favored at high temperatures
Low concentrations of X 2 also favor substitution

Regioselectivity Issues
Spin densities at the inequivalent ends of an asymmetric allyl radical are similar, so mixtures of products will result
For ideal results, both ends of the allylic system should be equivalent—separating the isomers is difficult

Section 11. 4
ALLYLIC ANIONS

Allylic Anions
Just as positive charge in allylic cations and spin density in allylic radicals are delocalized, negative charge in allylic anions is delocalized
Electrostatic potential maps show delocalization of charge
Allylic C–H’s are much stronger acids than alkyl C–H’s
Approximate p K a Values

Section 11.5
CLASSES OF DIENES: CONJUGATED AND OTHERWISE
Classes of Dienes

Section 11.6
RELATIVE STABILITIES OF DIENES

Heats of Hydrogenation
Dienes are destabilized relative to alkenes, but conjugated dienes are more stable than isolated dienes .

Allenes and Relative Stabilities
Allenes have heats of hydrogenation more than twice as large as related alkenes—they are relatively unstable
The order of diene stability is thus:
Conjugated dienes (most stable)
Isolated dienes
Allenes (least stable )

Section 11.7
BONDING IN CONJUGATED DIENES
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Bonding in Conjugated Dienes
The single bond connecting the two inner carbons is relatively short (derived from sp 2 - sp 2 overlap)
The stability of conjugated π systems comes from delocalization of π electrons over four atoms

Conformations about the Single Bond
The central single bond can rotate; two conformations permit ideal overlap of the inner p orbitals
The letter s in s-cis and s-trans refers to conformations around the single bond
Steric crowding is minimized in the s - trans conformation
These conformations are very important in pericyclic reactions of dienes, which we will encounter shortly!

Section 11.8
BONDING IN ALLENES
Geometry of Allenes

Allenes May Be Chiral
When two different groups are bound to both ends of an allene, the structure is chiral!
The simplest example is (CH 3 )HC = C = CH(CH 3 )
Enantiomeric allenes resemble oppositely handed screws, which are also chiral .

Section 11.9
PREPARATION OF DIENES

Preparation of Dienes
Industrially, butadiene is prepared via thermal dehydrogenation
In the laboratory, elimination is used (naturally !)
Why are these reactions selective for the conjugated diene when isolated diene products are possible ?

Section 11.10
ADDITION OF HYDROGEN HALIDES TO CONJUGATED DIENES

Hydrohalogenation of Dienes
Like alkenes, dienes undergo electrophilic addition with the hydrogen halides HCl and HBr
These electrophilic additions are mechanistically complex!
Step 1: protonation of C = C to give an allylic cation
This cation has two electrophilic carbons !

Hydrohalogenation of Dienes-2
Like alkenes, dienes undergo electrophilic addition with the hydrogen halides HCl and HBr
These electrophilic additions are mechanistically complex!
Step 1: protonation of C = C to give an allylic cation
This cation has two electrophilic carbons !

Hydrohalogenation of Dienes-3
Like alkenes, dienes undergo electrophilic addition with the hydrogen halides HCl and HBr
These electrophilic additions are mechanistically complex!
Step 2: halide can attack at either electrophilic carbon to give 1,2-addition or 1,4-addition products

1,2- versus 1,4-Addition
At low temperatures, 1,2-addition is the dominant pathway. 1,2-Addition is faster
At high temperatures, 1,4-addition predominates. The 1,4 product is more thermodynamically stable
1,2- versus 1,4-Addition-1

Section 11.11
HALOGEN ADDITIONS TO DIENES

Halogenation of Dienes
Mixtures of 1,2- and 1,4 addition of X 2 are observed when conjugated dienes react with chlorine or bromine
The 1,4-addition product is dominant in most cases, and the reaction is highly selective for ( E )- alkenes

Section 11.12
THE DIELS-ALDER REACTION
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

The Diels-Alder Reaction
The conjugate or 1,4-addition of alkenes to dienes is called the Diels-Alder reaction ([4+2] cycloaddition)
The alkene that reacts with the diene is called the dienophile ; the reaction is called cycloaddition because a ring forms
The reaction is a concerted single-step process! The Diels-Alder reaction is one example of a pericyclic reaction

Substituent Effects
The “parent” Diels-Alder reaction has high activation energy and is thus generally slow
To increase the speed of the reaction, the trick is to make one species electrophilic (usually the dienophile) and one species nucleophilic (usually the diene)
Other examples of activated dienophiles:

Conformational Effects
Because both new C–C bonds form simultaneously, the diene must adopt the s-cis conformation during the reaction
Dienes with severe steric hindrance in the s-cis conformation react slowly in Diels-Alder reactions ( since [ s- cis ] is small relative to [ s-trans ])
1000 x slower in [4+2] cycloadditions
Some cyclic and highly strained alkenes cannot access s - cis at all !

Stereospecificity: Dienophile E / Z
The configuration of the dienophile’s alkene is retained in the product
If the dienophile is ( E ), the product is trans
If the dienophile is ( Z ), the product is cis
Both bonds to the diene are formed simultaneously, so the dienophile C = C has no time to rotate

Stereospecificity: Diene E / Z
The configurations of the diene’s alkenes are retained in the product
If in the s - cis conformation both groups are “out” or both groups are “in,” they will be cis in the product
If in the s - cis conformation one group is “out” and one is “in,” they will be trans in the product
The two bonds to the CH 2 group are considered “in”; the implicit H’s are “out .”

Stereoselectivity: endo versus exo
We’ve seen specificity in the diene and dienophile configurations separately, but what about relative orientation of the diene and dienophile substituents?
Two orientations are possible
endo: diene groups cis to dienophile “out” groups
exo: diene groups trans to “out” groups
Across many [4+2] reactions, the endo product is favored.
The endo Rule: A Kinetic Effect

Section 11.13
INTRAMOLECULAR DIELS-ALDER REACTIONS

Intramolecular Diels-Alder Reactions
Intramolecular Diels-Alder reactions can be used to construct complex cyclic frameworks containing at least one cyclohexene
The newly formed rings must be stable (usually five- or six-membered), but unactivated dienes and dienophiles can be used

Section 11.14
RETROSYNTHETIC ANALYSIS AND THE DIELS-ALDER REACTION

A Powerful Tool for Synthesis
The Diels-Alder reaction is the way to make six-membered rings from acyclic precursors. Look for cyclohexenes and even cyclohexanes in the target
Make sure the proposed starting materials are viable (activated)
Combine with elimination, halogenation, and other reactions to simplify to very basic starting materials

A Powerful Tool for Synthesis-1
The Diels-Alder reaction is the way to make six-membered rings from acyclic precursors. Look for cyclohexenes and even cyclohexanes in the target
Make sure the proposed starting materials are viable (activated)
Combine with elimination, halogenation, and other reactions to simplify to very basic starting materials

A Powerful Tool for Synthesis-2
The Diels-Alder reaction is the way to make six-membered rings from acyclic precursors. Look for cyclohexenes and even cyclohexanes in the target
Make sure the proposed starting materials are viable (activated)
Combine with elimination, halogenation, and other reactions to simplify to very basic starting materials

Section 11.15
MOLECULAR ORBITAL ANALYSIS OF THE DIELS-ALDER REACTION

Frontier Orbital Analysis
The molecular orbital version of “nucleophiles react with electrophiles” is “HOMOs overlap with LUMOs”
The HOMO of the (nucleophilic) diene must be able to overlap with the LUMO of the (electrophilic) dienophile on both ends!
Note the matching colors on both ends of both species. [4+2] is symmetry allowed .

A Symmetry-forbidden Reaction
Not all cycloadditions are symmetry allowed!
In some cases, it is impossible for the HOMO of one component and the LUMO of the other (or vice versa ) to overlap constructively on both ends at the same time
[2 + 2] is symmetry forbidden .

Section 11.16
THE COPE AND CLAISEN REARRANGEMENTS

The Cope Rearrangement
Other structures can engage in cyclic electron flow
For example, a 1,5-diene can rearrange to form a new 1,5-diene . This concerted process is called the Cope rearrangement
Synthetically useful Cope rearrangements give distinct products

The Claisen Rearrangement
When an oxygen is in conjugation with one of the double bonds of the 1,5-diene, rearrangement produces a carbonyl compound
This is called the Claisen rearrangement . The products are usually strongly favored due to the stability of C = O
Contrast with the Cope, which produces only C = C and may favor reactants

Chapter 12
Arenes and Aromaticity
