Section 15.8
AN ORGANOZINC REAGENT FOR CYCLOPROPANE SYNTHESIS

Organozinc Compounds
Like Mg, Zn prefers to be a +2 cation. Zn “inserts” into R–X bonds like Mg, forming organozinc halides
When RX is a geminal dihalide R 2 CX 2 , an interesting product forms…

Organozinc Compounds-1
Like Mg, Zn prefers to be a +2 cation. Zn “inserts” into R–X bonds like Mg, forming organozinc halides
When RX is a geminal dihalide R 2 CX 2 , an interesting product forms…

The Simmons-Smith Reaction
The organozinc reagent ( IZn) CH 2 I is both nucleophilic and electrophilic—similar to a C=C π bond!
Simmons-Smith reaction: electron flow in one direction forges a C–C bond at one carbon of C=C ; electron flow in the other direction forges C–C at the other carbon of C=C
A cyclopropane product forms! Both bonds form simultaneously, so this reaction is stereospecific

Section 15.9
CARBENES AND CARBENOIDS

Carbenes and Carbenoids
Carbenes are neutral molecules in which one of the carbon atoms has six valence electrons
Such carbons are divalent; they are directly bonded to only two other atoms and have no multiple bonds.
Carbenes are formed as intermediates in certain other reactions that convert alkenes to cyclopropanes.

Carbenes and Carbenoids-1
Bonding in dihalocarbenes is based on sp 2 hybridization of carbon
Two of carbon’s sp 2 hybrid orbitals are involved in σ bonds to the halogen. The third sp 2 orbital contains the unshared electron pair, and the unhybridized 2p orbital is vacant.

Section 15.10
TRANSITION-METAL ORGANOMETALLIC COMPOUNDS

d -Block Elements
The transition metals or d -block elements occupy the center of the periodic table
They can form cations with a variety of charges and often sit at the center of complexes with organic molecules at the periphery
Transition-metal complexes are extremely important in modern chemical synthesis
Properties of transition metals vary (almost) continuously
Set of possible ligands is enormous
Transition-metal complexes have unique reactivity

Transition-metal Complexes
Transition metals are usually cationic and Lewis acidic in stable compounds
A Lewis-acidic transition metal atom/ion surrounded by Lewis basic organic molecules ( ligands ) is called a transition-metal complex

Transition-metal Complexes-1
Transition metals are usually cationic and Lewis acidic in stable compounds
A Lewis-acidic transition metal atom/ion surrounded by Lewis basic organic molecules ( ligands ) is called a transition-metal complex
Ligands are almost always formally anionic or neutral; a lone pair or π electrons bond to the metal

The Eighteen-electron Rule
Transition metals have valence electrons in the d subshell, and thus have their own analogue of the octet rule
Eighteen-electron rule: the sum of electrons donated by ligands plus the number of valence electrons at the metal equals 18 in stable TM complexes
Some ligands bond using more than two electrons!

Metallocenes
Like benzene, cyclopentadienyl anion is aromatic. It’s a much stronger ligand due to its anionic charge
Metallocenes contain cyclopentadienide ligands and are commonly used for catalysis

Section 15.11
ORGANOCOPPER REAGENTS

Gilman Reagents
R–Li add twice to copper(I) halides to form compounds of anionic [ R 2 Cu − ] called Gilman reagents
Like most organometallics, Gilman reagents are nucleophilic
They react with electrophilic organohalides in cross-coupling reactions to form C–C
Works for any organohalide except 2 degrees and 3 degrees alkyl (elimination is an issue ).

Cross-coupling
Oxidative addition: copper inserts into the R–X bond, going from Cu(I) to Cu(III).

Cross-coupling-1
Trends in the cross-coupling of Gilman reagents with alkyl halides resemble those of S N 2 :
Rl > RBr > RCl > RF
Inversion of configuration
However, sp 2 and sp organohalides react too, so S N 2 is unlikely
Instead, a mechanism involving oxidative addition and reductive elimination is more likely
Reductive elimination: R and CH 3 “de-insert,” converting Cu(III) to Cu(I) again.

Cross-coupling-2
Trends in the cross-coupling of Gilman reagents with alkyl halides resemble those of SN2 :
Rl > RBr > RCl > RF
Inversion of configuration
However, sp 2 and sp organohalides react too, so S N 2 is unlikely
Instead, a mechanism involving oxidative addition and reductive elimination is more likely

Section 15.12
PALLADIUM-CATALYZED CROSS-COUPLING
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education.

Extending Cross-coupling
If we consider the reaction of RLi with CuX, Gilman cross-coupling appears to have three steps :
Transmetalation of R from lithium to copper
Oxidative addition of R’X to copper
Reductive elimination of R–R’
Overall process is stoichiometric, not catalytic, because a full equivalent of Cu is required
When palladium (Pd) is used with more mild R–M than organolithiums, all three steps can be carried out catalytically !
Oxidative addition of the electrophile happens first, followed by transmetallation from R’M and reductive elimination.

Palladium-catalyzed Cross-coupling
Different Pd - catalyzed cross-couplings involve different nucleophiles R’M; electrophiles are organohalides or pseudohalides ( usually sp 2 or sp )
Stille coupling: organotin nucleophiles

Palladium-catalyzed Cross-coupling-1
Different Pd-catalyzed cross-couplings involve different nucleophiles R’M; electrophiles are organohalides or pseudohalides (usually sp 2 or sp )
Negishi coupling: organozinc nucleophiles

Palladium-catalyzed Cross-coupling-2
Different Pd-catalyzed cross-couplings involve different nucleophiles R’M; electrophiles are organohalides or pseudohalides (usually sp 2 or sp )
Suzuki coupling: organoboron nucleophiles

Palladium-catalyzed Cross-coupling-3
Different Pd-catalyzed cross-couplings involve different nucleophiles R’M; electrophiles are organohalides or pseudohalides (usually sp 2 or sp )
Heck coupling: alkenes + organohalides

Other Nucleophiles
Oxygen-, nitrogen-, and sulfur-based nucleophiles can also be used in palladium-catalyzed cross-couplings

Section 15.13
HOMOGENEOUS CATALYTIC HYDROGENATION

Homogeneous Hydrogenation
Hydrogenations we have seen so far have been heterogeneous, involving a metal catalyst suspended in solvent
Homogeneous hydrogenation is also possible using a catalyzed dissolved in the reaction solvent
Wilkinson’s catalyst , based on rhodium, is a popular choice for homogeneous hydrogenation

Hydrogenation with Wilkinson’s Catalyst
Wilkinson’s catalyst is selective for unhindered C=C
Addition is stereospecifically syn , as in heterogeneneous hydrogenation reactions

Hydrogenation with Wilkinson’s Catalyst-1
Mechanism involves oxidative addition, insertion of C=C into M–H, and reductive elimination to form second C–H

Hydrogenation with Wilkinson’s Catalyst-2
Mechanism involves oxidative addition, insertion of C=C into M–H, and reductive elimination to form second C–H

Enantioselective Hydrogenation
Chiral ligands make the catalyst chiral, leading to selective formation of one enantiomer in hydrogenations that establish a stereocenter
The biaryl ligand BINAP is highly selective in combination with rhodium and other metals

Section 15.14
OLEFIN METATHESIS

Olefin Metathesis
In an olefin metathesis reaction, the ends of two C=C ’s “trade partners” (however, the reaction is thermodynamically controlled )
The reaction involves metal carbenoids M=CR 2 . Insertion of C=C into the M=C π bond is followed by de-insertion
Complexes A and B react via the same steps, eventually generating products.

Olefin Metathesis-1
In an olefin metathesis reaction, the ends of two C=C ’s “trade partners” (however, the reaction is thermodynamically controlled )
The reaction involves metal carbenoids M=CR 2 . Insertion of C=C into the M=C π bond is followed by de-insertion
Complexes A and B react via the same steps, eventually generating products.

Olefin Metathesis-2
In an olefin metathesis reaction, the ends of two C=C ’s “trade partners” (however, the reaction is thermodynamically controlled )
The reaction involves metal carbenoids M=CR 2 . Insertion of C=C into the M=C π bond is followed by de-insertion
Complexes A and B react via the same steps, eventually generating products.

Ring-closing Metathesis
Two terminal alkenes within the same molecule react intramolecularly to give C 2 H 4 (gas) and a cyclic alkene
This reaction is known as ring-closing metathesis

Ring-opening Metathesis
Metathesis can also be used to open cyclic alkenes to form alkenic polymers
This reaction is called ring-opening metathesis ; when used for polymerization it’s called ROMP

Section 15.15
ZIEGLER-NATTA CATALYSIS OF ALKENE POLYMERIZATION

Coordination Polymerization
Coordination to a Lewis-acidic metal renders alkenes electrophilic (similar to cationic polymerization )
Addition of certain metals ( Ziegler’s catalysts ) leads to very long polymers
Furthermore, the polymers thus formed were isotactic

Ziegler-Natta Polymerization
In modern times, Zr-based metallocenes are used together with methylalumoxane (MAO) as Lewis-acidic promoter

Mechanism of Z-N Polymerization
Step 1: a methyl group from MAO displaces Cl − in the Zr metallocene ; Cl − departs to form a cationic complex
Step 2: the alkene coordinates to Zr
Step 3: the alkene inserts into the Zr– CH 3 bond
Step 4: another molecule of alkene inserts into the Zr– CH 2 CH 2 CH 3 bond
Step 4 repeats to form a long linear alkyl chain

Chapter 16
Alcohols, Diols, and Thiols
© 2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education.

Section 16.1
SOURCES OF ALCOHOLS

Sources of Alcohols
Methanol ( CH 3 OH ) is a byproduct in the production of charcoal from wood; nowadays, it is produced mostly by reduction of CO
Natural alcohols are produced during the fermentation process
Ethanol and isopropanol are produced from petroleum via hydration of ethylene and propene
Many reactions we have encountered previously yield alcohols as products !
Reactions that Yield Alcohols
Reactions That Yield Alcohols-1
Reactions That Yield Alcohols-2

Section 16.2
PREPARATION OF ALCOHOLS BY REDUCTION OF ALDEHYDES AND KETONES

Reduction via Hydrogenation
Heterogeneous metal catalysts and hydrogen gas reduce R 2 C=O to R 2 HCOH
Metals used include Pt, Pd, Ni, or Ru

Reduction via Metal Hydrides
Laboratory reductions of carbonyl compounds use metal hydrides ( H − ) followed by acidic workup ( H + ) to add H 2 across C=O
Sodium borohydride is mild and works in water or alcohols
These reactions do not involve H 2 , but instead the sequential addition of H − and H + .

Reduction via Metal Hydrides-1
Laboratory reductions of carbonyl compounds use metal hydrides ( H − ) followed by acidic workup ( H + ) to add H 2 across C=O
Lithium aluminum hydride (LAH) reacts violently with water and alcohols and requires strictly aprotic conditions

Mechanisms of Metal Hydride Reduction
Note that the new C–H comes from the metal hydride; the new O–H comes from the workup acid (typically water)

Section 16.3
PREPARATION OF ALCOHOLS BY REDUCTION OF CARBOXYLIC ACIDS

Reduction of Carboxylic Acids
If H − could displace OH − in a carboxylic acid, – CO 2 H could be reduced to CH 2 OH , a primary alcohol
Only the strongest reducing agents (LAH) can accomplish this
NaBH 4 does not work!

Section 16.4
PREPARATION OF ALCOHOLS FROM EPOXIDES

Opening of Epoxides
The alcohol product contains two new carbons relative to the starting alkyl halide (precursor to the nucleophile)

Opening of Epoxides-1
C–O bonds in epoxides are readily cleaved by carbon nucleophiles (RLi and RMgX) and other nucleophiles via S N 2
Other epoxides may be used to yield secondary and tertiary alcohols, but regioselectivity issues exist (more in Chapter 17 )

Section 16.5
PREPARATION OF DIOLS

Reduction of Dicarbonyls
Dicarbonyl compounds are reduced under hydrogenation or metal hydride reduction conditions to yield diols
Note the nomenclature of the diol product
Pentanediol
Two locants are necessary to locate the –OH groups

Dihydroxylation Yields Vicinal Diols
Alkenes react with Os O 4 to give cyclic osmate esters …
…which are cleaved in the presence of an oxidant and base to give vicinal diols

Dihydroxylation Yields Vicinal Diols-1
Alkenes react with Os O 4 to give cyclic osmate esters …
…which are cleaved in the presence of an oxidant and base to give vicinal diols
This reaction is catalytic! Nice !

Dihydroxylation Yields Vicinal Diols-2
Simply adding the alkene with Os O 4 , oxidant, and base gives the product after several hours
Both bonds to Os O 4 are made simultaneously, so dihydroxylation is stereospecifically syn
In the presence of a chiral base catalyst, the reaction is highly enantioselective as well

Section 16.6
REACTIONS OF ALCOHOLS: A REVIEW AND A PREVIEW
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education.

Reaction with Hydrogen Halides
We have previously seen the reaction of ROH with hydrogen halides HX to give alkyl halides
Alcohols can be transformed into a variety of other functional groups

Functional Group Transformations
SOCl 2 and PBr 3 also convert ROH to alkyl halides. Oxygen acts as a nucleophile in these reactions

Functional Group Transformations-1
Oxygen also acts as a nucleophile in the conversion of ROH to sulfonates

Elimination
Dehydration of alcohols using strong, non-nucleophilic acid yields alkenes
Rearrangements are sometimes observed; reactivity trend is 3 degrees > 2 degrees > 1 degrees

Section 16.7
CONVERSION OF ALCOHOLS TO ETHERS
Synthesis of Ethers

Synthesis of Ethers-1
The oxygen in a protonated alcohol ROH 2 + is electrophilic
The oxygen of an alcohol ROH is nucleophilic
When ROH is heated with an acid catalyst, nucleophilic O in ROH attacks the electrophilic carbon, displacing water
The product is a symmetric ether
Diols can react intramolecularly to give cyclic ethers

Section 16.8
ESTERIFICATION

Fischer Esterification
Under acidic conditions, alcohols and carboxylic acids form esters reversibly
Adding an excess of one reactant can drive the reaction forward

Fischer Esterification-1
Under acidic conditions, alcohols and carboxylic acids form esters reversibly
Removing a product (H 2 O) also drives the reaction forward
The mechanism will be discussed in a later chapter; for now, note that the alcohol C–O bond remains intact .

Acid Chlorides
Acid chlorides are strongly electrophilic at the carbonyl carbon; they react with nucleophilic alcohols (usually with some base) to form esters
Anhydrides can also be used

Section 16.9
OXIDATION OF ALCOHOLS

Oxidation of Alcohols
Just as carbonyls can be reduced to alcohols, alcohols can be oxidized to carbonyls
Oxidation can be stopped at the aldehyde stage or carried through to the carboxylic acid (depending on the oxidant used)
Chromic acid (H 2 CrO 4 ) is a strong oxidant used to form carboxylic acids from primary alcohols

Mild Oxidation to Form Aldehydes
More mildly acidic chromate salts can be used to oxidize primary alcohols to aldehydes
Pyridinium chlorochromate (PCC)
Pyridinium dichromate (PDC )
Secondary alcohols form ketones, but tertiary alcohols do not react or form complicated mixtures of products .

Mechanism of Chromate Oxidation
The general mechanism of alcohol oxidation involves two stages:
The alcohol hydrogen (–OH) is transformed into a good leaving group (–OLG)
β -Elimination across C–O eliminates H–LG and forms C=O
Mechanism of Chromate Oxidation-1

Other Oxidants: Swern Oxidation
This reaction is called the Swern oxidation

Section 16.10
BIOLOGICAL OXIDATION OF ALCOHOLS

Biological Oxidation of ROH
Oxidation of alcohols and reduction of carbonyls are important biological processes
For example, ethanol is oxidized to acetaldehyde by the enzyme alcohol dehydrogenase
Chromium-based oxidants are far too strong for a biological context !
The nicotinamide group is “nature’s oxidant .”

Mechanism of NAD + Oxidation
The alcohols transfers hydride (C–H) to NAD + , forming NADH
Deprotonation of O–H by a basic group in the enzyme occurs simultaneously

Section 16.11
OXIDATIVE CLEAVAGE OF VICINAL DIOLS
Copyright © 2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. No reproduction or distribution without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Oxidative Cleavage of Vicinal Diols
Vicinal (1,2) diols undergo oxidation—loss of H 2 —and rearrangement to form two separated aldehydes or ketones
The oxidant is periodic acid ( HIO 4 )

Section 16.12
THIOLS

Thiols: Sulfur Analogues of Alcohols
Thiols (mercaptans) contain the –SH group and resemble alcohols in structure and reactivity
The terrible odor of thiols is legendary
There are some important differences in reactivity between thiols and alcohols

Reactivity of Thiols
Thiols are considerably more acidic than alcohols and water
Naturally, thiol ate anions are less basic than alkoxides, but they are excellent nucleophiles toward C

Oxidation of Thiols
At first glance, oxidation of thiols should produce thiocarbonyl compounds
However, sulfur is a strong nucleophile toward oxygen— sulfoxides are the usual products of oxidation of thiols
Precursor to sulfonates!

Oxidation of Thiols-1
Oxidation can also produce a disulfide , the sulfur analogue of a peroxide ( RO 2 R cannot generally be prepared this way )
This type of oxidation is important in a biological context

Section 16.13
SPECTROSCOPIC ANALYSIS OF ALCOHOLS AND THIOLS

Infrared Spectroscopy
Alcohols give a highly characteristic, broad peak from 3200 – 3500 cm − 1 due to the O–H stretch
Thiols give a weak band from 2550 – 2700 cm − 1 due to the S–H stretch

1 H NMR Spectroscopy
The alcohol C–H proton appears between δ 3.3 – 4; the shift of the O–H proton is highly variable
C–H in thiols is not quite as deshielded as in alcohols :

Mass Spectrometry
Loss of water is common in mass spectra of alcohols; the molecular ion peak tends to be weak
Primary alcohols ( RCH 2 OH ) fragment to give protonated formaldehyde ( CH 2 OH + , m ∕ z =31 )

Chapter 17
Ethers, Epoxides, and Sulfides
©Evgeniy Ivanao/Getty Image: ©Bill grove/Getty image
© 2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education.

Ethers, Epoxides, and Sulfides
Ethers (ROR) are relatively unreactive; their most important reactivity is the Lewis basicity of the oxygen atom
Epoxides are an important exception to this rule
Sulfides (RSR), the sulfur analogues of ethers, have different physical properties and reactivity

Section 17.1
NOMENCLATURE OF ETHERS, EPOXIDES, AND SULFIDES

Nomenclature of Ethers
Ethers can be named as alkoxyalkanes (substitutive) or as alkyl alkyl’ ethers (functional class )
Ethers with identical R groups are symmetrical ; ethers with different R groups are unsymmetrical
Cyclic ethers have unique names; the oxygen is treated as position 1

Nomenclature of Sulfides
Sulfides can be named using substitutive (alkylthio-) or functional class (alkyl alkyl’ sulfide) nomenclature
Sulfur analogues of epoxides are called thiiranes

Section 17.2
STRUCTURE AND BONDING IN ETHERS AND EPOXIDES

Structure and Bonding
The oxygen in alkyl ethers is sp 3 hybridized, but the typical bond angle is wider than 109.5 degrees due to strain
The ether oxygen behaves largely like a CH 2 unit from a conformational perspective

Section 17.3
PHYSICAL PROPERTIES OF ETHERS

Physical Properties of Ethers
Ether molecules, unlike alcohols, lack the ability to hydrogen bond with themselves
Boiling and melting points are similar to alkanes of comparable size
Ethers can hydrogen bond with H-bond donors such as water
Solubility of ethers in water is generally high as a result

Section 17.4
CROWN ETHERS

Crown Ethers: Applied Basicity
The most important principle of reactivity for ethers is that the ether oxygen can act as a Lewis base
Cyclic polyethers have multiple oxygens that can bind ( chelate ) to a Lewis acid at multiple points
Crown ethers are cyclic polyethers of various sizes that bind various cations

Complexation of Cations
The Lewis basic cavity of a crown ether provides a “pocket” where cations can bind
For example, addition of 18-crown-6 increases the solubility of KF in benzene, a nonpolar solvent
The hydrophobic outer edge is solvated by benzene
The Lewis basic inner core binds K +

Section 17.5
PREPARATION OF ETHERS

Preparation of Ethers
Symmetrical ethers can be prepared via acid-catalyzed condensation of alcohols, a substitution process
Ethers can also be prepared via addition of O and H across C=C, acid-catalyzed hydration

Section 17.6
THE WILLIAMSON ETHER SYNTHESIS

Williamson Ether Synthesis
Alkoxides ( RO − ) are great nucleophiles; reaction of RO − with an alkyl halide RX forms a new C–O bond
This is an attractive method for the synthesis of ethers !
To avoid E2 elimination, the alkyl halide should be primary
The substitution of the alkoxide is largely irrelevant

Section 17.7
REACTIONS OF ETHERS: A REVIEW AND A PREVIEW
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Reactivity of Ethers
Ethers are unreactive in general except in combustion reactions with O 2 . Ethers are highly flammable!
Ethers are oxidized slowly over time to explosive hydroperoxides
Hydrohalic acids can cleave ethers to alkyl halides (more in the next section)

Section 17.8
ACID-CATALYZED CLEAVAGE OF ETHERS

Acid-catalyzed Cleavage of Ethers
Hydrohalic acids protonate ethers, converting –OR to –(H) OR + (a good leaving group!)
Nucleophilic attack by halide ion X − produces an alkyl halide
Heat and excess of HX are typically used, causing a second nucleophilic substitution to occur

Mechanism
Step 1: protonation generates a good leaving group within the ether
Both carbons attached to O become electrophilic
The intermediate is “primed” for nucleophilic substitution

Mechanism-1
Step 2: halide ion attacks one of the electrophilic carbons; alcohol is formed as a leaving group
A similar mechanism leads to displacement of H 2 O from the alcohol by X −

Section 17.9
PREPARATION OF EPOXIDES

Two Methods for Epoxide Synthesis
There are two general methods used for synthesis of epoxides
Method 1: install an alcohol oxygen two atoms from an electrophilic carbon; perform intramolecular S N 2
Method 2: use an oxidant to establish both "C–O" bonds at once across a "C=C" π bond
Both start ultimately from an alkene "C=C"
Method 1 requires two steps but Method 2 only requires one
For both methods, the π bond must be a good nucleophile
We focus on Method 2 here and Method 1 in the next section

Epoxidation with Peracids
A peracid or peroxyacid can be used to prepare epoxides from alkenes with syn stereospecificity

Sharpless Epoxidation
The Sharpless epoxidation works on similar principles, but involves a hydroperoxide instead of a peracid and a transition-metal catalyst
This reaction is highly enantioselective and occurs stereospecifically with syn addition

Section 17.10
CONVERSION OF VICINAL HALOHYDRINS TO EPOXIDES

Epoxidation via Vicinal Halohydrin
Oxygen can act as a good nucleophile and alkyl halide carbon as an electrophile in S N 2
When the nucleophilic O and electrophilic C are connected via one C, an epoxide is the product!
Step 1: synthesis of vicinal halohydrin via addition of X and OH to alkene ( cohalogenation )

Epoxidation via Vicinal Halohydrin-1
Oxygen can act as a good nucleophile and alkyl halide carbon as an electrophile in S N 2
When the nucleophilic O and electrophilic C are connected via one C, an epoxide is the product!
Step 2: treatment with base to generate the alkoxide; spontaneous intramolecular S N 2
Stereochemistry of Epoxidation

Section 17.11
REACTIONS OF EPOXIDES WITH ANIONIC NUCLEOPHILES

Nucleophilic Substitution of Epoxides
Epoxides are unique among ethers: their carbons are highly electrophilic
Ring-opening provides a big incentive to substitution of C–O
A variety of anionic nucleophiles may be used, including RLi, RMgX, alkoxides, etc .

Regioselectivity and Stereoselectivity
The mechanisms of these reactions are universally S N 2
Substitution occurs with inversion of configuration at the electrophilic carbon
Substitution occurs preferentially at the less substituted electrophilic carbon

Section 17.12
ACID-CATALYZED RING OPENING OF EPOXIDES

Acid-catalyzed Nucleophilic Substitution
Acids can catalyze nucleophilic substitution of epoxides
These reactions do not involve a discrete carbocation intermediate—still S N 2 with inversion of configuration
Reaction occurs selectively at the more substituted position

Acid-catalyzed Nucleophilic Substitution-1
Acids can catalyze nucleophilic substitution of epoxides
These reactions do not involve a discrete carbocation intermediate—still S N 2 with inversion of configuration
Reaction occurs selectively at the more substituted position
Reaction occurs selectively at the carbon better able to support positive charge .

Application: anti Dihydroxylation
Acid-catalyzed epoxide opening with water ( hydrolysis ) leads to net anti dihydroxylation of alkenes

Section 17.13
EPOXIDES IN BIOLOGICAL PROCESSES
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Monooxygenases
Some monooxygenase enzymes catalyze epoxidation of biological alkenes by O 2
A reducing agent (NADH) is required along with an “oxidizing” proton
Epoxidation of squalene is an important example

Section 17.14
PREPARATION OF SULFIDES

Preparation of Sulfides via 𝐒 𝐍 𝟐
Thiolate anions are great nucleophiles
Sulfides can be prepared via S N 2 of thiolates with alkyl halides
The usual trends and limitations of S N 2 still apply to these reactions .

Section 17.15
OXIDATION OF SULFIDES: SULFOXIDES AND SULFONES
Oxidation of Sulfides

Section 17.16
ALKYLATION OF SULFIDES: SULFONIUM SALTS

Alkylation of Sulfides
Neutral sulfides react with alkyl halides in S N 2 reactions to form sulfonium salts
Sulfoniums are much more stable than analogous oxonium salts of ethers

Nature’s Methylating Agent
Nature uses a methylsulfonium salt as an electrophilic methylating agent: S - adenosylmethionine (SAM)

Section 17.17
SPECTROSCOPIC ANALYSIS OF ETHERS, EPOXIDES, AND SULFIDES
Infrared Spectroscopy
𝟏 𝐇 NMR Spectroscopy
𝟏𝟑 𝐂 NMR Spectroscopy

𝟏𝟑 𝐂 NMR Spectroscopy-2
The carbons of the C – O – C group in an ether are about 10 ppm less shielded than those of an alcohol and appear in the range δ 57–87.
The carbons of the C – S – C group in a sulfide are significantly more shielded than those of an ether .

Mass Spectrometry
Ethers and sulfides can both lose an alkyl radical to form an O − or S-stabilized carbocation
Often, this fragment is more abundant than the molecular ion

Chapter 18
Aldehydes and Ketones
©Evgeniy Ivanao/Getty Image: ©Bill grove/Getty image
© 2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education.

Aldehydes and Ketones
Aldehydes and ketones are extremely common examples of carbonyl compounds
In this chapter:
Structure and bonding of the carbonyl group
Nucleophilic addition as a paradigmatic reaction of carbonyls
Examples of reactivity
Spectroscopic signatures of aldehydes and ketones

Section 18.1
NOMENCLATURE

Nomenclature of Aldehydes
Aldehydes contain the formyl group ( – CHO) . They are named by replacing –e in the name of the parent alkane with – al
Rings with a formyl substituent are named as carbaldehydes
Note that the formyl group does not need a locant .

Nomenclature of Aldehydes-1
A few aldehydes have common names acceptable to IUPAC
Aldehydes take precedence over alcohols (ranking is done by oxidation state )

Nomenclature of Ketones
To name ketones, –e in the name of the parent alkane is replaced with –one
The carbonyl carbon is given a locant (smallest possible); C–1 in cyclic ketones
Aldehydes take precedence over ketones
Ketones can also be named using functional class nomenclature as alkyl alkyl’ ketones

Common Ketones
A few ketones have acceptable common names (all of which end in –one )

Section 18.2
STRUCTURE AND BONDING: THE CARBONYL GROUP

Structure and Hybridization
The carbonyl carbon is sp 2 hybridized, with trigonal planar geometry and (approximately) 120 degrees bond angles
Bonding and Polarity

Bonding and Polarity-1
Nucleophiles (anions, lone pairs) bond to the carbonyl carbon; electrophiles (proton, Lewis acids) bond to the carbonyl oxygen .

Section 18.3
PHYSICAL PROPERTIES

Physical Properties
Aldehydes and ketones experience strong dipole-dipole forces
Boiling and melting points are generally higher than alkenes of comparable molecular weight
Boiling and melting points are lower than related alcohols, however (why?)

Section 18.4
SOURCES OF ALDEHYDES AND KETONES

Natural Aldehydes and Ketones
Nature produces aldehydes and ketones usually via oxidation of alcohols

Laboratory Synthesis
Redox reactions of alcohols and carbonyls are extremely versatile—aldehydes and ketones can be accessed from almost any C–O oxidation level
For example, a reduce-then-oxidize method is useful for aldehyde synthesis from RCO 2 H

Laboratory Synthesis-1
Ketones can be prepared from aldehydes via Grignard addition followed by oxidation
Grignard addition forms an intermediate secondary alcohol
Chromic acid oxidation yields the ketone
Other Reactions Yielding Carbonyls
Other Reactions Yielding Carbonyls-1
Other Reactions Yielding Carbonyls-2

Section 18.5
REACTIONS OF ALDEHYDES AND KETONES: A REVIEW AND A PREVIEW

Past Reactions of Carbonyls
We have seen several reactions of ketones and aldehydes previously:
Reduction to alkanes: Wolff-Kishner or Clemmensen
Reduction to alcohols: catalytic hydrogenation or metal hydride reduction
Nucleophilic additions: RLi or RMgX
These last two reactions in particular illustrate that carbonyls engage in nucleophilic addition at carbon
We will examine the reactivity of other nucleophiles toward C=O throughout this chapter

Section 18.6
PRINCIPLES OF NUCLEOPHILIC ADDITION: HYDRATION OF ALDEHYDES AND KETONES
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Nucleophilic Addition of Water
Water acts as a nucleophile toward ketones and aldehydes. Addition of –OH and –H across C=O is called hydration
Hydration is often very rapid; we are often interested in the equilibrium constant for this process
Is a ketone really a ketone, or is it mostly a hydrate ?
The value of K hydr is governed by intuitive structural effects

Aldehydes versus Ketones
Ketones are hydrated to a much smaller extent than aldehdyes
Inductively donating alkyl groups stabilize the carbonyl C, making hydration disfavorable

Other Electronic Effects
Electron-donating alkyl groups stabilize carbonyl C and decrease K hydr
Electron-withdrawing groups, such as perfluoroalkyl, make the carbonyl C more electrophilic and increase K hydr

Steric Effects
Large alkyl groups tend to disfavor addition
Addition brings groups from 120 degrees bond angles to smaller 109.5 degrees bond angles, increasing steric crowding

Mechanism: Base-catalyzed Hydration
Step 1: hydroxide (base) serves as a nucleophile and adds to the carbonyl carbon, forming an alkoxide intermediate
This is a classic example of nucleophilic addition to a π bond!

Mechanism: Base-catalyzed Hydration-1
Step 1: hydroxide (base) serves as a nucleophile and adds to the carbonyl carbon, forming an alkoxide intermediate
This is a classic example of nucleophilic addition to a π bond!
Step 2 : the alkoxide deprotonates water, regenerating the base

Mechanism: Acid-catalyzed Hydration
Step 1: acid protonates the carbonyl oxygen, making the carbonyl carbon even more electrophilic

Mechanism: Acid-catalyzed Hydration-1
Step 1: acid protonates the carbonyl oxygen, making the carbonyl carbon even more electrophilic
Step 2: water adds to the carbonyl carbon

Mechanism: Acid-catalyzed Hydration-2
Step 3: proton transfer from the intermediate back to water

Section 18.7
CYANOHYDRIN FORMATION

Cyanohydrin Formation
Addition of –CN and –H across C=O forms a cyanohydrin
HCN is rarely used; instead MCN is used followed by acidic workup
Mechanistically, nucleophilic addition of CN − is followed by protonation of the former carbonyl oxygen
Note the newly formed C–C bond !

Thermodynamics and Equilibrium
Trends in equilibrium constants for cyanohydrin formation mirror those for hydration to some degree
Generally, aldehydes and unhindered ketones give good yields of cyanohydrins

Section 18.8
REACTION WITH ALCOHOLS: ACETALS AND KETALS

Acetal and Ketal Formation
Nucleophilic addition of an alcohol to an aldehyde (–OR and –H across C=O ) should produce a hemiacetal

Acetal and Ketal Formation-1
Nucleophilic addition of an alcohol to an aldehyde (–OR and –H across C=O ) should produce a hemiacetal
Usually, this intermediate is not isolable—it reacts further to form an acetal
Analogous products from ketones are called ketals
Acetal formation is often thermodynamically favorable; ketal formation usually is not

Mechanism of Acetal Formation
Steps 1 – 3: acid-catalyzed addition of ROH (analogous to hydration)
From here, nucleophilic substitution of –OR for –OH occurs via an acid-promoted ( S N 1 ) mechanism

Mechanism of Acetal Formation-1
Step 4: protonation of the hydroxyl oxygen by acid
The protonated hemiacetal contains a good leaving group!

Mechanism of Acetal Formation-2
Step 5: loss of H 2 O as a leaving group
The cationic intermediate is stabilized by resonance

Mechanism of Acetal Formation-3
Step 6: nucleophilic addition by ROH to the “carbonyl” carbon
The resulting intermediate is the conjugate acid of the product
Step 7: deprotonation of the intermediate (by alcohol) to give the acetal product

Converting Acetals to Carbonyls
Excess alcohol is usually used to drive acetal formation toward products
Excess water can be used to drive this reaction back toward the carbonyl
It’s quite easy to interchange carbonyls and acetals !

Section 18.9
ACETALS AND KETALS AS PROTECTING GROUPS

Protection in Organic Synthesis
At a late stage in synthesis, we have to be careful not to destroy functional groups we’ve installed previously
For example, alkylation of an acetylide anion will not work for this transformation:
The carbonyl group reacts with strong nucleophiles! Carbonyls are base sensitive
We must convert ( protect ) the carbonyl into a functional group that does not react with bases/nucleophiles…

Acetals as Protecting Groups
Like ethers, acetals are unreactive to bases/nucleophiles
Conversion of a carbonyl to an acetal protects it from base; the acetal is called a protecting group
Reactions involving strong base are carried out when the protecting group is present …

Acetals as Protecting Groups-1
Like ethers, acetals are unreactive to bases/nucleophiles
Conversion of a carbonyl to an acetal protects it from base; the acetal is called a protecting group
Then the substrate is deprotected via hydrolysis

Section 18.10
REACTION WITH PRIMARY AMINES: IMINES

Formation of Imines
Carbonyls react with primary amines to give imines via a two-stage process:
Nucleophilic addition of –NHR and –H across R 2 C=O
Elimination of water to form R 2 C=NR
Imine formation is generally reversible, but can be driven forward by removal of water
Note that the amine must be primary

Mechanism of Imine Formation
Step 1: nucleophilic addition of amine to the carbonyl carbon

Mechanism of Imine Formation-1
Step 2: intermolecular proton transfers produce a hemiaminal intermediate

Mechanism of Imine Formation-2
Step 3: protonation of the hemiaminal oxygen
The intermediate is now poised to lose H 2 O and a proton to form the imine product

Mechanism of Imine Formation-3
Step 4: elimination of water, forming C=N
Step 5: loss of a proton from N, forming the imine

Section 18.11
REACTION WITH SECONDARY AMINES: ENAMINES
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Nucleophilic Addition of 𝟐 degrees Amines
Secondary amines have no hydrogen to eliminate after addition across C=O

Mechanism of Enamine Formation
As for 1 degree amines, the first steps involve formation of a hemiaminal
The hemiaminal expels water to give a resonance-stabilized iminium ion
Deprotonation at the α -position gives the enamine

Section 18.12
THE WITTIG REACTION

Alkenes from Carbonyls
The Wittig reaction involves the synthesis of alkenes from carbonyls and phosphonium ylides
The ylide is, like C=O both nucleophilic and electrophilic
The nucleophilic ylide carbon bonds to the carbonyl carbon, eventually forming C=C

Preparation of Phosphonium Ylides
To prepare phosphonium ylides, phosphines are first reacted with alkyl halides to form the P–C bond …
…then the phosphonium is deprotonated using a strong base, such as the conjugate base of DMSO

Mechanism of the Wittig Reaction
Treatment of the ylide with a carbonyl compound gives a new C=C bond , usually with trans configuration where applicable
This reaction is driven by the great strength of the P=O bond
The mechanism involves “cycloaddition” followed by “retro-cycloaddition”

Section 18.13
STEREOSELECTIVE ADDITION TO CARBONYL GROUPS

Diastereoselective Addition
Chiral ketones and aldehydes have two distinct “faces” above and below the carbonyl group
Addition usually occurs selectively at the less hindered face
This sense of addition is both kinetically and thermodynamically favored

Enzymatic Enantioselective Addition
Enzymes catalyze the enantioselective addition of nucleophiles to carbonyl groups
The chiral environment of the enzyme shields one face from nucleophilic attack

Section 18.14
OXIDATION OF ALDEHYDES

Aldehydes to Carboxylic Acids
Aldehydes are oxidized to carboxylic acids by chromic acid, H 2 CrO 4 (and conditions that generate it)
The hydrated aldehyde (geminal diol) is likely involved
Because the hydrate is more reactive than an alcohol, any aqueous oxidation conditions will convert RCH 2 OH to RCO 2 H

Biological Oxidation
Aldehydes are converted to carboxylic acids by aldehyde dehydrogenase enzymes
NAD + acts as a hydride acceptor in these reactions; water is the source of – OH
The body uses a dehydrogenase to convert toxic acetaldehyde into harmless acetic acid

Section 18.15
SPECTROSCOPIC ANALYSIS OF ALDEHYDES AND KETONES

Infrared Spectroscopy
Aldehydes and ketones give a very strong, characteristic peak between 1710 – 1750 cm( − 1)
Aldehyde C–H stretch also appears in spectra of aldehydes (2720, 2820 cm( − 1)

1 H NMR
The aldehyde proton is highly deshielded ( δ 9 – 10) and very characteristic
α -Protons in aldehydes and ketones are deshielded relative to alkane protons ( δ 2 – 3)

13 C NMR
The carbonyl carbon appears far downfield in 13 C NMR
α -Carbons are generally deshielded somewhat relative to alkane carbons

UV-Vis Spectroscopy
The C=O group has two important excitations in the ultraviolet region
Aldehydes and ketones are usually transparent in the absence of additional conjugation

Mass Spectrometry
Molecular ion peaks are generally prominent
Loss of an alkyl radical from the molecular radical cation gives a stabilized acylium ion

Carboxylic Acids
Carboxylic acids contain the functional group –C ( =O )OH
Carboxylic acids are ubiquitious in biochemistry and other applications of organic chemistry
The bound –OH group makes them significantly different from aldehydes and ketones

Section 19.1
Carboxylic Acid Nomenclature

Many carboxylic acids are known by their common names
Formic acid
Acetic acid
Benzoic acid
Butyric acid
Valeric acid

Propanedioic acid (malonic acid )
Naming Carboxylic Acids-1

Section 19.2
Structure and Bonding

Structure of Carboxylic Acids
Carboxylic acids have sp 2 hybridization at the carbonyl carbon, one C=O (σ + π) and one C–OH
Carboxylic acids contain a delocalized π system
The carbonyl carbon receives electron density from – O H, and is less electrophilic than carbonyl C in ketones and aldehydes
The best electrophile in RCO 2 H is the acidic hydrogen!
The best nucleophile is the carbonyl oxygen ( not the –OH oxygen )

Section 19.3
Physical Properties

Physical Properties of RCO 2 H
The melting and boiling points of RCO 2 H are considerably higher than those of related compounds
RCO 2 H can hydrogen twice in an intramolecular fashion
Dimeric structures lead to high melting and boiling points

Section 19.4
Acidity of Carboxylic Acids

Acidity of Carboxylic Acids
Carboxylic acids are moderately acidic at CO 2 H . p K a ’s range from 2 – 6
Note that carboxylic acids are still weak acids!
The conjugate base of a carboxylic acid ( carboxylate ) is stable relative to an alkoxide

Acidity of Carboxylic Acids-1
Carboxylic acids are moderately acidic at CO 2 H . p K a ’s range from 2 – 6
Note that carboxylic acids are still weak acids!
The conjugate base of a carboxylic acid ( carboxylate ) is stable relative to an alkoxide
The carbonyl group is inductively electron withdrawing

Acidity of Carboxylic Acids-2
Carboxylic acids are moderately acidic at CO 2 H . p K a ’s range from 2 – 6
Note that carboxylic acids are still weak acids!
The conjugate base of a carboxylic acid ( carboxylate ) is stable relative to an alkoxide
Resonance illustrates delocalization of negative charge in carboxylate

Carboxylic Acids in Solution
In a buffered solution of pH 7, a carboxylic acid with p K a 5 is mostly in the carboxylate form !
From the given pH and p K a , we can calculate the concentrations of acid and conjugate base
Under biochemical conditions (pH 7), acids are usually referred to as carboxylates ( pyruvate , lactate )

Section 19.5
SubstiTuents and Acids Strength

Substituent Effects: Induction
Alkyl substituents have a minimal effect on acidity; all alkyl acids have p K a around 5
Electronegative substituents increase acidity via inductive effects
The usual distance dependence of induction is observed
Survey of RCO 2 H Acidity

Section 19.6
Ionization of Substituted Benzoic Acids

Acidity of Benzoic Acids
Benzoic acid is slightly more acidic than acetic acid due to electronegativity of the sp 2 hybridized benzene carbon
Substituents that are withdrawing or donating by resonance exert strong effects on acidity when positioned ortho or para

Acidity of Benzoic Acids-1

Neutralization of RCO 2 H
In the presence of strong base (e.g., MOH ), carboxylic acids form carboxylate salts RCO 2 − M +
Metal carboxylates are often soluble in water (they’re ionic salts, after all!)

Micelles
Carboxylates with long alkyl chains have a polar end and a nonpolar end—they are amphiphilic
In a polar solvent, the polar − CO 2 − groups point outward; the hydrophobic alkyl groups cluster inward

Micelles-1
Carboxylates with long alkyl chains have a polar end and a nonpolar end—they are amphiphilic
The resulting spherical structure is called a micelle
In amphiphilic detergents , dirt and grease dissolve in the center of the micelles.

Section 19.8
DICARBOXYLIC ACIDS
Dicarboxylic Acids

−CO 2 H Is Inductively Withdrawing
A nearby − CO 2 H substituent increases the acidity of − CO 2 H via an inductive effect
The effect diminishes as the distance between the groups increases
Note the remarkable acidity of oxalic acid

Section 19.9
CARBONIC ACID

Formation of Carbonic Acid
Carbonic acid contains two hydroxyl groups bound to a common carbonyl group
Carbonic acid is formed via the hydration of CO 2 dissolved in water
Bicarbonate and hydronium form to a small degree: K for the overall process is 4.3 × 10 -7 ( p K 6.4)
Most dissolved CO 2 in water is still CO 2

Ionization Constants of Carbonic Acid
The net equilibrium constant from the last slide is p K a1 for carbonic acid (6.4)
Like all diacids, the second ionization constant p K a2 is much larger (10.2)
Bicarbonate is a weaker acid than carboxylic acids, but is stronger than water and alcohols

Section 19.10
Sources of Carboxylic Acids

Sources of Carboxylic Acids-1
There are many different types of natural carboxylic acids
Fatty acids
Amino acids
Etc.
Industrially, acids are produced via synthetic oxidation
In the lab, nucleophilic attack on CO 2 is also used
Reactions Yielding RCO 2 H
Reactions Yielding RCO 2 H-1
Reactions Yielding RCO 2 H-2

Section 19.11
Synthesis of Carboxylic Acids by the Carboxylation of Grignard Reagents

Nucleophilic Attack on CO 2
Alkyl halides can be converted to carboxylic acids via a three-step sequence involving (a) Grignard reagent formation, (b) nucleophilic attack on CO 2 , and (c) acidification
The central carbon of CO 2 is electrophilic (bound to two withdrawing oxygen atoms)

From RX to RCO 2 H
For this method to work, the alkyl halide cannot contain any acidic groups (no OH or NH)

Section 19.12
SYNTHESIS OF CARBOXYLIC ACIDS BY THE PREPARATION AND HYDROLYSIS OF NITRILES

RCO 2 H via Nitriles
In the last section, the future carbonyl carbon was electrophilic…
…we can also use the nucleophilic carbon in cyanide ( CN − ) as a precursor to a carboxylic acid
Cyanide engages in S N 2 with 1 degree or 2 degrees alkyl halides
Subsequent acidic hydrolysis gives a carboxylic acid
Cyanohydrins undergo hydrolysis under similar conditions to give α - hydroxyacids
OH and NH are well tolerated

Section 19.13
REACTIONS OF CARBOXYLIC ACIDS: A REVIEW AND A PREVIEW

Reactivity of Carboxylic Acids
Carboxylic acids themselves are acidic
Carboxylate anions can act as nucleophiles at oxygen
The carboxylic acid oxygen can act as a nucleophile
Carboxylic acids can engage in…
Acid halide formation
Esterification
Reduction

Reactions of RCO 2 H
Reaction (section) and comments General equation and specific example
Conversion to acyl chlorides (see Section 13.7) Thionyl chloride reacts with carboxylic acids to yield acyl chlorides.
Lithium aluminum hydride reduction (see Section 16.3) Carboxylic acids are not reducible by catalytic hydrogenation, but are converted to primary alcohols by lithium aluminum hydride. Sodium borohydride does not reduce carboxylic acids .

Reactions of RCO 2 H-1
Esterification (see Section 16.8) In the presence of an acid catalyst, carboxylic acids and alcohols react to form esters (Fischer esterification). The reaction is an equilibrium process, but can be driven to completion by removing the water that is formed

Section 19.14
MECHANISM OF ACID-CATALYZED ESTERIFICATION
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Esterification Revisited
We could imagine the carboxylic acid as nucleophile and the alcohol as electrophile or vice versa
These two mechanisms vary in the identity of the “non-carbonyl oxygen ”:
Experimentally, this oxygen comes from the alcohol
The alcohol acts as a nucleophile in this mechanism .

Mechanism of Esterification
Step 1: the carbonyl oxygen of RCO 2 H is protonated by acid
This step activates the carbonyl carbon as an electrophile !
A decent nucleophile, methanol, is a byproduct of this step

Mechanism of Esterification-1
Step 2: nucleophilic addition of the alcohol to the carbonyl carbon
The resulting tetrahedral intermediate contains the new C–O bond
Step 3: the intermediate is deprotonated, regenerating acid

Mechanism of Esterification-2
Step 4: one of the hydroxyl groups is protonated by acid
Water is now poised to act as a leaving group
Elimination of H + and H 2 O would give the ester product!

Mechanism of Esterification-3
Step 5: E1-like loss of water, giving a protonated ester intermediate
Step 6: proton transfer to ROH, regenerating acid

General Ideas of Esterification
Note that the carbonyl oxygen is protonated—not the hydroxyl oxygen!
We see three general stages in this mechanism:
Electrophilic activation of the carbonyl group
Nucleophilic addition
Elimination
These steps are common for carboxylic acid derivatives. We will see them again!

Section 19.15
INTRAMOLECULAR ESTER FORMATION: LACTONES

Lactones and Lactonization
Cyclic esters are called lactones
Lactone formation is catalyzed by acid and benefits from the large “effective concentration” of RCO 2 H and ROH

Naming Lactones
–oic acid in the parent carboxylic acid is replaced by –olide , and a locant identifies the oxygenated carbon
Macrolides are lactones with large ring sizes

Section 19.16
DECARBOXYLATION OF MALONIC ACID AND RELATED COMPOUNDS

Decarboxylation
Loss of CO 2 from a carboxylic acid is called decarboxylation
Decarboxylation of unactivated acids is very rarely encountered
Decarboxylation can be assisted by a nearby carbonyl group
Malonic acids undergo decarboxylation at high temperatures
β - ketoacids very easily surrender CO 2 to form ketones

Mechanisms of Decarboxylation
The nearby C=O group assists in deprotonation and loss of CO 2
Similar mechanism for R = alkyl (yields ketone, as above) and R = OH (yields carboxylic acid )

Section 19.17
SPECTROSCOPIC ANALYSIS OF CARBOXYLIC ACIDS
Infrared Spectroscopy

The carboxylic acid proton is strongly deshielded—the most deshielded of all commonly encountered protons
Adding D 2 O destroys this signal via H-D exchange

The carbonyl carbon of a carboxylic acid appears at δ 160 – 185, not quite as deshielded as ketone or aldehyde C=O

Mass Spectrometry

Minor fragment peak corresponding to loss of OH and formation of acylium ion
Minor fragment peak corresponding to loss of CO 2 H

Carboxylic Acid Derivatives: Nucleophilic Acyl Substitution
©arlindo71/Getty Images

Carboxylic Acid Derivatives
Carboxylic acid derivatives contain a carbonyl group bound to an electronegative heteroatom
CADs are generated via nucleophilic acyl substitution, where X is a good leaving group (halide, etc.)

Section 20.1
NOMENCLATURE OF CARBOXYLIC ACID DERIVATIVES

Naming Acid Chlorides
To generate the name of the acyl group, replace –ic acid in the parent carboxylic acid with –yl
Add chloride after the name of the acyl group (functional class nomenclature)

Naming Acid Anhydrides
If the two acyl groups are the same, acid in the parent carboxylic acid is replaced by anhydride
If the two acyl groups differ, names of the corresponding acids are listed in alphabetical order
Naming Esters

Naming Amides
Replace –oic acid in the parent acid with –amide
Substituents listed in alphabetical order
Substituents on nitrogen are denoted with the locant N -

Naming Nitriles
Replace the -ic acid or -oic acid ending of the corresponding carboxylic acid with -onitrile.
Alternatively, they are sometimes given functional class IUPAC names as alkyl cyanides.
The suffix -carbonitrile is used when a -CN group is attached to ak ring.

Section 20.2
STRUCTURE AND REACTIVITY OF CARBOXYLIC ACID DERIVATIVES

General Structural Ideas
Carboxylic acid derivatives have a potential leaving group bound to the carbonyl carbon
The electronegativity of the heteroatom determines the quality of the leaving group and reactivity
Reactivity: acyl chlorides > anhydrides > esters > amides
Resonance forms show the importance of electron donation from the heteroatom in stabilizing the carbonyl group
As X becomes more electronegative, stabilization becomes weaker
Structure and Reactivity

Anhydrides versus Esters
Esters are more stable and less reactive than anhydrides
Both involve oxygen donating a pair of electrons to the carbonyl group
However, the central anhydride oxygen is “split” between two carbonyl groups

Amides
N-to-C electron donating is very strong in amides
The C–N bond has considerable double bond character, including a non-negligible barrier to rotation!

Section 20.3
NUCLEOPHILIC ACYL SUBSTITUTION MECHANISMS

Nucleophilic Acyl Substitution
Nucleophilic acyl substitution can take place via several different mechanisms:
Addition-elimination: a tetrahedral intermediate forms after rate-limiting addition; elimination of a leaving group follows
S N 1-like : loss of leaving group to form an acylium ion, followed by nucleophilic attack (e.g., Friedel-Crafts acylation)

Nucleophilic Acyl Substitution-1
Nucleophilic acyl substitution can take place via several different mechanisms:
S N 2-like : (extremely rare) loss of leaving group and nucleophilic attack occur simultaneously
The addition-elimination mechanism is the most common of the three, and occurs under basic or acidic conditions in many cases

Section 20.4
NUCLEOPHILIC ACYL SUBSTITUTION IN ACYL CHLORIDES

Substitution of Acyl Chlorides
Acyl chlorides can be readily converted into any other carboxylic acid derivative (or RCO 2 H itself!)
Rates are faster than substitution of related alkyl halides
Pyridine is often used as a catalyst to make RCOCl more electrophilic and “mop up” hydrochloric acid product

Substitution of Acyl Chlorides-1
TABLE 20.1 Conversion of Acyl Chlorides to Other Carboxylic Acid Derivatives
Reaction (section) and comments General equation and specific example
Reaction with carboxylic acids (see Section 20.4) Acyl chlorides react with carboxylic acids to yield acid anhydrides. When this reaction is used for preparative purposes , pyridine is normally added. Pyridine is a catalyst for the reaction and also acts as a base to neutralize the hydrogen chloride that is formed .
Reaction with alcohols ( see Section 16.8) Acyl chlorides react with alcohols to form esters . The reaction is typically carried out in the presence of pyridine .

Substitution of Acyl Chlorides-2
Reaction with ammonia and amines (see Section 20.12) Acyl chlorides react with ammonia and amines to form amides. A base such as sodium hydroxide is normally added to react with the hydrogen chloride produced .
Hydrolysis (see Section 20.4) Acyl chlorides react with water to yield carboxylic acids. In base, the acid is converted to its carboxylate salt. The reaction has little preparative value because the acyl chloride is nearly always prepared from the carboxylic acid itself.

Section 20.5
NUCLEOPHILIC ACYL SUBSTITUTION IN ACID ANHYDRIDES

Substitution of Anhydrides
Anhydrides may be readily converted to esters, amides, or acids, but not acid chlorides
One of the carbonyl groups ends up in an acid; the other in a carboxylic acid derivative of the nucleophile
Substitution of unsymmetrical anhydrides is…complicated
Acid or base catalysis may be involved

Substitution of Anhydrides-1
TABLE 20.2 Conversion of Acid Anhydrides to Other Carboxylic Acid Derivatives
Reaction (section) and comments
General equation and specific example
Reaction with alcohols (see Section 16.8) Acid anhydrides react with alcohols to form esters. The reaction may be carried out in the presence of pyridine or it may be catalyzed by acids. Only one acyl group of an anhydride is incorporated into the ester; the other becomes the acyl group of a carboxylic acid.
Reaction with ammonia and amines (see Section 20.12} Acid anhydrides react with ammonia and amines to form amides. Only one acyl group of an anhydride is incorporated into the amide; the other becomes the acyl group of the amine salt of the carboxylic acid .

Section 20.6
PHYSICAL PROPERTIES AND SOURCES OF ESTERS

Physical Properties of Esters
Esters are moderately polar and experience dipole-dipole forces
Boiling and melting points are lower than similar alcohols due to lack of hydrogen bonding
Esters can act as hydrogen bond acceptors ; they are somewhat water soluble

Sources of Esters
Esters occur in many contexts in nature
Flavor molecules
Signaling molecules
Esters of fatty acids ( triglycerides , phospholipids )

Preparation of Esters
Esters may be prepared from:
Carboxylic acids and alcohols, with acid catalyst
Acid chlorides and alcohols, with pyridine as catalyst
Acid anhydrides and alcohols, with acid or base catalyst
Ketones via Baeyer- Villiger oxidation

Section 20.7
REACTIONS OF ESTERS: A PREVIEW

Substitution of Esters
Esters react with amines and ammonia to form amides
Under acidic or basic conditions, esters react with water to form acids and alcohols

Section 20.8
ACID-CATALYZED ESTER HYDROLYSIS

Acid-catalyzed Hydrolysis of Esters
We have seen acid-catalyzed (Fischer) esterification previously. Recall that esterification is reversible!
The reverse reaction is called hydrolysis , since water cleaves an ester into an acid and an alcohol
A large excess of water is used to promote hydrolysis

Mechanism of Hydrolysis
Step 1: protonation of the carbonyl oxygen of the ester
Protonation at the other oxygen does not lead to a stabilized intermediate

Mechanism of Hydrolysis-1
Step 2: nucleophilic attack by water at the carbonyl carbon
Step 3: deprotonation of intermediate

Mechanism of Hydrolysis-2
Step 4: re-protonation at the “ alkoxy ” oxygen
Step 5: elimination of alcohol

Mechanism of Hydrolysis-3
Step 6: deprotonation of the protonated acid by water, regenerating acid catalyst

Section 20.9
ESTER HYDROLYSIS IN BASE: SAPONIFICATION

Saponification of Esters
Basic hydrolysis of esters ( saponification ) is irreversible due to the formation of carboxylate anions
A separate acidification step is used to isolate the carboxylic acid
Two mechanisms can be envisioned: S N 2 at the alkyl group ( CH 3 ) or nucleophilic acyl substitution

Evidence for Acyl Substitution
Labeled “ alkoxy ” oxygen ends up faithfully in the alcohol product, not the carboxylate
Configuration at the “alkoxy” carbon is retained, ruling out an S N 2 mechanism

Mechanism of Saponification
Step 1: nucleophilic addition of OH − to C=O
Rate-determining (slow) step

Mechanism of Saponification-1
Step 2: elimination of alkoxide
Step 3: proton transfers to give carboxylate and alcohol

Section 20.10
REACTIONS OF ESTERS WITH AMMONIA AND AMINES

Esters and Ammonia/Amines
Ammonia reacts with esters to give primary amides; aqueous ammonia may be used
Amines react in an analogous way; only primary and secondary amines may be used (tertiary amines have no H to lose)
Mechanism similar to the nucleophilic acyl substitutions we’ve seen previously

Section 20.11
REACTIONS OF ESTERS WITH GRIGNARD AND ORGANOLITHIUM REAGENTS AND LITHIUM ALUMINUM HYDRIDE

Double Addition of RM to Esters
Esters react with two equivalents of strong organometallic nucleophiles to give tertiary alcohols
This double addition mechanism involves nucleophilic acyl substitution followed by nucleophilic addition
The ketone intermediate formed after substitution is more reactive than the ester starting material

Reduction of Esters with LAH
Lithium aluminum hydride reacts similarly with esters—the products are primary alcohols via double addition
The intermediate aldehyde is more reactive than the ester starting material

Section 20.12
AMIDES

Physical Properties and Acidity
Amides are highly polar; secondary and primary amides can engage in strong hydrogen bonding
Amide N–H is about as acidic as water ( p K a = 16 ); doubly activated imides have p K a around 10

Synthesis of Amides
Amides can be prepared from RCOCl, anhydrides, and esters
In reactions with acid chlorides and anhydrides, two equivalents of amine are used to consume generated acid ( HCl or RCO 2 H )
Externally added base (e.g., hydroxide) can be used to reduce the amine to 1 equivalent

Synthesis of Amides-1
Amides can be prepared from RCOCl , anhydrides, and esters
Mechanisms all follow the basic pattern of nucleophilic acyl substitution

Section 20.13
HYDROLYSIS OF AMIDES

Hydrolysis of Amides
Amides undergo nucleophilic acyl substitution only under very harsh conditions (strong acid or base)
When water acts as the nucleophile, carboxylic acid and amine products are produced (nominally)
Under acidic conditions, the amine is protonated to form an ammonium salt
Under basic conditions, the acid is deprotonated to form a carboxylate salt

Amide Hydrolysis in Acid
The carbonyl oxygen is protonated to give an electrophilically activated amide
Protonation of the amide nitrogen does not give a stabilized cation
Nucleophilic addition, proton transfers, and elimination follow

Amide Hydrolysis in Base
Addition of water to C=O occurs first
Proton transfers and elimination of amine follow
Complete deprotonation of the carboxylic acid makes this mechanism irreversible

Amide Hydrolysis in Base-1
Addition of water to C=O occurs first
Proton transfers and elimination of amine follow
Complete deprotonation of the carboxylic acid makes this mechanism irreversible

Amide Hydrolysis in Base-2
Addition of water to C=O occurs first
Proton transfers and elimination of amine follow
Complete deprotonation of the carboxylic acid makes this mechanism irreversible

Amide Hydrolysis in Base-3
Addition of hydroxide to C=O occurs first
Proton transfers and elimination of amine follow
Complete deprotonation of the carboxylic acid makes this mechanism irreversible

Amide Hydrolysis in Base-4
Addition of hydroxide to C=O occurs first
Proton transfers and elimination of amine follow
Complete deprotonation of the carboxylic acid makes this mechanism irreversible

Amide Hydrolysis in Base-5
Addition of hydroxide to C=O occurs first
Proton transfers and elimination of amine follow
Complete deprotonation of the carboxylic acid makes this mechanism irreversible

Section 20.14
LACTAMS

Lactams
Lactams are cyclic amides, the nitrogen analogue of lactones
Most lactams are known by their common names
β -lactams have considerable importance as antibiotics

Section 20.15
PREPARATION OF NITRILES

Preparation of Nitriles
We have seen that nitriles can be prepared by two methods:
S N 2 of primary and secondary alkyl halides
Dehydration of amides offers another route to nitriles

Dehydration of Amides
Loss of water from a primary amide gives a nitrile
A dehydrating agent, which converts O to a good leaving group, is necessary; P 4 O 10 is typically used

Section 20.16
HYDROLYSIS OF NITRILES

Hydrolysis of Nitriles
The reverse of amide dehydration is nitrile hydrolysis. Further reaction gives a carboxylic acid
Harshly acidic or basic conditions are necessary, similar to amide hydrolysis
In fact, an amide is involved as an intermediate
The cyano carbon can act as an electrophile.

Section 20.17
ADDITION OF GRIGNARD REAGENTS TO NITRILES

From Nitriles to Ketones
Amide hydrolysis involves nucleophilic attack of water or hydroxide at the cyano carbon
Stronger nucleophiles (RLi, RMgX) can also react at this carbon, forming imines
Subsequent acidic hydrolysis gives ketones
This is another example of the electrophilic nature of the cyano carbon. Nitriles are analogous to ketones and aldehydes.

Section 20.18
SPECTROSCOPIC ANALYSIS OF CARBOXYLIC ACID DERIVATIVES

Infrared Spectroscopy
The position of the strong carbonyl band is highly sensitive to the groups bound to the carbonyl
Electron donation generally decreases ν C =O . Why?

1 H NMR Spectroscopy
The acyl and alkyl groups of esters can be distinguished by chemical shift
Alkyl group hydrogens bonded to the “alkoxy” oxygen are usually more deshielded than acyl group hydrogens
The N–H of primary and secondary amides is generally broad and found in the range δ 5 – 8

13 C NMR Spectroscopy
Carbonyl carbons are strongly deshielded and in the range δ 160 – 180
Carboxylic acid carbons are more shielded than ketone and aldehyde carbons
Nitrile carbons appear around δ 120

UV-VIS
The following values are typical for the n→π* absorption associated with the C=O group of carboxylic acid derivatives.

Mass Spectrometry
Acylium ion formation via loss of a heteroatomic radical is common in carboxylic acid derivatives
