Chapter 21
Enols and Enolates
© 2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education.

Enols and Enolates
The conjugate base of a carbonyl compound (via deprotonation at α -carbon ) is called an enolate
Protonation of an enolate at oxygen gives an enol
Enols and enolates are nucleophilic at carbon

Section 21.1
ALDEHYDE, KETONE, AND ESTER ENOLATES

The Acidic α -Carbon
Simple ketones are deprotonated to a significant degree by hydroxide (though not completely!)

Bases for Enolate Formation-1
Very strong bases such as lithium diisopropylamide (LDA) give the kinetic enolate selectively
LDA can also be used to form ester enolates quantitatively
Equilibrium mixture of R′CO 2 R + enolate can be generated using alkoxide base RO − (why not hydroxide?)

Section 21.2
THE ALDOL CONDENSATION

Aldol Addition
Equilibrium mixtures of carbonyl + enolate (via OH − or OR − ) don’t just sit there—the two react!
The enolate acts as a nucleophile and the carbonyl as an electrophile. Nucleophilic addition of the enolate to C=O
This reaction is called aldol addition
The entire process is reversible; aldol addition is not always spontaneous
Aldehyde enolate mixtures favor the aldol product; ketone enolate mixtures favor the separated ketone + enolate

Mechanism of Aldol Addition
Step 1: hydroxide base deprotonates the carbonyl compound, forming an enolate
Step 2: nucleophilic addition of enolate to C=O

Mechanism of Aldol Addition-1
Step 3: protonation of alkoxide intermediate by water
The entire process is reversible!
Ketones are relatively hindered and tend not to give aldol products unless forced by a full equivalent of enolate (see next section )

Aldol Condensation
Dehydration of an alcohol in base ?! For β -hydroxy carbonyls, yes!

Section 21.3
Mixed and Directed Aldol Reactions

Mixed Aldol Reactions
We cannot simply mix two different carbonyl compounds, add base, and hope to obtain our desired aldol product
Synthetically useful mixed aldol reactions use a non- enolizable electrophile
Formaldehyde
Aromatic aldehydes, diarylketones, etc. (lacking α - hydrogens )

Directed Aldol Reactions
Alternatively, in directed aldol reactions an enolate is formed quantitatively first, then the electrophile is added
Note the use of the strong base LDA—the kinetic enolate forms selectively in step 1

Biochemical Aldol Reactions
Enzyme-catalyzed aldol reactions are very common
Enzymes use hyper-selective pockets to bind substrates and perform mixed aldol reactions selectively
Although oxaloacetic acid is more acidic than acetyl CoA, it is not deprotonated and acts as the electrophile in citrate synthase.

Acylation of Enolates: The Claisen and Related Condensations
Section 21.4
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Claisen Condensation
The aldol reaction is a nucleophilic addition to C=O ; enolates can also engage in nucleophilic substitution
In the Claisen condensation , two equivalents of ester react to form a β - keto ester via substitution
One nucleophilic ester molecule is deprotonated and reacts with another electrophilic ester molecule
Why is this process thermodynamically favorable?

Mechanism of Claisen Condensation
Step 1: deprotonation by base to form an enolate
Step 2: nucleophilic addition to C=O

Mechanism of Claisen Condensation-1
Step 3: elimination of alkoxide
All steps have been reversible to this point (!)
What drives this reaction forward?

Mechanism of Claisen Condensation-2
Step 3: elimination of alkoxide
All steps have been reversible to this point (!)
What drives this reaction forward? An acid-base process

Mechanism of Claisen Condensation-3
Step 4: irreversible deprotonation of β - keto ester
Acid is added in workup to isolate the β - keto ester

Riffs on the Claisen Theme
The intramolecular Claisen condensation is called the Dieckmann condensation
Mixed Claisen condensations involve a non- enolizable electrophilic ester and a different nucleophilic ester
Ketones can be used as the source of the enolate rather than esters; β - keto ketones ( β - dicarbonyls ) are the products

ALKYLATION OF ENOLATES: THE ACETOACETIC ESTER AND MALONIC ESTER SYNTHESES
Section 21.5

Problems with Enolate Alkylation
Enolates of simple ketones and aldehydes undergo alkylation only with 1 degree and 2 degrees alkyl halides
Mixtures of different α -alkylated products can result, even from pre-formed enolates
Polyalkylation can occur
Aldol addition is an important competitive side reaction

β - dicarbonyl compounds are alkylated regioselectively at the position between the carbonyl groups
Two electron-withdrawing groups influence the central hydrogens, increasing their acidity
The conjugate base is stabilized by charge delocalization

Acetoacetic and Malonic Ester Syntheses
Acetoacetic and malonic esters contain highly acidic C–H positioned between two carbonyl groups
The ester can be removed in a later step via hydrolysis and decarboxylation
The three step method of alkylation - hydrolysis - decarboxylation enables regioselective monoalkylation of enolates

Acetoacetic and Malonic Ester Syntheses-1
Acetoacetic and malonic esters contain highly acidic C–H positioned between two carbonyl groups
The ester can be removed in a later step via hydrolysis and decarboxylation
The three step method of alkylation - hydrolysis - decarboxylation enables regioselective monoalkylation of enolates

Applications
Two successive alkylations of the ester lead to dialkylated products
α , ω - dihalides react twice to give cycloalkyl acids

ENOL CONTENT AND ENOLIZATION
Section 21.6

Keto -enol Equilibrium
Carbonyls and enols are tautomers —constitutional isomers differing in the position of hydrogen
Keto-enol tautomerization is an important equilibrium for carbonyl compounds
K eq for keto-enol equilibrium can vary widely
For simple aldehydes and ketones, the keto form dominates
When the enol form is particularly stabilized by conjugation, the enol form dominates
The mechanism of tautomerization involves sequential protonation and deprotonation (or vice versa ), not concerted intramolecular proton transfer!

Keto -enol Equilibrium-1
Carbonyls and enols are tautomers —constitutional isomers differing in the position of hydrogen
Keto-enol tautomerization is an important equilibrium for carbonyl compounds
K eq for keto-enol equilibrium can vary widely

Mechanism of Tautomerization
Mechanism involves sequential protonation and deprotonation (or vice versa ), not concerted intramolecular proton transfer!
Under acid catalysis, protonation at O occurs first, followed by deprotonation at C

Mechanism of Tautomerization-1
Mechanism involves sequential protonation and deprotonation (or vice versa ), not concerted intramolecular proton transfer!
Under base catalysis, deprotonation at C occurs first (forming an enolate!), followed by deprotonation at O

THE HALOFORM REACTION
Section 21.7
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Halogenation in Basic Solution
In acidic solution, ketones undergo halogenation with X 2 via an enol intermediate
In basic solution, a similar reaction of enolates occurs
The product α - haloketone is more acidic than the starting material—trihalogenation occurs!

The Haloform Reaction
− CX 3 is not a bad leaving group, and in the presence of OH − it can be displaced to form a carboxylate and haloform HCX 3
The complete transformation from methyl ketone to carboxylic acid is called the haloform reaction
The ketone must have only one enolizable position, the methyl group

Mechanism
Step 1: deprotonation generates an enolate
Step 2: S N 2 at X–X, forging the first C–X bond

Mechanism-1
Steps 3 – 6: steps 1 and 2 repeat twice more, to introduce two additional bromines at the α carbon.
Step 7: nucleophilic addition of hydroxide to C=O

Mechanism-2
Step 8: elimination of CX 3 −
Step 9: proton transfer from the acid to CX 3 −

Mechanism-3
Step 9: proton transfer from the acid to CX 3 −

SOME CHEMICAL AND STEREOCHEMICAL CONSEQUENCES OF ENOLIZATION
Section 21.8

Substitution of Deuterium
Substitution of deuterium for hydrogen at the α-carbon atom of an aldehyde or a ketone is a convenient way to introduce an isotopic label into a molecule and is readily carried out by treating the carbonyl compound with deuterium oxide (D 2 O) and base.

Substitution of Deuterium-1
Only the α hydrogens are replaced by deuterium in this reaction. The key intermediate is the enolate ion formed by proton abstraction from the α-carbon atom of cyclopentanone.
Transfer of deuterium from the solvent D 2 O to the enolate gives cyclopentanone containing a deuterium atom in place of one of the hydrogens at the α carbon.

Substitution of Deuterium-2
Only the α hydrogens are replaced by deuterium in this reaction. The key intermediate is the enolate ion formed by proton abstraction from the α-carbon atom of cyclopentanone.
Transfer of deuterium from the solvent D 2 O to the enolate gives cyclopentanone containing a deuterium atom in place of one of the hydrogens at the α carbon.

Stereochemical Concerns
When the α -carbon is a chirality center, deprotonation “flattens” it and destroys any enantioenrichment (optical activity)
Chiral, optically active ketones are racemized via enol formation

CONJUGATION EFFECTS IN α , β -UNSATURATED ALDEHYDES AND KETONES
Section 21.9

α , β -unsaturated carbonyls are the products of aldol condensation
Unlike unconjugated carbonyls, they have two electrophilic carbons
Nucleophilic addition may occur to either electrophilic carbon.
Note the strongly deshielded nature of the β -carbon in trans -2-butenal.

1,2- versus 1,4-Addition
Strong nucleophiles such as RLi and RMgX add at the 2-position of the O=C–C=C conjugated system (the carbonyl carbon)
This is termed 1,2-addition
Weaker nucleophiles add reversibly to the 2-position, forming the more stable 1,4-addition product over time

The Michael Reaction
“Soft” enolates of β - dicarbonyls react via 1,4-addition in a process called the Michael reaction
This is a very important C–C bond forming reaction!

Robinson Annulation
Following up a Michael reaction with an intramolecular aldol condensation gives a cyclic product
This is called the Robinson annulation

Conjugate Addition of Dialkylcuprates
Dialkylcuprates are “soft” nucleophiles that add at the 4-position of conjugated carbonyl compounds

Chapter 22
Amines
©Evgeniy Ivanao/Getty Image: ©Bill grove/Getty image
© 2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education.

Amines
Amines are organic derivatives of ammonia
They have alkyl or aryl groups (or H) bound to nitrogen
The basic lone pair on nitrogen dictates much of the reactivity of amines

Section 22.1
AMINE NOMENCLATURE

Classifying Amines
Amines are classified according to their degree of substitution at nitrogen
Primary amines have one alkyl group, secondary amines have two, and tertiary amines have three

Naming Amines
Primary amines may be named as alkylamines or alkanamines
Secondary and tertiary amines are named as N -substituted derivatives of primary amines
Aromatic amines are called anilines

Priority of −𝐍𝐇 𝟐
The − NH 2 group has low priority because the atomic number of N is less than O
Lower priority than alcohols
Lower priority than C=O
Amines with other functional groups are rarely named as amines

Section 22.2
STRUCTURE AND BONDING

Alkylamines
N in alkylamines is sp 3 hybridized
The amine lone pair is moderately basic
Slightly nonideal bond angles: H–N–H 106 degrees; C–N–H 112 degrees

Arylamines
Somewhat shallower bond angles facilitate overlap between the lone pair orbital and adjacent π orbitals
Resonance structures illustrate delocalization of N lone pair
N lone pair is much less basic

Amides
N lone pair in amides experiences strong delocalization into C=O
Trigonal planar structure and sp 2 hybridization at N
Negligible basicity

Section 22.3
PHYSICAL PROPERTIES

Physical Properties of Amines
Polarity of amines is intermediate between alkanes and alcohols—intermediate BPs and MPs
Hydrogen bonding is important in 1 degree and 2 degrees amines !

Substituted Amines
Among isomeric amines, 1 degree amines have the strongest hydrogen bonds and highest BPs
Note the large jump down from 2 degrees to 3 degrees amines

Section 22.4
BASICITY OF AMINES

Principles of Acid-base Equilibria
Recall the relation between the strength of a base and its conjugate acid:
The stronger the base, the weaker the conjugate acid
Alkylamines are moderately strong bases; their conjugate acids have relatively high p K a ’s (9 - 11)
Any acid with p K a lower than 10 will tend to protonate an amine at equilibrium

Principles of Acid-base Equilibria-1
Recall the relation between the strength of a base and its conjugate acid:
The stronger the base, the weaker the conjugate acid
Alkylamines are moderately strong bases; their conjugate acids have relatively high p K a ’s (9 - 11)
Any base whose conjugate acid has p K a greater than 10 will tend to deprotonate ammonium at equilibrium
Survey of Amine Basicity
Survey of Amine Basicity-1

The Aniline Anomaly
Anilines are much less basic than alkylamines!
In other words, aniliniums are much stronger acids
Additional aromatic rings bound to N make anilines even less basic
Electron-withdrawing groups bound to the aromatic ring—especially ortho or para —have the same effect

The Aniline Anomaly-1
Draw resonance structures to illustrate the effect of para − NO 2 on the basicity of N

Other N-containing Compounds
Consider the hybridization of N to judge basicity
Pyridine is more basic than piperidine
sp 2 lone pair is less basic than sp 3 lone pair
Imidazole is an interesting case study of basicity
Which N is more basic? Why?
Why is imidazole a much stronger base than pyridine ?

Section 22.5
TETRAALKYLAMMONIUM SALTS AS PHASE-TRANSFER CATALYSTS

The Biphasic Problem
Imagine you wanted to carry out the following reaction :
BuBr is a liquid, but NaCN is not soluble in it! Painfully slow reaction rate
How can we encourage the reactants to come into contact and dissolve one another ?

An Amphiphilic Solution
Imagine you wanted to carry out the following reaction:
BuBr is a liquid, but NaCN is not soluble in it! Painfully slow reaction rate
An amphiphilic molecule can be used to pull Na + and CN − into solution
Quaternary ammonium salts with large alkyl groups can play the role nicely!
Since they transfer species between phases, they are called phase transfer catalysts
Phase Transfer Catalysis

Section 22.6
REACTIONS THAT LEAD TO AMINES: A REVIEW AND A PREVIEW

Amine-forming Reactions
Reactions that form C–N bonds
Nitrogen as nucleophile: S N 2 , nucleophilic addition, nucleophilic acyl substitution
Nitrogen as electrophile: electrophilic aromatic substitution (nitration )
Amine-forming Reactions-1
Amine-forming Reactions-2
Amine-forming Reactions-3

Section 22.7
PREPARATION OF AMINES BY ALKYLATION OF AMMONIA
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Issues with Alkylation of Ammonia
Ammonia is a good nucleophile, and alkylation with alkyl halides is possible in principle :
Polyalkylation is a critical problem, even when an excess of NH 3 is used
Not only is the yield negatively affected; the mono- and polyalklated products are also difficult to separate .

Section 22.8
THE GABRIEL SYNTHESIS OF PRIMARY ALKYLAMINES

“Masked” Ammonia for 𝐑𝐍𝐇 𝟐 Synthesis
How can we circumvent the problems with alkylation of ammonia?
Use an ammonia surrogate R 2 NH that only undergoes monoalkylation to R 2 NR′
“ R 2 ” acts like a protecting group : removal affords H 2 NR′ ( a primary amine !)
The potassium salt of phthalimide is used for this purpose in the Gabriel synthesis

Gabriel Synthesis
S N 2 with potassium phthalimide gives a tertiary imide
The two carbonyl groups can be replaced with –H using aqueous acid, aqueous base, or hydrazine ( H 2 NNH 2 )

Section 22.9
PREPARATION OF AMINES BY REDUCTION

Reduction to Form Amines
In the Gabriel synthesis, an imide is reduced to RNH 2
Other N-containing functional groups can be reduced to amines
For example, azides can be reduced to amines with LAH or catalytic hydrogenation

Reduction to Form Amines-1
In the Gabriel synthesis, an imide is reduced to RNH 2
Other N-containing functional groups can be reduced to amines
Nitriles can be similarly reduced to amines with LAH or catalytic hydrogenation

Reduction of Nitro Groups
The nitro group ( − NO 2 ) is readily reduced using a variety of methods
Hydrogenation
Acidic iron(0), then base

Reduction of Nitro Groups-1
The nitro group ( − NO 2 ) is readily reduced using a variety of methods
Hydrogenation
Acidic tin(0), then base

Reduction of Amides
Azides and nitro compounds are reduced to primary amines…what about 2 degrees and 3 degrees amines?
Amides can be reduced with LAH to 1 degree, 2 degrees , or 3 degrees amines

Section 22.10
REDUCTIVE AMINATION

Reductive Amination
Imines contain a C=N double bond. Reduction of imines can furnish amines!
Two steps (imine formation; reduction) convert carbonyls to amines
Reduction can be accomplished using hydrogenation
What is remarkable about the second example ?

Reductive Amination-1
A moderately reactive hydride salt, sodium cyanoborohydride ( NaBH 3 CN ) can be used for reduction

Section 22.11
REACTIONS OF AMINES: A REVIEW AND A PREVIEW

Reactions of Amines
The critical feature of amines is the basic/nucleophilic lone pair on nitrogen
In molecules where nitrogen is conjugated with a π system (e.g., arylamines, amides), the Lewis basicity of nitrogen can be transferred to other atoms
Reactions of Amines-1
Reactions of Amines-2

Section 22.12
REACTION OF AMINES WITH ALKYL HALIDES

𝐒 𝐍 𝟐 of Amines and Alkyl Halides
Amines react with primary RX to form substituted amines
Polyalkylation (potentially all the way to R 4 N + ) can be a problematic issue
Excess amine is used to minimize odds of polyalkylation

Quaternary Ammonium Salts
Quaternary ammonium halides ( R 4 N + X − ) are usually generated via deliberate polyalkylation with the cheapest and smallest alkyl halide ( CH 3 I )
Ammonium salts can be further elaborated—consider the leaving group ability of − N(CH 3 ) 3 + !

Section 22.13
THE HOFMANN ELIMINATION
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Hofmann Elimination
Quaternary ammonium hydroxides can be prepared via treatment of ammonium halides with aqueous Ag 2 O
On heating, E2 elimination occurs to give an alkene
This reaction is called the Hofmann elimination
What makes this better than E2 of alkyl halides? The ammonium was probably prepared from an alkyl halide anyway…

Unique Regioselectivity
Regioselectivity of Hofmann elimination is opposite Zaitsev’s rule!
The least substituted carbon is deprotonated preferentially
A consequence of the bulkiness of the leaving group

A Closer Look
E2 anti elimination of a secondary or tertiary C–H necessarily involves a gauche interaction with the LG
E2 of a primary C–H does not!

Section 22.14
ELECTROPHILIC AROMATIC SUBSTITUTION IN ARYLAMINES

Reactivity of Anilines
Recall that the − NR 2 group is strongly electron donating and is an ortho , para director
− NR 2 is such a good donating group that protonation and oxidation are problematic under electrophilic aromatic substitution conditions
The amino nitrogen must be protected with an electron-withdrawing group ( C=O !)
Nucleophilic acyl substitution
Electrophilic aromatic substitution of N - acetylarylamines proceeds without issue .

Example: Nitration of Anilines
Sequence of protect - substitute - deprotect
Mononitration occurs with no problematic side products

Example: Halogenation
Sequence of protect - substitute - deprotect
What protection step preceded this reaction?
Direct halogenation of anilines gives trihaloarene products; protection of N is necessary

Example: Friedel-Crafts Reactions
Sequence of protect - substitute - deprotect
Anilines coordinate the Lewis acid ( AlCl 3 ) strongly at N, preventing reaction
What protection step preceded this reaction?

Section 22.15
NITROSATION OF ALKYLAMINES

Nitrosation of 2 degrees Amines
Nitrite ( NO 2 − ) in acid decomposes to form NO + , a useful electrophile called the nitrosyl cation
Nucleophilic attack by a 2 degrees amino nitrogen forges an N–N bond and leads to a nitrosamine

Nitrosation of 1 degree Amines
Primary amines–alkyl or aryl—eliminate water to form diazonium salts

Aryldiazonium Salts
Alkyldiazoniums decompose readily to form carbocations and products thereof (alcohols, alkenes )
Aryldiazonium salts are much more stable, as loss of N 2 would lead to an unstable aryl cation
Electrophilic substitution of aryldiazonium salts is a very important class of reactions; more in the next section

Section 22.16
NITROSATION OF ARYLAMINES

Nitrosation of Arylamines
Primary arylamines form aryldiazoniums when combined with acidified NO 2 − , just like 1 degree alkylamines
Secondary arylamines give N - nitroso anilines
Tertiary amines undergo electrophilic substitution at the para position !
Punch line: only 1 degree amines give diazonium salts under nitrosation conditions.

Transformations of Aryldiazoniums
View the − N 2 + group as a pseudohalide—it’s a glorified leaving group !

Nitrosation of Primary Anilines
Nitrosation converts the electron-donating amino group into an electron-withdrawing group
Product is stable at 0 − 5 degrees C for a reasonable period of time

Section 22.17
SYNTHETIC TRANSFORMATIONS OF ARYLDIAZONIUM SALTS

Electrophilic Substitution of 𝐀𝐫𝐍 𝟐 +
In general, a nucleophile displaces N 2 irreversibly in reactions of aryldiazoniums
However, an aryl cation is often involved (like S N 1 )
For example, heating in water forms phenols
Treatment with potassium iodide forms aryl iodies

Schiemann Reaction
Instead of an alkali metal fluoride, HBF 4 is used to convert the diazonium to its fluoroborate salt
Delivery of fluoride releases N 2 and BF 3

Copper(I) Bromide and Chloride
Copper(I) salts are most effective for bromination and chlorination (alkali metal halides less so )

Sandmeyer Reactions
Reactions involving CuX salts (where X − is the nucleophile and RN 2 + is the electrophile) are called Sandmeyer reactions

Reductive Deamination
Replacement of − N 2 + with “hydride” (note quotes) gives unsubstituted derivatives
These are radical processes involving hydrogen-atom abstraction and aryl radicals
Now, we can use a nitro or amine group to direct substitution, then obliterate it !

Section 22.18
AZO COUPLING

Azo Coupling
The N–N bond in diazoniums is a polarized π bond; nucleophilic addition can take place!
Electron-rich arenes add across the N–N bond in diazoniums, forming azo compounds after deprotonation
Azo compounds are beautifully colored and were used as dyes in the mid-1800’s (and synthesized using this method !)

Section 22.19
SPECTROSCOPIC ANALYSIS OF AMINES

Infrared Spectroscopy
Primary amines give two peaks in the range 3500 - 3000 cm − 1 due to N–H stretches
Secondary amines give a single peak in this region
Tertiary amines are transparent in this region, but look out for the C–N stretch

𝟏 𝐇 NMR Spectroscopy-1
Amino protons H–N are somewhat more shielded than O–H protons; the same is true for H – C – NR 2

𝟏 𝐇 NMR Spectroscopy
Amino protons H–N are somewhat more shielded than O–H protons; the same is true for H – C – NR 2

Mass Spectrometry
Note that compounds with one N will have odd molecular weight—a relatively rare circumstance
N is great at stabilizing adjacent carbocations. Fragmentation will reflect this fact

Chapter 23
Carbohydrates
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education.

Carbohydrates
Once described as “hydrates of carbon” because of their general formula C n (H 2 O) m
Now best conceived as polyhydroxylated aldehydes or ketones
Used biologically for cell signaling, structure, and other applications
We will study the structure and reactivity of carbohydrates

CLASSIFICATION OF CARBOHYDRATES
Section 23.1

Classes of Carbohydrates: Length
Monosaccharides (sugars) are not cleaved into smaller carbohydrates under acidic conditions
Disaccharides contain two monosaccharide units
Oligosaccharides contain a few monosaccharide units, chained together
Polysaccharides contain many monosaccharide units chained together; they are polymers

Classes of Carbohydrates: Structure
Aldoses contain an aldehyde group chained to
– CH(OH) – and – CH 2 OH groups
Ketoses contain an internal ketone, surrounded by
– CH(OH) – and – CH 2 OH groups
Prefix indicates the length of the carbon chain of monosaccharides

FISCHER PROJECTIONS AND D,L NOTATION
Section 23.2

Fischer Projections
Carbohydrates with the same molecular formula can differ in stereochemistry
By convention (and because of their many stereocenters), carbohydrates are represented as Fischer projections
A “top-down” view in which:
Horizontal bonds are pointing towards you
Vertical bonds are pointing away from you

Glyceraldehyde & d - and l -Sugars
The triose glyceraldehyde is one of the simplest monosaccharides
It has a single stereocenter which can be ( R ) or ( S )
By convention, the ( R )-isomer is called d -glyceraldehyde
The ( S )-isomer is called l -glyceraldehyde

THE ALDOTETROSES
Section 23.3

Aldotetroses
The aldotetroses contain four carbons, –CHO, and two stereocenters
All of these compounds are 2,3,4-trihydroxybutanal, but differ in stereochemistry
d - and l - erythrose have hydroxyl groups on the same side of the Fischer projection:

Aldotetroses-1
The aldotetroses contain four carbons, –CHO, and two stereocenters
All of these compounds are 2,3,4-trihydroxybutanal, but differ in stereochemistry
d - and l - threose have hydroxyl groups on opposite sides of the Fischer projection:
Note that diastereomeric sugars have different names; enantiomeric sugars are d/l .

ALDOPENTOSES AND ALDOHEXOSES
Section 23.4

Aldopentoses
The aldopentoses contain five carbons, –CHO, and three stereocenters ( 2 3 = 8 stereoisomers total)
The l - aldopentoses are enantiomeric to these!

Aldohexoses
The aldohexoses contain six carbons, –CHO, and four stereocenters ( 2 4 = 16 stereoisomers total)
Very common and well known sugars in this group
The l - aldohexoses are enantiomeric to these!

Aldohexoses-1
The aldohexoses contain six carbons, –CHO, and four stereocenters ( 2 4 = 16 stereoisomers total)
Very common and well known sugars in this group
The l - aldohexoses are enantiomeric to these!

A MNEMONIC FOR CARBOHYDRATE CONFIGURATIONS
Section 23.5

A Mnemonic for the Aldohexoses
All altruists gladly make gum in gallon tanks.
How on earth can we recall the names and structures of the aldohexoses?
Write eight blank Fischer projections
Add C-5 hydroxyl to the right, guaranteeing d
Write first four C-4 hydroxyls to the right and next four to the left (RRRRLLLL)
Write the C-3 hydroxyls as RRLLRRLL
Write the C-2 hydroxyls as RLRLRLRL
Use the mnemonic:

A Mnemonic for the Aldohexoses-1
How on earth can we recall the names and structures of the aldohexoses?
Write eight blank Fischer projections
Add C-5 hydroxyl to the right, guaranteeing d
Write first four C-4 hydroxyls to the right and next four to the left (RRRRLLLL)
Write the C-3 hydroxyls as RRLLRRLL
Write the C-2 hydroxyls as RLRLRLRL
Use the mnemonic:
All altruists gladly make gum in gallon tanks.
allose, altrose, glucose, mannose, gulose, idose, galactose, talose!

CYCLIC FORMS OF CARBOHYDRATES: FURANOSE FORMS
Section 23.6

Cyclization of Aldoses
Sugars include both C=O and –OH, which can react with one another to form acetals or hemiacetals!
Intramolecular hemiacetal formation yields a cyclic isomer of the monosaccharide
Five-membered rings are called furanoses ; six-membered rings are pyranoses
The former carbonyl carbon is called the anomeric carbon

Cyclization of Aldoses-1
Carefully convert open-chain Fischer projections into eclipsed line-angle forms primed for cyclization
Acid- or base-catalyzed addition of –OH across C=O

Cyclization of Aldoses-2
Whether a furanose or pyranose forms depends on the hydroxyl group involved

Stereochemistry of Cyclization
Cyclization generates a new stereocenter (the anomeric carbon) with two possible configurations
The two possible products are diastereomers

Haworth Projections
Place the anomeric carbon on the right and the cyclized oxygen in the back
This view is called a Haworth projection
When the drawing is in this orientation, the structure with the new hydroxyl up is alpha ( α ) and down is beta ( β ) for d -sugars; opposite for l -sugars

Furanose Forms of Higher Aldoses
Hexopyranoses come from cyclization of the terminal –OH group, which is relatively easy to deal with
Hexofuranoses come from cyclization of an internal –OH group! An additional mental rotation is necessary

Furanose Forms of Higher Aldoses-1
Cyclization generates α and β isomers
Note that the C-4 configuration is identical in both diastereomeric products

CYCLIC FORMS OF CARBOHYDRATES: PYRANOSE FORMS
Section 23.7

Pyranose Forms
Aldohexoses readily form six-membered pyranoses
The terminal hydroxyl group is involved in cyclization
Again, two diastereomers are possible
Open-chain Form to Pyranose

Conformations of Pyranoses
Pyranoses look like (heavily) substituted cyclohexanes, with an oxygen in place of a carbon
Pyranoses also adopt chair-like conformations! Chair with more equatorial groups is favored
Which diastereomer would you expect to be favored?
You may be surprised!

Distribution of Isomers
Cyclic forms contain more σ bonds and tend to be favored over the open-chain form
Pyranoses in particular (six membered!) are favored

Lipids
©Evgeniy Ivanao/Getty Image: ©Bill grove/Getty image
© 2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education.

Lipids
Lipids are defined by their greater solubility in nonpolar solvents
Carbon-rich structures derived ultimately from glucose
Acetate, derived from pyruvate, is a key player in the biosynthesis of lipids

Section 24.1
ACETYL COENZYME A

Acetyl Coenzyme A
Coenzyme A acts as a carrier of an electrophilic acyl group throughout biosynthesis
Pyruvate is converted to acetyl coenzyme A via decarboxylation and oxidation
Nucleophilic addition, acyl substitution, and reactions at the α -carbon are all possible for acetyl CoA
Reactions of Acetyl CoA

Section 24.2
FATS, OILS, AND FATTY ACIDS

Fats and Oils: Triacylglycerols
Fats (solid) and oils (liquid) are an important class of lipids
They are triesters of glycerol, with very long hydrocarbon-containing acyl groups
Highly efficient for energy storage (ester hydrolysis is very exergonic )

Examples of Triacylglycerols
Note the long acyl groups !
Hydrogenation straightens out a “kink” in the chain, resulting in better packing and higher mp .

Fatty Acids
The long acyl groups in TAGs are derived from fatty acids , carboxylic acids with long hydrocarbon chains

Fatty Acids-1
May contain only C–C single bonds ( saturated ) or a few cis C=C double bonds ( unsaturated )

Fatty Acids-2
Most fatty acids contain an even number of carbons and unbranched chains

Trans Fats
Trans fats contain trans double bonds!
Partial hydrogenation can be accompanied by isomerization from cis to trans
Similar to saturated fats in their cholesterol-raising effect

Fatty Acids in Other Contexts
Fatty acids are mostly found in triacylglycerols
However, they can be found in other contexts too (bound to other heteroatoms )

Section 24.3
FATTY ACID BIOSYNTHESIS

Fatty Acid Biosynthesis
After some preparatory chemistry, a repeated series of nucleophilic acyl substitutions occurs
Four key molecules involved
Malonyl-ACP serves as an α -carbon nucleophile towards the growing acyl-ACP, which serves as an electrophile
Catalyzed by mammalian fatty acid synthase (mFAS )

Generation of Acyl-ACP Compounds
Malonyl coenzyme A is generated from bicarbonate and acetyl coenzyme A, via phosphorylation
Acyl substitution leads to replacement of –S(CoA ) with – S(ACP )

Generation of Acetoacetyl-ACP
Decarboxylation of malonyl-ACP gives a good nucleophile, which substitutes at C=O of acetyl-ACP
This is really a two-step process !

Generation of Saturated Acyl-ACP
Reduction of the “hanging” carbonyl group, dehydration, and reduction of C=C give a saturated acyl-ACP
The butanoyl-ACP (electrophile) then migrates to react with another malonyl-ACP (nucleophile)
Reduction-dehydration-reduction occurs again

Later “Pruning” Steps
Hydrolysis of the acyl-ACP generates the fatty acid
Other steps are possible:
Further chain extension
Addition of double bonds via radical oxidation and dehydration
Acyl transfer to form esters or amides
Acyl transfer may involve the intermediacy of a phospholipid (for example, in TAG synthesis )

Section 24.4
PHOSPHOLIPIDS

Phospholipids in TAG Biosynthesis
The species acylated in TAG biosynthesis is not glycerol itself, but glycerol phosphate

Phospholipids are Amphiphilic
Phospholipids contain a polar region and a nonpolar region
The polar regions point outward toward polar solvent; the nonpolar regions cluster in the center
The result is a lipid bilayer , a membrane separating two sides
The Fluid Mosaic Model

Section 24.5
WAXES

Waxes
Waxes are hydrophobic solids that form the outer coatings of many types of plants and animals
Commonly composed of fatty acid esters
For example, beeswax contains the highly hydrophobic ester triacontyl hexadecanoate
Earwax has similar properties

Section 24.6
PROSTAGLANDINS

Prostaglandins
Lipid fraction of semen contains compounds that exert profound influences on smooth muscle
The compounds responsible, prostaglandins , were later shown to be ubiquitous in animal tissues
Long-chain hydrocarbons containing a carboxylic acid and cyclopentane ring

Biosynthesis of Prostaglandins
Animals obtain the key precursor, linoleic acid , from plants
Chain extension and dehydrogenation give arachidonic acid
Cyclization and oxidation leads to PGG 2

Biosynthesis of Prostaglandins-1
Animals obtain the key precursor, linoleic acid , from plants
Chain extension and dehydrogenation give arachidonic acid
Other enzymes convert PGH2 to a variety of products
For example, one enzyme catalyzes O–O bond cleavage

Other Icosanoids
The C 20 carboxylic acid is icosanoic acid, and prostaglandins are known as icosanoids
Thromboxanes are a second class of icosanoids
Prostacyclins are a third class of icosanoids

Other Icosanoids-1
The C 20 carboxylic acid is icosanoic acid, and prostaglandins are known as icosanoids
Leukotrienes are not derived from PGH 2 , but come directly from arachidonic acid

Section 24.7
TERPENES: THE ISOPRENE RULE

Terpenes
Many essential oils are in a class of organic compounds called terpenes
Terpenes are characterized by one or more copies of the isoprene unit , C–C(CH 3 )–C–C
The head is the terminal carbon nearer the CH 3 group; the tail is the other terminal carbon
The idea that terpenes are composed of isoprene units is called the isoprene rule .
Terpenes may be hydrocarbons or functionalized organic compounds .
Classification of Terpenes

Other Examples of Terpenes
Terpenes may be functionalized or cyclic
Tail-to-tail linkages are observed as well
Isoprene does not occur naturally; how do biological organisms synthesize terpenes? What is the biological isoprene unit ?

Section 24.8
ISOPENTENYL DIPHOSPHATE: THE BIOLOGICAL ISOPRENE UNIT

The Biological Isoprene Unit
Acetate is the ultimate source of the isoprenoid unit
Note that elimination from isopentenyl diphosphate would give isoprene—this is nature’s isoprene unit!
Diphosphate is a decent leaving group
Isomerization gives an allylic diphosphate, with two electrophilic carbons !

Section 24.9
CARBON–CARBON BOND FORMATION IN TERPENE BIOSYNTHESIS

Biosynthesis of Terpenes
Dimethylallyl diphosphate (DMADP) and isopentenyl diphosphate (IPDP) couple with one another to give a precursor to diterpenes
IPDP acts as nucleophile and DMADP acts as electrophile
The cation can undergo deprotonation to give a diene :

Biosynthesis of Terpenes-1
A second coupling can occur to give a precursor to triterpenes
Elimination and hydrolysis give farnesyl diphosphate and farnesol

Cyclization of Prenyl Diphosphates
Prenyl diphosphates commonly react with π -nucleophiles
Intramolecular reactions of this type lead to cyclic terpenes

Section 24.10
THE PATHWAY FROM ACETATE TO ISOPENTENYL DIPHOSPHATE

From Acetate to Mevalonate
Mevalonate is the biochemical precursor to isopentenyl diphosphate :
The six-carbon mevalonate is synthesized from three acetates
Step 1: decarboxylative Claisen (just like fatty acid synthesis !)

From Acetate to Mevalonate-1
Step 2: aldol addition of acetyl CoA (nucleophile) across the “hanging” C=O of acetoacetyl CoA (electrophile)
Step 3: hydrolysis and reduction (enzymatic, not LAH!)

How Do We Know?
Acetate labeled with 13 C is fed to plants, and the distribution of 13 C in the products is determined

Section 24.11
STEROIDS: CHOLESTEROL

Cholesterol and Steroids
Cholesterol (“bile solid”) is the prototypical steroid
Most important and abundant steroid
Highly complex structure
Plaque responsible for atherosclerosis
Steroids are characterized by the ABCD ring system shown in (b ).
Remarkably, we can biosynthesize cholesterol from acetate !

Biosynthesis of Cholesterol
A key intermediate in the biosynthesis of cholesterol is squalene
Epoxidation gives an intermediate poised for cyclization

Biosynthesis of Cholesterol-1
Ring expansion then gives a cyclic carbocation
Further cyclization gives the prosterol cation

Biosynthesis of Cholesterol-2
Deprotonation accompanied by rearrangement gives lanosterol
Lanosterol is converted to cholesterol through a series of enzyme-catalyzed reactions

Section 24.12
VITAMIN D

Vitamin 𝐃 𝟑
7-Dehydrocholesterol undergoes an interesting rearrangement to give Vitamin D3
Sunlight is necessary to cause this rearrangement
Insufficient time in the sun leads to vitamin D 3 deficiency!
Vitamin D 3 is important for proper bone growth

Ergosterol and Vitamin 𝐃 𝟐
Ergosterol undergoes a similar rearrangement when irradiated with light
The product is vitamin D 2 , another important vitamin for bone growth

BILE ACIDS

Bile Acids
A significant amount of cholesterol is converted to bile acids
Emulsifying agents that aid the digestion of fats
Note that one face of the molecule is polar and the other nonpolar!

Section 24.14
CORTICOSTEROIDS

Corticosteroids: Adrenal Hormones
The cortex of the adrenal gland produces several derivatives of cholesterol called corticosteroids
Cortisone and cortisol differ in the oxidation state of a central carbon
Involved in many physiological functions:
Controlling electrolyte levels in body fluids
Allergic response
Metabolism of carbohydrates

Section 24.15
SEX HORMONES

Sex Hormones
Derivatives of cholesterol are secreted by the sex glands and act as hormones in sexual and reproductive development
These sex hormones are found in extremely minute concentrations in the body

Oral Contraception
Progesterone suppresses ovulation during certain stages of the menstrual cycle and pregnancy
A synthetic derivative forms the basis of oral contraception for women

Amino Acids and Polypeptides
Amino acids contain an amino group and a carboxylic acid group
The amino and acid groups can bond to form an amide; the resulting linkage is called a peptide bond
Polypeptides or proteins are polymers of amino acids
The structural and functional diversity of proteins is huge !

Section 25.1
CLASSIFICATION OF AMINO ACIDS

Distance Between Amine and Acid
A Greek letter is used to designate the position of the amino group on the carbon chain bearing the acid
Naturally occuring amino acids are (almost) universally α -amino acids

Characteristics of Amino Acids
There are 20 amino acids that occur naturally in proteins
12 can be synthesized by the body
8 must be obtained from the diet
The standard amino acids differ in characteristics:
Size and shape
Polarity
Acidity/basicity
The side chains are the key points of difference between the amino acids
Amino Acids with Nonpolar Side Chains
Polar but Non-ionized Side Chains
Acidic Side Chains
Basic Side Chains

Nonstandard Amino Acids
Biochemical modifications can produce nonstandard amino acids
Some have also been artificially incorporated into synthetic proteins
Remarkably, DNA codes for these two nonstandard amino acids :

Section 25.2
STEREOCHEMISTRY OF AMINO ACIDS

Configurations of Amino Acids
Glycine ( R=H ) is the only achiral amino acid
The α -carbon is a stereocenter in most amino acids
Amino acids in proteins have the l configuration, but d - amino acids do occur naturally
Amino group at the left when the carboxyl group is at the top of the Fischer projection
Isoleucine and threonine include a second stereocenter in the side chain .

Section 25.3
ACID-BASE BEHAVIOR OF AMINO ACIDS

Acid-base Behavior of Glycine
The neutral formula of glycine suggests that it should be soluble in nonpolar solvents …but it isn’t!
Glycine includes an acidic functional group (carboxylic acid) and a basic functional group (amine)
The form favored in neutral solution ( pH 7 ) is a zwitterion
How does the dominant ionization state of glycine change with pH? Hint: it is never neutral !

Titration of Glycine
Respect pK a . Draw amino acids in solution as zwitterions, not neutral molecules .
− CO 2 − is deprotonated “first” …
…then − NH 3 + is deprotonated !

p K a Values and Isoelectric Point
Amino acid p K a values are influenced by the structure of the side chain and differ slightly from one another
The pH at which [zwitterion] is maximal is called the isoelectric point (pI)
I.e., the pH at which the amino acid has no net charge
The isoelectric point is the equivalence point of the titration!

Section 25.4
SYNTHESIS OF AMINO ACIDS

Nucleophilic Substitution
Ammonia reacts with α - haloacids to give α - amino acids in a nucleophilic substitution reaction
Note the need for two equivalents of ammonia, to deprotonate the acid and engage in substitution

Strecker Synthesis
The cyanide anion (CN − ) is a “masked” version of a carboxylate (CO 2 − )
Addition of cyanide to an imine followed by hydrolysis gives an α -amino acid!
The imine is generated in situ from an aldehyde
Amenable to enantioselective catalysis

α- Amino Malonates
Direct alkylation of amino acid enolates with RX is impractical. But we’ve seen this before for other enolates!
Application of the malonic ester synthesis to α -amino malonates works well and follows the spirit of enolate alkylation
Deprotonation of carbonyl compound and alkylation with RX. The amine should be protected as N -acetyl

𝛂-Amino Malonates-1
Direct alkylation of amino acid enolates with RX is impractical. But we’ve seen this before for other enolates!
Application of the malonic ester synthesis to α -amino malonates works well and follows the spirit of enolate alkylation
Hydrolysis to free the amine and carboxylates
Heat to promote decarboxylation

Section 25.5
REACTIONS OF AMINO ACIDS

Reactions of Amino Acids
Amino acids undergo many of the reactions of amines and carboxylic acids:
Acetylation of N. Very important for suppressing the nucleophilicity of nitrogen (protecting group !)
Different deprotection methods are available for amides of these groups :

Section 25.6
PEPTIDES

Peptides and Peptide Bonds
The amino group is a nucleophile and the carboxyl group (appropriately activated) an electrophile
Coupling to form an amide is known as peptide coupling ; the amide is called a peptide bond
The free amine is the N - terminus (left) and the free carboxylate is the C -terminus (right)
Each amino acid is called a residue ; the sequence of residues is called the primary sequence

Geometry of the Peptide Bond
Recall that amides are planar — the nitrogen is sp 2 hybridized !
Rotation about the C (=O )–N bond is fairly difficult. Can you draw resonance structures to illustrate why?
Hydrogen bonding of N–H and C=O (with backbone and side chains ) is extremely important for protein structure

Disulfide Bonds & Covalent Linkages
The disulfide bond is an important covalent linkage in proteins
Two cysteine residues are oxidized from 2 SH to S–S
An important structural feature in the hormone oxytocin
Side-chain amides (e.g. lysine + glutamic acid) are also observed in some protein structures (much more rare, however )

Section 25.7
INTRODUCTION TO PEPTIDE STRUCTURE DETERMINATION

Determining Polypeptide Sequence
Determining primary sequence is tough
Polypeptides contain many copies of the same type of bond
Selective cleavage is difficult
Structural differences between amino acids are subtle
Several different methods are involved in rigorous determination of peptide sequence
Determine what amino acids are present and their molar ratios.
Cleave the peptide into smaller fragments, separate these fragments, and determine the amino acid composition of the fragments.
Identify the N-terminal and the C-terminal amino acid in the original peptide and in each fragment.
Organize the information so that the amino acid sequences of small fragments can be overlapped to reveal the full sequence .

Section 25.8
AMINO ACID ANALYSIS
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Amino Acid Composition
How do we determine the amino acid composition of a protein, irrespective of its primary sequence?
Hydrolysis in 6 M hydrochloric acid cleaves all peptide bonds
Amino acids “tagged” with a fluorescent group
Chromatographic analysis (HPLC) separates and characterizes the amino acids

Section 25.9
PARTIAL HYDROLYSIS AND END GROUP ANALYSIS

Cleavage by Peptidases
Acidic hydrolysis is a blunt instrument—all peptides are cleaved
Peptidase enzymes are more selective and cleave only at certain peptide bonds (dependent on side chains)
Trypsin : cleaves when the carboxyl end of the peptide bond is lysine or arginine
Chymotrypsin : cleaves when the carboxyl end of the peptide bond has an aromatic side chain
Carboxypeptidase : cleaves at the C -terminus
How could we use carboxypeptidase to sequence a protein?

N -terminal Characterization
The N -terminus is a unique nucleophile on the peptide backbone
Nucleophilic aromatic substitution at C–F in Sanger’s reagent tags the N -terminal amino acid selectively
Subsequent hydrolysis reveals the identity of this amino acid
N -terminal Characterization-1

Section 25.10
INSULIN

Structure of Insulin
Bovine insulin consists of two polypeptide chains, A and B
Fragmentation with pepsin, trypsin, and acidic hydrolysis produced the fragments shown below
Overlapping fragments were used to determine the overall sequence.

Detailed Structure of Insulin
Disulfide bonds connect the A and B chains
Human insulin differs only in two amino acid residues

Section 25.11
EDMAN DEGRADATION AND AUTOMATED SEQUENCING OF PEPTIDES

One N -terminus at a Time
Could we cleave at the N -terminus one amino acid at a time, characterizing the protein sequentially ?
Thanks to Pehr Edman and the Edman degradation , we can!
The reagent phenyl isothiocyanate (PTC) , which forms a cleavable structure with the N -terminus only, is the key
Treat with PTC, forming a phenylthiocarbamoyl derivative
Treat with HCl to cleave the N -terminus from the chain as a phenylthiohydantoin (PTH)
Characterize the phenylthiohydantoin
Repeat steps 1 – 3 until the C -terminus is reached

Reaction with PTC
The nucleophilic N -terminus adds to the electrophilic carbon of PTC
This reaction occurs selectively at the N -terminus!

Cleavage of PTH
Treatment with HCl causes cyclization and loss of the N -terminus as a thiazolone, which isomerizes to a PTH

Limitations of Edman Degradation
Less than 100% yield in any cleavage step leaves some undegraded peptide along with products
In practice, about 20 amino acids can be sequenced using the Edman procedure
Other methods for sequencing exist:
Protein crystallography
Independent synthesis!

Section 25.12
MASS SPECTROMETRY OF PEPTIDES  AND PROTEINS

Protein Sequencing
Protein sequencing by mass spectrometry is generally used for peptides prepared by chemical synthesis, for proteins unknown to a database, and for particularly difficult peptides, such as ones that might be found in proteins with unusual post-translational modifications.
One utility of mass spectrometry in organic chemistry is that molecular ions containing certain function groups tend to break into fragments in predictable ways. The m/z of these fragments limit and often define the functional groups contained in the unknown molecule.

Tandem Mass Spectroscopy
In tandem mass spectrometry, peptide ions are deflected into a separate chamber and are broken into smaller ions (collision-induced dissociation). Each bond of the peptide backbone can fragment at the three different types of bond: CO – NH , CH – CO and NH – CH .
For simplicity, the locations of only CO – NH bond cleavages are shown. Each bond breakage produces two species, one charged and one uncharged. Charged N-terminal fragments are called B-ions, while charged C-terminal fragments are called Y-ions.

Section 25.13
THE STRATEGY OF PEPTIDE SYNTHESIS

Issues in Peptide Synthesis
We have seen methods for amide synthesis previously. Why can’t we simply apply these to synthesize polypeptides?
Every amino acid includes both the nucleophile and electrophile!
We must prevent homocoupling and “backwards” coupling
If we want Phe-Gly, we must ensure that Gly acts as the nucleophile and Phe as the electrophile
Protecting groups will be key!

Strategy for Peptide Synthesis
Protect the amino group of the N-terminal amino acid and the carboxyl group of the C-terminal amino acid.
Couple the two protected amino acids by amide bond formation between them.
Deprotect the amino group at the N terminus and the carboxyl group at the C terminus.

Section 25.14
AMINO AND CARBOXYL GROUP PROTECTION AND DEPROTECTION

Amino Group (De)protection
The amino group is protected by tying up nitrogen’s lone pair in an electrophilic π system ( C=O )
We’ve seen this before—acetylation!
tert - Butoxycarbonyl (Boc), fluorenylmethoxycarbonyl (Fmoc), benzyloxycarbonyl (Z)
Boc and Z are cleaved with acid

Amino Group (De)protection-1
The amino group is protected by tying up nitrogen’s lone pair in an electrophilic π system ( C=O )
We’ve seen this before—acetylation!
tert - Butoxycarbonyl (Bo c), fluorenylmethoxycarbonyl (Fmoc), benzyloxycarbonyl (Z)
Z can also be removed via hydrogenation with Pd; nice if acid or base sensitivity is an issue

Amino Group (De)protection-2
The amino group is protected by tying up nitrogen’s lone pair in an electrophilic π system ( C=O )
We’ve seen this before—acetylation!
tert - Butoxycarbonyl (Boc), fluorenylmethoxycarbonyl (Fmoc), benzyloxycarbonyl (Z)
Fmoc is cleaved by base

Carboxyl Group (De)protection
The carboxyl group is usually protected as an ester
How do we convert an ester to a carboxylic acid? Saponification!
Note that hydrogenolysis also cleaves off a benzyl group from benzyl esters (see last slide )

Section 25.15
PEPTIDE BOND FORMATION

Electrophilic Activation of –CO 2 H
Amines are fine nucleophiles, but carboxylic acids are not good electrophiles!
The acid must be activated as an electrophile. Basically, –OH must be transformed into a good leaving group
How might we accomplish this?

Electrophilic Activation of – CO 2 H-1
Amines are fine nucleophiles, but carboxylic acids are not good electrophiles!
The acid must be activated as an electrophile. Basically, –OH must be transformed into a good leaving group
How might we accomplish this?
Formation of acyl chloride with SOCl 2

Electrophilic Activation of – CO 2 H-2
Amines are fine nucleophiles, but carboxylic acids are not good electrophiles!
The acid must be activated as an electrophile. Basically, –OH must be transformed into a good leaving group
How might we accomplish this?
Formation of acyl chloride with SOCl 2 good in theory, but messy

Electrophilic Activation of – CO 2 H-3
Amines are fine nucleophiles, but carboxylic acids are not good electrophiles!
The acid must be activated as an electrophile. Basically, –OH must be transformed into a good leaving group
How might we accomplish this?
Carbodiimides such as dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCCI )

Electrophilic Activation of – CO 2 H-4
Amines are fine nucleophiles, but carboxylic acids are not good electrophiles!
The acid must be activated as an electrophile. Basically, –OH must be transformed into a good leaving group
How might we accomplish this?
Carbodiimides such as dicyclohexylcarbodiimide (DCCI)
Addition of the acid to DCCI generates a good leaving group, an “ isourea ”
Consider resonance stabilization in the leaving group

Peptide Coupling
The O - acylisourea formed from acid + DCCI reacts with amine in an acyl substitution process to give the amide
The mechanism involves addition followed by elimination (like amination of an acyl chloride)
What is the structure of the byproduct ?

Lengthening the Chain
The dipeptide product is deprotected at nitrogen, exposing a new nucleophile
An N -protected electrophilic amino acid is treated with DCCI, then introduced to the nucleophilic chain
Coupling occurs again!
Rinse and repeat for as many residues as needed
One issue: how to separate byproducts from chain at each step ?

Section 25.16
SOLID-PHASE PEPTIDE SYNTHESIS: THE MERRIFIELD METHOD

Solid-phase Peptide Synthesis
Anchoring the growing polypeptide chain to a solid support or resin enables easy separation of byproducts after each iteration
Since the polypeptide is in the solid phase, this method is called solid-phase peptide synthesis
Vastly expanded the scope and ease of polypeptide synthesis; concept extended to other types of synthesis
Carboxylate of the C -terminal amino acid reacts with the electrophilic resin, establishing the “anchor”

Preparatory Steps
Anchoring to resin
Deprotection of N

Coupling Steps
Peptide coupling
Deprotection of new amine
Repeat these steps to lengthen chain to desired length and sequence .
Isolation of Polypeptide

Section 25.17
SECONDARY STRUCTURES OF PEPTIDES AND PROTEINS

Secondary Structure
Polypeptides exhibit certain long-range conformational patterns; these patterns are called secondary structures
Held in place by hydrogen bonding and ionic interactions between backbone or side-chain groups
The two most important secondary structures are α -helices and β -sheets
In both structures, N–H ––– O=C hydrogen bonding is key

β - Sheets : Planar Structures
Adjacent strands in an anti conformation line up so that C=O groups are across from N–H groups
May be parallel or antiparallel —note positions of the termini

β-Sheets: Planar Structures-1
Adjacent strands in an anti conformation line up so that C=O groups are across from N–H groups
Side chains alternate above and below the plane of the sheet

α -Helices : Tube-like Structures
C=O and N–H groups align “vertically,” resulting in regularly spaced hydrogen bonds
Side chains stick out from the helical core
Most helices are right handed as in this figure ; some are left handed

Overall Protein Structure
Not all amino acid residues are part of α -helices or β -sheets
Large regions of random coils connect these secondary structures
We often see secondary structures arranged in higher-order patterns .

Section 25.18
TERTIARY STRUCTURE OF POLYPEPTIDES AND PROTEINS

Tertiary Structure
Higher-order arrangements of helices and sheets encountered in many proteins are called tertiary structure
A common classification scheme distinguishes globular and fibrous proteins
Where are hydrophobic residues likely to be in globular proteins ?

Tertiary Structure-1
Higher-order arrangements of helices and sheets encountered in many proteins are called tertiary structure
A common classification scheme distinguishes globular and fibrous proteins
Where are hydrophobic residues likely to be in globular proteins ?
The hydrophobic effect causes nonpolar residues to occupy the center of globular proteins.
Polar residues can be found on the periphery .

Origins of Higher-order Structure
Ultimately, the primary sequence of a protein determines its secondary and tertiary structure
Covalent and (mostly) noncovalent interactions between side chains drive the folding of proteins
Predicting the folded state of a protein from its primary sequence remains extremely difficult, but …
We understand the interactions responsible for folding
Interactions in Proteins

Why Understand Folding?
The function of a protein is intimately related to its conformational structure (i.e., how it is folded)
For example, enzymes are proteins that catalyze chemical reactions
Enzymes contain a pocket called the active site where the reaction actually occurs
The shape of the active site and residues within affect stereoselectivity, reaction rate, and product structure

Section 25.19
COENZYMES

Coenzymes: Enzymatic Helpers
The reactivity of enzymatic side chains is limited (more or less) to proton transfers and addition to carbonyls
Coenzymes perform additional chemistry inside active sites
Cofactors are not covalently bound to the enzyme (e.g. NAD + /NADH )
Prosthetic groups are bound to the enzyme (e.g. heme , PLP )
Heme is a critical cofactor in hemoglobin that binds the O 2 we breathe .

Section 26.20
PROTEIN QUATERNARY STRUCTURE: HEMOGLOBIN

Quaternary Structure & Multiple Chains
Some proteins contain multiple polypeptide chains with unique functions
The arrangements of these chains are called the quaternary structure of the protein
For example, hemoglobin includes four chains (two α and two β ) arranged to bind O 2 optimally
Modification of a single residue in patients with sickle-cell anemia leads to critical disruption of quaternary structure

Quaternary Structure & Multiple Chains-1
The X-ray crystal structure of carboxypeptidase A locates this Zn2+ ion in a hydrophobic cavity near the center of the enzyme.
The substrate in the case of carboxypeptidase A is a peptide, especially a peptide with a hydrophobic C-terminal amino acid such as phenylalanine or tyrosine.

Section 25.21
COENZYMES IN REACTIONS OF AMINO ACIDS

Coenzymes in Reactions of Amino Acids
These substances are called prosthetic groups or cofactors , and they can be organic or inorganic. Enzymes that require a prosthetic group for catalytic activity are called coenzymes .
The protein portion of the coenzyme is called the apoenzyme , and the combination of the protein and cofactor is called the holoenzyme .
Coenzymes with particular cofactors tend to catalyze similar transformations .
Coenzymes that contain pyridoxal pyrophosphate ( PLP ) , the active form of vitamin B6 , catalyze reactions that involve carbanionic intermediates.

Transimination-1
All PLP - containing coenzymes contain an imine bond between the protein and the cofactor in the resting state.
The imine is the result of a chemical reaction between PLP and a lysine residue in the enzyme active site .

Transimination-2
Reaction of the enzyme-bound PLP with an amino acid substrate exchanges one imine linkage for another. This is a transimination reaction .

Decarboxylation
The pyridine ring of PLP, especially when protonated, facilitates several kinds of reactions at the amino acid’s α carbon by acting as an electron-withdrawing group. One reaction is decarboxylation .

Synthetic Polymers

Synthetic Polymers
Polymers are composed of macromolecules , very long molecules containing identical repeating units
Polymers are not necessarily homogeneous!
Reactions used to prepare polymers are fundamentally the same as reactions of small molecules we’ve already seen

SOME BACKGROUND
Section 27.1

A Brief History of Polymers
1839: Goodyear treats rubber with sulfur to strengthen it (vulcanization)
1910: Bakeland patents the plastic Bakelite , the first fully synthetic polymer
Most chemists believe polymers to be colloidal dispersions of small molecules
1920 – 1929: Staudinger, Carothers, and others convincingly demonstrate that polymers have high molecular weight
Structural studies and polymer synthesis accelerate rapidly; polymers become an industry

POLYMER NOMENCLATURE
Section 27.2

Polymer Nomenclature
Polymers are typically named according to source-based nomenclature as poly(monomers)
The name of the monomer is prefixed with poly-
The number of repeating units n is assumed to be large
Some polymers made from the same source with different molecular weights have different names
Poly(ethylene oxide) or poly(ethylene glycol)
Some polymers, such as Teflon, are best known by their trade names.

CLASSIFICATION OF POLYMERS: REACTION TYPE
Section 27.3

Classification of Polymers
There are many different classification schemes for polymers

Addition Polymers
Polymers formed via additions across double bonds (usually C=C , but not always) are called addition polymers
A single monomer produces a homopolymer , while two different monomers give a copolymer
Polymers prepared from alkenes are known as polyolefins .

Condensation Polymers
Condensation polymers are derived from substitution processes that release a small molecule as a leaving group
The key is to use two different difunctional monomers, giving an alternating polymeric product
Polyesters, polyamides, and polycarbonates
What type of reaction is occurring here?!

CLASSIFICATION OF POLYMERS: CHAIN GROWTH AND STEP GROWTH
Section 27.4

Chain Growth
A chain-growth polymer grows from one end of the polymer chain—the other end is “capped”
The end of the chain can react only with monomer; chain ends cannot couple with one another
Generally (but not always!) associated with addition polymerization

Step Growth
In a step-growth process, chain ends can couple with one another
Short, oligomeric chains couple with one another to create long polymer chains
Monomer is rapidly consumed and coupling of oligomers follows
Generally (but not always!) associated with condensation polymerization

CLASSIFICATION OF POLYMERS: STRUCTURE
Section 27.5

Types of Polymer Structure
Linear polymers (e.g., the vast majority of proteins) consist of single chains without branching
Branched polymers contain internal branches
Cross-linked polymers have branches that connect multiple polymer chains

Types of Polymer Structure-1
Cross-linking increases the strength and solidity of polymers relative to unlinked polymer.

Polymer Crystallinity
Most polymers contain random coil regions interspersed with regions of regular structure (secondary structure!) called crystallites
More crystalline polymers tend to be more dense and tougher

Classification of Polymers: Properties
Section 27.6
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Thermal Response
Thermoplastic polymers soften when heated
Glass transition: phase change from a solid to a rubbery state at T g (leading gradually to liquification for amorphous polymers)
Melting transition: crystalline polymers transform from a solid to a liquid state at a specific temperature T m
Thermosetting polymers pass through a liquid state and then solidify
Irreversible reactions create crosslinks between polymer chains, which eventually force the polymer into a solid state
Examples include bakelite and melamine (used in plastic dinnerware)

Addition polymers: A review and a Preview
Section 27.7
Review of Alkene Polymerizations
Review of Alkene Polymerizations-1

Initiation of Polymerization
Radical initiators such as AIBN and peroxides are used to initiate radical polymerization
Transition-metal catalysts are used for coordination polymerization

Termination of Alkene Polymerization
In radical polymerization, reactions between radical chains cause polymerization to terminate
Direct coupling of two radical chains together
Disproportionation via hydrogen atom abstraction

Termination of Alkene Polymerization-1
In coordination polymerizations, elimination of a β -hydrogen from the chain produces an alkene and terminates the chain

Chain Branching in Free-radical Polymerization
Section 27.8

Branched versus Linear PE
Radical polymerization produces branched , low-density polyethyelene; coordination polymerization produces linear PE
Why does radical polymerization produced branched polymers ?
Note that the propagation step of polymerization cannot explain the presence of branches; side reactions are necessary
These side reactions produce internal rather than terminal radicals via abstraction of a hydrogen atom within the chain

Intramolecular Hydrogen Abstraction
Internal abstraction gives a four-carbon branch and creates a new site for polymer growth:
This process is exothermic and kinetically favorable due to the six-membered cyclic transition state

Intermolecular Hydrogen Abstraction-1
A growing chain abstracts hydrogen from an internal carbon on a terminated chain, giving a new growth site!
Growth from the new radical gives a branched polymer

Anionic polymerization: Living Polymers
Section 27.9

Anionic Polymerization
Organometallic intiators are associated with anionic polymerization , which involves anionic intermediates
Certain advantages over other polymerization methods
Proceeds via rapid addition of Bu − to alkene, then repeated slower addition of resulting benzylic anion to monomer

Living Polymers
After repeated nucleophilic additions, the polymer remains an organolithium compound
This is still an excellent nucleophile—polymerization continues if additional monomer is added!
Because the polymer “responds” to additional monomer, it is called a living polymer
Living Polymers & Block Copolymerization

CATIONIC POLYMERIZATION
Section 27.10

Cationic Polymerization
Lewis or Brønsted acids can serve as initators for cationic polymerization
BF 3 containing a small amount of water is a common choice, and generates a strong Brønsted acid

Mechanism of Cationic Polymerization
Step 1: protonation of monomer to give a carbocation
Step 2: electrophilic addition to another monomer
Step 3: repeat step 2 many times

Termination
Termination is caused by processes we’re familiar with from nucleophilic substitution and elimination chemistry
Loss of a proton (E1 elimination)
Coordination of a nucleophile (“ S N 1-like ”)

Polyamides
Section 27.11
©2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education .

Polyamides
Nylon is a polyamide made from condensation of an amine with a carboxylic acid
Nylon 66 comes from a six-carbon diacid and six-carbon diamine
Nylon 6 comes from a six-carbon amino acid
Polyamides can hydrogen bond much like polypeptide chains

Polyesters
Section 27.12

Polyesters
Similar to nylon 66, poly(ethylene terephthalate) or PETE is made from a diacid and a diol
Alkyd resins are made from glycerol and o -phthalic acid

Polyesters-1
Lactic acid and glycolic acid have the functional groups necessary for polyester formation
Heating forms a dimer, which polymerizes upon exposure to a Lewis acid catalyst

Polycarbonates
Section 27.13

Polycarbonates
The polycarbonate Lexan is derived from bisphenol A and the highly toxic phosgene
Transparent plastic with high impact resistance; used in glasses, helmets, CDs, cell phones, …

Polyurethanes
Section 27.14

Polyurethanes
Diisocyanate is usually mixed with polymeric diol
Note that this is addition polymerization, but it occurs via a step-growth mechanism.

Copolymers
Section 27.15

Copolymers
Copolymers contain two or more monomers
Greater flexibility and control of properties is possible for copolymers than homopolymers
Three types:
Block copolymers
Random copolymers
Graft copolymers

Random Copolymers
Monomer units are incorporated into chains at similar rates, leading to a (more or less) random distribution of monomers
Good example is styrene-butadiene rubber, which is a random copolymer formed via anionic or radical polymerization

Block Copolymers
Well defined blocks of monomers A and B alternate along the polymer chain
Living polymerization methods are great for preparation of block copolymers: add and use up A, add and use up B, add A again, etc.
SBR can be prepared as a block copolymer using living anionic polymerization

Graft Copolymers
A main chain of one monomer is prepared, then branches of a second monomer are “grafted” onto the main chain
A graft copolymer of styrene and 1,3-butadiene is a tough material called “high-impact polystrene”
1,3-butadiene is polymerized, then a radical initiator is used to generate alkyl radicals within the chain, which serve as sites for polymerization of stryene

Graft Copolymerization
A radical initiator abstracts hydrogen from the poly(butadiene) chain
Addition to styrene kicks off formation of a polystyrene branch

Graft Copolymerization-1
Addition to styrene kicks off formation of a polystyrene branch
Polystyrene grafts on a poly(1,3-butadiene) chain are the result.

Experiment 4: GC/MS Analysis of Essential Oils
CHEM-C 344

Outline
About mass spectrometry
MS instrumentation
molecular ion (M)
isotopes and alkyl halides (M + 2)
fragmentation -alkanes (most stable carbocation) -aldehydes/ketones ( α cleavage) -alcohols ( α cleavage) -amines ( α cleavage)
Using GC/MS for analysis of mixtures
Mass spectral libraries
[Speaker Notes] Draw possible products

About mass spectrometry
A mass spectrum gives information on the molecular weight and structure of a molecule
Use it to confirm/identify structures by observing isotope patterns, fragmentation patterns
High resolution mass spectrometry (HRMS) can be used to unambiguously confirm a particular molecular formula
Three stages: 1) ionization; 2) ion separation; 3) ion detection

The Mass Spectrometer
How can you tell the difference?
Melting point?
IR spectroscopy?
1 H NMR spectroscopy
[Speaker Notes] Draw possible products

Ionization
Converts a neutral molecule into an ion
Ionization methods
Electron impact (EI) ionization  knocks out electron
Electrospray ionization (ESI)  protonates/deprotonates ionizable groups
Matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization (MALDI)
EI ionization:

Mass spectrum = histogram of all the different ions detected
Some molecules are too fragile for EI ionization
Mass spectrum = histogram of all the different ions detected

Isotope Patterns – how to identify presence of Br & Cl
Different isotopes can be observed on mass spec
Isotope Patterns – how to identify presence of Br & Cl

Isotope Patterns
Different isotopes show up on mass spec

Outline
About mass spectrometry
MS instrumentation
molecular ion (M)
isotopes and alkyl halides (M + 2)
fragmentation -alkanes (most stable carbocation) -aldehydes/ketones ( α cleavage) -alcohols ( α cleavage) -amines ( α cleavage)
Using GC/MS for analysis of mixtures
Mass spectral libraries
[Speaker Notes] Draw possible products
Fragmentation patterns in alkanes

Fragmentation patterns in alkanes
More stable ions have a higher intensity on mass spectrum (Think carbocation stability)

Fragmentation patterns in aldehydes and ketones
Often observe α cleavage to form resonance-stabilized acylium ion
Example: methyl ethyl ketone

Fragmentation patterns in amines
Often observe α cleavage to form resonance-stabilized carbocation

Fragmentation patterns in alcohols
Often observe: 1) α cleavage to form resonance-stabilized carbocation
2) dehydration
Example: cyclohexanol

Your Experiment

GC-MS Analysis of Orange Extract
[Speaker Notes] Draw possible products

Library Search
[Speaker Notes] Draw possible products

Library Search
[Speaker Notes] Draw possible products

1.
As you should know, this is a​ free radical halogenation reaction ​and even if you don’t 
know, don’t worry, the hint here is that you see​ light (hν)​ on or under the reaction 
arrow, indicating that there are radicals involved. So the ​characteristic of a stable 
radical apart from being a benzylic is the more substituted the carbon atom, the 
more stable the corresponding radical, so the major product of this reaction is the 
3º alkyl halide. 
 
 
2.
a)​ There are​ five different carbons in 2-methylbutane, so hypothetically, you 
should get five different products, 20% A, 20% B, 20% C, 20% D, and 20% E.​ But 
notice that A and B are the same product!​ So ​only four products​ are possible 
(A/B , C, D, E) 
 
  


=== PAGE BREAK ===

b)​ If you chose A (or B) to be your answer for the major product, well… sadly you’re 
wrong. Because do remember in Question 1, we mentioned that apart from benzylic 
radicals,​ the most substituted radical is also the most stable​. ​A/B and E are 
primary radicals while D is a secondary radical so, the major product will be C, 
which is tertiary. 
This happens because the intermediate is a radical, and radical stability goes 3° > 2° 
> 1°. 
  
c) ​You should know that ​chlorine reacts faster than bromine​, and so this means 
that ​chlorine is less selective than bromine​. 
To make it much more relatable, Bromine is the careful meticulous person who does 
things at their own pace and is attentive to details and gets good results in the end 
while Chlorine is that person who thinks, “As long as I get the job done, I’m good.”  
 
So for example, since we have concluded that the 3° halide will be the major 
product:  
-
With chlorine, the proportion might turn out to be ~70% for 3° radical , 20% 
for 2° radical, and 10% for 1° radical. 
-
But with bromine who reacts more slowly, you will get better selectivity 
(better yield). The proportion of products might be something like ~95% for 
3° radical  , 4% for 2° radical, and 1% for 1° radical. 
 
3. The hint here is to start with a carbonyl and a Grignard reagent. Since we have 
been using MgBr mostly as a Grignard reagent, you just have to decide the ‘tail end’ 
that connects to the -MgBr part. Then decide on your carbonyl by looking at the 
product.  
Imagine the whole structure like a puzzle piece or a paper where you can just cut 
into two fragments that can form back the whole picture. The dotted lines are 
where you use your scissors to cut.  
For: 
a) ​the alcohol is a tertiary alcohol with a tail of 4 carbons (including the primary 
carbon). So choose whether you’d like your carbonyl to have that tail there or you’d 
like to add your tail from the -MgBr.  
b) ​There are two rings in your alcohol product. So you can make life easier by having 
one ring each from the carbonyl and from the Grignard (MgBr).   


=== PAGE BREAK ===

 
 
 
 
4. I know this might be confusing… but it’s just some extra practice/knowledge and 
just in case that you have a cyclic ring ketone or aldehyde, it’ll break open the ring 
and form a straight chain alcohol. This is basically the whole reaction mechanism. 
*If you struggle with carbons, ALWAYS MAKE SURE TO NUMBER YOUR 
CARBONS* 
In summary, reduced by (attacked by) the negative H from LiAlH4 and oxygen1 
takes electrons (removing double bond) →​ ​oxygen1 shares back its electron 
(forming aldehyde) and oxygen2 takes electron (breaking the ring forming 
negatively charged oxygen2) →​ ​the negative H attacks again because oxygen1 is 
stubborn and it takes back its electron again forming negative charge →​ water 
gives away it’s H (attacked by oxygen1 and oxygen2) and finally the alcohol is 
formed!  


=== PAGE BREAK ===

 
 
5. Just analyze the side groups and note which one is an electron withdrawing group or 
an electron donating group.

Useful Spectroscopy Tables 
 
CHEM-C 341 
 
  
Fall 2020 
Here are some useful tables & charts for spectroscopy problems with differing levels of detail: 
 
Regions of the 1H NMR Spectrum 
 
      
   
 
 
 
 


=== PAGE BREAK ===

 
 
Regions of the 13C NMR Spectrum 
      
 
 
 
Regions of the IR Spectrum 
 
 
 


=== PAGE BREAK ===

 
 
Tips for Interpreting Spectra 
1. Calculate double bond equivalents using given molecular formula or use mass spectrometry 
data to arrive at the formula. (Look for isotope patterns in MS to identify Cl and Br in the 
formula.) Propose possible functional groups. 
2. Use any IR spectroscopy data to confirm or rule out functional groups 
3. Look for symmetry by a) comparing hydrogen count in formula versus 1H NMR spectrum and b) 
comparing total number of 13C signals 
4. Look at characteristic chemical shifts in NMR spectra to determine functional groups present 
5. Guess at possible structures until you arrive at a structure that is consistent with all signals/data 
Tips: 
-integral value of 3 usually means methyl group (6 means two equivalent -CH3 groups) 
-integral value of 2 usually means methylene (CH2) groups (4 means two equiv. -CH2- groups) 
-CH2 or CH group 2.5-4 ppm generally is attached to an electronegative atom 
-peaks at 7-8 ppm mean there is a benzene ring, integration tells you how many substituents 
-use splitting patterns to make different fragments – then put the whole molecule together 
 
 
An equation for calculating double bond equivalents (DBE) 
 
𝐷𝐵𝐸= 2#𝐶+ #𝑁−(#𝐻+ #𝑋)

Rules of Engagement
Part 1: Isolation, Purification, and Melting Point of Unknown
• Bring only a writing implement. You will be given blank paper at the 
beginning of the laboratory period. 
• Do not bring your textbook or laboratory notebook.
• Specific procedural questions should be addressed to the instructor.
• Turn in your written work at the end of the laboratory period (5 hours).
Part 2: Spectroscopic Identification of Unknown
• You will be given your written work from and additional blank paper. You 
may also bring pages 16 - 27 of the lab practical notes.
• After reporting the melting point, the instructor will give you a packet of 
data that has the empirical formula, IR spectrum and 1H and 13C NMR 
spectra of the unknown.
• From the empirical formula and melting point, preliminarily identify your 
unknown by a literature search (e.g., Aldrich catalog). Based on the 
melting point and spectroscopic data, defend the identification of your 
unknown.
• Turn in your full report.
